冥祥记 - Records of Netherworld Auspicious Signs

    Home

    《冥祥记》[南朝齐]王琰
    　　
    　　　自序 [1]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [1]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 自序 - Formal title meaning 'self-introduction' or 'preface,' commonly used in classical Chinese texts to introduce the author or the work.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 冥祥記 - Title suggesting a collection of stories or accounts related to supernatural phenomena, omens, or divine interventions, common in classical Chinese literature.
    • 南朝齊 - Southern Qi Dynasty, a period of political fragmentation and cultural flourishing in southern China (479–502 CE).
    • 王琰 - Author's name, likely a historian or scholar from the Southern Qi period.

    Proper Nouns

    • 冥祥記 - Title of the text, rendered as 'Records of the Supernatural' or 'Narratives of the Mysterious and Auspicious.'
    • 南朝齊 - Southern Qi Dynasty, rendered as 'Southern Qi' in English.
    • 王琰 - Author's name, transliterated as 'Wang Yan.'

    Preface

    Records of the Supernatural [Southern Qi] Wang Yan

    This translation maintains the formal tone of the original text while providing clear context for the reader. The title "冥祥记" is rendered as "Records of the Supernatural" to convey the genre of the text, and the author and dynasty are transliterated as "Wang Yan" and "Southern Qi," respectively. The opening line "自序" is translated as "Preface" to indicate the introductory nature of the text. [1]

    琰稚年在交阯。彼土有贤法师者，道德僧也。见授五戒，以观世音金像一躯，见与供养；形制异今，又非甚古，类元嘉中作。镕镌殊工，似有真好。琰奉以还都。时年在龆龀，与二弟常尽勤至，专精不倦。后治改弊庐，无屋安设，寄京师南涧寺中。于时百姓竞铸钱，亦有盗毁金像以充铸者。时像在寺，已经数月。琰昼寝，梦见立于座隅；意甚异之。时日已?，即驰迎还。其夕，南涧十余躯像，悉遇盗亡。其后久之，像于曛?闲放光。显照，三尺许地，金辉秀起，焕然夺目。琰兄弟及仆役同睹者十余人。于时幼小，不即题记；比加撰录，忘其日月；是宋大明七年秋也。至泰始末，琰移居乌衣，周旋僧以此像权寓多宝寺。琰时暂游江都，此僧仍适荆楚；不知像处，垂将十载。常恐神宝，与因俱绝。宋升明末，游踬峡表，经过江陵，见此沙门，乃知像所。其年，琰还京师，即造多宝寺访焉。寺主爱公，云无此寄像。琰退虑此僧孟浪，将遂失此像，深以惆怅。 [2]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [2]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 琰稚年在交阯 - Yan was young when he was in Jiaozhi, where '稚年' means 'young age' and '交阯' refers to the ancient Chinese region of Jiaozhi (modern-day northern Vietnam).
    • 彼土有賢法师者，道德僧也 - There was a virtuous monk in that land, a man of morality, where '賢法师' means 'virtuous monk' and '道德僧' means 'monk of virtue.'
    • 见授五戒，以观世音金像一躯，见与供养 - He was given the Five Precepts and a gold statue of Guanyin, which he was entrusted to worship, where '见授' means 'was given' and '供养' means 'worship' or 'venerate.'
    • 形制异今，又非甚古，类元嘉中作 - Its shape is different from today's, and it is not very ancient, likely made during the Yuanjia period, where '形制' means 'shape' or 'form,' and '元嘉' refers to the reign of Emperor Wen of Liu Song (424–453 CE).
    • 镕镌殊工，似有真好 - The casting and engraving are exceptionally fine, as if they are genuine and exquisite, where '镕镌' means 'casting and engraving,' and '殊工' means 'exceptionally fine work.'
    • 琰奉以还都 - Yan respectfully brought it back to the capital, where '奉以' means 'respectfully brought.'
    • 时年在龆龀 - At that time, he was in his childhood, where '龆龀' refers to early childhood.
    • 治改弊庐，无屋安设 - When he renovated his dilapidated house, there was no room to place it, where '弊庐' means 'dilapidated house' and '安设' means 'to place' or 'to set up.'
    • 寄京师南涧寺中 - He temporarily placed it in the Nanjian Temple in the capital, where '寄' means 'temporarily placed' and '京师' means 'capital.'
    • 百姓竞铸钱，亦有盗毁金像以充铸者 - The common people were competing to mint coins, and some even destroyed the gold statue to use the metal for casting, where '竞铸' means 'competing to mint' and '盗毁' means 'destroyed secretly.'
    • 时像在寺，已经数月 - The statue had been in the temple for several months, where '已经' means 'had been' and '数月' means 'several months.'
    • 琰昼寝，梦见立于座隅 - Yan napped during the day and dreamed that the statue stood by his side, where '昼寝' means 'napped during the day' and '座隅' means 'by his side.'
    • 意甚异之 - He found this very strange, where '意甚异之' means 'found it very strange.'
    • 时日已?，即驰迎还 - When the sun had set, he immediately rushed to bring it back, where '时日已?' means 'when the sun had set' and '驰迎' means 'rushed to bring.'
    • 其夕，南涧十余躯像，悉遇盗亡 - That night, over ten statues in the Nanjian Temple were all stolen, where '悉遇盗亡' means 'all were stolen.'
    • 其后久之，像于曛?闲放光 - After a long time, the statue began to emit light in the twilight, where '曛?' means 'twilight' and '放光' means 'emit light.'
    • 显照，三尺许地，金辉秀起，焕然夺目 - The light illuminated about three feet of ground, and the golden radiance was dazzling, where '显照' means 'illuminate,' '金辉秀起' means 'golden radiance rising,' and '焕然夺目' means 'dazzling.'
    • 琰兄弟及仆役同睹者十余人 - Yan, his brothers, and over ten servants witnessed this together, where '同睹' means 'witnessed together.'
    • 于时幼小，不即题记 - At that time, being young, he did not immediately record it, where '不即题记' means 'did not immediately record.'
    • 比加撰录，忘其日月 - When he later compiled a record, he forgot the date and month, where '比加撰录' means 'when he later compiled a record' and '忘其日月' means 'forgot the date and month.'
    • 是宋大明七年秋也 - This was in the autumn of the seventh year of the Daming era of the Liu Song dynasty (466 CE), where '宋大明七年' refers to the seventh year of the Daming era.
    • 至泰始末，琰移居乌衣，周旋僧以此像权寓多宝寺 - By the end of the Taishi era, Yan moved to Wuyi, and the monk entrusted the statue to the Dabao Temple for temporary storage, where '周旋僧' means 'the monk' and '权寓' means 'temporarily stored.'
    • 琰时暂游江都，此僧仍适荆楚 - When Yan briefly traveled to Jiangdu, the monk went to Jingchu, where '暂游' means 'briefly traveled' and '适荆楚' means 'went to Jingchu.'
    • 不知像处，垂将十载 - He did not know the location of the statue for nearly ten years, where '垂将十载' means 'nearly ten years.'
    • 常恐神宝，与因俱绝 - He often feared that the divine treasure would be lost along with the opportunity, where '常恐' means 'often feared' and '与因俱绝' means 'lost along with the opportunity.'
    • 宋升明末，游踬峡表，经过江陵，见此沙门，乃知像所 - At the end of the Shengming era of the Liu Song dynasty, when he traveled through the Xiang River area and passed through Jiangling, he saw this monk and learned the location of the statue, where '游踬峡表' means 'traveled through the Xiang River area' and '乃知像所' means 'learned the location of the statue.'
    • 其年，琰还京师，即造多宝寺访焉 - That year, Yan returned to the capital and immediately visited the Dabao Temple to inquire about it, where '即造' means 'immediately visited.'
    • 寺主爱公，云无此寄像 - The abbot, Aigong, said there was no such statue in the temple, where '寺主' means 'abbot' and '云无此寄像' means 'said there was no such statue.'
    • 琰退虑此僧孟浪，将遂失此像，深以惆怅 - Yan was worried that the monk had acted rashly and that he might lose the statue, deeply lamenting this, where '退虑' means 'was worried' and '深以惆怅' means 'deeply lamenting.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 交阯 - The ancient Chinese name for the region that is now northern Vietnam.
    • 五戒 - The Five Precepts, which are basic ethical guidelines in Buddhism, including abstaining from killing, stealing, sexual misconduct, lying, and intoxicants.
    • 观世音 - Guanyin, the bodhisattva of compassion in Mahayana Buddhism.
    • 元嘉 - The reign title of Emperor Wen of Liu Song (424–453 CE).
    • 大明七年 - The seventh year of the Daming era of the Liu Song dynasty (466 CE).
    • 泰始末 - The end of the Taishi era of the Liu Song dynasty (472–479 CE).
    • 乌衣 - A district in Nanjing, known for its historical significance and association with the elite.
    • 多宝寺 - The Dabao Temple, a Buddhist temple in Nanjing.
    • 江都 - An ancient city, now part of Yangzhou, Jiangsu Province.
    • 荆楚 - An ancient region in central and western China, roughly corresponding to modern-day Hubei and Hunan provinces.
    • 升明 - The reign title of Emperor Houzhu of Liu Song (477–479 CE).
    • 峡表 - The area around the Xiang River, which flows through Hunan Province.
    • 江陵 - An ancient city, now part of Jingzhou, Hubei Province.
    • 沙门 - A term for Buddhist monks.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • 琰稚年在交阯 - This phrase introduces the setting and the young age of Yan.
    • 彼土有贤法师者，道德僧也 - This clause introduces the virtuous monk and his moral qualities.

    When Yan was young, he was in Jiaozhi. In that land, there was a virtuous monk, a man of morality. He was given the Five Precepts and a gold statue of Guanyin, which he was entrusted to worship. The statue's shape is different from today's, and it is not very ancient, likely made during the Yuanjia period. The casting and engraving are exceptionally fine, as if they are genuine and exquisite. Yan respectfully brought it back to the capital. At that time, he was in his childhood, and with his two younger brothers, they devoted themselves to worshiping the statue with unwavering dedication and tireless effort. Later, when he renovated his dilapidated house, there was no room to place the statue, so he temporarily placed it in the Nanjian Temple in the capital. At that time, the common people were competing to mint coins, and some even destroyed the gold statue to use the metal for casting. The statue had been in the temple for several months. Yan napped during the day and dreamed that the statue stood by his side. He found this very strange. When the sun had set, he immediately rushed to bring it back. That night, over ten statues in the Nanjian Temple were all stolen. After a long time, the statue began to emit light in the twilight. The light illuminated about three feet of ground, and the golden radiance was dazzling. Yan, his brothers, and over ten servants witnessed this together. At that time, being young, he did not immediately record it. When he later compiled a record, he forgot the date and month. This was in the autumn of the seventh year of the Daming era of the Liu Song dynasty (466 CE). By the end of the Taishi era, Yan moved to Wuyi, and the monk entrusted the statue to the Dabao Temple for temporary storage. When Yan briefly traveled to Jiangdu, the monk went to Jingchu. He did not know the location of the statue for nearly ten years. He often feared that the divine treasure would be lost along with the opportunity. At the end of the Shengming era of the Liu Song dynasty, when he traveled through the Xiang River area and passed through Jiangling, he saw this monk and learned the location of the statue. That year, Yan returned to the capital and immediately visited the Dabao Temple to inquire about it. The abbot, Aigong, said there was no such statue in the temple. Yan was worried that the monk had acted rashly and that he might lose the statue, deeply lamenting this. [2]

    其夜，梦人见语云，像在多宝，爱公忘耳，当为得之。见将至寺，与人手自开殿，见像在殿之东众小像中，的的分明。诘旦造寺，具以所梦请爱公。爱公乃为开殿，果见此像在殿之东，如梦所睹。遂得像还。时建元元年七月十三日也。像今常自供养，唐释道宣三宝感通录卷二引像今常自供养至末庶必永作津梁。循复其事，有感深怀；沿此征觌，缀成斯记。夫镜接近情，莫踰仪像；瑞验之发，多自此兴。 [3]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [3]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 的的分明 - Emphasizes clarity; means 'clearly and distinctly.'
    • 循复其事 - Means 'following up on this matter' or 'revisiting.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 多寶 - Refers to a place of great value or a temple, possibly a Buddhist temple.
    • 爱公 - Refers to a person of respect, likely a monk or a respected individual.
    • 建元元年 - Refers to the first year of the Jianyuan era, a specific period in Chinese history.
    • 唐释道宣 - Refers to the Tang Dynasty monk Daoxuan, known for his works on Buddhist texts.
    • 三宝感通录 - A text compiled by Daoxuan, which records various miraculous events and their significance.
    • 津梁 - A metaphor for a bridge or a means of connection, often used in religious contexts to denote a spiritual link.
    • 夫镜接近情，莫踰仪像 - A philosophical statement suggesting that while a mirror reflects emotions, an image (likely a religious or artistic one) has a deeper emotional and spiritual impact.
    • 瑞验之发，多自此兴 - A statement indicating that many miraculous signs and events originate from the image or the place where the image is found.

    Proper Nouns

    • 多寶 - A place of great value or a temple.
    • 爱公 - A respected individual, likely a monk.
    • 建元元年 - The first year of the Jianyuan era.
    • 唐释道宣 - The Tang Dynasty monk Daoxuan.
    • 三宝感通录 - A text compiled by Daoxuan.
    • 津梁 - A metaphor for a bridge or a means of connection.
    • 夫镜接近情，莫逾仪像 - A philosophical statement.
    • 瑞验之发，多自此兴 - A statement about miraculous signs.

    That night, I dreamed of someone who spoke to me, saying that the image was in the treasure, and you, Master Ai, have forgotten it, but you will obtain it. I saw [someone] leading me to the temple, and with the help of someone, I opened the hall myself. I found the image clearly among other smaller images in the east of the hall. The next morning, I visited the temple and reported my dream in full detail to Master Ai. Master Ai then opened the hall, and indeed, I found the image in the east of the hall, just as I had seen in my dream. Thus, I obtained the image and returned. It was on the thirteenth day of the seventh month of the first year of Jianyuan. The image is now always kept for my own veneration. In the second volume of the 'San Bao Gan Tong Lu' by Tang Dynasty monk Daoxuan, it is recorded that the image is now always kept for my own veneration, and it is hoped that it will forever serve as a bridge. Following up on this matter, I am deeply moved; following this line of inquiry, I have compiled this record. The mirror is close to emotion, but nothing surpasses the image; miraculous signs often arise from this. [3]

    经云，镕斲图缋类形相者，爰能行动，及放光明。今西域释迦弥勒二像，晖用若冥，一引作真盖得相乎。今华夏景楷，一引作东今夏景摸　感通彔冥作真楷作模神应亟着，亦或当年群生，因会所感，假冯木石，以见幽异，不必克由容好，而能然也。故沈石浮深，实阐闽吴之化；尘金泻液，用舒彭宋之祸。感通录作用绵彭宋之福其余铨示繁方，虽难曲辨；率其大抵，允归自从。感通录作允归日从若夫经塔显效，旨证亦同；事非殊贯，故继其末。法苑珠林十七又十四引像今常自供养已下 [4]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [4]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 經云 - Classical phrase meaning 'according to the scriptures' or 'as the scriptures say.'
    • 镕斲图缋类形相者 - Compound phrase: '镕斲' (róng zhuó) refers to the process of refining and shaping metal; '图缋' (tú huì) refers to painting or illustration; '类形相者' (lèi xíng xiàng zhě) refers to those who create images or representations.
    • 爰能行动 - Means 'thus they can move' or 'therefore they can act.'
    • 及放光明 - Means 'and emit light' or 'and shine forth.'
    • 晖用若冥 - Means 'the light is as if in darkness' or 'the light is dim.'
    • 一引作真 - Variant reading that suggests 'one interpretation makes it true.'
    • 真楷作模 - Means 'true model' or 'authentic example.'
    • 神应亟着 - Means 'divine responses are often recorded' or 'divine manifestations are frequently documented.'
    • 因会所感 - Means 'due to the occasion or event, they are moved.'
    • 假冯木石 - Means 'relying on wood and stone' or 'using wood and stone as a medium.'
    • 以见幽异 - Means 'to reveal the mysterious and extraordinary.'
    • 沈石浮深 - Refers to a story where a stone sinks and then floats, symbolizing a transformation or revelation.
    • 尘金泻液 - Refers to a story where dust and gold are mixed, symbolizing a miraculous event.
    • 铨示繁方 - Means 'various methods of illustration' or 'multiple ways of representation.'
    • 允归自从 - Means 'truly return to their own nature' or 'truly follow their own path.'
    • 经塔显效 - Refers to the effectiveness or miracles associated with Buddhist stupas.
    • 旨证亦同 - Means 'the intent and evidence are the same.'
    • 事非殊贯 - Means 'the events are not different in essence.'
    • 故继其末 - Means 'therefore, following it up.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 释迦弥勒 - Refers to the historical Buddha (Siddhartha Gautama) and the future Buddha (Maitreya).
    • 西域 - Refers to the Western Regions, which historically included areas west of China, including parts of Central Asia and India.
    • 华夏 - Refers to the Chinese civilization or the central plains of China.
    • 景楷 - Refers to a type of Buddhist statue or image.
    • 法苑珠林 - A large compendium of Buddhist stories and teachings compiled during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 感通录 - A collection of stories about spiritual connections and divine interventions.

    Proper Nouns

    • 释迦 - Shakyamuni (Siddhartha Gautama), the historical Buddha.
    • 弥勒 - Maitreya, the future Buddha.
    • 法苑珠林 - A large compendium of Buddhist stories and teachings compiled during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 感通录 - A collection of stories about spiritual connections and divine interventions.
    • 闽吴 - Regions in southeastern China.
    • 彭宋 - Regions in central China.

    According to the scriptures, those who refine and shape metal, paint, and create images can move and emit light. The statues of Shakyamuni and Maitreya in the Western Regions have a light that is as if in darkness, and one interpretation makes them true. The images in China, also known as Eastern images, are different interpretations. The records of spiritual connections show true models, and divine responses are often recorded. They may also be moved by events, using wood and stone to reveal the mysterious, without necessarily relying on beauty. Therefore, the story of a stone sinking and floating reveals the transformation in the regions of Min and Wu; the story of dust and gold being mixed alleviates the suffering in the regions of Peng and Song. The records of spiritual connections show the blessings in the regions of Peng and Song, and other methods of illustration, though difficult to explain, generally follow their own nature. The effectiveness of Buddhist stupas is the same, and the events are not different in essence, so they are followed up. The Fa Yuan Zhu Lin (Treasury of the Buddhist Law) and the Gan Tong Lu (Record of Spiritual Connections) cite these images, which are now often self-supplied. [4]

    冥祥记
    　　汉明帝梦见神人：形垂二丈，身黄金色，项佩日光。以问群臣。或对曰：“西方有神，其号曰佛，形如陛下所梦，得无是乎？”于是发使天竺，写致经像，表之中夏。自天子王侯，咸敬事之。闻人死精神不灭，莫不惧然自失。初使者蔡愔，将西域沙门迦叶摩腾等赍优填王画释迦佛像；帝重之，如梦所见也。乃遣画工图之数本，于南宫清凉台及高阳门显节寿陵上供养。又于白马寺壁，画千乘万骑绕塔三?之像，如诸传备载。法苑珠林十三三宝感通录二 [5]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [5]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 形垂二丈 - Describes the figure as being two zhang in height, emphasizing its imposing stature.
    • 身黄金色 - Describes the figure's body as golden in color.
    • 项佩日光 - Describes the figure wearing a sunbeam as a pendant around the neck.
    • 得无是乎 - A rhetorical question meaning 'Is it not this?' or 'Could it be this?'
    • 写致经像 - Means 'to write and bring the scriptures and images.'
    • 表之中夏 - Means 'to present in the Central Plains' or 'to spread in the Central Plains.'
    • 咸敬事之 - Means 'all revere and serve him/her.'
    • 惧然自失 - Means 'feeling fear and losing one's composure.'
    • 乃遣画工图之数本 - Means 'then sent painters to draw several copies.'
    • 于南宫清凉台及高阳门显节寿陵上供养 - Means 'to place them on the Qingliang Terrace in the Nan Gong, the Gaoyang Gate, and the Xianjie Shouling.'
    • 又于白马寺壁，画千乘万骑绕塔三匝之像 - Means 'also on the walls of the White Horse Temple, paintings of thousands of chariots and horses circling the stupa three times.'
    • 如诸传备载 - Means 'as recorded in various biographies.'
    • 法苑珠林十三 - Refers to the *Fa Yuan Zhu Lin* (Jewel Forest of the Dharma Garden), a collection of Buddhist stories, specifically the 13th volume.
    • 三宝感通录二 - Refers to the *San Bao Gan Tong Lu* (Record of the Three Treasures' Responsive Communication), specifically the second volume.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 汉明帝 - Refers to Emperor Ming of Han, the fourth emperor of the Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 天竺 - Refers to ancient India, the birthplace of Buddhism.
    • 蔡愔 - A Chinese envoy sent by Emperor Ming to India to retrieve Buddhist scriptures and images.
    • 西域沙门迦叶摩腾 - Refers to the Indian monk Kasyapa Matanga, one of the first Buddhist monks to arrive in China.
    • 优填王画释迦佛像 - Refers to the statue of the Buddha created by King Udayana of Gandhara, which was brought to China.
    • 南宫清凉台 - A terrace in the Nan Gong (Southern Palace) where the Buddha images were placed.
    • 高阳门 - A gate in the imperial city of Luoyang.
    • 显节寿陵 - A tomb in the imperial cemetery.
    • 白马寺 - The first Buddhist temple in China, built in the capital city of Luoyang.
    • 千乘万骑绕塔三匝 - A common Buddhist motif depicting a scene of reverence and devotion.
    • 法苑珠林 - A collection of Buddhist stories and anecdotes compiled during the Tang dynasty.
    • 三宝感通录 - A record of miraculous events involving the Three Treasures (Buddha, Dharma, Sangha).

    Mysterious Auspicious Records

    Emperor Ming of Han dreamed of a divine being: two zhang tall, with a golden body, and a sunbeam pendant around the neck. He asked his ministers about it. Some replied, "There is a deity from the West, known as Buddha, whose form matches what Your Majesty has seen in your dream. Could it be this?" Thus, he sent envoys to India to bring scriptures and images, spreading them in the Central Plains. From the emperor down to the nobles, all revered and served this deity. Hearing that the spirit does not perish after death, none remained calm. Initially, the envoy Cai Yin brought Indian monks Kasyapa Matanga and others with the statue of the Buddha created by King Udayana of Gandhara. The emperor valued it greatly, just as he had seen in his dream. He then sent painters to create several copies, placing them on the Qingliang Terrace in the Nan Gong, the Gaoyang Gate, and the Xianjie Shouling for veneration. Also on the walls of the White Horse Temple, they painted images of thousands of chariots and horses circling the stupa three times, as recorded in various biographies. Jewel Forest of the Dharma Garden, Volume 13; Record of the Three Treasures' Responsive Communication, Volume 2. [5]

    晋羊太傅祜，字叔子，泰山人也。西晋名臣，声冠区夏。年五岁时，尝令乳母取先所弄指环。乳母曰：“汝本无此，于何取耶？”祜曰：“昔于东垣边弄之，落桑树中。”乳母曰：“汝可自觅。”祜曰：“此非先宅，儿不知处。”后因出门游望，迳而东行。乳母随之。至李氏家，乃入至东垣树下，探得小环。李氏惊怅曰：“吾子昔有此环，常爱弄之。七岁暴亡。亡后不知环处。此亡儿之物也，云何持去？”祜持环走。李氏遂问之。乳母既说祜言。李氏悲喜；遂欲求祜，还为其儿。里中解喻，然后得止。祜年长，常患头风。医欲攻治。祜曰：“吾生三日时，头首北户，觉风吹顶，意其患之，但不能语耳。病源既久，不可治也。”祜后为荆州都督，镇襄阳，经给武当寺殊余精舍。或问其故，祜默然。后因忏悔，叙说因果。乃曰，前身承有诸罪，赖造此寺，故获申济，所以使供养之情偏殷勤重也。法苑珠林二十六 [6]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [6]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 尝令乳母取先所弄指环 - Once asked his wet nurse to fetch the finger ring he had played with before.
    • 吾生三日时，头首北户，觉风吹顶 - When I was three days old, my head was at the northern door, and I felt the wind blowing on my head.
    • 病源既久，不可治也 - The disease has been long-standing, it cannot be cured.
    • 前身承有诸罪，赖造此寺，故获申济 - In my previous life, I committed various sins, thanks to the construction of this temple, I was thus able to be saved.
    • 所以使供养之情偏殷勤重也 - Which is why my devotion to offerings is particularly diligent and sincere.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋朝 - The Western Jin Dynasty, a period in Chinese history known for its political turmoil and cultural achievements.
    • 太傅 - A high-ranking official position, often held by individuals with significant influence and respect.
    • 泰山 - Mount Tai, one of the Five Great Mountains in China, often associated with longevity and immortality.
    • 武当寺 - Wudang Temple, a significant Buddhist temple in China, often associated with spiritual and religious practices.
    • 因果 - The concept of cause and effect, a fundamental principle in Buddhism.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋羊太傅祜 - Yang Hu, a high-ranking official during the Western Jin Dynasty.
    • 泰山 - Mount Tai, a sacred mountain in China.
    • 武当寺 - Wudang Temple, a significant Buddhist temple in China.
    • 法苑珠林 - A Buddhist text that compiles various stories and teachings.

    Yang Hu, a high-ranking official of the Western Jin Dynasty, was known by his courtesy name Shuzi and hailed from Mount Tai. Renowned as a distinguished minister, his fame spread throughout the land. When he was five years old, he once asked his wet nurse to fetch the finger ring he had played with before. The wet nurse said, "You never had this ring; where could it be?" Hu replied, "I played with it at the eastern wall in the past, and it fell into a mulberry tree." The wet nurse said, "You can find it yourself." Hu said, "This is not my ancestral home; I don't know where it is." Later, when he went out to sightsee, he headed east. The wet nurse followed him. He arrived at the home of a family named Li and went to the eastern wall, where he found the small ring under a mulberry tree. The Li family was surprised and saddened, saying, "Our son once had this ring and loved to play with it. He died at the age of seven. After his death, we did not know where the ring was. This is the ring of our deceased son; why are you taking it away?" Hu took the ring and ran. The Li family then asked him about it. The wet nurse explained what Hu had said. The Li family was both sad and happy; they wanted to adopt Hu as their son. The villagers explained the situation, and the Li family then stopped.

    As Hu grew older, he often suffered from headaches. A doctor wanted to treat him. Hu said, "When I was three days old, my head was at the northern door, and I felt the wind blowing on my head. I suspect that this is the cause of my illness, but I could not speak about it at the time. The disease has been long-standing; it cannot be cured."

    Later, Hu became the governor of Jing Province and stationed himself in Xiangyang. He visited the Wudang Temple and its other excellent hermitages. Someone asked him why. Hu remained silent. Later, during a confession, he explained the cause and effect. He said, "In my previous life, I committed various sins. Thanks to the construction of this temple, I was thus able to be saved. This is why my devotion to offerings is particularly diligent and sincere." (From Fayuan Zhulin) [6]

    晋沙门仕行慧皎高僧传作士行者，颍川人也。姓朱氏。气志方远，识宇沈正，循心直诣，荣辱不能动焉。时经典未备，唯有小品；而章句阙略，义致弗显。魏甘露五年，发迹雍州，西至于阗，寻求经藏。踰历诸国。西域僧徒，多小乘学，闻仕行求方等诸经，咸骇怪不与。曰，边人不识正法，将多惑乱。仕行曰，“经云，千载将末，法当东流。若疑非佛说，请以至诚验之。”乃焚柴灌油。烟炎方盛，仕行捧经涕泪，稽颡誓曰：“若果出金口，应宣布汉地。 [7]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [7]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 晋沙门仕行慧皎高僧传作士行者 - Introduces the subject, 'Jin monk Shixing (also known as Shihang in the *Gao Seng Zhuan*), indicating he is a monk from the Jin dynasty, and his name is recorded differently in the *Gao Seng Zhuan*.
    • 气志方远，识宇沈正 - Describes Shixing's character: 'with a lofty spirit and far-reaching aspirations, and a deep and upright understanding.'
    • 循心直诣，荣辱不能动焉 - Means 'following his heart directly, he was not moved by honor or disgrace.'
    • 经典未备，唯有小品 - Indicates that the Buddhist scriptures were incomplete, with only the shorter versions available.
    • 章句阙略，义致弗显 - Means 'the verses and sentences were incomplete, and the meaning was not clearly expressed.'
    • 发迹雍州，西至于阗 - Describes Shixing's journey: 'he set out from Yongzhou and traveled west to Yutian (ancient name for Khotan).'
    • 踰历诸国 - Means 'he passed through various countries.'
    • 小乘学 - Refers to 'Hinayana Buddhism,' which is contrasted with Mahayana Buddhism.
    • 边人不识正法，将多惑乱 - Means 'people on the border do not know the true Dharma, and they will be easily confused.'
    • 千载将末，法当东流 - Refers to a prophecy that the Dharma will spread to the East in the latter days.
    • 若疑非佛说，请以至诚验之 - Means 'if you doubt that it is the Buddha's teaching, please test it with utmost sincerity.'
    • 焚柴灌油 - Describes the act of burning wood and pouring oil, likely as a form of ritual or test.
    • 烟炎方盛，仕行捧经涕泪 - Means 'as the smoke and flames were at their peak, Shixing held the scriptures and wept.'
    • 稽颡誓曰 - Means 'he knelt and swore, saying.'
    • 若果出金口，应宣布汉地 - Means 'if it truly came from the Buddha's mouth, it should be announced in Han territory.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋 - Refers to the Jin dynasty (265–420 CE), a period in Chinese history.
    • 雍州 - Refers to Yongzhou, a region in ancient China.
    • 于阗 - Refers to Yutian, an ancient kingdom in what is now Xinjiang, China.
    • 小乘学 - Refers to Hinayana Buddhism, which emphasizes individual liberation.
    • 正法 - Refers to the true or correct Dharma, the teachings of the Buddha.
    • 千载将末，法当东流 - Refers to a prophecy in Buddhist scriptures that the Dharma will spread to the East in the latter days.
    • 金口 - A term used to refer to the Buddha's teachings, implying that they are as valuable as gold.
    • 汉地 - Refers to the Han territories, indicating China.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋 - The Jin dynasty (265–420 CE).
    • 雍州 - Yongzhou, an ancient region in China.
    • 于阗 - Yutian, an ancient kingdom in what is now Xinjiang, China.
    • 小乘学 - Hinayana Buddhism.
    • 正法 - The true Dharma, the teachings of the Buddha.
    • 金口 - A term used to refer to the Buddha's teachings.
    • 汉地 - The Han territories, indicating China.

    A Jin dynasty monk named Shixing (also known as Shihang in the Gao Seng Zhuan), originally from Yingchuan, bore the surname Zhu. He possessed a lofty spirit and far-reaching aspirations, with a deep and upright understanding. Following his heart directly, he was not moved by honor or disgrace. At the time, the Buddhist scriptures were incomplete, with only the shorter versions available; the verses and sentences were incomplete, and the meaning was not clearly expressed. In the fifth year of the Ganlu era of the Wei dynasty, he set out from Yongzhou and traveled west to Yutian, seeking the Buddhist canon. He passed through various countries. The monks of the Western Regions mostly practiced Hinayana Buddhism. When they heard that Shixing was seeking the Mahayana sutras, they were all startled and refused to give them, saying, "People on the border do not know the true Dharma and will be easily confused." Shixing replied, "The scriptures say that in the latter days, the Dharma will spread to the East. If you doubt that it is the Buddha's teaching, please test it with utmost sincerity." He then burned wood and poured oil. As the smoke and flames were at their peak, Shixing held the scriptures and wept, kneeling and swearing, "If it truly came from the Buddha's mouth, it should be announced in Han territory." [7]

    诸佛菩萨，宜为证明。”于是投经火中，腾燎移景。既而一积煨尽，文字无毁，皮牒若故。举国欣敬。因留供养。遣弟子法饶，赍送梵本，还至陈留浚仪仓垣诗寺。高僧传云陈留仓垣水南寺出之，凡九十篇，二十万言。河南居士竺叔兰，练解方俗，深善法味，亲共传译，今放光首品是也。仕行八十乃亡，依阇维之火灭。经日尸形犹全。国人惊异，皆曰，若真得道法，当毁坏。应声碎散。乃敛骨起塔。慧志道人先师相传。释公亦具载其事也。法苑珠林二十八 [8]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [8]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 諸佛菩薩 - Refers to the Buddhas and Bodhisattvas, the enlightened beings in Buddhism.
    • 宜為證明 - Means 'should serve as witnesses' or 'should certify,' indicating a formal endorsement or attestation.
    • 投經火中 - Literally 'throwing the scripture into the fire,' a ritual act of burning scriptures, often symbolic of purification or destruction.
    • 騰燎移景 - Describes the rising flames and the movement of shadows, likely a poetic way to describe the burning process.
    • 一積煨盡 - Means 'all the ashes have burned away,' indicating the complete consumption of the material.
    • 文字無毀 - Means 'the characters remained undamaged,' suggesting that the script or text was preserved despite the fire.
    • 皮牒若故 - Means 'the parchment or document seemed as if it were still new,' indicating that the physical appearance of the document was unchanged.
    • 舉國欣敬 - Means 'the entire country rejoiced and respected,' indicating widespread approval and reverence.
    • 因留供养 - Means 'therefore, they were kept for worship,' indicating the decision to preserve the document for religious purposes.
    • 遣弟子法饶 - Means 'sent a disciple named Fa Rao,' indicating the sending of a specific individual to carry out a task.
    • 赍送梵本 - Means 'to carry and deliver the Sanskrit version,' indicating the transportation of a religious text.
    • 還至陈留浚仪仓垣诗寺 - Means 'returned to the Cangyuan Temple in Junyi, Chenliu,' indicating the destination of the text.
    • 高僧传云 - Means 'as recorded in the Biography of Eminent Monks,' indicating a historical source.
    • 陈留仓垣水南寺出之 - Means 'as recorded by the Cangyuan Temple in Chenliu,' indicating the origin of the text.
    • 凡九十篇，二十万言 - Means 'totaling ninety chapters and two hundred thousand words,' indicating the size and scope of the text.
    • 河南居士竺叔兰 - Means 'the lay Buddhist scholar Zhubolan from Henan,' indicating the translator.
    • 练解方俗 - Means 'well-versed in local customs and dialects,' indicating Zhubolan's expertise.
    • 深善法味 - Means 'deeply appreciates the flavor of the Dharma,' indicating a profound understanding of Buddhist teachings.
    • 亲共传译 - Means 'personally involved in the translation,' indicating Zhubolan's active role in the translation process.
    • 今放光首品是也 - Means 'this is the first chapter of the *Flower Adornment Sutra*,' indicating the specific text being discussed.
    • 仕行八十乃亡 - Means 'served for eighty years before passing away,' indicating the length of Zhubolan's career.
    • 依阇维之火灭 - Means 'by the fire of cremation,' indicating the method of Zhubolan's death.
    • 经日尸形犹全 - Means 'after several days, the body remained intact,' indicating the unusual preservation of the body.
    • 国人惊异 - Means 'the people were surprised and amazed,' indicating the reaction of the community.
    • 若真得道法 - Means 'if truly attained the Dharma,' indicating a condition for the body's preservation.
    • 应声碎散 - Means 'it would break apart at the sound,' indicating the expected outcome if the body were not truly enlightened.
    • 乃敛骨起塔 - Means 'they then collected the bones and built a stupa,' indicating the posthumous treatment of Zhubolan's remains.
    • 慧志道人先师相传 - Means 'as recorded by the monk Huizhi, who inherited the teachings from his predecessors,' indicating a historical source.
    • 释公亦具载其事也 - Means 'Master Shi also recorded this event,' indicating another historical source.
    • 法苑珠林二十八 - Means 'Chapter 28 of the *Jewels of the Dharma Garden*,' indicating the source of the passage.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 诸佛菩萨 - Refers to the enlightened beings in Buddhism, central figures in Buddhist cosmology.
    • 投经火中 - A ritual act of burning scriptures, often symbolic of purification or destruction, common in Buddhist tradition.
    • 腾燎移景 - Describes the rising flames and the movement of shadows, likely a poetic way to describe the burning process, reflecting the beauty and solemnity of the ritual.
    • 举国欣敬 - Indicates widespread approval and reverence, reflecting the importance of the event in the community.
    • 因留供养 - Indicates the decision to preserve the document for religious purposes, reflecting the value placed on sacred texts.
    • 遣弟子法饶 - Indicates the sending of a specific individual to carry out a task, reflecting the organizational structure of Buddhist monasteries.
    • 赍送梵本 - Indicates the transportation of a religious text, reflecting the importance of preserving and disseminating sacred texts.
    • 陈留仓垣水南寺出之 - Indicates the origin of the text, reflecting the historical and geographical context of the translation.
    • 河南居士竺叔兰 - Indicates the translator, reflecting the role of lay scholars in the transmission of Buddhist texts.
    • 练解方俗 - Indicates Zhubolan's expertise in local customs and dialects, reflecting the importance of cultural adaptation in translation.
    • 深善法味 - Indicates a profound understanding of Buddhist teachings, reflecting the translator's deep engagement with the Dharma.
    • 亲共传译 - Indicates Zhubolan's active role in the translation process, reflecting the collaborative nature of translation in Buddhism.
    • 今放光首品是也 - Indicates the specific text being discussed, reflecting the importance of the *Flower Adornment Sutra* in Buddhist tradition.
    • 仕行八十乃亡 - Indicates the length of Zhubolan's career, reflecting the dedication and longevity of his service.
    • 依阇维之火灭 - Indicates the method of Zhubolan's death, reflecting the Buddhist practice of cremation.
    • 经日尸形犹全 - Indicates the unusual preservation of the body, reflecting the belief in the sanctity of the body of enlightened beings.
    • 国人惊异 - Indicates the reaction of the community, reflecting the awe and respect accorded to enlightened beings.
    • 若真得道法 - Indicates a condition for the body's preservation, reflecting the belief in the supernatural powers of enlightened beings.
    • 应声碎散 - Indicates the expected outcome if the body were not truly enlightened, reflecting the belief in the miraculous nature of enlightened beings.
    • 乃敛骨起塔 - Indicates the posthumous treatment of Zhubolan's remains, reflecting the Buddhist practice of building stupas to honor the dead.
    • 慧志道人先师相传 - Indicates a historical source, reflecting the importance of oral and written tradition in preserving historical accounts.
    • 释公亦具载其事也 - Indicates another historical source, reflecting the multiplicity of sources in historical documentation.
    • 法苑珠林二十八 - Indicates the source of the passage, reflecting the importance of encyclopedic works in Buddhist literature.

    The Buddhas and Bodhisattvas should serve as witnesses. Thus, the scripture was thrown into the fire, and the flames rose, casting moving shadows. Soon after, all the ashes had burned away, yet the characters remained undamaged, and the parchment seemed as if it were still new. The entire country rejoiced and respected this miracle. Therefore, they were kept for worship. A disciple named Fa Rao was sent to carry and deliver the Sanskrit version of the text, returning it to the Cangyuan Temple in Junyi, Chenliu. As recorded in the Biography of Eminent Monks, this text originated from the Cangyuan Temple in Chenliu, totaling ninety chapters and two hundred thousand words. The lay Buddhist scholar Zhubolan from Henan, well-versed in local customs and dialects, deeply appreciated the flavor of the Dharma, and personally involved in the translation, produced the first chapter of the Flower Adornment Sutra. He served for eighty years before passing away by the fire of cremation. After several days, his body remained intact. The people were surprised and amazed, and all said, "If truly attained the Dharma, it should break apart at the sound." It would break apart at the sound. They then collected his bones and built a stupa. As recorded by the monk Huizhi, who inherited the teachings from his predecessors, and Master Shi also recorded this event, Chapter 28 of the Jewels of the Dharma Garden documents this story. [8]

    晋赵泰，字文和，清河贝丘人也。祖父京兆广记引作清河太守。泰，郡举孝廉；公府辟，不就。精思典籍，有誉乡里。当晚乃膺仕，终于中散大夫。泰年三十五时，尝卒心痛，须臾而死。下尸于地，心暖不已，屈伸随人。留尸十日。平旦，喉中有声如雨。俄而稣活。说初死之时，梦有一人，来近心下。复有二人，乘黄马。从者二人，夹扶泰腋。径将东行，不知可几里。至一大城，崔嵬高峻。城色青黑，状锡。广记引作城邑青黑色将泰向城门入。经两重门。有瓦屋可数千间；男女大小，亦数千人，行列而立。吏着皂衣。有五六人条疏姓字，云当以科呈府君。泰名在三十。须臾，将泰与数千人男女，一时俱进。府君西向坐，简视名簿讫，复遣泰南入黑门。有人着绛衣坐大屋下，以次呼名，问生时所事：“作何孽罪，孽字依广记引补行何福善？谛汝等辞以实言也。此恒遣六部使者，常在人间，疏记善恶，具有条状。 [9]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [9]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 卒心痛 - A classical medical term referring to a sudden, severe heart pain, often associated with a near-death experience.
    • 须臾而死 - Means 'died in an instant,' emphasizing the suddenness of the event.
    • 下尸于地 - Literally 'lay the body on the ground,' describing the physical act of placing the body after death.
    • 心暖不已 - Means 'the heart remained warm,' an unusual detail that suggests the body was not fully cold, which was often seen as a sign of potential revival in classical Chinese medical beliefs.
    • 屈伸随人 - Means 'the body could be bent and stretched by others,' indicating that the body was still somewhat flexible despite being dead.
    • 留尸十日 - Means 'the body was left for ten days,' which is an unusually long period and suggests a deliberate delay in burial or cremation.
    • 平旦，喉中有声如雨 - Means 'at dawn, there was a sound in the throat like rain,' describing a strange auditory phenomenon that occurred during the revival.
    • 俄而稣活 - Means 'soon after, he revived,' indicating a sudden return to life.
    • 梦有一人，来近心下 - Means 'in a dream, there was a person who came close to his heart,' describing a supernatural or spiritual encounter.
    • 复有二人，乘黄马 - Means 'there were two more people riding yellow horses,' adding to the dream's surreal and symbolic nature.
    • 从者二人，夹扶泰腋 - Means 'two attendants supported him under the armpits,' describing the physical support provided in the dream.
    • 径将东行 - Means 'they took him eastward,' indicating a direction of travel in the dream.
    • 不知可几里 - Means 'they did not know how far it was,' suggesting a sense of distance or uncertainty in the dream.
    • 崔嵬高峻 - Means 'tall and steep,' describing the appearance of the city in the dream.
    • 城色青黑 - Means 'the city was blue-black in color,' adding to the dream's vivid and unusual imagery.
    • 状锡 - Means 'like tin,' describing the appearance of the city walls.
    • 广记引作城邑青黑色 - A reference to a source that describes the city as blue-black.
    • 将泰向城门入 - Means 'they took him to the city gate and entered,' describing the movement into the city in the dream.
    • 经两重门 - Means 'they passed through two gates,' indicating the complexity of the city's structure.
    • 有瓦屋可数千间 - Means 'there were thousands of tile-roofed houses,' describing the city's architecture.
    • 男女大小，亦数千人，行列而立 - Means 'men, women, and children, numbering in the thousands, stood in rows,' describing the city's inhabitants.
    • 吏着皂衣 - Means 'officials wore black clothes,' describing the attire of the city's officials.
    • 有五六人条疏姓字 - Means 'there were five or six people who listed names and surnames,' indicating a bureaucratic process.
    • 云当以科呈府君 - Means 'they said they would present the list to the governor,' describing the purpose of the bureaucratic process.
    • 泰名在三十 - Means 'Tai's name was on the thirtieth list,' indicating his position in the list.
    • 须臾，将泰与数千人男女，一时俱进 - Means 'soon, Tai and thousands of others were led forward together,' describing a collective action.
    • 府君西向坐 - Means 'the governor sat facing west,' indicating the direction of the governor's seat.
    • 简视名簿讫 - Means 'he reviewed the list of names,' describing the governor's action.
    • 复遣泰南入黑门 - Means 'he then sent Tai south into a black gate,' indicating a further journey in the dream.
    • 有人着绛衣坐大屋下 - Means 'there was a person wearing red clothing sitting under a large house,' describing another figure in the dream.
    • 以次呼名，问生时所事 - Means 'they called out names in order and asked about their deeds in life,' describing a questioning process.
    • 作何孽罪，行何福善 - Means 'what sins did they commit, and what good deeds did they perform?' describing the nature of the questions asked.
    • 谛汝等辞以实言也 - Means 'speak the truth about your deeds,' emphasizing the importance of honesty.
    • 此恒遣六部使者，常在人间，疏记善恶，具有条状 - Means 'these six officials are always sent to record good and evil deeds, with detailed records,' describing the ongoing surveillance and judgment process.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋赵泰 - Refers to Zhao Tai, a historical figure from the Jin Dynasty.
    • 清河贝丘 - Refers to Beiqiu, a place in Qinghe, indicating Zhao Tai's birthplace.
    • 郡举孝廉 - Refers to the practice of recommending talented individuals for government service.
    • 公府辟，不就 - Refers to the practice of being invited to serve in a government office but declining.
    • 中散大夫 - Refers to a government rank, indicating Zhao Tai's final position.
    • 梦 - Refers to a dream, which is often used in classical Chinese literature to convey spiritual or supernatural experiences.
    • 府君 - Refers to a governor or local official, indicating a high-ranking authority figure in the dream.
    • 六部使者 - Refers to officials responsible for recording good and evil deeds, indicating a system of moral judgment and surveillance.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋赵泰 - Zhao Tai, a historical figure from the Jin Dynasty.
    • 清河贝丘 - Beiqiu, a place in Qinghe, indicating Zhao Tai's birthplace.
    • 中散大夫 - A government rank, indicating Zhao Tai's final position.
    • 府君 - A governor or local official, indicating a high-ranking authority figure in the dream.
    • 六部使者 - Officials responsible for recording good and evil deeds, indicating a system of moral judgment and surveillance.

    Zhao Tai, courtesy name Wenhé, was from Beiqiu, Qinghe. His grandfather was the governor of Jingzhao, as noted in the Guangji (Record of Wonders). Zhao Tai was recommended by his county as a filial and clean official, but he declined the invitation from the imperial court to serve. He devoted himself to studying classical texts and earned a good reputation in his hometown. He began his official career later in life and eventually became a Zhongshangdaifu (Middle Scattering Doctor). At the age of thirty-five, Zhao Tai once suffered a sudden and severe heart attack, dying almost instantly. His body was laid on the ground, but his heart remained warm, and his limbs could still be bent and stretched by others. The body was left for ten days. At dawn, there was a sound in his throat like rain. Soon after, he revived. He recounted that when he first died, he dreamed of a person coming close to his heart. Two more people arrived, riding yellow horses, with two attendants supporting him under the armpits. They took him eastward, not knowing how far they traveled. They reached a large city, tall and steep, with blue-black walls like tin, as noted in the Guangji. They led Zhao Tai to the city gate and entered through two gates. There were thousands of tile-roofed houses, and men, women, and children, numbering in the thousands, stood in rows. Officials wore black clothes. Five or six people listed names and surnames, saying they would present the list to the governor. Zhao Tai's name was on the thirtieth list. Soon, Zhao Tai and thousands of others were led forward together. The governor, sitting facing west, reviewed the list of names. He then sent Zhao Tai south into a black gate. There was a person wearing red clothing sitting under a large house, calling out names in order and asking about their deeds in life: "What sins did you commit, and what good deeds did you perform? Speak the truth about your deeds." These six officials are always sent to record good and evil deeds, with detailed records. [9]

    不可得虚。”泰答，“父兄仕宦皆二千石。我少在家修学而已，无所事也，亦不犯恶。”乃遣泰为水官监作使，将二千余人运沙裨岸。昼夜勤苦。后转泰水官都督，知诸狱事。给泰马兵，令案行地狱。所至诸狱，楚毒各殊。或针贯其舌，流血竟体。或被头露发，裸形徒跣，相牵而行。有持大杖，从后催促。铁床铜柱，烧之洞然；驱迫此人，抱卧其上。赴即焦烂，寻复还生。或炎炉巨镬，焚煮罪人。身首碎堕，随沸翻转。有鬼持叉，倚于其侧。有三四百人，立于一面，次当入镬，相抱悲泣。或剑树高广。广记引有此字不知限量。根茎枝叶，皆剑为之。人众相訾，自登自攀，若有欣意。广记引作欣竞而身首割截，尺寸离断。泰见祖父母及二弟，在此狱中。相见涕泣。泰出狱门，见有二人赍文书来，语狱吏，言有三人，其家为其于塔寺中县旛烧香，救解其罪，可出福舍。俄见三人，自狱而出；已有自然衣服，完整在身。 [10]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [10]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 不可得虚 - A phrase meaning 'cannot be obtained' or 'cannot be achieved', likely referring to something unattainable.
    • 父兄仕宦皆二千石 - A phrase meaning 'my father and elder brothers all hold the rank of 2,000 stones', a reference to a high-ranking official position in the Han dynasty.
    • 我少在家修学而已 - A phrase meaning 'I have only studied at home since my youth', indicating a focus on personal study rather than official career.
    • 无所事也 - A phrase meaning 'I have nothing to do', suggesting a lack of involvement in official duties.
    • 亦不犯恶 - A phrase meaning 'Nor have I committed any evil', indicating a moral integrity.
    • 乃遣泰为水官监作使 - A phrase meaning 'He was then sent to serve as the supervisor of water management', indicating an official appointment.
    • 将二千余人运沙裨岸 - A phrase meaning 'Leading over two thousand people to transport sand and reinforce the embankment', describing a specific task.
    • 昼夜勤苦 - A phrase meaning 'Working day and night with great effort', indicating intense labor.
    • 后转泰水官都督 - A phrase meaning 'Later, he was promoted to the position of supervisor of water management', indicating a career advancement.
    • 知诸狱事 - A phrase meaning 'To oversee all matters related to the prisons', indicating a new official responsibility.
    • 给泰马兵 - A phrase meaning 'He was given horses and soldiers', indicating a change in status or resources.
    • 令案行地狱 - A phrase meaning 'To inspect the underworld', a metaphorical or symbolic task.
    • 楚毒各殊 - A phrase meaning 'The tortures vary', indicating different forms of punishment.
    • 或针贯其舌 - A phrase meaning 'Some have needles piercing their tongues', describing a specific form of torture.
    • 流血竟体 - A phrase meaning 'Blood flows all over their bodies', describing the severity of the torture.
    • 或被头露发 - A phrase meaning 'Some have their heads uncovered and hair exposed', describing the state of the prisoners.
    • 裸形徒跣 - A phrase meaning 'Naked and barefoot', describing the physical state of the prisoners.
    • 相牵而行 - A phrase meaning 'Dragging each other along', describing the movement of the prisoners.
    • 有持大杖 - A phrase meaning 'There are those holding large sticks', describing the tools used for punishment.
    • 从后催促 - A phrase meaning 'Pushing them from behind', indicating the force used to move the prisoners.
    • 铁床铜柱，烧之洞然 - A phrase meaning 'Iron beds and copper pillars, burning fiercely', describing the instruments of torture.
    • 驱迫此人，抱卧其上 - A phrase meaning 'Forcing these people to lie on them', indicating the use of the instruments of torture.
    • 赴即焦烂，寻复还生 - A phrase meaning 'They immediately burn and rot, but soon regenerate', describing a cycle of punishment and recovery.
    • 或炎炉巨镬 - A phrase meaning 'Some are in blazing furnaces and huge cauldrons', describing another form of torture.
    • 焚煮罪人 - A phrase meaning 'Burning and boiling criminals', indicating the method of punishment.
    • 身首碎堕，随沸翻转 - A phrase meaning 'Their bodies and heads are shattered and tossed about', describing the physical effects of the torture.
    • 有鬼持叉 - A phrase meaning 'There are demons holding spears', describing the presence of supernatural beings.
    • 倚于其侧 - A phrase meaning 'Standing by their side', indicating the proximity of the demons.
    • 有三四百人，立于一面 - A phrase meaning 'There are several hundred people standing on one side', describing the crowd.
    • 次当入镬 - A phrase meaning 'Next to enter the cauldron', indicating the impending punishment.
    • 相抱悲泣 - A phrase meaning 'Holding each other and weeping', describing the emotional state of the prisoners.
    • 或剑树高广 - A phrase meaning 'Some have tall and wide sword trees', describing a specific form of torture.
    • 广记引有此字不知限量 - A phrase meaning 'The *Guangji* (Comprehensive Records) mentions this word but does not specify its limits', indicating uncertainty about the extent of the torture.
    • 根茎枝叶，皆剑为之 - A phrase meaning 'The roots, stems, leaves, and branches are all made of swords', describing the nature of the torture device.
    • 人众相訾 - A phrase meaning 'People criticize each other', indicating the social dynamics among the prisoners.
    • 自登自攀，若有欣意 - A phrase meaning 'They climb and grasp each other, as if with joy', describing the prisoners' actions and emotions.
    • 广记引作欣竞而身首割截 - A phrase meaning 'The *Guangji* records that they compete with joy, and their bodies and heads are cut apart', indicating a different interpretation of the text.
    • 尺寸离断 - A phrase meaning 'Cut into pieces', describing the physical effects of the torture.
    • 泰见祖父母及二弟，在此狱中 - A phrase meaning 'Tai saw his grandparents and two younger brothers in this prison', indicating a personal connection to the prisoners.
    • 相见涕泣 - A phrase meaning 'They wept upon seeing each other', describing the emotional reaction.
    • 泰出狱门 - A phrase meaning 'Tai left the prison gate', indicating the end of his inspection.
    • 见有二人赍文书来 - A phrase meaning 'He saw two people bringing documents', indicating the arrival of officials.
    • 语狱吏 - A phrase meaning 'Speaking to the prison guards', indicating communication.
    • 言有三人 - A phrase meaning 'Saying there are three people', indicating the number of prisoners.
    • 其家为其于塔寺中县幡烧香 - A phrase meaning 'Their families have hung banners and burned incense for them in the pagoda temple', indicating a religious or spiritual act.
    • 救解其罪，可出福舍 - A phrase meaning 'To save them from their sins and release them into a place of happiness', indicating the purpose of the act.
    • 俄见三人，自狱而出 - A phrase meaning 'Soon he saw three people leaving the prison', indicating the outcome of the act.
    • 已有自然衣服，完整在身 - A phrase meaning 'They were already dressed in natural clothes, complete and whole', describing the state of the prisoners after their release.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 父兄仕宦皆二千石 - A reference to the high-ranking official position of 2,000 stones in the Han dynasty, indicating a prestigious family background.
    • 水官监作使 - A reference to the position of supervisor of water management, indicating a specific official role.
    • 水官都督 - A reference to the position of supervisor of water management, indicating a higher rank.
    • 地狱 - A metaphorical or symbolic reference to the underworld, often used in Chinese literature to describe a place of punishment.
    • 楚毒 - A reference to various forms of torture, often used in Chinese literature to describe the suffering of prisoners.
    • 铁床铜柱 - References to specific instruments of torture, often used in Chinese literature to describe the severity of punishment.
    • 炎炉巨镬 - References to specific instruments of torture, often used in Chinese literature to describe the severity of punishment.
    • 剑树 - A reference to a specific form of torture involving sword trees, often used in Chinese literature to describe the suffering of prisoners.
    • 塔寺 - A reference to Buddhist temples, indicating a religious context.
    • 县幡烧香 - References to religious practices such as hanging banners and burning incense, indicating a spiritual act.

    It cannot be obtained." Tai replied, "My father and elder brothers all hold the rank of 2,000 stones. I have only studied at home since my youth, and have nothing to do, nor have I committed any evil." He was then sent to serve as the supervisor of water management, leading over two thousand people to transport sand and reinforce the embankment. They worked day and night with great effort. Later, he was promoted to the position of supervisor of water management, overseeing all matters related to the prisons. He was given horses and soldiers, and ordered to inspect the underworld. Everywhere he went, the tortures varied. Some had needles piercing their tongues, with blood flowing all over their bodies. Others had their heads uncovered and hair exposed, naked and barefoot, dragging each other along. There were those holding large sticks, pushing them from behind. Iron beds and copper pillars, burning fiercely; forcing these people to lie on them. They immediately burn and rot, but soon regenerate. Some were in blazing furnaces and huge cauldrons, burning and boiling criminals. Their bodies and heads were shattered and tossed about. There were demons holding spears, standing by their side. There were several hundred people standing on one side, next to enter the cauldron, holding each other and weeping. Some had tall and wide sword trees, the Guangji (Comprehensive Records) mentions this word but does not specify its limits. The roots, stems, leaves, and branches are all made of swords. People criticize each other, climbing and grasping each other, as if with joy. The Guangji records that they compete with joy, and their bodies and heads are cut apart, cut into pieces. Tai saw his grandparents and two younger brothers in this prison. They wept upon seeing each other. Tai left the prison gate, and saw two people bringing documents, speaking to the prison guards, saying there are three people, whose families have hung banners and burned incense for them in the pagoda temple, to save them from their sins and release them into a place of happiness. Soon he saw three people leaving the prison; they were already dressed in natural clothes, complete and whole. [10]

    南诣一门，云名开光大舍；有三重门，朱采照发。见此三人，即入舍中。泰亦随入。前有大殿珍宝周饰，精光耀目。金玉为床。见一神人，姿容伟异，殊好非常，坐此座上。边有沙门立侍，甚众。见府君来，恭敬作礼。泰问此是何人，府君致敬。吏曰，号名世尊度人之师，有愿令恶道中人皆出听经。时云有百万九千人，皆出地狱，入百里城。在此到者，奉法众生也。行虽亏殆，尚当得度，故开经法。七日之中，随本城作善恶多少，差次免脱。泰未出之顷，已见十人，升虚而去。出此舍，复见一城，方二百余里，名为受变形城。地狱考治已毕者，当于此城，更受变报。泰入其城，见有土瓦屋数千区，各有坊巷。广记引作房舍正中有瓦屋高壮，阑槛采饰。有数百局吏，对校文书云，杀生者当作蜉蝣，朝生?死；劫盗者当作猪羊，受人屠割；淫泆者作鹤鹜獐麋，两舌者作?枭鸺鹠，捍债者为驴骡牛马。泰案行毕，还水官处。 [11]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [11]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 南诣 - To the south; implies visiting or approaching.
    • 开光大舍 - Term referring to a sacred hall or temple, possibly associated with Buddhist or Taoist practices.
    • 朱采照发 - Vivid, radiant appearance of the doors.
    • 即 - Immediately or right away, indicating prompt action.
    • 三重门 - Three layers or tiers of doors, emphasizing the grandeur of the structure.
    • 金玉为床 - Luxurious nature of the bed, made of gold and jade.
    • 号名 - Known as or referred to as.
    • 世尊 - Title for a revered figure, commonly used for the Buddha in Buddhist texts.
    • 度人之师 - Teacher who guides people across.
    • 恶道 - Lower realms of existence in Buddhist cosmology, such as hell, hungry ghosts, and animals.
    • 听经 - To listen to teachings.
    • 时云 - It is said or according to reports.
    • 百里城 - City spanning 100 miles.
    • 奉法 - To follow the Dharma or adhere to the teachings.
    • 众生 - All sentient beings, encompassing all living creatures.
    • 行虽亏殆 - Though their actions may be flawed.
    • 尚当得度 - They should still be saved.
    • 七日之中 - Within seven days.
    • 随本城作善恶多少 - Depending on the amount of good or bad deeds they have done in their previous life.
    • 差次免脱 - They will be released in a graded manner.
    • 升虚而去 - To ascend into the void and depart.
    • 复见一城 - To see another city.
    • 方二百余里 - City spanning over 200 miles.
    • 名为受变形城 - City of transformation, where beings undergo changes in form.
    • 考治已毕者 - Those whose punishment is complete.
    • 更受变报 - Will undergo further transformation.
    • 土瓦屋 - Houses made of clay and tiles.
    • 坊巷 - Neighborhoods or alleys.
    • 高壮 - Tall and sturdy.
    • 阑槛采饰 - Ornamental railings and decorations.
    • 数百局吏 - Hundreds of officials.
    • 对校文书 - To compare and verify documents.
    • 杀生者 - Those who kill.
    • 当作蜉蝣 - Will become mayflies, which live for a short time.
    • 劫盗者 - Thieves.
    • 当作猪羊 - Will become pigs or sheep, which are often slaughtered.
    • 淫泆者 - Those who engage in promiscuity.
    • 鹤鹜獐麋 - Various animals, each symbolizing different types of transgressions or karmic consequences.
    • 两舌者 - Those who spread rumors or engage in divisive speech.
    • 捍债者 - Those who evade or refuse to pay debts, often seen as a form of dishonesty.
    • 驴骡牛马 - Donkeys, mules, cows, and horses, symbolizing different forms of punishment.
    • 案行毕 - Inspection completed.
    • 还水官处 - Return to the water official's office.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 开光大舍 - Term likely referring to a sacred hall or temple, possibly associated with Buddhist or Taoist practices.
    • 世尊 - Title for a revered figure, commonly used for the Buddha in Buddhist texts.
    • 恶道 - Lower realms of existence in Buddhist cosmology, such as hell, hungry ghosts, and animals.
    • 奉法 - Following the Dharma or Buddhist teachings.
    • 众生 - All sentient beings, encompassing all living creatures.
    • 受变形城 - City in Buddhist cosmology where beings undergo transformations as part of their karmic retribution.
    • 蜉蝣 - Type of insect with a very short lifespan, often used as a metaphor for impermanence.
    • 鹤鹜獐麋 - Various animals, each symbolizing different types of transgressions or karmic consequences.
    • 两舌者 - Those who spread rumors or engage in divisive speech.
    • 捍债者 - Those who evade or refuse to pay debts, often seen as a form of dishonesty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 开光大舍 - Term referring to a sacred hall or temple, possibly associated with Buddhist or Taoist practices.
    • 世尊 - Title for a revered figure, commonly used for the Buddha in Buddhist texts.
    • 恶道 - Lower realms of existence in Buddhist cosmology, such as hell, hungry ghosts, and animals.
    • 奉法 - Refers to following the Dharma or Buddhist teachings.
    • 众生 - Refers to all sentient beings, encompassing all living creatures.
    • 受变形城 - City in Buddhist cosmology where beings undergo transformations as part of their karmic retribution.
    • 蜉蝣 - Type of insect with a very short lifespan, often used as a metaphor for impermanence.
    • 鹤鹜獐麋 - Various animals, each symbolizing different types of transgressions or karmic consequences.
    • 两舌者 - Those who spread rumors or engage in divisive speech.
    • 捍债者 - Those who evade or refuse to pay debts, often seen as a form of dishonesty.

    To the south, there is a gate, said to be named the "Hall of Enlightened Light." It has three tiers of doors, each adorned with vivid red pigments that glow brilliantly. Upon seeing these three individuals, they immediately entered the hall. Ta also followed them in. In front stood a grand hall, its walls and furnishings encrusted with precious jewels, their brilliance dazzling to the eye. The bed was made of gold and jade. There they saw a divine being, whose appearance was majestic and extraordinary, unlike anything ordinary, seated upon this throne. Beside him stood numerous monks in attendance. When the Lord saw them, he greeted them with deep respect. Ta asked, "Who is this person?" The Lord responded with reverence. The official replied, "He is known as the 'Worldly Respected One, the Teacher Who Guides People.' He has vowed to lead all beings from the lower realms to listen to the teachings." It is said that there were a million nine thousand people who emerged from hell and entered the city spanning a hundred miles. Those who have arrived here are all beings who follow the Dharma. Though their actions may be flawed, they should still be saved, which is why the teachings of the scriptures are opened. Within seven days, depending on the amount of good or bad deeds they have done in their previous life, they will be released in a graded manner. Before Ta had even left, he had already seen ten people ascend into the void and depart. Exiting this hall, Ta saw another city, measuring over two hundred miles in area, named the "City of Transformation." Those whose punishment in hell has been completed will undergo further transformations in this city. Ta entered the city and saw thousands of houses made of clay and tiles, each with its own neighborhoods and alleys. Records indicate that these are houses. In the center stood a tall and sturdy building, its railings and decorations splendid. There were hundreds of officials, comparing and verifying documents, saying that those who kill will become mayflies, living only for a short time; those who rob will become pigs or sheep, to be slaughtered by others; those who engage in promiscuity will become cranes, ducks, deer, and boars; those who spread rumors will become owls and screech owls; those who evade debts will become donkeys, mules, cows, or horses. After inspecting everything, Ta returned to the office of the water official. [11]

    主者语泰，“卿是长者子，以何罪过，而来在此？”泰答，“祖父兄弟，皆二千石。我举孝廉；公府辟，不行。修志念善，不染众恶。”主者曰：“卿无罪过，故相使为水官都督。不尔，与地狱中人无以异也。”泰问主者曰：“人有何行，死得乐报？”主者唯言：奉法弟子，精进持戒，得乐报，无有滴罚也。泰复问曰：“人未事法时，所行罪过，事法之后，得以除不？”答曰：“皆除也。”语毕，主者开縢箧，检泰年纪，尚有余算三十年在。 [12]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [12]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 主者語泰 - The phrase '主者語泰' means 'the master (or overseer) spoke to Tai.' '主者' refers to the person in charge or an overseer, and '語泰' means 'spoke to Tai.'
    • 卿是長者子 - This phrase translates to 'you are the son of a respected elder' or 'you are the son of a prominent family.' '卿' is a respectful way of addressing someone, '長者' means 'elder' or 'prominent person,' and '子' means 'son' or 'offspring.'
    • 以何罪過 - This phrase means 'for what crime or fault.' '以' means 'for' or 'because of,' '何' means 'what,' and '罪過' means 'crime' or 'fault.'
    • 修志念善 - This phrase means 'to cultivate one's aspirations and think of goodness.' '修志' means 'to cultivate one's aspirations,' and '念善' means 'to think of goodness.'
    • 不染众恶 - This phrase means 'not tainted by many evils.' '不染' means 'not tainted' or 'not affected,' and '众恶' means 'many evils' or 'various vices.'
    • 故相使为水官都督 - This phrase means 'therefore, I have appointed you as the water official supervisor.' '故' means 'therefore' or 'thus,' '相使' means 'to appoint' or 'to command,' '为' means 'as' or 'to be,' and '水官都督' means 'water official supervisor.'
    • 与地狱中人无以异也 - This phrase means 'there is no difference from those in hell.' '与' means 'and' or 'with,' '地狱中人' means 'people in hell,' and '无以异也' means 'there is no difference.'
    • 人有何行，死得乐报 - This phrase means 'what actions does a person take that result in a happy afterlife?' '人' means 'person,' '有何行' means 'what actions,' '死' means 'death,' and '得乐报' means 'receive a happy reward.'
    • 奉法弟子 - This phrase means 'disciples who follow the law.' '奉法' means 'to follow the law' or 'to adhere to the rules,' and '弟子' means 'disciples' or 'followers.'
    • 精进持戒 - This phrase means 'to diligently observe precepts.' '精进' means 'diligent' or 'zealous,' and '持戒' means 'to observe precepts' or 'to keep the rules.'
    • 无有滴罚也 - This phrase means 'there is no punishment at all.' '无有' means 'there is no,' '滴罚' means 'punishment' or 'penalty,' and '也' is a particle that emphasizes the statement.
    • 人未事法时，所行罪过 - This phrase means 'before a person follows the law, the sins they commit.' '人' means 'person,' '未事法时' means 'before following the law,' and '所行罪过' means 'the sins they commit.'
    • 事法之后，得以除不 - This phrase means 'after following the law, can these sins be forgiven?' '事法之后' means 'after following the law,' '得以' means 'can be,' '除' means 'forgiven' or 'cleared,' and '不' is a particle that emphasizes the question.
    • 尚有余算三十年在 - This phrase means 'there are still thirty years of life remaining.' '尚有' means 'still have,' '余算' means 'remaining years' or 'leftover lifespan,' and '三十年在' means 'thirty years remaining.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 主者 - This term refers to an overseer or supervisor, often in a religious or administrative context. In this passage, it likely refers to a figure in the afterlife or a spiritual realm.
    • 水官都督 - This term means 'water official supervisor,' which could refer to a position in the afterlife or a metaphorical role related to water or purification.
    • 地狱中人 - This phrase refers to people in hell, a concept from Chinese Buddhism and Taoism, indicating a place of punishment for the wicked.
    • 奉法弟子 - This term refers to disciples who follow the law, which could be interpreted as followers of Buddhist or Taoist teachings.
    • 精进持戒 - This phrase refers to the practice of diligently observing precepts, which is a key aspect of Buddhist and Taoist practice.
    • 乐报 - This term means 'happy reward' or 'blessing,' often used in the context of karma and the afterlife in Buddhist and Taoist traditions.
    • 滴罚 - This term means 'punishment' or 'penalty,' often used in the context of karma and the afterlife in Buddhist and Taoist traditions.

    Proper Nouns

    • 泰 - This is the name of the person being addressed by the overseer. It is a proper noun and should be consistently translated as 'Tai.'
    • 水官都督 - This is a title or position, likely referring to a role in the afterlife or a metaphorical role. It should be consistently translated as 'water official supervisor.'
    • 地狱中人 - This phrase refers to people in hell, a concept from Chinese Buddhism and Taoism. It should be consistently translated as 'people in hell.'
    • 奉法弟子 - This term refers to disciples who follow the law, which could be interpreted as followers of Buddhist or Taoist teachings. It should be consistently translated as 'disciples who follow the law.'
    • 精进持戒 - This phrase refers to the practice of diligently observing precepts, which is a key aspect of Buddhist and Taoist practice. It should be consistently translated as 'diligently observe precepts.'
    • 乐报 - This term means 'happy reward' or 'blessing,' often used in the context of karma and the afterlife in Buddhist and Taoist traditions. It should be consistently translated as 'happy reward.'
    • 滴罚 - This term means 'punishment' or 'penalty,' often used in the context of karma and the afterlife in Buddhist and Taoist traditions. It should be consistently translated as 'punishment.'

    The overseer spoke to Tai, saying, "You are the son of a respected elder. For what crime or fault have you come here?" Tai replied, "My grandfather and uncles were all officials of the rank of 2,000 stones. I was recommended as a filial and upright official; I was summoned by the imperial court but did not go. I cultivate my aspirations and think of goodness, not tainted by many evils." The overseer said, "You have no crimes or faults, so I have appointed you as the water official supervisor. Otherwise, you would be no different from those in hell." Tai asked the overseer, "What actions does a person take that result in a happy afterlife?" The overseer replied, "Disciples who follow the law, diligently observe precepts, receive a happy reward, and there is no punishment at all." Tai asked again, "Before a person follows the law, the sins they commit, after following the law, can these sins be forgiven?" The overseer answered, "They can all be forgiven." When the conversation was over, the overseer opened a bamboo box, checked Tai's age, and found that he still had thirty years of life remaining. [12]

    乃遣泰还。临别，主者曰：“已见地狱罪报如是，当告世人，皆令作善。善恶随人，其犹影响，可不慎乎？”时亲表内外○视泰者，五六十人，同闻泰说。泰自书记，以示时人。时晋太始五年七月十三日也。乃为祖父母二弟延请僧众，大设福会。皆命子孙改意奉法，课劝精进。时人闻泰死而复生，多见罪福，互来访问。时有太中大夫武城孙丰，关内侯常山郝伯平等十人，同集泰舍，款曲寻问，莫不惧然，皆即奉法也。法苑珠林七太平广记三百七十七 [13]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [13]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 乃遣泰還 - Sequence of events indicating Tai was sent back.
    • 臨別，主者曰 - At the moment of parting, the host said.
    • 已見地狱罪报如是 - Having seen the punishment in hell like this.
    • 当告世人，皆令作善 - Should inform the world, all should do good deeds.
    • 善恶随人，其犹影响 - Good and evil follow people, just as shadows follow shapes.
    • 可不慎乎 - Can one not be careful?
    • 时亲表内外 - At that time, relatives inside and outside.
    • 视泰者，五六十人 - Those who visited Tai, fifty or sixty people.
    • 同闻泰说 - Heard Tai's words together.
    • 泰自书记，以示时人 - Tai himself wrote it down, to show the people of the time.
    • 时晋太始五年七月十三日也 - At that time, July 13th of the fifth year of the Tai Shi era in the Jin dynasty.
    • 乃为祖父母二弟延请僧众 - Then invited monks for his ancestors and two younger brothers.
    • 大设福会 - Greatly set up a blessing assembly.
    • 皆命子孙改意奉法 - All commanded their descendants to change their minds and follow the law.
    • 课劝精进 - Encourage diligent progress.
    • 时人闻泰死而复生 - The people of the time heard that Tai died and came back to life.
    • 多见罪福 - Often see punishment and blessings.
    • 互来访问 - Came and went to visit.
    • 时有太中大夫武城孙丰 - There was the Grand Middle Doctor Sun Feng from Wucheng.
    • 关内侯常山郝伯平等十人 - The Inner Pass Marquis Hao Boping and ten others.
    • 同集泰舍 - Gathered together at Tai's house.
    • 款曲寻问 - Carefully asked.
    • 莫不惧然 - No one did not be frightened.
    • 皆即奉法也 - All immediately followed the law.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋太始五年七月十三日 - Specific date in the Tai Shi era of the Jin dynasty, July 13th of the fifth year.
    • 地狱罪报 - Concept of punishment in hell, common in Buddhist and Taoist literature.
    • 善恶随人，其犹影响 - Metaphor comparing consequences of good and evil actions to natural shadow following a shape.
    • 祖父母二弟 - Tai's ancestors and two younger brothers, highlighting family importance in traditional Chinese culture.
    • 大设福会 - Large gathering for blessings, common in Buddhist and Taoist rituals.
    • 法苑珠林七 - Chapter Seven of the *Jewel Forest of the Dharma Garden*, a comprehensive Buddhist text.
    • 太平广记三百七十七 - Chapter 377 of the *Comprehensive Records of Peace and Prosperity*, a collection of stories and anecdotes from Chinese history.

    Tai was then sent back. At parting, the host said, "Having seen the punishment in hell like this, you should inform the world, all should do good deeds. Good and evil follow people, just as shadows follow shapes; can one not be careful?" At that time, relatives inside and outside, those who visited Tai, numbered fifty or sixty. They heard Tai's words together. Tai himself wrote it down, to show the people of the time. It was July 13th of the fifth year of the Tai Shi era in the Jin dynasty. Then, Tai invited monks for his ancestors and two younger brothers, greatly set up a blessing assembly. All commanded their descendants to change their minds and follow the law, encouraging diligent progress. The people of the time heard that Tai died and came back to life, often seeing punishment and blessings, and came and went to visit. There were the Grand Middle Doctor Sun Feng from Wucheng, the Inner Pass Marquis Hao Boping and ten others, who gathered together at Tai's house, carefully asked, and no one did not be frightened. All immediately followed the law. Chapter Seven of the Jewel Forest of the Dharma Garden, Chapter 377 of the Comprehensive Records of Peace and Prosperity. [13]

    晋沙门支法衡，晋初人也。得病旬日亡。经三日而稣活。说死时，有人将去，见如官曹舍者数处，不肯受之。俄见有铁轮，轮上有铁爪，从西转来；无持引者，而转驶如风。有一吏呼罪人当轮立；轮转来轹之，翻还；如此，数人碎烂。吏呼衡道人来当轮立。衡恐怖自责，悔不精进，“今当此轮乎？”语毕，谓衡曰：“道人可去！”于是仰首，见天有孔，不觉倏尔上升。以头穿中，两手搏两边，四向顾视，见七宝宫殿，及诸天人。衡甚踊跃，不能得上。疲而复还下所。将衡去。人笑曰：“见何等物，不能上乎？”乃以衡付船官。船官行船，使为柂工。衡曰：“我不能持柂。”强之。有船数百，皆随衡后。衡不晓捉柂，跄沙洲上。吏司推衡，汝道而失，以法应斩。 [14]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [14]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 晋沙门支法衡 - Jin monk Zhi Faheng, where '沙门' (shāmén) refers to a Buddhist monk, and '支法衡' is the name of the monk.
    • 得病旬日亡 - Died after being ill for ten days, where '旬日' (xúnrì) is a classical term meaning 'ten days'.
    • 经三日而稣活 - Revived after three days, where '稣活' (sūhuó) means 'revive' or 'resuscitate'.
    • 如官曹舍者数处 - Several places like government offices, where '官曹舍' (guān cáo shè) refers to government offices or halls.
    • 不肯受之 - Would not accept him, where '受之' (shòu zhī) means 'accept him'.
    • 见如铁轮，轮上有铁爪，从西转来 - Saw a wheel made of iron, with iron claws on it, turning from the west, where '铁轮' (tiě lún) means 'iron wheel' and '铁爪' (tiě zhǎo) means 'iron claws'.
    • 无持引者，而转驶如风 - Without anyone holding or pulling it, it turned swiftly like the wind, where '持引者' (chí yǐn zhě) means 'someone holding or pulling it'.
    • 有一吏呼罪人当轮立 - A clerk called out for a sinner to stand before the wheel, where '吏' (lì) means 'clerk' or 'official'.
    • 轮转来轹之，翻还 - The wheel turned and crushed him, then turned back, where '轹之' (lì zhī) means 'crushed him' and '翻还' (fānhuán) means 'turned back'.
    • 如此，数人碎烂 - Like this, several people were crushed to pieces, where '碎烂' (suì làn) means 'crushed to pieces'.
    • 吏呼衡道人来当轮立 - The clerk called out for Zhi Faheng to stand before the wheel, where '道人' (dào rén) means 'monk'.
    • 衡恐怖自责，悔不精进 - Zhi Faheng was terrified and blamed himself, regretting not having been diligent, where '恐怖' (kǒngbù) means 'terrified', '自责' (zìzé) means 'blamed himself', and '悔不精进' (huǐ bù jīngjìn) means 'regretting not having been diligent'.
    • 今当此轮乎 - Will I be hit by this wheel now?, where '今当此轮乎' (jīn dāng cǐ lún hū) is a rhetorical question.
    • 道人可去 - Monk, you can go, where '道人可去' (dào rén kě qù) means 'monk, you can go'.
    • 仰首，见天有孔，不觉倏尔上升 - He looked up and saw a hole in the sky, and suddenly he ascended, where '仰首' (yǎng shǒu) means 'looked up', '见天有孔' (jiàn tiān yǒu kǒng) means 'saw a hole in the sky', and '倏尔上升' (shū'ěr shàngshēng) means 'suddenly ascended'.
    • 以头穿中，两手搏两边，四向顾视 - He put his head through the hole, held onto both sides with his hands, and looked around in all directions, where '以头穿中' (yǐ tóu chuān zhōng) means 'put his head through the hole', '两手搏两边' (liǎng shǒu bó liǎng biān) means 'held onto both sides with his hands', and '四向顾视' (sì xiàng gù shì) means 'looked around in all directions'.
    • 见七宝宫殿，及诸天人 - He saw palaces made of seven treasures and various heavenly beings, where '七宝宫殿' (qībǎo gōngdiàn) means 'palaces made of seven treasures' and '诸天人' (zhū tiān rén) means 'various heavenly beings'.
    • 衡甚踊跃，不能得上 - Zhi Faheng was very excited but could not ascend, where '踊跃' (yuèyǒng) means 'excited' or 'enthusiastic'.
    • 疲而复还下所 - He grew tired and returned to the lower place, where '疲而复还下所' (pí ér fù huán xià suǒ) means 'grew tired and returned to the lower place'.
    • 将衡去 - They took Zhi Faheng away, where '将衡去' (jiāng héng qù) means 'took Zhi Faheng away'.
    • 人笑曰 - People laughed and said, where '人笑曰' (rén xiào yuē) means 'people laughed and said'.
    • 见何等物，不能上乎 - What did you see that you couldn't ascend?, where '见何等物，不能上乎' (jiàn hé děng wù, bù néng shàng hū) is a rhetorical question.
    • 乃以衡付船官 - Then they handed Zhi Faheng over to the ship officials, where '乃以衡付船官' (nǎi yǐ héng fù chuán guān) means 'then they handed Zhi Faheng over to the ship officials'.
    • 船官行船，使为柂工 - The ship officials were operating the ship and made him the helmsman, where '船官行船' (chuán guān xíng chuán) means 'ship officials were operating the ship' and '使为柂工' (shǐ wéi yǐ gōng) means 'made him the helmsman'.
    • 我不能持柂 - I cannot hold the tiller, where '我不能持柂' (wǒ bù néng chí yǐ) means 'I cannot hold the tiller'.
    • 强之 - Forced him, where '强之' (qiǎng zhī) means 'forced him'.
    • 有船数百，皆随衡后 - There were hundreds of ships following behind him, where '有船数百' (yǒu chuán shùbǎi) means 'there were hundreds of ships' and '皆随衡后' (jiē suí héng hòu) means 'following behind him'.
    • 衡不晓捉柂，跄沙洲上 - Zhi Faheng did not know how to hold the tiller and crashed onto a sandbar, where '不晓捉柂' (bù xiǎo zhuō yǐ) means 'did not know how to hold the tiller' and '跄沙洲上' (qiāng shāzhōu shàng) means 'crashed onto a sandbar'.
    • 吏司推衡，汝道而失，以法应斩 - The officials pushed Zhi Faheng and said, 'You led the ship astray, and according to the law, you should be executed', where '吏司推衡' (lì sī tuī héng) means 'officials pushed Zhi Faheng', '汝道而失' (rǔ dào ér shī) means 'you led the ship astray', and '以法应斩' (yǐ fǎ yìng zhǎn) means 'according to the law, you should be executed'.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋 - Refers to the Jin Dynasty (266-420 CE).
    • 沙门 - Refers to a Buddhist monk.
    • 官曹舍 - Refers to government offices or halls.
    • 七宝宫殿 - Refers to palaces made of seven treasures, a common motif in Buddhist and Taoist texts.
    • 诸天人 - Refers to various heavenly beings, often depicted in Buddhist and Taoist literature.
    • 船官 - Refers to ship officials or maritime authorities.
    • 柂工 - Refers to the helmsman or steersman of a ship.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • 得病旬日亡 - This phrase can be read as 'died after being ill for ten days' or 'died after a ten-day illness'.
    • 经三日而稣活 - This phrase can be read as 'revived after three days' or 'revived after'.

    A monk named Zhi Faheng from the Jin Dynasty fell ill for ten days and died. After three days, he revived. He recounted that upon his death, someone took him to several places resembling government offices, but they refused to accept him. Soon, he saw an iron wheel with iron claws on it, turning from the west. There was no one holding or pulling it, yet it turned swiftly like the wind. A clerk called out for a sinner to stand before the wheel. The wheel turned and crushed the person, then turned back. This happened to several people, who were crushed to pieces. The clerk then called out for Zhi Faheng to stand before the wheel. Terrified and blaming himself, Zhi Faheng regretted not having been diligent. "Will I be hit by this wheel now?" he asked. The clerk then told him, "Monk, you can go!" Zhi Faheng looked up and saw a hole in the sky, and suddenly he ascended. He put his head through the hole, held onto both sides with his hands, and looked around in all directions. He saw palaces made of seven treasures and various heavenly beings.
