He held a grand gathering where incense was burned, and he bid farewell to the crowd. Just as he was about to distribute the incense, a monk suddenly appeared and sat in the upper seat. His clothes were dusty and dirty, and his face was yellow and swollen. Zhu Fa Jin found him strange and lowly and pulled him to a lower seat. However, the monk repeatedly returned to the upper seat. Zhu Fa Jin pulled him three times before he disappeared. Once the crowd had settled down, Zhu Fa Jin only then ate at the lower seat. Suddenly, a strong wind blew sand, and the trays and tables tipped over. Zhu Fa Jin repented and blamed himself, and then stopped and did not enter the mountains. People at the time believed that the world was about to be in great turmoil, and Zhu Fa Jin should not enter the mountains. Both Buddhist monks and laypeople who were deeply concerned earnestly tried to persuade and admire him, and therefore, this supernatural event occurred to stop his intention to leave. (From Zhu Lin, Chapter 42) [33]

    晋周闵，汝南人也。晋护军将军。家世奉法。苏峻之乱，都邑人士，皆东西波迁。广记引作播迁闵家有大品一部，以半幅八丈素反覆书之。又有余经数台，大品亦杂在其中。既当避难，单行不能得尽持去；尤惜大品，不知在何台中。仓卒应去，不展寻搜，广记引无此句徘徊叹吒。不觉大品忽自出外。闵惊喜持去。周氏遂世宝之。今云尚在。一说云：周嵩妇胡母氏，有素书大品。素广五寸，而大品一部尽在焉。又并有舍利，银罂贮之。并缄于深箧。永嘉之乱，胡母将避兵南奔，经及舍利，自出箧外。因取怀之。以渡江东。又尝遇火，不暇取经。及屋尽火灭，得之于灰尽之下，俨然如故。会稽王道子就嵩，曾云求以供养。后尝蹔在新渚寺。刘敬叔云，曾亲见此经：字如麻大，巧密分明。新渚寺，今天安是也。此经盖得道僧释慧则所写也。或云，尝在简靖寺，靖首尼读。珠林十八广记一百十三 [34]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [34]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 晋周闵，汝南人也 - Standard biographical formula meaning 'Jin Zhou Min, a person from Ruyang.'
    • 晋护军将军 - Refers to Zhou Min's official title, 'Guardian Army General' during the Jin dynasty.
    • 家世奉法 - Means 'his family has always adhered to the law,' indicating a tradition of legalistic or Confucian values.
    • 苏峻之乱 - Refers to the rebellion led by Su Jun during the Eastern Jin period.
    • 都邑人士，皆东西波迁 - People from the capital cities all moved east and west. The word '波' (bo) here is unusual and may be a typographical error or variant; it could be intended as '播' (bo), meaning 'scatter' or 'disperse.'
    • 大品一部 - Refers to a complete set of a Buddhist scripture, likely the *Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra* (大般若波罗蜜多经).
    • 半幅八丈素反覆书之 - Written back and forth on a half-width eight-zhang piece of white silk. This describes the method of writing on a large scroll, with '素' (su) referring to unbleached silk used for writing.
    • 余经数台 - Several other scriptures. The word '台' (tai) is unusual in this context and may be a variant of '帙' (zhi), meaning 'scroll' or 'volume.'
    • 仓卒应去 - In the hurry of departure, indicating the urgency of the situation during the chaos of the rebellion.
    • 徘徊叹吒 - Wandering about, sighing and murmuring, describing Zhou Min's emotional state.
    • 忽自出外 - Suddenly appeared outside, referring to the unexpected emergence of the scripture.
    • 遂世宝之 - Was thus treasured by the family for generations, indicating the enduring value of the scripture.
    • 一说云 - Another account says, introducing an alternative version of the story.
    • 周嵩妇胡母氏 - Zhou Song's wife, Lady Humu, introducing a secondary figure in the narrative.
    • 素书大品 - A white silk-written *Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra*.
    • 素广五寸 - Five inches wide, describing the width of the silk.
    • 并有舍利 - Also contains relics, referring to Buddhist relics.
    • 银罂贮之 - Stored in a silver vessel, describing the container for the relics.
    • 缄于深箧 - Sealed in a deep chest, indicating the careful storage of the relics.
    • 永嘉之乱 - Refers to the rebellion during the Western Jin dynasty, particularly the invasion by the Xiongnu.
    • 胡母将避兵南奔 - Lady Humu fled south to avoid the war, describing her escape.
    • 经及舍利，自出箧外 - The scripture and relics, they spontaneously appeared outside the chest, describing the miraculous event.
    • 因取怀之 - She took them in her arms, indicating her care for the sacred items.
    • 以渡江东 - To cross the river eastward, referring to her journey to the eastern part of China.
    • 又尝遇火 - She once encountered a fire, describing a dangerous situation.
    • 及屋尽火灭 - When the house was destroyed and the fire extinguished, indicating the aftermath of the fire.
    • 得之于灰烬之下，俨然如故 - Found them under the ashes, still as they were, describing the miraculous preservation of the items.
    • 会稽王道子就嵩，曾云求以供养 - The Prince of Kuaiji, Daozi, once sought to obtain them for worship, introducing another historical figure.
    • 新渚寺 - New Zhu Temple, a place where the scripture was once kept.
    • 刘敬叔云，曾亲见此经 - Liu Jingshu said he once saw this scripture personally, introducing a witness to the event.
    • 字如麻大，巧密分明 - The characters were as large as hemp, intricately and clearly written, describing the appearance of the scripture.
    • 慧则 - Hui Ze, the name of a monk who wrote the scripture.
    • 简靖寺 - Jianjing Temple, another place where the scripture was kept.
    • 靖首尼读 - Read by the nun Jingshou, introducing a nun who studied the scripture.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋周闵 - Zhou Min, a historical figure during the Jin dynasty.
    • 汝南 - Ruyang, a place name in Henan province.
    • 苏峻之乱 - The rebellion led by Su Jun in 328 CE, a significant event in the Eastern Jin period.
    • 大品 - The *Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra*, a major Buddhist scripture.
    • 永嘉之乱 - The rebellion during the Western Jin dynasty, particularly the invasion by the Xiongnu in 311 CE.
    • 会稽王道子 - Daozi, the Prince of Kuaiji, a prominent figure during the Eastern Jin period.
    • 刘敬叔 - Liu Jingshu, a historian and writer from the Southern Song dynasty.
    • 慧则 - Hui Ze, a monk who wrote the scripture.
    • 简靖寺 - Jianjing Temple, a Buddhist temple where the scripture was kept.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋周闵 - Zhou Min, a historical figure during the Jin dynasty.
    • 汝南 - Ruyang, a place name in Henan province.
    • 苏峻 - Su Jun, a rebel leader during the Eastern Jin period.
    • 大品 - The *Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra*.
    • 永嘉 - A period in the Western Jin dynasty marked by rebellion and invasion.
    • 会稽王道子 - Daozi, the Prince of Kuaiji.
    • 刘敬叔 - Liu Jingshu, a historian and writer.
    • 慧则 - Hui Ze, a monk.
    • 简靖寺 - Jianjing Temple.
    • 靖首尼 - Nun Jingshou.

    A Man Named Zhou Min from Ruyang

    Zhou Min was a man from Ruyang during the Jin dynasty, holding the title of Guardian Army General. His family had always adhered to the law. During the rebellion led by Su Jun, people from the capital cities all moved east and west. Zhou Min's family had a complete set of the Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra, written back and forth on a half-width eight-zhang piece of white silk. They also had several other scriptures, with the Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra mixed among them. When they had to flee, they could not take everything with them; they especially cherished the Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra and did not know which scroll it was in. In the hurry of departure, they could not thoroughly search, and Zhou Min wandered about, sighing and murmuring. Suddenly, the Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra appeared outside. Zhou Min was overjoyed and took it with him. The Zhou family thus treasured it for generations, and it is said to still be in existence. Another account says: Zhou Song's wife, Lady Humu, had a white silk-written Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra. The silk was five inches wide, and the entire Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra was written on it. It also contained relics, stored in a silver vessel, sealed in a deep chest. During the rebellion of the Western Jin dynasty, Lady Humu fled south to avoid the war. The scripture and relics spontaneously appeared outside the chest. She took them in her arms and crossed the river eastward. She once encountered a fire, and when the house was destroyed and the fire extinguished, she found them under the ashes, still as they were. The Prince of Kuaiji, Daozi, once sought to obtain them for worship. Later, the scripture was temporarily kept at New Zhu Temple. Liu Jingshu said he once saw this scripture personally: the characters were as large as hemp, intricately and clearly written. New Zhu Temple is now known as Tian'an Temple. This scripture was written by the monk Hui Ze. It is also said that it was once kept at Jianjing Temple, where it was read by the nun Jingshou. [34]

    晋史世光者，襄阳人也。咸和八年，于武昌死。七日，沙门支法山转小品，疲而微卧。闻灵座上，如有人声。史家有婢，字张信，见世光在灵上着衣帢，具如平生。语信云：“我本应堕龙中，广记引作狱中支和尚为我转经，昙护昙坚迎我上第七梵天快乐处矣。护坚并是山之沙弥，已亡者也。后支法山复往为转大品，又来在坐。世光生时，以二旛供养。时在寺中。乃呼张信持旛送我。信曰“诺，”便绝死。将信持旛，俱西北飞上一青山。上如?璃色。到山顶，望见天门，世光乃自提旛，遣信令还；与一青香，如巴豆，曰：“以上支和尚。”信未还，便遥见世光直入天门。信复道而还，倏忽醒活；亦不复见手中香也；旛亦故在寺中。 [35]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [35]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 咸和八年 - Specific date in the Eastern Jin dynasty, corresponding to 333 AD.
    • 沙门 - Refers to Buddhist monks.
    • 转小品 - Refers to reciting or chanting a shorter Buddhist text (Sūtra).
    • 灵座上 - Refers to the place where a deceased person is placed for worship.
    • 如有人声 - Means 'as if there were human voices.'
    • 灵上着衣帢 - Refers to the deceased person wearing clothes and a hat.
    • 本应堕龙中 - Means 'originally should have fallen into the dragon realm' (likely referring to hell or suffering).
    • 第七梵天 - Refers to the seventh level of the heavenly realm in Buddhist cosmology.
    • 倏忽醒活 - Means 'suddenly woke up alive.'
    • 西北飞上一青山 - Describes a sudden flight to the northwest to a green mountain.
    • 如琉璃色 - Means 'like the color of lapis lazuli' (a deep blue color).
    • 天门 - Refers to the gate to heaven.
    • 提幡 - Means 'to hold a banner.'
    • 遣信令还 - Means 'to send O Zhang back.'
    • 青香，如巴豆 - Refers to a green incense that looks like the fruit of the castor oil plant.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋史世光 - A historical figure from the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 襄阳 - A city in present-day Hubei Province, China.
    • 武昌 - A city in present-day Hubei Province, China.
    • 支法山 - A Buddhist monk known for his recitation of sutras.
    • 小品 - A shorter Buddhist text, often a condensed version of a larger sutra.
    • 大品 - A longer Buddhist text, often a more complete version of a sutra.
    • 昙护昙坚 - Two deceased monks who are mentioned as welcoming the deceased to heaven.
    • 沙弥 - Refers to novice monks.
    • 旛 - Refers to a banner or flag used in religious ceremonies.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋史世光 - A historical figure.
    • 襄阳 - A city.
    • 武昌 - A city.
    • 支法山 - A monk.
    • 小品 - A Buddhist text.
    • 昙护昙坚 - Deceased monks.
    • 沙弥 - Novice monks.
    • 旛 - A religious banner.

    Jin Shi Shiguang, a man from Xiangyang, died in Wuchang in the eighth year of Xianhe (333 AD). Seven days later, the monk Zhi Fashan chanted the shorter sutra, growing tired and taking a slight nap. He heard what seemed like human voices coming from the spirit seat. A servant of the Shishi household, named Zhang Xin, saw Shiguang on the spirit seat, dressed and wearing a hat, just as he had been in life. Shiguang spoke to Zhang Xin, saying, "I was originally destined to fall into the dragon realm, but the monk Zhi Fashan chanted sutras for me, and the deceased monks Tan Hu and Tan Jian welcomed me to the seventh level of the heavenly realm, a place of great joy. Tan Hu and Tan Jian were both novices of Zhi Fashan and have since passed away. Later, Zhi Fashan returned to chant the longer sutra, and came back to sit with us. When I was alive, I offered two banners as a gift. At that time, they were in the temple. I called Zhang Xin to take the banners and send me off. Zhang Xin said, 'Yes,' and then died. I took Zhang Xin with me, holding the banners, and we flew northwest to a green mountain. The mountain was like lapis lazuli in color. When we reached the summit, I saw the gate to heaven. I took the banners and sent Zhang Xin back, giving him a green incense that looked like the fruit of the castor oil plant, and said, 'Offer this to the monk Zhi Fashan.' Before Zhang Xin returned, I saw Shiguang enter the gate to heaven directly. Zhang Xin returned by the same path and suddenly woke up alive; he no longer saw the incense in his hand, but the banners were still in the temple. [35]

    世光与信，于家去时，其六岁儿见之，指语祖母曰：“阿爷飞上天，婆为见不？”世光后复与天人十余，俱还其家，徘徊而去。每来必见簪帢，去必露髻。信问之。答曰：“天上有冠，不着此也。”后乃着天冠与群天神，鼓琴行歌，经上母堂。信问何用屡来。曰，“我来，欲使汝辈知罪福也；亦兼娱乐阿母。”琴音清妙，不类世声；家人大小，悉得闻之。然闻其声，如隔壁障，不得亲察也。唯信闻之，独分明焉。有顷去，信自送。广记引有送字见世光入一黑门，有顷来出，广记引作寻即出来谓信曰：“舅在此，日见榜挞，楚痛难胜。省视还也。舅生犯杀罪，故受此报。可告舅母：会僧转经，当稍免脱。”舅即轻车将军报终也。珠林五广记一百十二 [36]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [36]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 阿爺飛上天 - Colloquial expression where '阿爺' means 'grandfather' or 'father,' and '飛上天' means 'flying to heaven,' suggesting a supernatural event.
    • 婆為見不 - Colloquial question where '婆' means 'grandmother' and '為見不' means 'can see or not.'
    • 簪帢 - '簪' refers to a hairpin, and '帢' refers to a cap or hat.
    • 露髻 - Means 'uncovered hair,' often associated with a more casual or informal appearance.
    • 天冠 - Refers to a heavenly crown or headdress.
    • 鼓琴行歌 - Means 'playing the lute and singing while walking.'
    • 經上母堂 - Means 'passing by the mother's room.'
    • 欲使汝輩知罪福也 - Means 'to make you all understand the consequences of good and evil deeds.'
    • 亦兼娛樂阿母 - Means 'also to entertain the grandmother.'
    • 琴音清妙 - Means 'the sound of the lute is clear and exquisite.'
    • 不类世声 - Means 'not like the sounds of this world.'
    • 悉得闻之 - Means 'all can hear it.'
    • 唯信闻之，独分明焉 - Means 'only Xin can hear it clearly.'
    • 廣記引有送字見世光入一黑門 - Means 'In the *Extensive Records*, it mentions the word 'send' and describes Shi Guang entering a black gate.'
    • 舅在此，日見榜挞，楚痛難勝 - Means 'Uncle is here, and he is beaten daily, suffering unbearable pain.'
    • 省視還也 - Means 'I will visit and return.'
    • 舅生犯殺罪，故受此報 - Means 'Uncle committed a murder, so he is receiving this punishment.'
    • 可告舅母：會僧轉經，當稍免脫 - Means 'Tell Aunt that gathering monks to recite sutras will gradually alleviate his suffering.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 世光 - A name that may refer to a historical figure or a fictional character.
    • 信 - A name that may refer to a historical figure or a fictional character.
    • 天人 - Refers to celestial beings or deities.
    • 天冠 - A heavenly crown, often associated with divine or supernatural beings.
    • 鼓琴行歌 - Playing the lute and singing, a common form of entertainment in ancient China.
    • 輕車將軍 - A title for a military officer, suggesting a high-ranking position.
    • 珠林五 - Refers to a specific section or chapter in a historical or literary text.
    • 廣記一百十二 - Refers to a specific section or chapter in another historical or literary text.

    Proper Nouns

    • 世光 - A name that may refer to a historical figure or a fictional character.
    • 信 - A name that may refer to a historical figure or a fictional character.
    • 天人 - Celestial beings or deities.
    • 輕車將軍 - A title for a military officer.
    • 珠林五 - A specific section or chapter in a historical or literary text.
    • 廣記一百十二 - A specific section or chapter in another historical or literary text.

    Shi Guang and Xin were leaving home when their six-year-old son saw something and pointed to his grandmother, saying, "Grandfather is flying to heaven, can you see him?" Later, Shi Guang returned with more than ten celestial beings, all of whom went back to his home and lingered before leaving. Every time they came, they wore hairpins and hats, but when they left, their hair was uncovered. Xin asked them why. They replied, "In heaven, there are crowns, so we don't wear these." Afterward, they wore heavenly crowns and accompanied a group of divine beings, playing the lute and singing as they walked past the mother's room. Xin asked why they came so often. They said, "We come to make you all understand the consequences of good and evil deeds; we also come to entertain the grandmother." The sound of the lute was clear and exquisite, unlike the sounds of this world; all the family members could hear it. However, the sound was like hearing through a wall, and they could not see them clearly. Only Xin could hear it distinctly. After a while, they left, and Xin accompanied them. In the Extensive Records, it mentions the word "send" and describes Shi Guang entering a black gate. After a while, he came out and said to Xin, "Uncle is here, and he is beaten daily, suffering unbearable pain. I will visit and return. Uncle committed a murder, so he is receiving this punishment. Tell Aunt that gathering monks to recite sutras will gradually alleviate his suffering." The uncle was the General of Light Chariots, and this is the end of his story. This is recorded in Zhu Lin Volume Five and Extensive Records Chapter One Hundred and Twelve. [36]

    晋张应者，历阳人。本事俗神，鼓舞淫祀。咸和八年，移居芜湖。妻得病。应请祷备至，财产略尽。妻，法家弟子也，谓曰：“今病日困，求鬼无益，乞作佛事。”应许之。往精舍中，见竺昙铠。昙铠曰：“佛如愈病之药。见药不服，虽视无益。”应许当事佛。昙铠与期明日往斋。应归，夜梦见一人，长丈余，从南来。入门曰：“汝家狼藉，乃尔不净。”见昙铠随后，曰：“始欲发意，未可责之。”应先巧眠觉，便炳火作高座，及鬼子母座。昙铠明往，应具说梦。遂受五戒。斥除神影，大设福供。妻病即闲，寻都除愈。咸康二年，应至马沟籴盐。还泊芜湖浦宿。梦见三人，以○钩钩之。应曰：“我佛弟子。”牵终不置，曰：“奴叛走多时。 [37]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [37]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 本事俗神 - Literally 'to serve the vulgar gods,' refers to engaging in the worship of local or popular deities.
    • 鼓舞淫祀 - Literally 'to dance and sing in licentious rituals,' describes the practice of performing rituals that are considered excessive or immoral.
    • 法家弟子 - Refers to a follower of the Legalist school of thought, which emphasized strict laws and harsh punishments.
    • 佛如愈病之藥 - A metaphor comparing the Buddha to medicine that cures illness.
    • 見藥不服，雖視無益 - Literally 'seeing medicine but not taking it, even though looking at it is of no benefit,' means that merely observing something without acting on it is ineffective.
    • 斥除神影 - Literally 'to expel the shadows of the gods,' refers to removing or dismissing the influence of the gods.
    • 大设福供 - Literally 'to make a great offering of merit,' describes the act of performing significant religious rituals to accumulate good karma.
    • 奴叛走多时 - Literally 'a slave has run away for a long time,' is used metaphorically to suggest that the person being addressed has been absent or unresponsive for a prolonged period.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋张应者 - Refers to Zhang Ying, a person from the Jin dynasty.
    • 历阳 - A place name, likely referring to a region in present-day Anhui province.
    • 芜湖 - Another place name, referring to a city in present-day Anhui province.
    • 竺昙铠 - A monk or religious figure during the Jin dynasty.
    • 五戒 - Refers to the Five Precepts in Buddhism, which are basic ethical guidelines for lay Buddhists.
    • 咸和八年 - The eighth year of the Xianhe era, which corresponds to 333 CE.
    • 咸康二年 - The second year of the Xiankang era, which corresponds to 336 CE.
    • 马沟 - A place name, likely referring to a location near the salt production area.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋张应 - Zhang Ying, a person from the Jin dynasty.
    • 历阳 - A place name, likely referring to a region in present-day Anhui province.
    • 芜湖 - A place name, referring to a city in present-day Anhui province.
    • 竺昙铠 - A monk or religious figure during the Jin dynasty.
    • 五戒 - The Five Precepts in Buddhism.
    • 咸和八年 - The eighth year of the Xianhe era, 333 CE.
    • 咸康二年 - The second year of the Xiankang era, 336 CE.
    • 马沟 - A place name, likely referring to a location near the salt production area.

    Zhang Ying of the Jin dynasty was from Lüyang. He used to serve the vulgar gods and engage in licentious rituals. In the eighth year of the Xianhe era (333 CE), he moved to Wuhu. His wife fell ill. Zhang Ying prayed fervently and spent all his wealth on offerings. His wife, a follower of the Legalist school, said to him, "Now my illness is worsening, praying to the gods is of no benefit. Please perform Buddhist rites." Zhang Ying agreed. He went to a temple and met Zhi Tan Kai. Zhi Tan Kai said, "The Buddha is like medicine that cures illness. Seeing medicine but not taking it, even though looking at it is of no benefit." Zhang Ying agreed to follow Buddhist practices. Zhi Tan Kai set a date for the next day to conduct the rites. That night, Zhang Ying had a dream where a man over ten feet tall came from the south and entered the house, saying, "Your home is in disarray and not clean enough." He saw Zhi Tan Kai following and said, "You have just started to have an intention, so you cannot be blamed." Zhang Ying woke up and immediately lit a fire and prepared a high seat and a seat for the Ghost Mother. The next day, Zhi Tan Kai arrived, and Zhang Ying told him about the dream. He then received the Five Precepts. He expelled the influence of the gods and made a great offering of merit. His wife's illness improved and soon she was completely cured. In the second year of the Xiankang era (336 CE), Zhang Ying went to Magou to buy salt. On his return, he stopped at the Wuhu harbor to spend the night. He dreamed of three people who hooked him with a hook. He said, "I am a disciple of the Buddha." They continued to pull him, saying, "A slave has run away for a long time." [37]

    ”应怖谓曰：“放我，当与君一升酒调。”乃放之。谓应，但畏后人复取汝耳。眠觉，腹痛泄痢；达家大困。应与昙铠，问绝已久。病甚，遣呼之。适值不在。应寻气绝。经日而稣活。说有数人以○钩钩将北去。下一阪岸。岸下见有镬汤刀剑，楚毒之具。应时悟是地狱。欲呼师名，忘昙铠字，但唤“和上救我！”亦时唤佛。有顷，一人从西面来，形长丈余，执金杵，欲撞此钩人，曰：“佛弟子也，何入此中？”钩人怖散。长人引应去，谓曰：“汝命已尽，不复久生。可蹔还家。颂呗三偈，并取和上名字，三日当复命过，即生天矣。”应既稣，即复怵然。既而三日，持斋颂呗，遣人疏取昙铠名。至日中，食毕，礼佛读呗，遍与家人辞别。澡洗着衣，如眠便尽。珠林六十二 [38]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [38]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 應怖謂曰 - A compound phrase meaning 'Ying Bo said to him.'
    • 放我，當與君一升酒調 - A colloquial expression meaning 'Let me go, and I will give you a measure of wine.'
    • 但畏后人復取汝耳 - A colloquial expression meaning 'I am only afraid that someone else will take you.'
    • 眠覺，腹痛泄痢 - A literal description of symptoms: 'After sleeping, he felt abdominal pain and diarrhea.'
    • 達家大困 - A colloquial expression meaning 'He was very weak when he returned home.'
    • 應與昙铠，问绝已久 - A colloquial expression meaning 'Ying Bo and Tan Kai had been out of touch for a long time.'
    • 病甚，遣呼之 - A colloquial expression meaning 'He was very ill and sent for him.'
    • 適值不在 - A colloquial expression meaning 'He happened to be away.'
    • 應尋气绝 - A colloquial expression meaning 'Ying Bo soon died.'
    • 经日而稣活 - A colloquial expression meaning 'After a day, he revived.'
    • 说有数人以○钩钩将北去 - A colloquial expression meaning 'He said that several people were dragging him north with hooks.'
    • 下一阪岸 - A colloquial expression meaning 'They descended to a slope by the bank.'
    • 岸下见有镬汤刀剑，楚毒之具 - A literal description of the scene: 'At the bottom of the bank, there were cauldrons of boiling water, swords, and instruments of torment.'
    • 应时悟是地狱 - A colloquial expression meaning 'Ying Bo realized he was in hell.'
    • 欲呼师名，忘昙铠字 - A colloquial expression meaning 'He wanted to call out his teacher's name but forgot Tan Kai's name.'
    • 但唤“和上救我！”亦时唤佛 - A colloquial expression meaning 'He only called out 'Save me, Venerable One!' and also called out to the Buddha.'
    • 有顷，一人从西面来，形长丈余，执金杵 - A colloquial expression meaning 'After a while, a man came from the west, over ten feet tall, holding a golden pestle.'
    • 欲撞此钩人，曰：“佛弟子也，何入此中？” - A colloquial expression meaning 'He was about to strike the people with hooks, saying, 'You are a disciple of the Buddha, why are you here?''
    • 钩人怖散 - A colloquial expression meaning 'The people with hooks were terrified and scattered.'
    • 长人引应去，谓曰：“汝命已尽，不复久生。可蹔还家。颂呗三偈，并取和上名字，三日当复命过，即生天矣。” - A colloquial expression meaning 'The tall man led Ying Bo away and said, 'Your life is over, you will not live long. You can return home temporarily. Recite three verses of chanting and mention your teacher's name. In three days, you will be reborn in heaven.'
    • 应既稣，即复怵然 - A colloquial expression meaning 'After reviving, Ying Bo was once again terrified.'
    • 既而三日，持斋颂呗，遣人疏取昙铠名 - A colloquial expression meaning 'After three days, he observed a fast, recited chants, and had someone write down Tan Kai's name.'
    • 至日中，食毕，礼佛读呗，遍与家人辞别 - A colloquial expression meaning 'At noon, after eating, he paid respects to the Buddha, recited chants, and bid farewell to his family.'
    • 澡洗着衣，如眠便尽 - A colloquial expression meaning 'He bathed, dressed, and then died as if falling asleep.'
    • 珠林六十二 - A reference to the *Zhu Lin* (Jewel Forest) volume 62, a collection of Buddhist stories.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 應怖 - Ying Bo, the main character in the story.
    • 昙铠 - Tan Kai, Ying Bo's teacher.
    • 和上 - A respectful term for a senior monk or teacher.
    • 地狱 - Hell, a place of torment in Buddhist cosmology.
    • 镬汤刀剑 - Cauldrons of boiling water and swords, instruments of torment in hell.
    • 楚毒之具 - Instruments of torture, used to describe the tools of punishment in hell.
    • 颂呗 - Chanting, a form of Buddhist devotional practice.
    • 三偈 - Three verses of chanting, often used in Buddhist rituals.
    • 珠林六十二 - *Zhu Lin* volume 62, a collection of Buddhist stories and teachings.

    Proper Nouns

    • 應怖 - Ying Bo, the main character.
    • 昙铠 - Tan Kai, Ying Bo's teacher.
    • 和上 - A respectful term for a senior monk or teacher.
    • 地狱 - Hell, a place of torment in Buddhist cosmology.
    • 镬汤刀剑 - Cauldrons of boiling water and swords, instruments of torment in hell.
    • 楚毒之具 - Instruments of torture, used to describe the tools of punishment in hell.
    • 颂呗 - Chanting, a form of Buddhist devotional practice.
    • 三偈 - Three verses of chanting, often used in Buddhist rituals.
    • 珠林六十二 - *Zhu Lin* volume 62, a collection of Buddhist stories and teachings.

    Ying Bo said to him, "Let me go, and I will give you a measure of wine." The person released him. He told Ying Bo, "I am only afraid that someone else will take you." After sleeping, he felt abdominal pain and diarrhea; he was very weak when he returned home. Ying Bo and Tan Kai had been out of touch for a long time. He was very ill and sent for him, but he happened to be away. Ying Bo soon died. After a day, he revived. He said that several people were dragging him north with hooks. They descended to a slope by the bank. At the bottom of the bank, there were cauldrons of boiling water, swords, and instruments of torment. Ying Bo realized he was in hell. He wanted to call out his teacher's name but forgot Tan Kai's name. He only called out, "Save me, Venerable One!" and also called out to the Buddha. After a while, a man came from the west, over ten feet tall, holding a golden pestle. He was about to strike the people with hooks, saying, "You are a disciple of the Buddha, why are you here?" The people with hooks were terrified and scattered. The tall man led Ying Bo away and said, "Your life is over, you will not live long. You can return home temporarily. Recite three verses of chanting and mention your teacher's name. In three days, you will be reborn in heaven." After reviving, Ying Bo was once again terrified. After three days, he observed a fast, recited chants, and had someone write down Tan Kai's name. At noon, after eating, he paid respects to the Buddha, recited chants, and bid farewell to his family. He bathed, dressed, and then died as if falling asleep. Zhu Lin volume 62. [38]

    晋董吉者，于潜人也。奉法三世，至吉尤精进。恒斋戒诵首楞严经。屯中有病，辄请吉读经；所救多愈。同县何晃者，亦奉法士也。咸和中，卒得山毒之病，守困。晃兄惶遽，驰往请吉。董何两舍，相去六七十里。复隔大溪，五月中，大雨。晃兄初渡时，水尚未至。吉与期设中食后。此字广记引有比往而山水暴涨，不复可涉，吉不能泅；迟回叹息；坐岸良久；欲下不敢渡。吉既信直。必欲赴期。乃恻然发心，自誓曰：“吾救人苦急，不计躯命。克冀如来大士，当照乃诚。”便脱衣，以囊经戴置头上，迳入水中，量其深浅，乃应至颈。及吉渡，正着膝耳。既得上岸，失囊经，甚惋恨。进至晃家，三礼忏悔，流涕自责。俯仰之闲，便见经囊在高座上。吉悲喜取看，邑邑如有湿气。开囊视经，尚燥如故。 [39]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [39]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 奉法三世 - Formal expression meaning 'having practiced the Dharma for three generations,' indicating a long-standing tradition of religious practice.
    • 恒斋戒诵首楞严经 - Means 'constantly observing斋戒 and reciting the *Shou Leng Yan Jing*,' where *斋戒* refers to fasting and abstaining from worldly activities as a form of spiritual discipline.
    • 屯中有病 - Colloquial phrase meaning 'when someone in the village is sick,' where *屯* refers to a small community or village.
    • 辄请吉读经 - Means 'they would always invite Ji to read the sutras,' where *辄* indicates habitual action.
    • 所救多愈 - Means 'most of those he helped recovered,' where *愈* means 'to recover.'
    • 亦奉法士也 - Means 'is also a practitioner of the Dharma,' where *奉法士* refers to someone who follows the teachings of Buddhism.
    • 咸和中 - Formal expression meaning 'during the Xianhe period,' referring to a specific era in Chinese history (326–334 CE).
    • 卒得山毒之病 - Means 'suddenly contracted a poisonous mountain illness,' where *山毒* refers to a disease believed to be caused by mountain spirits or poisonous plants.
    • 守困 - Means 'became severely ill and bedridden,' where *守困* indicates a state of prolonged suffering.
    • 惶遽 - Means 'in great distress or panic,' where *惶遽* conveys a sense of urgency and fear.
    • 驰往请吉 - Means 'rushed to ask Ji for help,' where *驰* indicates speed or haste.
    • 六七十里 - Colloquial expression meaning 'about sixty to seventy li,' where *里* is an ancient Chinese unit of distance.
    • 复隔大溪 - Means 'separated by a large river,' where *溪* refers to a stream or river.
    • 五月中 - Means 'in the middle of May,' where *中* indicates the midpoint of the month.
    • 大雨 - Means 'heavy rain,' where *雨* refers to precipitation.
    • 水尚未至 - Means 'the water had not yet risen,' where *尚未* indicates that something has not yet occurred.
    • 吉与期设中食后 - Means 'Ji agreed to meet after midday meal,' where *期设* refers to setting a time for an appointment.
    • 山水暴涨 - Means 'the mountain torrent suddenly rose,' where *暴涨* indicates a sudden and dramatic increase in water level.
    • 不复可涉 - Means 'it was no longer possible to cross,' where *涉* refers to crossing a body of water.
    • 吉不能泅 - Means 'Ji could not swim,' where *泅* specifically refers to swimming across water.
    • 迟回叹息 - Means 'hesitated and sighed,' where *迟回* indicates hesitation or indecision.
    • 坐岸良久 - Means 'sat by the riverbank for a long time,' where *良久* indicates a prolonged period.
    • 欲下不敢渡 - Means 'wanted to go down but dared not cross,' where *欲下* indicates the intention to proceed.
    • 吉既信直 - Means 'Ji, being honest and straightforward,' where *信直* refers to honesty and integrity.
    • 必欲赴期 - Means 'determined to keep his appointment,' where *赴期* indicates keeping a promise or commitment.
    • 恻然发心 - Means 'felt compassion and made a vow,' where *恻然* indicates a deep emotional response.
    • 自誓曰 - Means 'vowed to himself,' where *自誓* indicates a personal vow.
    • 吾救人苦急 - Means 'I will save people in their time of urgent suffering,' where *苦急* emphasizes the urgency and severity of the situation.
    • 不计躯命 - Means 'regardless of my own life,' where *躯命* refers to one's physical existence.
    • 克冀如来大士 - Means 'I hope the Buddha or Bodhisattva will grant me this wish,' where *克冀* indicates a strong desire or hope.
    • 当照乃诚 - Means 'will illuminate my sincerity,' where *照乃诚* suggests that the Buddha or Bodhisattva will recognize the sincerity of the vow.
    • 迳入水中 - Means 'directly entered the water,' where *迳* indicates a direct action without hesitation.
    • 量其深浅 - Means 'measured the depth,' where *深浅* refers to the depth of the water.
    • 乃应至颈 - Means 'it should have reached up to the neck,' where *应至颈* indicates the expected depth.
    • 及吉渡，正着膝耳 - Means 'when Ji crossed, it only reached his knees,' where *正着膝耳* indicates the actual depth.
    • 既得上岸 - Means 'after successfully crossing the river,' where *既得* indicates completion of an action.
    • 失囊经 - Means 'lost the bag containing the sutra,' where *失* indicates loss.
    • 甚惋恨 - Means 'was very regretful,' where *惋恨* conveys a sense of deep regret.
    • 进至晃家 - Means 'went to He's house,' where *进至* indicates movement toward a destination.
    • 三礼忏悔 - Means 'performed three prostrations and repentance,' where *三礼* refers to three bows or prostrations, and *忏悔* means repentance.
    • 流涕自责 - Means 'shed tears and blamed himself,' where *流涕* indicates shedding tears, and *自责* means self-blame.
    • 俯仰之间 - Means 'in a moment,' where *俯仰* indicates a brief period of time.
    • 便见经囊在高座上 - Means 'suddenly saw the bag containing the sutra on the high seat,' where *高座* refers to a high platform or seat.
    • 悲喜取看 - Means 'took it with a mix of sadness and joy,' where *悲喜* indicates mixed emotions.
    • 邑邑如有湿气 - Means 'seemed damp and discolored,' where *邑邑* indicates a state of dampness, and *湿气* means moisture.
    • 开囊视经，尚燥如故 - Means 'opened the bag and saw the sutra was still dry as before,' where *尚燥如故* indicates that the sutra remained dry despite the apparent dampness.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋 - Refers to the Jin Dynasty (266–420 CE), a period in Chinese history.
    • 董吉 - The name of a historical figure, likely a Buddhist monk or practitioner.
    • 于潜 - A place name, likely referring to a region in Zhejiang Province, China.
    • 奉法 - Refers to the practice of following the Dharma, specifically Buddhism.
    • 首楞严经 - The *Shou Leng Yan Jing* (Surangama Sutra), a significant Buddhist scripture.
    • 咸和中 - Refers to the Xianhe period (326–334 CE) during the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 山毒之病 - A term for a disease believed to be caused by mountain spirits or poisonous plants, reflecting traditional Chinese beliefs about the supernatural and natural world.
    • 斋戒 - A Buddhist practice of fasting and abstaining from worldly activities as a form of spiritual discipline.
    • 佛 - Refers to the Buddha, the founder of Buddhism.
    • 菩萨 - Refers to Bodhisattvas, enlightened beings who delay their entry into nirvana to help others achieve enlightenment.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • 晋董吉者，于潜人也 - This opening clause introduces the subject and his place of origin, but it is elliptical and requires some interpretation to clarify the relationship between the subject and the place.
    • 奉法三世，至吉尤精进 - This clause describes the subject's long-standing tradition of practicing the Dharma, with a particular emphasis on his dedication. The phrase '至吉尤精进' can be read as 'especially devoted to Ji,' but it is unclear whether '至吉' refers to Ji himself or the tradition up to Ji.
    • 恒斋戒诵首楞严经 - This clause describes Ji's daily practice, but the exact nature of the practice (e.g., whether he recites the sutra continuously or intermittently) is not specified.
    • 屯中有病，辄请吉读经 - This clause describes the villagers' habit of inviting Ji to read the sutras when someone is sick, but the exact timing and frequency of these invitations are not specified.
    • 所救多愈 - This clause states that most of those Ji helped recovered, but it is unclear whether this refers to physical recovery or spiritual liberation.
    • 亦奉法士也 - This clause introduces He as another practitioner of the Dharma, but the exact nature of his practice is not specified.
    • 咸和中，卒得山毒之病，守困 - This clause describes He's sudden contraction of a poisonous mountain illness and his subsequent severe illness and bedridden state.

    During the Jin Dynasty, there was a man named Dong Ji from Yuchen. He had been practicing the Dharma for three generations, and by the time of Ji, his devotion was especially deep. He constantly observed斋戒 and recited the Shou Leng Yan Jing (Surangama Sutra). When someone in the village was sick, they would always invite Ji to read the sutras, and most of those he helped recovered. There was also a man named He Huang from the same county who was also a practitioner of the Dharma. During the Xianhe period, He suddenly contracted a poisonous mountain illness and became severely ill and bedridden. His brother, in great distress, rushed to ask Ji for help. The distance between Dong and He's homes was about sixty to seventy li, separated by a large river. In the middle of May, heavy rain fell. When He's brother first crossed the river, the water had not yet risen. Ji agreed to meet after midday meal. However, as they were about to leave, the mountain torrent suddenly rose, and it was no longer possible to cross. Ji could not swim, so he hesitated and sighed, sitting by the riverbank for a long time, wanting to go down but daring not to cross. Ji, being honest and straightforward, determined to keep his appointment. Feeling compassion, he made a vow to himself, saying, "I will save people in their time of urgent suffering, regardless of my own life. I hope the Buddha or Bodhisattva will grant me this wish and illuminate my sincerity." He then took off his clothes, placed the bag containing the sutra on his head, and directly entered the water, measuring the depth, which should have reached up to his neck. When Ji crossed, it only reached his knees. After successfully crossing the river, he lost the bag containing the sutra and was very regretful. He went to He's house, performed three prostrations and repentance, shed tears, and blamed himself. In a moment, he suddenly saw the bag containing the sutra on the high seat. Ji took it with a mix of sadness and joy, noting that it seemed damp and discolored. When he opened the bag and saw the sutra, it was still dry as before. [39]

    于是屯人，一时奉法。吉所居西北，有一山，高峻，中多妖魅，犯害居民。吉以经戒之力，欲伐降之。于山际四五亩地，手伐林木，构造小屋，安设高座，转首楞严经。百余日中，寂然无闻。民害稍止。后有数人至吉所，语言良久。吉思惟此客言者，非于潜人；穹山幽绝，何因而来；疑是鬼神；乃谓之曰：“诸君得无是此中鬼耶？”答曰：“是也。闻君德行清肃，故来相观。并请一事，想必见听。吾世有此山，游居所托。君既来止，虑相逆冒，恒怀不安。今欲更作界分，当杀树为断。”吉曰：“仆贪此静寂。读诵经典，不相干犯。方为卿比，广记引作方喜为此愿见祐助。”鬼答：“亦复凭君，不见侵克也。”言毕而去。经一宿，前芟地，四际之外，树皆枯死，如火烧状。吉年八十七亡。珠林十八广记一百十二 [40]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [40]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 于是屯人，一时奉法 - At that time, the people gathered and obeyed the law.
    • 吉所居西北，有一山，高峻，中多妖魅 - Ji lived in the northwest, where there was a high and steep mountain with many demons and spirits.
    • 吉以经戒之力，欲伐降之 - Ji used the power of the scriptures and precepts to subdue them.
    • 于山际四五亩地，手伐林木，构造小屋，安设高座，转首楞严经 - He cleared a small area of land, built a small house, set up a high seat, and recited the *Shurangama Sutra*.
    • 百余日中，寂然无闻 - For over a hundred days, there was no sound or disturbance.
    • 民害稍止 - The harm to the people gradually ceased.
    • 后有数人至吉所，语言良久 - Later, several people came to Ji's place and spoke with him for a long time.
    • 吉思惟此客言者，非于潜人 - Ji thought that the guests were not from the local area.
    • 穹山幽绝，何因而来 - How did they come to this remote and secluded mountain?
    • 疑是鬼神 - He suspected they were ghosts or spirits.
    • 乃谓之曰：“诸君得无是此中鬼耶？” - He then said to them, 'Are you perhaps ghosts from this mountain?'
    • 答曰：“是也。” - They replied, 'Yes, we are.'
    • 闻君德行清肃，故来相观 - We have heard of your virtuous and pure conduct, so we came to observe you.
    • 并请一事，想必见听 - We also request one thing, which we hope you will grant.
    • 吾世有此山，游居所托 - We have lived in this mountain for generations, using it as our home.
    • 君既来止，虑相逆冒，恒怀不安 - Since you have come to live here, we fear we might offend you and are always uneasy.
    • 今欲更作界分，当杀树为断 - Now we want to make a boundary, and we will cut down trees to mark it.
    • 吉曰：“仆贪此静寂，读诵经典，不相干犯。” - Ji said, 'I love this quiet place and read scriptures here; I do not interfere with anyone.'
    • 方为卿比，广记引作方喜为此愿见祐助 - I am glad to help you and wish to see your wishes fulfilled.
    • 鬼答：“亦复凭君，不见侵克也。” - The ghosts replied, 'We also rely on you and will not invade or harm you.'
    • 言毕而去 - They finished speaking and left.
    • 经一宿，前芟地，四际之外，树皆枯死，如火烧状 - After one night, the trees outside the cleared area withered and died, as if burned.
    • 吉年八十七亡 - Ji died at the age of 87.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 吉 - Refers to a person named Ji, likely a Buddhist monk or a virtuous individual.
    • 于潜 - Refers to a place in ancient China, possibly a region known for its seclusion and tranquility.
    • 首楞严经 - Refers to the *Shurangama Sutra*, a significant Buddhist scripture known for its teachings on meditation and spiritual development.
    • 珠林 - Refers to the *Zhu Lin* (Jewel Forest), a collection of Buddhist stories and anecdotes.
    • 广记 - Refers to the *Guang Ji* (Comprehensive Records), another collection of stories and anecdotes.

    At that time, the people gathered and obeyed the law. Ji lived in the northwest, where there was a high and steep mountain with many demons and spirits that harmed the residents. Ji used the power of the scriptures and precepts to subdue them. He cleared a small area of land, built a small house, set up a high seat, and recited the Shurangama Sutra. For over a hundred days, there was no sound or disturbance, and the harm to the people gradually ceased. Later, several people came to Ji's place and spoke with him for a long time. Ji thought that the guests were not from the local area, wondering how they came to this remote and secluded mountain. He suspected they were ghosts or spirits and said to them, "Are you perhaps ghosts from this mountain?" They replied, "Yes, we are. We have heard of your virtuous and pure conduct, so we came to observe you. We also request one thing, which we hope you will grant. We have lived in this mountain for generations, using it as our home. Since you have come to live here, we fear we might offend you and are always uneasy. Now we want to make a boundary, and we will cut down trees to mark it." Ji said, "I love this quiet place and read scriptures here; I do not interfere with anyone. I am glad to help you and wish to see your wishes fulfilled." The ghosts replied, "We also rely on you and will not invade or harm you." They finished speaking and left. After one night, the trees outside the cleared area withered and died, as if burned. Ji died at the age of 87. Zhu Lin volume 18, Guang Ji volume 112. [40]

    晋周珰者，会稽剡人也。家世奉法。珰年十六，便菜食持斋，讽诵成具。及顷转经。正月长斋，竟延僧设，受八关斋。至乡市寺，请其师竺僧密及支法阶竺佛密，令持小品，斋日转读。至日，三僧赴斋，忘持小品。至中食毕。欲读经，方忆。意甚惆怅。珰家在阪怡屯，去寺三十里，无人遣取。至人定烧香讫，举家恨不得经。密益踧踖。有顷，闻有叩门者，言送小品。珰愕然心喜。开门，见一年少，着单衣?，先所不识，又非人行。时疑其神异，便长跪受经，要使前坐。年少不进，期夜当来听经。比道人出，忽不复见。香气遍一宅中。既而视之，乃密经也。道俗惊喜。密经先在厨中，缄钥甚谨。还视其钥，俨然如故。于是屯中十余家，咸皆奉佛。益敬爱珰。珰遂出家，字昙嶷。讽诵众经，至二十万言。珠林十八广记一百十 [41]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [41]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 会稽剡人也 - A phrase indicating the place of origin, meaning 'a native of Yandian, Kuaiji.'
    • 家世奉法 - Means 'the family has always adhered to the law' or 'the family has always followed the precepts.'
    • 菜食持斋 - Refers to a vegetarian diet and fasting, indicating a strict ascetic lifestyle.
    • 讽诵成具 - Means 'reciting scriptures thoroughly' or 'memorizing scriptures completely.'
    • 转经 - Refers to reciting or chanting scriptures.
    • 八关斋 - A type of Buddhist observance involving eight precepts.
    • 请其师竺僧密及支法阶竺佛密 - Means 'inviting his teachers Zhi Sengmi, Zhi Fahai, and Zhi Fomi.'
    • 持小品 - Means 'holding or reciting the *Small Sutra*.'
    • 人定烧香讫 - Means 'after the evening incense burning.'
    • 年少，着单衣? - Refers to a young man dressed in a simple robe.
    • 先所不识 - Means 'someone previously unknown.'
    • 神异 - Refers to supernatural or divine phenomena.
    • 珠林十八广记一百十 - Refers to the chapter or section in the *Zhu Lin* (Jewel Forest) and *Guang Ji* (Expanded Records).

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋周珰者 - Refers to Zhou Dang of the Jin dynasty, a historical figure.
    • 会稽剡 - Indicates that Zhou Dang is from Yandian, a place in Kuaiji, which is now part of modern-day Zhejiang Province.
    • 家世奉法 - Suggests a family tradition of religious or moral adherence, likely Buddhist.
    • 讽诵成具 - Indicates a high level of religious practice and knowledge.
    • 转经 - Refers to a common Buddhist practice of reciting scriptures.
    • 八关斋 - A specific Buddhist observance that involves strict precepts for a day.
    • 请其师竺僧密及支法阶竺佛密 - Refers to specific Buddhist teachers, indicating the religious community and hierarchy.
    • 小品 - Refers to a specific Buddhist text, likely the *Small Sutra*.
    • 人定烧香讫 - Refers to the evening ritual of burning incense, a common practice in Buddhist monasteries.
    • 珠林十八 - Refers to Chapter 18 of the *Zhu Lin*.
    • 广记一百十 - Refers to Chapter 110 of the *Guang Ji*.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋周珰 - Zhou Dang of the Jin dynasty.
    • 会稽剡 - Yandian, Kuaiji (modern-day Zhejiang Province).
    • 竺僧密 - Zhi Sengmi, a Buddhist teacher.
    • 支法阶 - Zhi Fahai, a Buddhist teacher.
    • 竺佛密 - Zhi Fomi, a Buddhist teacher.
    • 小品 - The *Small Sutra*.
    • 珠林十八 - Chapter 18 of the *Zhu Lin*.
    • 广记一百十 - Chapter 110 of the *Guang Ji*.

    Zhou Dang of the Jin dynasty was a native of Yandian, Kuaiji. His family had always adhered to the precepts. At the age of sixteen, he began to follow a vegetarian diet and fast, reciting scriptures thoroughly. As time passed, he also started to recite scriptures. During the first month, he observed a long fast and invited monks to set up a ceremony for the Eight Precepts. He invited his teachers Zhi Sengmi, Zhi Fahai, and Zhi Fomi to come and recite the Small Sutra on the day of the fast. On the day of the fast, the three monks arrived for the ceremony, but forgot to bring the Small Sutra. After the midday meal, when they were about to read the scriptures, they suddenly remembered. They felt very distressed. Zhou Dang's home was in Ban Yi Tun, thirty li away from the temple, and no one was sent to fetch the Small Sutra. By the time of evening incense burning, the whole family regretted not having the scriptures. The monks felt even more uneasy. After a while, they heard someone knocking on the door, saying they had brought the Small Sutra. Zhou Dang was surprised and delighted. He opened the door and saw a young man dressed in a simple robe, someone he had never seen before, and not someone who usually traveled. He suspected it might be a divine or supernatural being and knelt down to receive the scriptures, inviting him to sit. The young man did not move forward, promising to come back at night to listen to the scriptures. By the time the monks left, the young man had disappeared. The fragrance filled the entire house. When they looked at the scriptures, it was indeed the Small Sutra. The monks and laypeople were amazed. The Small Sutra had been kept in the kitchen, sealed and locked very carefully. When they checked the lock, it was still intact. Thus, more than ten families in the village began to follow Buddhism and respected and loved Zhou Dang. Zhou Dang eventually left home to become a monk, taking the name Tan Yi. He recited and memorized many scriptures, totaling over 200,000 words. This story is recorded in Chapter 18 of the Zhu Lin and Chapter 110 of the Guang Ji. [41]

    晋孙稚，字法晖，齐国般阳县人也。父祚，晋太中大夫。稚幼而奉法。年十八，以咸康元年八月病亡。祚后移居武昌。至三年四月八日，沙门于法阶行尊像，经家门。夫妻大小出观，见稚亦在人众之中，随侍像行。见父母，拜跪问讯。随共还家。祚先病，稚云：“无他祸祟，不自将护所致耳。五月当差。”言毕辞去。其年七月十五日，复归，跪拜问讯，悉如生时。说其外祖父为太山府君，见稚，说稚母字曰，“汝是某甲儿耶。”“未应便来，那得至此。”稚答：“伯父将来。欲以代谴。”有教推问。欲鞭罚之；稚救解得原。稚兄容，字思渊，时在其侧。稚谓曰：“虽离故形在优乐处，但读书无他作，愿兄勿复忧也。 [42]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [42]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 幼而奉法 - Literally 'obedient to the law from a young age,' indicating early piety or obedience.
    • 无他祸祟 - Indicates no other calamities or supernatural troubles.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋 - Refers to the Jin Dynasty, a historical period in China.
    • 齐国般阳县 - A specific location within the Jin Dynasty.
    • 武昌 - Another specific location, likely referring to the city of Wuchang.
    • 沙门 - Refers to Buddhist monks.
    • 太山府君 - Refers to the Lord of Tai Shan, a deity in Chinese mythology.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋孙稚 - The main subject of the passage.
    • 法晖 - The courtesy name of the main subject.
    • 齐国般阳县 - The place of origin of the main subject.
    • 祚 - The father of the main subject.
    • 武昌 - The city where the father moved.
    • 咸康元年 - A specific year during the Jin Dynasty.
    • 太山府君 - A deity in Chinese mythology.
    • 容 - The brother of the main subject.
    • 思渊 - The courtesy name of the brother.

    Translation:

    Jin Sun Zhi, courtesy name Fa Hui, was from Bangyang County in Qi State. His father, Zuò, held the position of Taizhong Dafu in the Jin court. Sun Zhi was obedient to the law from a young age. At eighteen, he died in August of the first year of Xiankang (335 AD). After his death, Zuò moved to Wuchang. In the third year, on the eighth day of the fourth month, a Buddhist monk performed a ritual involving a revered statue and passed by their home. Sun Zhi appeared among the crowd, following the statue. He saw his parents, bowed and greeted them. They returned home together. Zuò had fallen ill, and Sun Zhi said, "There are no other calamities; it's just that I did not take proper care of myself. I will be better by May." After speaking, he left. On the fifteenth day of the seventh month of that year, he returned, bowed and greeted, behaving as he did when alive. He said that his maternal grandfather was the Lord of Tai Shan, who saw Sun Zhi and asked, "Are you the son of someone named so-and-so?" "You shouldn't be here yet; how did you get here?" Sun Zhi replied, "My uncle will come; he wants to take my place." There was an order to investigate and punish him, but Sun Zhi was saved from punishment. His brother, Róng, courtesy name Siyuan, was present at the time. Sun Zhi said to him, "Although I have left my old form and am in a place of joy, I only read and do nothing else. I hope you will not worry further."

    [42]

    但勤精进，系念修善，福自随人矣。我二年学成，当生国王家。同辈有五百人，今在福堂，学成皆当上生第六天上。我本亦应上生，但以解救先人，因缘缠缚，故独生王家耳！”到五年七月七日，复归。说邾城当有寇难。事例甚多，悉皆如言。家人秘之，故无传者。又云：“先人多有罪?，宜为作福。我今受身人中，不须复营，但救先人也。愿父兄勤为功德。作福食时，务使鲜洁。一一如法者，受上福；次者，次福；若不能然，然后费设耳。当使平等，心无彼我，其福乃多。”祚时有婢，稚未还时，忽病殆死，周身皆痛。稚云：“此婢欲叛我，前与鞭，不复得去耳。推问婢，”云：“前实欲叛，与人为期日垂至而便住，”云云。珠林九十一 [43]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [43]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 但勤精進，系念修善 - Diligently progresses and keeps the mind focused on cultivating virtue.
    • 福自随人矣 - Good fortune will naturally accompany the person.
    • 我二年学成，当生国王家 - Will complete studies in two years and be born into the king's family.
    • 同辈有五百人，今在福堂，学成皆当上生第六天上 - Peers, numbering five hundred, are now in the Hall of Bliss, and upon completing their studies, they will all ascend to the Sixth Heaven.
    • 我本亦应上生，但以解救先人，因缘缠缚，故独生王家耳 - Should have also ascended, but because I was bound by karmic ties to save my ancestors, I was reborn alone in the king's family.

    But if one diligently progresses and keeps the mind focused on cultivating virtue, good fortune will naturally follow the person. I will complete my studies in two years and be born into the king's family. My peers, numbering five hundred, are now in the Hall of Bliss, and upon completing their studies, they will all ascend to the Sixth Heaven. I should have also ascended, but because I was bound by karmic ties to save my ancestors, I was reborn alone in the king's family! On the fifth year, the seventh day of the seventh month, I returned. I said that the city of Zhuzhou would face a threat of invasion. There were many examples of this, and all came true. The family kept it secret, so it was not widely known. It was also said: "My ancestors have many sins; it is appropriate to make merit for them. I am now born into the human world, and there is no need to make further efforts, but to save my ancestors. I hope my father and elder brothers diligently make merit and virtue. When making offerings, ensure that the food is fresh and clean. Those who strictly follow the rules will receive the highest blessings; those who do so moderately will receive lesser blessings; if they cannot do so, then it is acceptable to make offerings as they can. It should be done with equality, without distinguishing between self and others, and the blessings will be abundant." When Zuo had a maid, who was ill and nearly died before returning, her whole body was in pain. Zuo said: "This maid wanted to rebel against me, and before she could, I lashed her, and she could not leave. When I questioned the maid, she said: 'Before, I really wanted to rebel, and I had made an agreement with someone, but I stopped just as the day was about to arrive.' So on and so forth. Zhu Lin 91 [43]

    晋李恒，字元文，谯国人。少时，有一沙门造恒，谓曰：“君福报将至，而复对来随之。君能守贫修道，不仕宦者，福增对灭。君其勉之！”恒性躁，又寒门，但问仕当何所至，了不寻究修道意也。与一卷经，恒不肯取。又固问，荣途贵贱何如？沙门曰：“当带金紫，极于三郡。若能于一郡止者，亦为善也。”恒曰：“且当富贵，何顾后患？”因留宿。恒夜起，见沙门身满一床，入呼家人，大小窥视，复变为大鸟，跱屋梁上。天晓，复形而去。恒送出门，忽不复见。知是神人。因此事佛，而亦不能精至。后为西阳、江夏、庐江太守，加龙骧将军。大兴中，预钱凤之乱，被诛。珠林五十六 [44]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [44]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 福報將至 - Means 'good fortune is about to come,' referring to future blessings.
    • 對來 - Refers to negative consequences or counterbalancing forces.
    • 守貧修道 - Means 'to live in poverty and cultivate the Dao,' emphasizing asceticism and spiritual practice.
    • 仕宦 - Refers to holding an official position or engaging in government service.
    • 帶金紫 - A metaphor for high-ranking officials, as gold and purple were colors associated with nobility.
    • 一郡止者 - Means 'to stop at one prefecture,' suggesting a limit on ambition.
    • 且當富贵 - Means 'let's pursue wealth and status first,' reflecting a pragmatic approach.
    • 何顾后患 - Means 'why worry about future troubles,' showing a focus on immediate gains.
    • 忽不复见 - Means 'suddenly disappeared,' indicating an abrupt end to the encounter.
    • 神人 - Refers to a divine or supernatural being.
    • 因此事佛 - Means 'thereafter devoted to Buddhism,' showing a shift in religious practice.
    • 不能精至 - Means 'did not achieve great depth or purity,' suggesting a lack of commitment.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋李恒 - Refers to Li Heng, a historical figure from the Jin dynasty.
    • 沙门 - Refers to a Buddhist monk or ascetic.
    • 龙骧将军 - A military rank in the Jin dynasty, indicating a high position.
    • 大兴中 - Refers to the Daxing era, a period during the Jin dynasty.
    • 钱凤之乱 - Refers to a rebellion led by Qian Feng, a significant historical event in the Jin dynasty.
    • 珠林五十六 - Refers to the fifty-sixth chapter of the *Zhu Jin* (Treasury of Jewels), a Buddhist text that records various stories and teachings.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋李恒 - Li Heng, a historical figure from the Jin dynasty.
    • 沙门 - A Buddhist monk or ascetic.
    • 龙骧将军 - A military rank in the Jin dynasty.
    • 大兴中 - The Daxing era, a period during the Jin dynasty.
    • 钱凤 - Qian Feng, a historical figure who led a rebellion.
    • 珠林五十六 - The fifty-sixth chapter of the *Zhu Jin* (Treasury of Jewels).

    Li Heng of the Jin dynasty, courtesy name Yuanwen, was from Qiao. In his youth, a Buddhist monk visited him and said, "Your good fortune is about to come, but negative consequences will follow. If you can live in poverty and cultivate the Dao, and avoid holding an official position, your good fortune will increase and negative consequences will diminish. Strive for this!" Li Heng was impetuous and came from a poor family. He only asked about his prospects in government service and did not delve into the meaning of cultivating the Dao. The monk gave him a scroll of scripture, but Li Heng refused to take it. The monk persisted and asked, "What will be your status in the ranks of nobility?" The monk replied, "You will hold high office, reaching the level of a governor in three prefectures. If you can limit yourself to one prefecture, that would be good." Li Heng said, "Let's pursue wealth and status first; why worry about future troubles?" He then stayed overnight. That night, Li Heng woke up and saw the monk's body filling the bed. He called his family, and they all peeked in, only to see the monk transform into a large bird perched on the roof beam. At dawn, the monk returned to his original form and left. Li Heng saw him off at the door, and suddenly, the monk disappeared. Li Heng realized he was a divine being. Thereafter, he devoted himself to Buddhism, but did not achieve great depth or purity. Later, he became the governor of Xiyang, Jiangxia, and Lujiang, and was promoted to the rank of Longxiang General. During the Daxing era, he participated in the rebellion led by Qian Feng and was executed. (Chapter 56 of the Zhu Jin) [44]

    晋窦传者，河内人也。永和中，并州刺史高昌，冀州刺史吕护，各权部曲，相与不和。传为昌所用，作官长。护遣骑抄击，为所俘执。同伴六七人，共系入一狱。锁械甚严。克日当煞之。沙门支道山，时在护营中。先与传相识。闻其执厄，出至狱所候视之。隔户共语。传谓山曰：“今日困厄，命在漏刻，何方相救？”山曰：“若能至心归请，必有感应。”传先亦颇闻观世音，及得山语，○专心属念。昼夜三日，至诚自归。观其锁械如觉缓，解有异于常。 [45]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [45]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 永和中 - Refers to the Yonghe period (345-356 AD), a specific time frame in the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 各權部曲 - Means 'each controlling their own troops,' indicating a state of military autonomy.
    • 相與不和 - Means 'they were not in harmony with each other,' describing a state of conflict or disagreement.
    • 作官长 - Means 'to be appointed as an official,' referring to the position held by the person.
    • 克日当煞之 - Means 'to be executed on a fixed day,' indicating a scheduled execution.
    • 至心归请 - Means 'to sincerely pray for help,' emphasizing the sincerity of the request.
    • 专心属念 - Means 'to focus one's mind on something,' indicating intense concentration.
    • 昼夜三日 - Means 'for three days and nights,' describing a continuous period of time.
    • 至诚自归 - Means 'to sincerely return to oneself,' indicating a deep sense of repentance or devotion.
    • 锁械如觉缓 - Means 'the chains felt as if they were loosening,' describing a change in the physical condition of the prisoner.
    • 解有异于常 - Means 'there was something unusual about the release,' indicating an unexpected or extraordinary event.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋窦传 - Refers to a person named Duan Chuan from the Jin dynasty.
    • 河内人 - Indicates that Duan Chuan was from the region of Hebei.
    • 并州刺史高昌 - Refers to Gao Chang, the governor of Bingzhou.
    • 冀州刺史吕护 - Refers to Lü Hu, the governor of Jizhou.
    • 支道山 - Refers to a Buddhist monk named Zhi Daoshan, who was in Lü Hu's camp.
    • 观世音 - Refers to Guanyin, the bodhisattva of compassion in Mahayana Buddhism.
    • 锁械 - Refers to chains and shackles, symbols of imprisonment and punishment.
    • 感应 - Refers to a response or reaction from a higher power, often in the context of religious belief.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋窦传 - Duan Chuan, a historical figure from the Jin dynasty.
    • 河内 - A region in present-day Henan province.
    • 永和 - The Yonghe period (345-356 AD) of the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 并州 - A province in northern China.
    • 高昌 - Gao Chang, a historical figure.
    • 冀州 - A province in northern China.
    • 吕护 - Lü Hu, a historical figure.
    • 支道山 - Zhi Daoshan, a Buddhist monk.
    • 观世音 - Guanyin, a bodhisattva in Mahayana Buddhism.

    Duan Chuan, a man from Hebei, was during the Yonghe period. Gao Chang, the governor of Bingzhou, and Lü Hu, the governor of Jizhou, each controlled their own troops and were not in harmony with each other. Duan Chuan was appointed as an official by Gao Chang. Lü Hu sent riders to attack, and Duan Chuan was captured. He was imprisoned with six or seven companions in a single jail. The chains and shackles were very tight, and he was scheduled to be executed on a fixed day. The Buddhist monk Zhi Daoshan, who was at that time in Lü Hu's camp, had previously known Duan Chuan. Hearing of his predicament, he went to the prison to visit him. They spoke through the door. Duan Chuan told Zhi Daoshan, "Today I am in dire straits, my life is in the balance, what can you do to save me?" Zhi Daoshan said, "If you can sincerely pray for help, there will surely be a response." Duan Chuan had previously heard of Guanyin and, upon hearing Zhi Daoshan's words, focused his mind on this. For three days and nights, he sincerely returned to himself. It seemed as though his chains felt looser, and there was something unusual about the release. [45]

    聊试推荡，忽然离体。传乃复至心曰：“今蒙哀祐，已令桎梏自解。而同伴尚多，无心独去。观世音神力普济，当令俱免。”言毕，复牵挽余人，皆以次解落，若有割剔之者。遂开门走出，于警徼之闲，莫有觉者。便踰城迳去。时夜向晓，行四五里。天明不复进。共逃隐一榛中。须臾，觉失囚，人马络绎，四出寻捕。焚草践林，无不至遍。唯传所隐一亩许地，终无至者。遂得免还。乡里敬信异常，咸皆奉法。道山后过江，为谢居士敷具说其事。珠林十七
    　　晋大司马桓温，末年颇奉佛法，饭馔僧尼。有一比丘尼，失其名，来自远方，投温为檀越尼。才行不恒，温甚敬待，居之门内。尼每浴，必至移时。温疑而窥之：见尼裸身挥刀，破腹出脏，断截身首，支分脔切。温怪骇而还。及至尼出浴室，身形如常。温以实问。尼答云：“若遂淩君上，刑当如之。”时温方谋问鼎，闻之怅然。故以戒惧，终守臣节。尼后解去，不知所在。珠林三十三 [46]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [46]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 聊试推荡 - Phrase meaning 'to tentatively push or test,' used here to describe a temporary or experimental action.
    • 忽然离体 - Literally 'suddenly separated from the body,' likely a metaphor for a sudden release or liberation.
    • 传乃复至心曰 - 'The transmission then returned to the heart and said,' a formal way of introducing a voice or message.
    • 今蒙哀祐 - 'Now we are favored by mercy and protection,' a humble expression of gratitude or acknowledgment of divine aid.
    • 桎梏自解 - 'The shackles have naturally loosened,' a metaphor for freedom from constraints.
    • 同伴尚多，无心独去 - 'There are still many companions, and there is no desire to leave alone,' expressing a sense of solidarity or reluctance to depart.
    • 观世音神力普济 - 'The power of Guanyin to universally save all beings,' referring to the Bodhisattva Guanyin's compassionate intervention.
    • 当令俱免 - 'Will surely make all of us escape,' a statement of assurance or hope.
    • 复牵挽余人 - 'Again pulling and leading others,' describing a repeated or continuous action.
    • 皆以次解落 - 'All were gradually released,' indicating a process of liberation.
    • 若有割剔之者 - 'As if someone were cutting or removing them,' a vivid metaphor for the process of release.
    • 遂开门走出 - 'Then opened the door and walked out,' describing a decisive action.
    • 于警徼之间 - 'In the midst of the watchmen,' referring to a guarded or monitored area.
    • 莫有觉者 - 'No one noticed,' indicating a lack of observation or awareness.
    • 便踰城迳去 - 'Then leaped over the wall and went straight away,' describing a swift and direct escape.
    • 时夜向晓 - 'It was late at night, nearing dawn,' indicating the time of day.
    • 行四五里 - 'Walked four or five miles,' describing the distance traveled.
    • 天明不复进 - 'At dawn, they stopped moving forward,' indicating a halt in progress.
    • 共逃隐一榛中 - 'Together, they fled and hid in a thicket,' describing a shared act of concealment.
    • 须臾，觉失囚 - 'Soon, they realized the prisoners were missing,' indicating a sudden awareness.
    • 人马络绎 - 'People and horses came in a continuous stream,' describing a busy or chaotic scene.
    • 四出寻捕 - 'Searching and capturing everywhere,' indicating a widespread effort.
    • 焚草践林 - 'Burning grass and trampling through forests,' describing the thoroughness of the search.
    • 无不至遍 - 'No place was left unsearched,' emphasizing the completeness of the effort.
    • 唯传所隐一亩许地 - 'Only the place where they were hidden, about an acre in size,' indicating the specific location.
    • 终无至者 - 'No one came to that place,' indicating the success of the concealment.
    • 遂得免还 - 'Thus they were able to escape and return,' describing the outcome.
    • 乡里敬信异常 - 'The village respected and believed in them exceptionally,' indicating a high level of trust or reverence.
    • 咸皆奉法 - 'All followed the law,' indicating a general adherence to rules or norms.
    • 道山后过江 - 'After crossing the river, they went to the mountain,' describing a geographical movement.
    • 为谢居士敷具说其事 - 'For the layman named Xie, they prepared and recounted the story,' indicating a detailed explanation.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 桓温 - A prominent military general and statesman of the Eastern Jin dynasty, known for his political ambitions and military campaigns.
    • 谢居士 - A layman named Xie, likely a respected figure in the community.
    • 观世音 - The Bodhisattva Guanyin, a central figure in Mahayana Buddhism known for compassion and mercy.
    • 桎梏 - Shackles or restraints, often used metaphorically to represent constraints or limitations.
    • 乡里 - The local community or village, indicating a grassroots level of society.
    • 奉法 - Following the law or adhering to rules, indicating a sense of order or discipline.

    Proper Nouns

    • 桓温 - A historical figure, a military general and statesman of the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 谢居士 - A layman named Xie, likely a respected figure in the community.
    • 观世音 - The Bodhisattva Guanyin, a central figure in Mahayana Buddhism.
    • 珠林十七 - 'Jewel Forest Seventeen,' likely a reference to a specific text or collection.

    A tentative push was made, and suddenly the body was separated. The transmission then returned to the heart and said, "Now we are favored by mercy and protection, and the shackles have naturally loosened. There are still many companions, and there is no desire to leave alone. The power of Guanyin to universally save all beings will surely make all of us escape." After speaking, they again pulled and led others, all gradually being released, as if someone were cutting or removing them. They then opened the door and walked out, in the midst of the watchmen, and no one noticed. They leaped over the wall and went straight away. It was late at night, nearing dawn, and they walked four or five miles. At dawn, they stopped moving forward. Together, they fled and hid in a thicket. Soon, they realized the prisoners were missing, and people and horses came in a continuous stream, searching and capturing everywhere, burning grass and trampling through forests, no place was left unsearched. Only the place where they were hidden, about an acre in size, was not reached. Thus they were able to escape and return. The village respected and believed in them exceptionally, all followed the law. After crossing the river, they went to the mountain, and for the layman named Xie, they prepared and recounted the story. Jewel Forest Seventeen

    In the late years, the Grand Marshal of Jin, Huán Wēn, was quite devoted to Buddhism, providing food and drink to monks and nuns. There was a nun, whose name is unknown, who came from afar and sought Huán Wēn as a benefactor. Her behavior was not constant, but Huán Wēn held her in high regard and allowed her to reside within his household. The nun would bathe, and it would take a long time. Huán Wēn, suspicious, peered at her: he saw the nun naked, wielding a knife, cutting open her abdomen, removing her organs, severing her head and body, and slicing them into pieces. Huán Wēn was startled and returned. When the nun emerged from the bath, her body was as usual. Huán Wēn asked her about it. The nun replied, "If I were to defy the ruler, the punishment would be the same." At the time, Huán Wēn was plotting to usurp the throne, and upon hearing this, he was filled with regret. Thus, he was warned and ultimately maintained his loyalty to his duties. The nun later left, and her whereabouts are unknown. Jewel Forest Thirty-Three [46]

    宋案当作晋广记引无李清者，吴兴于潜人也。仕桓温大司马府参军督护。于府得病，还家而死。经久稣活。说云：初见传教，持信旛唤之，云公欲相见。清谓是温召，即起束带而去。出门，见一竹舆，便令入中。二人推之，疾速如驰。至一朱门，见阮敬；时敬死已三十年矣。敬问清曰：“卿何时来？知我家何似？”清云：“卿家异恶。”敬便雨泪，言，“知吾子孙如何？”答云：“具可。”敬云：二字广记引有“我今令卿得脱。汝能料理吾家否？”清云：“若能如此，不负大恩。”敬言，“僧达道人，是官师，甚被礼，敬当苦告之。”还内良久，遣人出云：“门前四层寺，官所起也。僧达常以平旦入寺礼拜，宜就求哀。”清往其寺，见一沙门，语曰：“汝是我前七生时弟子。已经七世受福，迷着世乐，忘失本业。背正就邪，当受大罪，今可改悔。 [47]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [47]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 宋案当作晋广记引无李清者 - Phrase indicating that the 'Song case' is recorded in the 'Jin Guang Ji' but does not mention Li Qing; '当作' suggests correction or clarification.
    • 仕桓温大司马府参军督护 - Formal title indicating Li Qing served as a military officer in the Grand Marshal's office under the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 还家而死 - Literally 'returned home and died,' likely means 'died after returning home.'
    • 经久稣活 - Rare phrase meaning 'revived after a long time,' possibly referring to a near-death experience or resurrection.
    • 初见传教，持信旛唤之 - Holding a flag of faith and calling to him, indicating a spiritual or religious encounter.
    • 卿何时来？知我家何似？ - Formal and archaic way of asking 'When did you come? How is my family?'
    • 卿家异恶 - Vague response, possibly meaning 'all is well' or 'it is as it should be.'
    • 我今令卿得脱 - I will now allow you to escape, suggesting a spiritual or metaphorical liberation.
    • 汝能料理吾家否？ - Can you manage my family? likely referring to handling his affairs or legacy.
    • 不负大恩 - I will not betray your great kindness, indicating a promise of gratitude.
    • 僧达道人，是官师，甚被礼 - Monk Da is an official teacher, highly respected, indicating a person of authority and respect.
    • 四层寺，官所起也 - The four-story temple, built by the government, indicating a significant and official religious institution.
    • 平旦入寺礼拜 - Enter the temple to worship at dawn, indicating a daily religious practice.
    • 汝是我前七生时弟子 - You were my disciple in my previous seven lives, indicating a spiritual connection across multiple lifetimes.
    • 已经七世受福，迷着世乐，忘失本业 - You have already received blessings in seven lives, but you are attached to worldly pleasures and have forgotten your true purpose.
    • 背正就邪，当受大罪 - Turning away from righteousness and towards evil, you will suffer great sin, indicating a moral warning.
    • 今可改悔 - Now you can repent, indicating a call to change one's ways.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋案 - A historical event or case during the Song dynasty.
    • 晋广记 - A historical or literary record from the Jin dynasty, possibly a collection of anecdotes or biographies.
    • 桓温 - A prominent military general and politician during the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 大司马府 - The office of the Grand Marshal, a high-ranking military position.
    • 参军督护 - A military officer responsible for overseeing troops and logistics.
    • 李清 - A historical figure, possibly a minor official or scholar, whose story is being recounted.
    • 阮敬 - A historical figure who died 30 years before Li Qing's encounter, likely a respected or influential person.
    • 僧达道人 - A monk or religious figure who is highly respected and serves as an official teacher.
    • 四层寺 - A four-story temple, likely a significant religious institution built by the government.
    • 七世 - Seven lifetimes, indicating a spiritual or Buddhist concept of reincarnation.

    Proper Nouns

    • 宋案 - A historical event or case during the Song dynasty.
    • 晋广记 - A historical or literary record from the Jin dynasty.
    • 桓温 - A prominent military general and politician during the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 大司马府 - The office of the Grand Marshal.
    • 参军督护 - A military officer responsible for overseeing troops and logistics.
    • 李清 - A historical figure, possibly a minor official or scholar.
    • 阮敬 - A historical figure who died 30 years before Li Qing's encounter.
    • 僧达道人 - A monk or religious figure who is highly respected and serves as an official teacher.
    • 四层寺 - A four-story temple, likely a significant religious institution built by the government.
    • 七世 - Seven lifetimes, indicating a spiritual or Buddhist concept of reincarnation.

    The Song case is recorded in the Jin Guang Ji but does not mention Li Qing. Li Qing was from Wuxing, Yuqian. He served as a military officer in the Grand Marshal's office under the Eastern Jin dynasty. While in the office, he fell ill, returned home, and died. After a long time, he was revived. He recounted: "I first encountered a missionary, who held a flag of faith and called to me, saying that the Grand Marshal wished to see me. I thought it was a summons from Grand Marshal Wen, so I immediately put on my belt and went. Outside the door, I saw a bamboo sedan chair and was told to get in. Two people pushed it, and it moved as swiftly as a galloping horse. We arrived at a red gate, where I saw Ruan Jing; at that time, Ruan Jing had been dead for thirty years. Ruan Jing asked Li Qing, 'When did you come? How is my family?' Li Qing replied, 'Your family is strange and unpleasant.' Ruan Jing then wept, saying, 'Do you know how my descendants are?' Li Qing answered, 'All is well.' Ruan Jing said, 'I will now allow you to escape. Can you manage my family?' Li Qing said, 'If you can do this, I will not betray your great kindness.' Ruan Jing said, 'Monk Da is an official teacher, highly respected. I will plead with him on your behalf.' After a long time inside, someone sent out a message saying, 'There is a four-story temple at the gate, built by the government. Monk Da often enters the temple to worship at dawn. You should go there to seek his pity.' Li Qing went to the temple and saw a monk, who said to him, 'You were my disciple in my previous seven lives. You have already received blessings in seven lives, but you are attached to worldly pleasures and have forgotten your true purpose. Turning away from righteousness and towards evil, you will suffer great sin. Now you can repent.' [47]

    和尚明出，当相佐助。”清还先舆中。夜寒噤冻。至晓门开，僧达果出至寺。清便随逐稽颡。僧达云：“汝当革心为善，归命佛法，归命比丘僧：受此三归，可得不横死。受持勤者，亦不，经苦难。”清便奉受。又见昨所遇沙门，长跪请曰：“此人僧乎宿世弟子。案乎字有讹广记引作达亦非忘正失法。方将○苦。先缘所追。今得归命，愿垂慈愍。”答曰：“先是福人，当易拔济耳。”便还向朱门。俄遣人出云：“李参军可去。”敬时亦出，与清一青竹枝，广记引作杖令闭眼骑之。清如其语，忽然至家。家中啼哭，及乡亲塞堂，欲入不得。会买材还，家人及客赴监视之。唯尸在地。清入至尸前，闻其尸臭。自念悔还。但外人逼突，不觉入尸时，于是而活。即营理敬家，分宅以居。于是归心三宝，勤信佛教，遂作佳流弟子。珠林九十五广记三百七十九 [48]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [48]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 和尚明出 - Formal expression meaning 'the enlightened monk came out' or 'the wise monk appeared.'
    • 当相佐助 - Means 'should assist you' or 'will help you.'
    • 夜寒噤冻 - Describes the cold night causing shivering or freezing.
    • 至晓门开 - Means 'until dawn when the gate opened.'
    • 僧达果出至寺 - Means 'the monk Da indeed came to the temple.'
    • 清便随逐稽颡 - Means 'Qing then followed and knelt down.'
    • 汝当革心为善 - Means 'you should change your heart and do good.'
    • 归命佛法 - Means 'take refuge in the Buddha and the Dharma.'
    • 归命比丘僧 - Means 'take refuge in the Sangha (the community of monks).'
    • 受此三归 - Means 'receive these three refuges.'
    • 可得不横死 - Means 'you can avoid a premature death.'
    • 受持勤者 - Means 'those who diligently receive and uphold.'
    • 亦不，经苦难 - Means 'will also not go through great suffering.'
    • 此人僧乎宿世弟子 - Means 'this person is a disciple of the monk from past lives.'
    • 案乎字有讹广记引作达亦非忘正失法 - Ambiguous phrase meaning 'the character '乎' is incorrect, as seen in the *Guangji* (Comprehensive Records), which reads '达' instead, and this is not a mistake but a deviation from the correct practice.'
    • 方将苦 - Means 'is about to suffer.'
    • 先缘所追 - Means 'pursued by past karma.'
    • 今得归命，愿垂慈愍 - Means 'now able to take refuge, I hope for your compassion.'
    • 先是福人，当易拔济耳 - Means 'he was a fortunate person, so it will be easier to save him.'
    • 便还向朱门 - Means 'then returned to the red gate.'
    • 俄遣人出云 - Means 'soon sent someone out saying.'
    • 李参军可去 - Means 'Lieutenant General Li can go.'
    • 敬时亦出 - Means 'Jing also came out.'
    • 与清一青竹枝 - Means 'gave Qing a green bamboo branch.'
    • 令闭眼骑之 - Means 'ordered him to close his eyes and ride it.'
    • 忽然至家 - Means 'suddenly arrived home.'
    • 家中啼哭 - Means 'cries at home.'
    • 及乡亲塞堂 - Means 'and relatives filled the hall.'
    • 欲入不得 - Means 'unable to enter.'
    • 会买材还 - Means 'when the coffin was bought and returned.'
    • 家人及客赴监视之 - Means 'family and guests came to watch over it.'
    • 唯尸在地 - Means 'only the body lay on the ground.'
    • 清入至尸前 - Means 'Qing entered and stood before the body.'
    • 闻其尸臭 - Means 'smelled the stench of the body.'
    • 自念悔还 - Means 'thought and regretted returning.'
    • 但外人逼突 - Means 'but was forced by outsiders.'
    • 不觉入尸时 - Means 'unaware of entering the body.'
    • 于是而活 - Means 'and thus revived.'
    • 即营理敬家 - Means 'then arranged for Jing's family.'
    • 分宅以居 - Means 'divided the house to live in.'
    • 于是归心三宝 - Means 'then devoted himself to the Three Jewels.'
    • 勤信佛教 - Means 'diligently believed in Buddhism.'
    • 遂作佳流弟子 - Means 'became a good disciple.'
    • 珠林九十五广记三百七十九 - Refers to specific entries in the *Zhu Lin* (Jewel Forest) and *Guangji* (Comprehensive Records).

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 和尚 - Refers to a Buddhist monk.
    • 僧达 - The name of a specific monk.
    • 三归 - Refers to taking refuge in the Three Jewels: the Buddha, the Dharma, and the Sangha.
    • 朱门 - Refers to a red gate, often associated with official or noble residences.
    • 参军 - A military rank, equivalent to a lieutenant general.
    • 敬 - The name of a person, likely a family member or close associate.
    • 珠林 - The *Zhu Lin* (Jewel Forest) is a collection of Buddhist stories and teachings.
    • 广记 - The *Guangji* (Comprehensive Records) is a large collection of historical and anecdotal stories.

    Proper Nouns

    • 和尚 - A general term for a Buddhist monk.
    • 僧达 - The name of a specific monk.
    • 清 - The name of the main character.
    • 敬 - The name of a person, likely a family member or close associate.
    • 珠林 - The *Zhu Lin* (Jewel Forest) is a collection of Buddhist stories and teachings.
    • 广记 - The *Guangji* (Comprehensive Records) is a large collection of historical and anecdotal stories.

    The Enlightened Monk Appeared

    The enlightened monk came out and said, "I will assist you." Qing returned to the carriage. It was a cold night, and he shivered from the cold. Until dawn when the gate opened, Monk Da indeed came to the temple. Qing then followed and knelt down. Monk Da said, "You should change your heart and do good, take refuge in the Buddha and the Dharma, and take refuge in the Sangha: by receiving these three refuges, you can avoid a premature death. Those who diligently receive and uphold will also not go through great suffering." Qing then accepted the refuges. He also saw the monk he had met the previous day, who knelt down and asked, "This person is a disciple of the monk from past lives. According to the Guangji (Comprehensive Records), the character '乎' is incorrect, as it reads '达' instead, and this is not a mistake but a deviation from the correct practice. He is about to suffer, pursued by past karma. Now able to take refuge, I hope for your compassion." The monk replied, "He was a fortunate person, so it will be easier to save him." Then he returned to the red gate. Soon, someone was sent out saying, "Lieutenant General Li can go." Jing also came out and gave Qing a green bamboo branch, instructing him to close his eyes and ride it. Qing followed the instructions, and suddenly arrived home. Cries filled the house, and relatives crowded the hall, unable to enter. When the coffin was bought and returned, family and guests came to watch over it. Only the body lay on the ground. Qing entered and stood before the body, smelling the stench of the body. He thought and regretted returning. But forced by outsiders, unaware of entering the body, he revived. He then arranged for Jing's family and divided the house to live in. Thus, he devoted himself to the Three Jewels, diligently believed in Buddhism, and became a good disciple. Zhu Lin 95, Guangji 379. [48]

    晋吕竦，字茂高，兖州人也。寓居始丰。其县南溪，流急岸峭，回曲如萦。又多大石，白日行者，犹怀危惧。竦自说，其父尝行溪中，去家十许里。日向?，天忽风雨，晦冥如漆，不复知东西。自分覆溺。唯归心观世音，且诵且念。须臾，有火光来岸，如人捉炬者，照见溪中了了。遥得归家，火常在前导，去船十余步。竦复与郗嘉宾周旋，郗所传说。珠林六十五 [49]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [49]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 归心观世音 - Focusing one's mind on the Bodhisattva Guanyin, seeking protection and guidance.
    • 且诵且念 - Recite and chant simultaneously, indicating a continuous and devoted act of prayer or meditation.
    • 日向?，天忽风雨，晦冥如漆，不复知东西 - Suddenly, a storm hits, making it impossible to discern directions, with the sky becoming as dark as ink.
    • 须臾，有火光来岸，如人捉炬者，照见溪中了了 - A firelight appears, illuminating the stream, allowing for clear visibility.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋吕竦 - Lu Song, a figure from the Jin Dynasty.
    • 兖州 - An ancient Chinese state, now part of Shandong Province.
    • 始丰 - An ancient county, possibly referring to a specific location in the region.
