    • 观世音 - The Bodhisattva Guanyin, a central figure in Mahayana Buddhism, known for compassion and the ability to save beings from suffering.
    • 珠林六十五 - Volume 65 of the "Jade Forest," likely a well-known text in the context of the time.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋吕竦 - Lu Song of the Jin Dynasty.
    • 兖州 - The ancient state of Yan.
    • 始丰 - The ancient county of Shifeng.
    • 观世音 - The Bodhisattva Guanyin.
    • 珠林六十五 - Volume 65 of the "Jade Forest."

    Lu Song, courtesy name Maogao, was from Yan State. He lived temporarily in Shifeng County. The southern stream of this county flows swiftly with steep banks, winding and twisting like a path. It is also filled with large rocks, and even during the day, those who travel there still feel a sense of danger. Lu Song recounted that his father once traveled in the stream, about ten or so miles from home. As the sun began to set, a sudden storm erupted, and the sky became as dark as ink, making it impossible to discern directions. His father felt he was about to drown. He focused his mind on the Bodhisattva Guanyin, continuously reciting and chanting. Soon, a firelight appeared on the shore, like someone holding a torch, illuminating the stream clearly. Guided by the firelight, he was able to return home, with the fire always leading the way ahead of the boat by about ten or so steps. Lu Song later spoke of this experience to Xie Jiajun, who also recounted it. This story is found in Volume 65 of the "Jade Forest." [49]

    晋徐荣者，琅琊人。尝至东阳，还经定山，舟人不惯，误堕洄澓中。游舞涛波，垂欲沈没。荣无复计。唯至心呼观世音。斯须闲，如有数十人齐力引船者，踊出澓中，还得平流。?江还下。日已向?，天大阴闇，风雨甚驶，不知所向。而涛波转盛。荣诵经不辍口。有顷，望见山头有火光赫然，回柁趣之，迳得还浦。举船安隐。既至，亦不复见光。同旅异之，疑非人火。明旦问浦中人，昨夜山上是何火光。众皆愕然曰：“昨风雨如此，岂如有火理，吾等并不见。”然后了其为神光矣。荣后为会稽府督护，谢敷闻其自说如此。时与荣同船者，有沙门支道蕴，谨笃士也，具见其事。后为傅亮言之，与荣所说同。珠林六十五 [50]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [50]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 晋徐荣者，琅琊人 - A person from the Jin dynasty, specifically from Langya.
    • 尝至东阳，还经定山 - Once went to Dongyang and returned via Ding Mountain.
    • 舟人不惯，误堕洄澓中 - The boatman was not accustomed and mistakenly fell into a whirlpool.
    • 游舞涛波，垂欲沈没 - Dancing in the waves, about to be submerged.
    • 荣无复计 - Had no other plan.
    • 唯至心呼观世音 - Only with sincere heart called upon Avalokiteshvara.
    • 斯须闲，如有数十人齐力引船者 - A short while later, as if dozens of people were pulling the boat together.
    • 踊出澓中，还得平流 - Jumped out of the whirlpool and returned to smooth waters.
    • 江还下 - Returned down the river.
    • 日已向?，天大阴闇，风雨甚驶 - The sky became very dark, and the wind and rain were very strong.
    • 不知所向 - Did not know which way to go.
    • 而涛波转盛 - The waves became even stronger.
    • 荣诵经不辍口 - Recited scriptures without stopping.
    • 有顷，望见山头有火光赫然 - After a while, saw a bright flame at the top of the mountain.
    • 回柁趣之，迳得还浦 - Turned the rudder and headed towards it, immediately returned to the harbor.
    • 举船安隐 - The whole boat was safe.
    • 既至，亦不复见光 - After arriving, the light was no longer visible.
    • 同旅异之，疑非人火 - The companions found it strange and suspected it was not a human-made fire.
    • 明旦问浦中人，昨夜山上是何火光 - The next morning, asked the people in the harbor about the firelight on the mountain last night.
    • 众皆愕然曰 - Everyone was surprised and said.
    • 昨风雨如此，岂如有火理 - The wind and rain were like this yesterday, how could there be fire?
    • 吾等并不见 - We did not see it.
    • 然后了其为神光矣 - Then they realized it was divine light.
    • 荣后为会稽府督护，谢敷闻其自说如此 - Later, Rong became the supervisor of the Kuaiji Prefecture, and Xie Fu heard him say this himself.
    • 时与荣同船者，有沙门支道蕴，谨笃士也，具见其事 - At that time, there was a monk named Zhi Daoyun on the same boat with Rong, a serious and devout person who witnessed the whole event.
    • 后为傅亮言之，与荣所说同 - Later, he told Fu Liang about it, and it was the same as what Rong had said.
    • 珠林六十五 - Likely a reference to a specific text or chapter in a larger work, possibly a collection of stories or historical records.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋 - Refers to the Jin dynasty.
    • 琅琊 - A place name in ancient China.
    • 东阳 - A place name in ancient China.
    • 定山 - A place name in ancient China.
    • 观世音 - Avalokiteshvara, a bodhisattva in Buddhism, often invoked for protection and assistance.
    • 会稽府 - A prefecture in ancient China.
    • 谢敷 - A historical figure, possibly a scholar or official.
    • 沙门支道蕴 - A monk named Zhi Daoyun, known for his devout nature.
    • 傅亮 - Another historical figure, possibly a scholar or official.
    • 珠林六十五 - Likely a reference to a specific text or chapter in a larger work, possibly a collection of stories or historical records.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋徐荣 - The name of a person from the Jin dynasty.
    • 琅琊 - A place name.
    • 东阳 - A place name.
    • 定山 - A place name.
    • 观世音 - The name of a bodhisattva.
    • 会稽府 - A prefecture in ancient China.
    • 谢敷 - A historical figure.
    • 沙门支道蕴 - A monk named Zhi Daoyun.
    • 傅亮 - A historical figure.
    • 珠林六十五 - A reference to a specific text or chapter.

    During the Jin dynasty, there was a man named Xu Rong from Langya. Once, he traveled to Dongyang and returned via Ding Mountain. The boatman was not accustomed to the waters and mistakenly fell into a whirlpool. Xu Rong danced in the waves, about to be submerged. With no other plan, he sincerely called upon the bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara. Shortly afterward, it seemed as if dozens of people were pulling the boat together, and it jumped out of the whirlpool, returning to smooth waters. He returned down the river. The day had turned dark, the sky became very dark, and the wind and rain were very strong. He did not know which way to go, and the waves became even stronger. Xu Rong recited scriptures without stopping. After a while, he saw a bright flame appear at the top of a mountain. He turned the rudder and headed towards it, immediately returning to the harbor. The whole boat was safe. After arriving, the light was no longer visible. His companions found it strange and suspected it was not a human-made fire. The next morning, he asked the people in the harbor what kind of firelight was on the mountain last night. Everyone was surprised and said, "The wind and rain were like this yesterday; how could there be fire? We did not see it." Then they realized it was divine light. Later, Xu Rong became the supervisor of the Kuaiji Prefecture, and Xie Fu heard him say this himself. At that time, there was a monk named Zhi Daoyun on the same boat with Xu Rong, who was a serious and devout person and witnessed the whole event. Later, he told Fu Liang about it, and it was the same as what Xu Rong had said. This is recorded in the Zhulin (Jade Forest) chapter 65. [50]

    晋兴宁中，沙门竺法义，山居好学。住在始宁保山，游刃众典，尤善法华。受业弟子，常有百余。至咸安二年，忽感心气疾病，已上六句依珠林十七引补积时攻治备至。而了不损。日就绵笃。遂不复自治。唯归诚观世音。如此数日。昼眠，梦见一道人，来○其病，因为治之：刳出肠胃，湔洗腑脏；见有结聚不净物甚多。洗濯毕，还内之。语义曰：“汝病已除。”眠觉，众患豁然。寻得复常。案其经云，或现沙门梵志之像；意者义公所梦其是乎。义以太元七年亡。自竺长舒至义六事，并宋尚书令傅亮所撰。二句广记引作宋尚书令傅亮撰其事迹亮自云，其先君与义游处。义每说其事，辄懔然增肃焉。珠林九十五又十七广记一百十　案傅亮所撰六事竺长舒已见前卷余不可考 [51]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [51]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 晋兴宁中 - Temporal phrase meaning 'during the Xingning period of the Jin dynasty.'
    • 山居好学 - Means 'living in the mountains and loving learning.'
    • 游刃众典 - Literally 'wielding a knife through many classics,' metaphorically meaning 'skilled in many classics.'
    • 尤善法华 - Means 'especially skilled in the Lotus Sutra.'
    • 受业弟子，常有百余 - Means 'disciples who receive instruction, often numbering over a hundred.'
    • 至咸安二年 - Temporal phrase meaning 'by the second year of the Xian'an period.'
    • 忽感心气疾病 - Means 'suddenly felt a heart and qi illness.'
    • 积时攻治备至 - Means 'after a long time of treatment and care.'
    • 而了不损 - Means 'but still did not improve.'
    • 日就绵笃 - Means 'gradually becoming more severe.'
    • 遂不复自治 - Means 'and then stopped treating himself.'
    • 唯归诚观世音 - Means 'only sought refuge in the Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara.'
    • 如此数日 - Means 'for several days like this.'
    • 昼眠，梦见一道人 - Means 'during the day, he dreamed of a hermit.'
    • 来○其病 - The character '○' is missing; it likely means 'to cure' or 'to treat.'
    • 因为治之 - Means 'and then treated him.'
    • 刳出肠胃，湔洗腑脏 - Means 'cut out the intestines and stomach, washed the internal organs.'
    • 见有结聚不净物甚多 - Means 'saw many accumulated impurities.'
    • 洗濯毕，还内之 - Means 'after washing, put them back.'
    • 语义曰：“汝病已除。” - Means 'told Yi, 'Your illness is gone.''
    • 眠觉，众患豁然 - Means 'woke up, and all his ailments were gone.'
    • 寻得复常 - Means 'soon returned to normal.'
    • 案其经云，或现沙门梵志之像 - Means 'According to the scriptures, sometimes he appears as a monk or a Brahmin.'
    • 意者义公所梦其是乎 - Means 'Perhaps what Master Yi dreamed was true.'
    • 义以太元七年亡 - Means 'Yi passed away in the seventh year of the Taie period.'
    • 自竺长舒至义六事，并宋尚书令傅亮所撰 - Means 'From Zhu Changshu to Yi, six stories, all compiled by Fu Liang, the Prime Minister of the Song dynasty.'
    • 其先君与义游处 - Means 'His father had been friends with Yi.'
    • 义每说其事，辄懔然增肃焉 - Means 'Whenever Yi talked about these things, he would become more solemn and respectful.'
    • 珠林九十五又十七广记一百十 - Refers to specific entries in the *Jade Forest* and *Widely Recorded* collections.
    • 案傅亮所撰六事竺长舒已见前卷余不可考 - Means 'According to Fu Liang's compilation, Zhu Changshu has been mentioned in previous volumes, but the rest are not verifiable.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋兴宁 - The Xingning period of the Eastern Jin dynasty (373-376 AD).
    • 咸安 - The Xian'an period of the Eastern Jin dynasty (371-372 AD).
    • 太元 - The Taie period of the Eastern Jin dynasty (386-396 AD).
    • 观世音 - The Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara, a central figure in Mahayana Buddhism.
    • 法华 - The Lotus Sutra, a major Mahayana Buddhist text.
    • 沙门梵志 - Refers to monks and Brahmins, respectively, indicating the diverse forms in which Avalokiteshvara might appear.
    • 宋尚书令傅亮 - Fu Liang, a high-ranking official and scholar of the Liu Song dynasty (420-479 AD).
    • 珠林九十五又十七广记一百十 - References to specific entries in the *Jade Forest* and *Widely Recorded* collections, which are important historical and religious texts.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋兴宁 - The Xingning period of the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 咸安 - The Xian'an period of the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 太元 - The Taie period of the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 观世音 - The Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara.
    • 法华 - The Lotus Sutra.
    • 沙门梵志 - Monks and Brahmins.
    • 宋尚书令傅亮 - Fu Liang, a high-ranking official and scholar of the Liu Song dynasty.
    • 珠林九十五又十七广记一百十 - Specific entries in the *Jade Forest* and *Widely Recorded* collections.
    • 竺长舒 - Zhu Changshu, a historical figure mentioned in the text.
    • 义公 - Master Yi, the subject of the story.

    During the Xingning period of the Jin dynasty, there was a monk named Zhu Fayi who lived in the mountains and loved learning. He resided in Shining Baoshan, skilled in many classics, especially the Lotus Sutra. He had over a hundred disciples who received instruction from him. By the second year of the Xian'an period, he suddenly felt a heart and qi illness. After a long time of treatment and care, his condition did not improve. Gradually, it became more severe, and he stopped treating himself. Instead, he sought refuge in the Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara. For several days, he continued to rely on this practice. One day, while napping during the day, he dreamed of a hermit who came to cure his illness. The hermit treated him by cutting out his intestines and stomach, washing his internal organs, and finding many accumulated impurities. After washing, the hermit put them back. The hermit told Yi, "Your illness is gone." When Yi woke up, all his ailments were gone, and he soon returned to normal. According to the scriptures, sometimes Avalokiteshvara appears as a monk or a Brahmin. Perhaps what Master Yi dreamed was true. Yi passed away in the seventh year of the Taie period. From Zhu Changshu to Yi, six stories, all compiled by Fu Liang, the Prime Minister of the Song dynasty. Two entries in the Widely Recorded collection are attributed to Fu Liang, who compiled the stories. Fu Liang mentioned that his father had been friends with Yi. Whenever Yi talked about these things, he would become more solemn and respectful. Entries 95 and 17 in the Jade Forest and 110 in the Widely Recorded collection. According to Fu Liang's compilation, Zhu Changshu has been mentioned in previous volumes, but the rest are not verifiable. [51]

    晋杜愿，字永平，梓潼涪人也。家巨富。有一男，名天保，愿爱念。年十岁，泰元三年，暴病而死。经数月日，家所养猪，生五子；一子最肥。后官长新到，愿将以作礼，捉就杀之。有一比丘，忽至愿前，谓曰：“此豚是君儿也。如前百余日中，而相忘乎？”言竟，忽然不见。四顾寻视，见西天腾空而去。香气充布，弥日乃歇。珠林五十二 [52]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [52]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 家巨富 - Indicates that the family is very wealthy.
    • 暴病而死 - Means 'died suddenly of an illness.'
    • 後官長新到 - Means 'after the new official arrived.'
    • 以作禮 - Means 'to use as a gift' or 'to offer as a present.'
    • 西天騰空而去 - Literally 'soared into the sky and departed to the West,' which is likely a metaphorical expression indicating the pig's transformation or spiritual ascension.
    • 珠林五十二 - This appears to be a reference to a specific chapter or section in a larger work, possibly a Buddhist text or a collection of stories.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋杜愿，字永平，梓潼涪人也 - Introduces the protagonist, Du Yuan (also known as Yongping), from Fuchong, Zitong, during the Jin Dynasty.
    • 梓潼涪 - Refers to Fuchong, a place in Zitong, which is now part of modern-day Sichuan Province.
    • 比丘 - Refers to a Buddhist monk, indicating a religious context.
    • 珠林五十二 - This reference suggests that the story is part of a larger collection of stories or a Buddhist text, possibly the 'Pearl Forest' (珠林) series, which is a collection of Buddhist stories.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋杜愿 - The full name of the protagonist, Du Yuan, during the Jin Dynasty.
    • 字永平 - His courtesy name, Yongping.
    • 梓潼涪 - The place of origin, Fuchong, Zitong.
    • 泰元三年 - The year of the main character's death, which corresponds to 378 AD in the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 比丘 - A term for a Buddhist monk.
    • 珠林五十二 - A specific chapter or section in a larger work, possibly a Buddhist text.

    Du Yuan, courtesy name Yongping, was from Fuchong, Zitong. His family was very wealthy. He had a son named Tianbao, whom he deeply loved. At the age of ten, Tianbao died suddenly of an illness in the third year of Taiyuan (378 AD). Several months later, a pig in the family gave birth to five piglets, one of which was particularly fat. After the new official arrived, Du Yuan planned to use this pig as a gift. Just as he was about to catch and kill it, a Buddhist monk suddenly appeared before him and said, "This pig is your son. Do you not remember him after more than a hundred days?" After speaking, the monk suddenly disappeared. Looking around, Du Yuan saw the monk soar into the sky and depart to the West. The fragrance spread and lasted for a whole day before dissipating. This story is recorded in the "Pearl Forest" (珠林) series, chapter fifty-two. [52]

    晋唐遵，字○道，上虞人也。晋太元八年，暴病而死。经夕得稣。云有人呼将去，至一城府，未进。顷见其从叔，自城中出，惊问遵：“汝何故来？”遵答：“违离姑姊，并历年载，欲往问讯，本明当发，夜见数人，急呼来此。即时可得归去，而不知还路。”从叔云：“汝姑丧已二年。汝大姊儿道文，近被录来。既蒙恩放，仍留看戏，不即还去，积日方归，家已殡殓。乃入棺中，又摇动棺器，冀望其家觉悟开棺。棺遂至路，落檀车下。其家或欲开之。乃向卜者。卜云不吉，遂不敢开。不得复生。今为把沙之役，辛勤极苦。汝宜速去，勿复住此。且汝小姊，又已丧亡。今与汝姑，共在地狱，日夕忧苦。不知何时，可得免脱。汝今还去，可语其儿：勤修功德，庶得免之。”于此示遵归路。 [53]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [53]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 暴病而死 - Means 'died suddenly from a severe illness.'
    • 经夕得苏 - Means 'regained consciousness after a night.'
    • 违离姑姊 - Means 'to be away from one's aunt and sister.'
    • 本明当发 - Means 'originally planned to depart.'
    • 夜见数人，急呼来此 - Means 'saw several people at night, who urgently called him here.'
    • 积日方归 - Means 'after many days, returned home.'
    • 家已殡殓 - Means 'the family had already performed the burial rites.'
    • 入棺中，又摇动棺器 - Means 'entered the coffin and shook the coffin lid.'
    • 把沙之役 - Refers to a laborious task involving sand, often used metaphorically to describe hard work.
    • 庶得免之 - Means 'perhaps one can escape from it.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋太元八年 - Refers to the year 383 AD during the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 上虞 - Refers to Shangyu, a county in Zhejiang Province.
    • 城府 - Refers to a city or administrative center.
    • 从叔 - Refers to a maternal uncle or cousin.
    • 姑 - Refers to an aunt.
    • 姊 - Refers to a sister.
    • 殡殓 - Refers to the funeral rites and burial process.
    • 地狱 - Refers to the Buddhist concept of hell.
    • 把沙之役 - A metaphorical reference to hard labor in hell.

    Proper Nouns

    • 唐遵 - The name of the narrator.
    • 道 - The narrator's courtesy name.
    • 上虞 - The county where the narrator is from.
    • 晋太元八年 - The year 383 AD.
    • 从叔 - The narrator's maternal uncle or cousin.
    • 姑 - The narrator's aunt.
    • 姊 - The narrator's sister.
    • 道文 - The narrator's eldest sister's son.
    • 地狱 - The Buddhist concept of hell.
    • 把沙之役 - A metaphorical reference to hard labor in hell.

    During the reign of Emperor Taiyuan in the Eastern Jin Dynasty, Tang Zun of Shangyu had a sudden and severe illness and died. After a night, he regained consciousness and said that someone called him away, leading him to a city or administrative center, but he did not enter. Soon, he saw his maternal uncle or cousin coming out of the city and asked him in surprise, "Why are you here?" Tang Zun replied, "I have been away from my aunt and sister for many years and wanted to visit them. I originally planned to depart, but I saw several people at night who urgently called me here. I could return immediately but did not know the way back." The maternal uncle or cousin said, "Your aunt has been dead for two years. Your eldest sister's son, Dao Wen, was recently taken away. He was released as a favor but stayed to watch a performance. He did not immediately return home and returned after many days. The family had already performed the burial rites. He entered the coffin and shook the coffin lid, hoping his family would realize and open the coffin. The coffin reached the road and fell under a wooden cart. The family considered opening the coffin but consulted a diviner who said it was inauspicious, so they did not dare to open it. Dao Wen could not be revived. He is now engaged in hard labor in hell, working extremely hard. You should leave quickly and not stay here. Your younger sister has also died. You and your aunt are now in hell, suffering daily. I do not know when you can be freed from hell. You should return and tell Dao Wen to diligently perform good deeds, perhaps he can escape from hell." At this point, the way back was shown to Tang Zun. [53]

    将别，又属遵曰：“汝得还生，良为殊庆。在世无几，儵如风尘。天堂地狱，苦乐报应；吾昔闻其语，今睹其实。汝宜深勤善业，务为孝敬。受法持戒，慎不可犯。一去人身，入此罪地。幽穷苦酷，自悔何及。勤以在心，不可忽也。我家亲属，生时不信罪福，今并遭涂炭，长受楚毒，焦烂伤痛，无时暂休。欲求一日改恶为善，当何得耶？悉我所具，故以嘱汝。劝化家内，共加勉励。”言已，涕泣，因此而别。遵随路而归，俄而至家。家治棺将竟，方营殡殓。遵既附尸，尸寻气通。移日稍差。劝示亲识，并奉大法。初遵姑○南郡徐汉，长姊○江夏乐瑜于，小姊○吴兴严晚。途路县远，久断音息。遵既差，遂至三郡，寻访姑及小姊。姊子果并丧亡。长姊亦说儿道文殓后，棺动堕车，皆如叔言。既闻遵说道文横死之意，姊追加痛恨，重为制服。珠林九十七 [54]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [54]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 将别 - Literally means 'before parting' or 'about to separate,' used to introduce the farewell scene.
    • 属遵曰 - Means 'entrusted to Zun saying,' indicating a direct address or command.
    • 汝得还生，良为殊庆 - Means 'you are able to return to life, which is indeed a great cause for celebration.'
    • 在世无几，倏如风尘 - Means 'life in the world is short, fleeting like dust in the wind.'
    • 天堂地狱，苦乐报应 - Refers to the Buddhist concepts of heaven and hell, and the consequences of good and bad actions.
    • 吾昔闻其语，今睹其实 - Means 'I heard about it before, and now I see it in reality.'
    • 汝宜深勤善业，务为孝敬 - Means 'you should diligently cultivate good deeds and be filial and respectful.'
    • 受法持戒，慎不可犯 - Means 'receive the teachings and keep the precepts, be cautious not to violate them.'
    • 一去人身，入此罪地 - Means 'once you leave the human body, you will enter this realm of sin.'
    • 幽穷苦酷，自悔何及 - Means 'endless suffering and cruelty, regretting it afterward will be too late.'
    • 勤以在心，不可忽也 - Means 'diligence should be in your heart, do not neglect it.'
    • 我家亲属，生时不信罪福，今并遭涂炭，长受楚毒，焦烂伤痛，无时暂休 - Means 'my family members, who did not believe in karma in life, are now suffering greatly, enduring long-lasting pain, burning and hurting, with no moment of respite.'
    • 欲求一日改恶为善，当何得耶 - Means 'if they want to change from evil to good for even a day, what can they do?'
    • 悉我所具，故以嘱汝 - Means 'knowing all this, I entrust you with this.'
    • 劝化家内，共加勉励 - Means 'persuade and guide your family, encourage each other.'
    • 言已，涕泣，因此而别 - Means 'after speaking, he wept, and they parted ways.'
    • 随路而归，俄而至家 - Means 'he returned home along the road, soon reaching his house.'
    • 家治棺将竟，方营殡殓 - Means 'the family was preparing the coffin, just about to perform the funeral rites.'
    • 遵既附尸，尸寻气通 - Means 'Zun touched the body, and soon the body regained breath.'
    • 移日稍差 - Means 'after a few days, he gradually recovered.'
    • 劝示亲识，并奉大法 - Means 'he persuaded and showed his relatives and acquaintances, all following the great teachings.'
    • 初遵姑○南郡徐汉，长姊○江夏乐瑜于，小姊○吴兴严晚 - Means 'initially, Zun's aunt in Nanjun was Xu Han, his elder sister in Jiangxia was Le Yu Yu, and his younger sister in Wuxing was Yan Wan.'
    • 途路县远，久断音息 - Means 'the distance was far, and communication had been cut off for a long time.'
    • 遵既差，遂至三郡，寻访姑及小姊 - Means 'after Zun recovered, he went to the three prefectures to find his aunt and younger sister.'
    • 姊子果并丧亡 - Means 'his sister's children had indeed died.'
    • 长姊亦说儿道文殓后，棺动堕车，皆如叔言 - Means 'his elder sister also said that after her son's burial, the coffin moved and fell from the cart, just as his uncle had said.'
    • 既闻遵说道文横死之意，姊追加痛恨，重为制服 - Means 'after hearing Zun's explanation of his brother's sudden death, his sister felt even more grief and anger, and wore mourning clothes again.'
    • 珠林九十七 - Refers to the source of the text, *Zhu Jin* (Pearl Forest), volume 97.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 天堂地狱，苦乐报应 - These are key Buddhist concepts of karma and the afterlife, emphasizing the consequences of one's actions.
    • 受法持戒，慎不可犯 - These are Buddhist teachings on following the Dharma (teachings) and keeping the precepts (rules of conduct).
    • 珠林九十七 - *Zhu Jin* (Pearl Forest) is a collection of Buddhist stories and parables, indicating the religious context of the text.
    • 南郡、江夏、吴兴 - These are historical prefectures in China, providing geographical context.

    Proper Nouns

    • 遵 - The name of the person being addressed.
    • 南郡徐汉 - The name of Zun's aunt in Nanjun.
    • 江夏乐瑜于 - The name of Zun's elder sister in Jiangxia.
    • 吴兴严晚 - The name of Zun's younger sister in Wuxing.
    • 珠林九十七 - The title of the source text, *Zhu Jin* (Pearl Forest), volume 97.

    As they were about to part, he entrusted Zun with these words: "You are able to return to life, which is indeed a great cause for celebration. Life in the world is short, fleeting like dust in the wind. Heaven and hell, the consequences of good and bad actions; I heard about it before, and now I see it in reality. You should diligently cultivate good deeds and be filial and respectful. Receive the teachings and keep the precepts, be cautious not to violate them. Once you leave the human body, you will enter this realm of sin. Endless suffering and cruelty, regretting it afterward will be too late. Diligence should be in your heart, do not neglect it. My family members, who did not believe in karma in life, are now suffering greatly, enduring long-lasting pain, burning and hurting, with no moment of respite. If they want to change from evil to good for even a day, what can they do? Knowing all this, I entrust you with this. Persuade and guide your family, encourage each other." After speaking, he wept, and they parted ways. Zun returned home along the road, soon reaching his house. The family was preparing the coffin, just about to perform the funeral rites. Zun touched the body, and soon the body regained breath. After a few days, he gradually recovered. He persuaded and showed his relatives and acquaintances, all following the great teachings. Initially, Zun's aunt in Nanjun was Xu Han, his elder sister in Jiangxia was Le Yu Yu, and his younger sister in Wuxing was Yan Wan. The distance was far, and communication had been cut off for a long time. After Zun recovered, he went to the three prefectures to find his aunt and younger sister. His sister's children had indeed died. His elder sister also said that after her son's burial, the coffin moved and fell from the cart, just as his uncle had said. After hearing Zun's explanation of his brother's sudden death, his sister felt even more grief and anger, and wore mourning clothes again. Zhu Jin (Pearl Forest), volume 97. [54]

    晋谢敷，字庆绪，会稽山阴人也，镇军将军輶之兄子也。少有高操，隐于东山，笃信大法，精勤不倦。手写首楞严经。当在都白马寺中，寺为灾火所延，什物余经，并成煨尽，而此经止烧纸头界外而已。文字悉存，无所毁失。敷死时，友人疑其得道。及闻此经，弥复惊异。至元嘉八年，河东蒲阪城中大灾火。火自隔河飞至，不可救灭；处戍民居，无不荡尽。唯精舍塔寺，并得不焚。里中小屋，有经像者，亦多不烧。或屋虽焚毁，而于煨尽之中，时得全经，纸素如故。一城叹异，相率敬信。珠林十八 [55]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [55]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 少有高操 - Means 'had high aspirations' or 'possessed noble character' from a young age.
    • 隱於東山 - A common idiom meaning 'retired to the east mountain,' often used to describe recluses or those who withdrew from public life.
    • 篤信大法 - Means 'firmly believed in the Great Law,' where '大法' likely refers to Buddhist teachings.
    • 精勤不倦 - Means 'diligently and tirelessly,' emphasizing sustained effort.
    • 手寫首楞嚴經 - Literally 'wrote the *Shurangama Sutra* by hand,' where *首楞嚴經* is the *Shurangama Sutra*, a significant Buddhist scripture.
    • 當在都白馬寺中 - Means 'while staying at the White Horse Temple in the capital.'
    • 寺為災火所延 - Means 'the temple was engulfed by a fire disaster,' where '災火' refers to a destructive fire.
    • 什物余經，並成煨盡 - Means 'all the belongings and other scriptures were reduced to ashes.'
    • 而此經止燒紙頭界外而已 - Means 'but this scripture was only burned at the edges of the paper,' suggesting it remained largely intact.
    • 文字悉存，無所毀失 - Means 'the text was completely preserved, with no damage or loss.'
    • 敷死時，友人疑其得道 - Means 'when he died, his friends suspected that he had attained enlightenment.'
    • 及聞此經，彌復驚異 - Means 'upon hearing of this scripture, they were even more amazed.'
    • 至元嘉八年 - Refers to the year 431 AD, during the Southern Dynasties period.
    • 河東蒲阪城中大災火 - Means 'a great fire disaster struck the city of Pu'an in Hedong (modern-day Yongji, Shanxi).'
    • 火自隔河飛至，不可救滅 - Means 'the fire crossed the river as if flying, and could not be extinguished.'
    • 處戍民居，無不蕩盡 - Means 'all the military outposts, civilian houses, and everything else were completely destroyed.'
    • 唯精舍塔寺，並得不焚 - Means 'only the monasteries and pagodas were spared from the fire.'
    • 里中小屋，有經像者，亦多不燒 - Means 'small houses in the neighborhood, if they contained scriptures or images, were also largely spared.'
    • 或屋雖焚毀，而於煨盡之中，時得全經，紙素如故 - Means 'sometimes even if a house was burned down, the scriptures within were still found intact, with the paper looking as good as new.'
    • 一城嘆異，相率敬信 - Means 'the entire city was amazed, and people began to revere and believe in Buddhism.'
    • 珠林十八 - Refers to the 18th chapter of the *Zhu Lin* (Jewel Forest), a collection of Buddhist stories and anecdotes.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 謝敷 - A historical figure from the Eastern Jin Dynasty, known for his reclusive lifestyle and deep Buddhist faith.
    • 鎮軍將軍輶之 - Indicates that Xie Fu was the nephew of General of the Army You, a high-ranking official.
    • 東山 - A symbol of reclusion and retreat, often associated with scholars and monks who withdrew from worldly affairs.
    • 大法 - Refers to Buddhist teachings, particularly in the context of a monk or devout practitioner.
    • 首楞嚴經 - The *Shurangama Sutra*, a highly revered Buddhist scripture that emphasizes the importance of mindfulness and meditation.
    • 白馬寺 - The White Horse Temple, one of the oldest Buddhist temples in China, located in Luoyang.
    • 元嘉八年 - The year 431 AD, during the reign of Emperor Wen of Liu Song, a period of relative stability and cultural flourishing in the Southern Dynasties.
    • 河東蒲阪 - The city of Pu'an in Hedong, modern-day Yongji, Shanxi, known for its historical significance in Chinese Buddhism.
    • 精舍塔寺 - Refers to monasteries and pagodas, which were often centers of Buddhist learning and practice.
    • 經像 - Refers to Buddhist scriptures and images, such as statues of the Buddha.
    • 珠林 - The *Zhu Lin* (Jewel Forest), a collection of Buddhist stories and anecdotes compiled during the Tang Dynasty.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • 少有高操，隱於東山 - This phrase introduces Xie Fu's character and lifestyle, but the relationship between his high aspirations and his retreat to the east mountain is somewhat ambiguous. It could mean that he had high aspirations and chose to retreat, or that his retreat itself was an expression of his high character.
    • 篤信大法，精勤不倦 - This phrase describes Xie Fu's dedication to Buddhist practice, but the connection between his faith and his diligence is somewhat implicit.
    • 手寫首楞嚴經 - This phrase is straightforward, but the significance of him personally writing the *Shurangama Sutra* is not immediately clear without additional context.
    • 寺為災火所延，什物余經，並成煨盡 - This phrase describes the fire disaster, but the specific details of how the fire spread and what was destroyed are somewhat vague.
    • 而此經止燒紙頭界外而已 - This phrase suggests that the *Shurangama Sutra* was only slightly damaged, but the exact nature of the damage is not specified.
    • 文字悉存，無所毀失 - This phrase emphasizes the preservation of the text, but the extent of the preservation is not explicitly stated.
    • 敷死時，友人疑其得道 - This phrase introduces the possibility that Xie Fu had attained enlightenment, but the basis for this suspicion is not fully explained.
    • 及聞此經，彌復驚異 - This phrase indicates that his friends were amazed by the preservation of the *Shurangama Sutra*, but the reasons for their amazement are not fully elaborated.
    • 至元嘉八年，河東蒲阪城中大災火 - This phrase introduces a new event, but the specific details of the fire and its impact are somewhat vague.
    • 火自隔河飛至，不可救滅 - This phrase describes the fire's spread, but the exact mechanics of how it crossed the river are not fully explained.
    • 處戍民居，無不蕩盡 - This phrase emphasizes the destruction caused by the fire, but the specific details of what was destroyed are somewhat vague.
    • 唯精舍塔寺，並得不焚 - This phrase highlights the survival of monasteries and pagodas, but the reasons for their survival are not fully explained.
    • 里中小屋，有經像者，亦多不燒 - This phrase suggests that houses containing Buddhist scriptures and images were also spared, but the reasons for this are not fully explained.
    • 或屋雖焚毀，而於煨盡之中，時得全經，紙素如故 - This phrase describes the preservation of scriptures even in burned houses, but the exact circumstances of this preservation are not fully explained.
    • 一城嘆異，相率敬信 - This phrase indicates that the entire city was amazed and began to revere Buddhism, but the specific details of this reaction are somewhat vague.

    Proper Nouns

    • 謝敷 - The historical figure Xie Fu.
    • 鎮軍將軍輶之 - General of the Army You, Xie Fu's uncle.
    • 首楞嚴經 - The *Shurangama Sutra*.
    • 白馬寺 - The White Horse Temple.
    • 元嘉八年 - The year 431 AD.
    • 河東蒲阪 - The city of Pu'an in Hedong, modern-day Yongji, Shanxi.
    • 精舍塔寺 - Monasteries and pagodas.
    • 珠林十八 - The 18th chapter of the *Zhu Lin* (Jewel Forest).

    Xie Fu, courtesy name Qing Xu, was from Shan Yin, Kuaiji, and was the nephew of General of the Army You. From a young age, he possessed noble character and retired to the East Mountain, firmly believing in the Great Law and diligently practicing without tiring. He personally wrote the Shurangama Sutra. While staying at the White Horse Temple in the capital, the temple was engulfed by a fire disaster, and all the belongings and other scriptures were reduced to ashes. However, this scripture was only burned at the edges of the paper, leaving the text completely preserved with no damage or loss. When he died, his friends suspected that he had attained enlightenment. Upon hearing of this scripture, they were even more amazed.

    In the eighth year of the Yungang era (431 AD), a great fire disaster struck the city of Pu'an in Hedong (modern-day Yongji, Shanxi). The fire crossed the river as if flying, and could not be extinguished. All the military outposts, civilian houses, and everything else were completely destroyed. Only the monasteries and pagodas were spared from the fire. Small houses in the neighborhood, if they contained scriptures or images, were also largely spared. Sometimes even if a house was burned down, the scriptures within were still found intact, with the paper looking as good as new. The entire city was amazed, and people began to revere and believe in Buddhism. This is recorded in the 18th chapter of the Zhu Lin (Jewel Forest). [55]

    晋济阴丁承，字德慎。建安中，案晋纪元无建安疑当作建元也为凝阴令。时北界居民妇，诣外井汲水。有胡人长鼻深目，左过井上，从妇人乞饮。饮讫，忽然不见。妇则腹痛，遂加转剧。啼呼有顷，卒然起坐，胡语指麾。邑中有数十家，悉共观视。妇呼索纸笔来，欲作书。得笔，便作胡书：横行，或如乙，或如己。满五纸，投着地，教人读此书。邑中无能读者。有一小儿，十余岁，妇即指此小儿能读。小儿得书，便胡语读之，观者惊愕，不知何谓。妇叫小儿起。小儿既起，翘足，以手弄相和。须臾各休。即以白德慎。德慎召见妇及儿，问之，云：当时忽忽不自觉，知德慎欲验其事，即遣吏赍书诣许下寺，以示旧胡。胡大惊，言佛经中闲，亡失道远，忧不能得。虽口诵，不具足。此乃本书，遂留写之。珠林十八 [56]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [56]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 建安中 - Refers to the Jian'an period (196–220 AD), a time of significant cultural and literary development in Chinese history.
    • 案晋纪元无建安疑当作建元 - Suggests a historical note indicating that the correct year should be Jianyuan (建元) instead of Jian'an, though this is a matter of scholarly debate.
    • 诣外井汲水 - Literal description of a woman going to a well to draw water.
    • 胡语指麾 - Indicates that the woman began to speak in a foreign language and gesture, suggesting a trance-like state.
    • 横行，或如乙，或如己 - Describes the writing as horizontal and resembling certain characters, indicating a form of script that is not standard Chinese.
    • 满五纸，投着地 - The woman wrote on five sheets of paper and then threw them on the ground.
    • 教人读此书 - She instructed people to read the text she had written.
    • 不知何谓 - The audience did not understand the meaning of the text.
    • 妇叫小儿起 - The woman called the child to stand up and perform some action, possibly a ritual.
    • 翘足，以手弄相和 - The child raised his foot and used his hands to make some gesture, likely part of the ritual.
    • 珠林十八 - Refers to a specific chapter or section in a larger text, possibly a collection of stories or records.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋济阴丁承，字德慎 - Refers to Ding Cheng, a person from the Jin dynasty who served as the magistrate of Jinyin (济阴).
    • 凝阴令 - The position of magistrate in the county of Jinyin.
    • 胡人 - Refers to people of non-Han Chinese ethnicity, often associated with the northern nomadic tribes.
    • 佛经中闲，亡失道远，忧不能得 - Indicates that the text written by the woman was similar to a lost Buddhist scripture, suggesting a connection to religious or mystical practices.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • 案晋纪元无建安疑当作建元 - Introduces a historical note that may be ambiguous, as it suggests a correction but also implies uncertainty.
    • 妇则腹痛，遂加转剧 - The sequence of events is clear but the intensity of the pain and its progression need to be carefully translated to convey the sudden and severe nature of the condition.
    • 满五纸，投着地，教人读此书 - This sequence of actions is straightforward but requires careful handling to maintain the flow and clarity of the narrative.
    • 珠林十八 - This reference to a specific chapter or section in a larger text adds a layer of complexity, as it may require additional context for modern readers.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋济阴丁承 - Ding Cheng, a historical figure from the Jin dynasty.
    • 凝阴 - Jinyin, a county in the Jin dynasty.
    • 建安 - Jian'an, a period of the Eastern Han dynasty (196–220 AD).
    • 建元 - Jianyuan, another period name, possibly a correction to Jian'an.
    • 珠林 - A collection of stories or records, possibly a specific text.

    Ding Cheng of Jinyin, styled De Shen, was the magistrate of Jinyin during the Jian'an period (196–220 AD). According to the Jin dynasty chronology, there is doubt whether it should be Jian'an or Jianyuan. At that time, a woman from the northern border of the county went to an outer well to draw water. A man with a long nose and deep-set eyes passed by the well and asked the woman for a drink. After drinking, he suddenly disappeared. The woman then felt abdominal pain, which intensified. She cried out for a while, and then suddenly sat up, speaking in a foreign language and gesturing. Several dozen households in the village gathered to watch. The woman called for paper and ink, wanting to write something. When she got the pen, she wrote in a foreign script: horizontal lines, sometimes resembling the character "乙," sometimes resembling "己." She filled five sheets of paper, threw them on the ground, and instructed people to read the text. No one in the village could read it. There was a boy, about ten years old, whom the woman pointed out as being able to read it. The boy took the paper and read it in the foreign language. The onlookers were amazed and did not understand what it meant. The woman called the boy to stand up and perform a ritual. The boy stood up, raised his foot, and used his hands to make some gestures. After a short while, they all stopped. The woman immediately reported this to Ding Cheng. Ding Cheng summoned the woman and the boy and asked them about it. They said that they had been unaware at the time but knew that Ding Cheng wanted to verify the incident. Ding Cheng then sent an official with the text to a temple in Xu (许), to show it to an old man from the foreign land. The old man was greatly surprised and said that the text resembled a lost Buddhist scripture, which had been lost due to the long distance and was feared to be unobtainable. Although he could recite it, it was not complete. This was indeed the original text, and he decided to copy it down. This story is recorded in Chapter Eighteen of Zhu Lin (珠林). [56]

    晋琅琊王凝之，妻晋左将军夫人谢氏奕之女也。尝频亡，二男悼惜过甚，哭泣累年，若居至艰。后忽见二儿俱还，皆着锁械，慰勉其母，宜自宽割。儿并有罪，若垂哀怜。可为作福。于是哀痛稍止，而勤功德。珠林三十三
    　　晋沙门支遁，字道林，陈留人也。神宇隽发，为老释风流之宗。常与其师，辨论物类。谓鸡卵生用未足，杀之，与诸蜎动，不得同罚。师寻亡。忽见形来，至遁前，手执鸡卵投地，破之，见有鸡雏，出壳而行。遁即惟悟，悔其本言。俄而师及鸡雏，并灭不见。珠林七十二 [57]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [57]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 尝频亡 - Means 'often died,' likely a euphemism for experiencing frequent losses, possibly referring to the death of children or other family members.
    • 哭泣累年 - Means 'crying for many years,' emphasizing the prolonged period of mourning.
    • 宜自宽割 - Means 'should be self-reliant and cut ties,' suggesting that she should move on and let go of her grief.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋琅琊王凝之 - Wang Ningzhi, a prince of the Jin dynasty, specifically the Prince of Langya.
    • 晋左将军夫人谢氏奕之女也 - Indicates that his wife was the daughter of General Xie Yi, a high-ranking official during the Jin dynasty.
    • 晋沙门支遁 - Zhi Dun, a famous Buddhist monk and scholar of the Jin dynasty, known for his contributions to Chinese Buddhism.
    • 神宇隽发 - Describes Zhi Dun as having a divine presence and sharp intellect, typical of a revered scholar-monk.
    • 老释风流之宗 - Refers to Zhi Dun as a leading figure in the fusion of Taoist and Buddhist thought.
    • 珠林三十三 - A reference to a specific chapter or section of the *Jade Forest* (Zhu Lin), a collection of Buddhist stories and teachings.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋琅琊王凝之 - Wang Ningzhi, Prince of Langya.
    • 晋左将军夫人谢氏奕之女也 - Daughter of General Xie Yi.
    • 晋沙门支遁 - Zhi Dun, a Buddhist monk.
    • 神宇隽发 - A description of Zhi Dun's divine and sharp nature.
    • 老释风流之宗 - A title indicating Zhi Dun's status as a leading figure in the fusion of Taoist and Buddhist thought.
    • 珠林三十三 - A reference to a specific chapter or section of the *Jade Forest*.

    Wang Ningzhi, Prince of Langya, was married to the daughter of General Xie Yi, a left general of the Jin dynasty. He often experienced the loss of family members, and his two sons mourned excessively, crying for many years as if facing the greatest hardship. Later, he suddenly saw his two sons return together, both wearing shackles and chains. They comforted and encouraged their mother, advising her to be self-reliant and cut ties. They said that they both had sins, and if she showed compassion, she could perform good deeds for them. As a result, his grief gradually subsided, and he became diligent in performing good deeds and cultivating virtue. (From the Jade Forest, Volume 33)

    Zhi Dun, a Buddhist monk of the Jin dynasty, was from Chenliu and was known by the name Dao Lin. He had a divine presence and sharp intellect, making him a leading figure in the fusion of Taoist and Buddhist thought. He often debated with his teacher about the moral status of various creatures. Zhi Dun argued that eggs were not yet fully developed and thus should not be killed, unlike small insects, which deserved different treatment. Soon after, his teacher passed away. One day, Zhi Dun suddenly saw his teacher appear before him, holding an egg and throwing it to the ground. The egg broke, and a chick emerged, walking out of its shell. Zhi Dun immediately realized his mistake and regretted his earlier words. Soon after, both his teacher and the chick disappeared. (From the Jade Forest, Volume 72) [57]

    晋庐山七岭，同会于东，共成峰崿。其崖穷绝，莫有升者。晋太元中，豫章太守范宁，将起学馆，遣人伐材其山。见人着沙门服，淩虚直上。既至，则回身踞其峰；良久，乃兴云气，俱灭。时有采药数人，皆共瞻睹。能文之士，咸为之兴。沙门释昙谛庐山赋曰：“应真淩云以踞峰，眇翳景而入冥者也。”珠林十九
    　　晋沙门释僧朗者，戒行明严，华戎敬异。尝与数人，俱受法请；行至中途。忽告同辈曰：“君等留寺衣物，似有窃者。”同旅即返，果及盗焉。晋太元中，于奉高县金舆山谷，起立塔寺，造制形像。符坚之末，降斥道人，惟敬朗一众，不敢毁焉。于时道俗信奉，每有来者。人数多少，未至一日，辄已逆知。使弟子为具，必如言果到。其谷旧多虎，常为暴害。立寺之后，皆如家畜。鲜卑慕容德，以二县祖课，充其朝中。至今号其谷为朗公谷也。珠林十九 [58]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [58]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 晋庐山七岭，同会于东，共成峰崿 - Describes the geographical feature of Mount Lu in Jiangxi, where seven ridges converge to the east, forming peaks.
    • 淩虚直上 - Literally means 'ascending into the void straight up,' used to describe someone rising into the air.
    • 回身踞其峰 - Means 'turning his body and sitting on the peak,' indicating a position of dominance or contemplation.
    • 兴云气，俱灭 - Means 'raising clouds and mist, then disappearing together,' describing a mystical or supernatural departure.
    • 应真淩云以踞峰 - '应真' refers to a Bodhisattva or enlightened being, '淩云以踞峰' means 'ascending into the clouds and sitting on the peak.'
    • 降斥道人 - Means 'suppressing or expelling monks,' indicating a policy of persecution.
    • 惟敬朗一众 - Means 'only respecting the one group led by Lang,' showing an exception to the general policy.
    • 人数多少，未至一日，辄已逆知 - Means 'the number of people, before they arrive, is already known in advance,' indicating a supernatural ability to foresee.
    • 使弟子为具，必如言果到 - Means 'having disciples prepare, it will certainly come true as promised,' showing a reliable prediction.
    • 虎常为暴害 - Means 'tigers often cause harm,' indicating a dangerous environment.
    • 皆如家畜 - Means 'all are like domesticated animals,' showing a change from wild to tame.
    • 鲜卑慕容德，以二县祖课，充其朝中 - Means 'the Xianbei ruler Murong De, using two counties' taxes, to support his court,' indicating a political and economic arrangement.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋庐山七岭 - Refers to the seven ridges of Mount Lu in Jiangxi during the Jin Dynasty.
    • 豫章太守范宁 - Refers to Fan Ning, the governor of Yuzhang (modern-day Nanchang) during the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 太元中 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin (376-396 AD).
    • 释昙谛 - A Buddhist monk who wrote the *Lu Mountain Assignment*.
    • 珠林十九 - Refers to the *Zhu Lin* (Jewel Forest), a collection of Buddhist stories and legends.
    • 沙门释僧朗 - Refers to Monk Sang Lang, a Buddhist monk known for his strict discipline and respect.
    • 符坚之末 - Refers to the late period of Former Qin, ruled by Fu Jian.
    • 降斥道人 - Refers to the policy of suppressing Buddhism during the late Former Qin period.
    • 鲜卑慕容德 - Refers to Murong De, a Xianbei ruler who established the Southern Yan state.
    • 朗公谷 - Refers to the valley named after Monk Sang Lang, where he built a temple.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋庐山 - Mount Lu in Jiangxi during the Jin Dynasty.
    • 豫章太守范宁 - Fan Ning, the governor of Yuzhang (modern-day Nanchang) during the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 太元中 - The reign of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin (376-396 AD).
    • 释昙谛 - A Buddhist monk who wrote the *Lu Mountain Assignment*.
    • 珠林十九 - The *Zhu Lin* (Jewel Forest), a collection of Buddhist stories and legends.
    • 沙门释僧朗 - Monk Sang Lang, a Buddhist monk known for his strict discipline and respect.
    • 符坚之末 - The late period of Former Qin, ruled by Fu Jian.
    • 鲜卑慕容德 - Murong De, a Xianbei ruler who established the Southern Yan state.
    • 朗公谷 - The valley named after Monk Sang Lang, where he built a temple.

    During the Jin Dynasty, the seven ridges of Mount Lu converged to the east, forming a series of peaks. The cliffs were so steep and rugged that no one could climb them. In the Taiyuan period, Fan Ning, the governor of Yuzhang, planned to build a school and sent people to gather materials from the mountain. They saw a man dressed in a monk's robe ascending into the void straight up. Upon reaching the peak, he turned his body and sat upon it. After a while, he raised clouds and mist, and then disappeared. At that time, several people who were gathering herbs all witnessed this. Those skilled in writing were all inspired by it. The Buddhist monk Tan Di wrote in his Lu Mountain Assignment: "The Bodhisattva ascended into the clouds and sat on the peak, disappearing into the void."

    In the Jin Dynasty, the Buddhist monk Sang Lang was known for his strict discipline and was respected by both Han and foreign people. Once, while traveling with a few companions, he suddenly told his fellow travelers, "You left your belongings in the temple, and it seems someone has stolen them." They immediately returned and indeed caught the thief. During the Taiyuan period, he built a pagoda and temple in the Jinju Valley of Fenggao County, creating statues and images. In the late period of Former Qin, when the emperor Fu Jian suppressed Buddhism, he alone respected the group led by Sang Lang and did not dare to destroy their temple. At that time, both monks and laypeople revered him, and he could predict the number of visitors before they arrived, always preparing accordingly. Before the temple was built, the valley was often plagued by tigers that caused harm. After the temple was established, the tigers became like domesticated animals. The Xianbei ruler Murong De used the taxes from two counties to support his court. To this day, the valley is known as Lang Gong Valley.

    [58]

    晋沙门释法相，河东人也。常独山居，精苦为业。鸟兽集其左右，驯若家兽。太山祠大石函，以盛财宝。相时山行，宿于其庙。见一人玄衣武冠，令相开函，言终不见。其函石盖重过千钧，相试提之飘然而开。于是取其财宝，以施贫民。后渡江南，住越城寺，忽敖游放荡，优俳滑稽，或时裸袒，干冒朝贵。镇北将军司马恬恶其不节，招而酖之。频倾三钟，神气清怡，恬然自若。年八十九，元兴末卒。珠林十九 [59]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [59]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 精苦为业 - Devoted to rigorous practice.
    • 驯若家兽 - Tame like domestic animals.
    • 以盛财宝 - To hold treasures.
    • 宿于其庙 - Stayed in its temple.
    • 令相开函 - Ordered him to open the box.
    • 重过千钧 - Extremely heavy (hyperbolic expression).
    • 飘然而开 - Opened effortlessly.
    • 以施贫民 - To distribute to the poor.
    • 忽敖游放荡 - Suddenly became carefree and unrestrained.
    • 优俳滑稽 - Skilled in jest and humor.
    • 或时裸袒 - Sometimes went naked.
    • 干冒朝贵 - Offended the nobility.
    • 恶其不节 - Disliked his lack of restraint.
    • 招而酖之 - Invited him and poisoned him.
    • 频倾三钟 - Drank three cups repeatedly.
    • 神气清怡，恬然自若 - Spirit remained clear and calm, as if nothing had happened.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋沙门释法相 - A Buddhist monk from the Jin dynasty.
    • 河东人也 - Indicates his origin from Hedong, a region in Shanxi province.
    • 太山祠 - Refers to a temple dedicated to Mount Tai, a sacred mountain in Shandong province.
    • 镇北将军司马恬 - A high-ranking military official during the Jin dynasty.
    • 元兴末 - Refers to the end of the Yuanxing era, a period in the Eastern Jin dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 释法相 - The name of the Buddhist monk.
    • 河东 - The region where he was from.
    • 太山祠 - The temple dedicated to Mount Tai.
    • 镇北将军司马恬 - The name of the military official who poisoned him.
    • 元兴 - The era during which he died.
    • 珠林十九 - The source of the story, 'Jewel Forest' (珠林).

    The Buddhist monk named Fashang (释法相) was from Hedong (河东). He often lived alone in the mountains, devoted to rigorous practice. Birds and animals gathered around him, tame like domestic animals. There was a large stone box at the Temple of Mount Tai, used to hold treasures. Fashang was traveling in the mountains and stayed in the temple. He saw a person dressed in a black robe and wearing a military hat, who ordered Fashang to open the box. When the person spoke, he disappeared. The stone lid of the box was extremely heavy, but Fashang managed to open it effortlessly. He then took the treasures and distributed them to the poor. Later, he crossed the river to the south and lived in Yuecheng Temple. Suddenly, he became carefree and unrestrained, skilled in jest and humor. Sometimes he went naked, offending the nobility. The Northern General Military Commander Sima Tian disliked his lack of restraint and invited him, then poisoned him. Fashang drank three cups repeatedly, but his spirit remained clear and calm, as if nothing had happened. He was aged 89 and died at the end of the Yuanxing era. This story is recorded in the "Jewel Forest" (珠林十九). [59]

    晋张崇，京兆杜陵人也。少奉法。晋太元中，符坚既败，长安百姓有千余家，南走归晋。为镇戍所拘。谓为游寇。杀其男丁，虏其子女。崇与同等五人，手脚共械，衔身掘坑，埋筑至腰。各相去二十步。明日将驰马射之，以为娱乐。崇虑望穷尽，唯洁心专念观世音。夜中，械忽自破，上得离身！因是便走，遂得免脱。崇既脚痛，同寻路，经一寺，乃复称观世音名，至心礼拜。以一石置前，发誓愿，言今欲过江东，诉乱晋帝，理此冤魂，救其妻息。若心愿获果，此石当分为二。崇礼拜已，石即破焉。崇遂至京师，发白虎撙，具列冤氏。帝乃悉加宥己。为人所略卖者，皆为编户。智生道人，目所亲见。法苑珠林六十五 [60]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [60]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 少奉法 - Phrase meaning 'young and devoted to the law' or 'younger and obedient to the law.'
    • 南走归晋 - Phrase meaning 'southern migration to return to Jin,' indicating the movement of people from the north to the south to rejoin the Jin dynasty.
    • 镇戍所拘 - Phrase meaning 'imprisoned by the local garrison,' indicating the detention by military forces.
    • 谓为游寇 - Phrase meaning 'considered as bandits,' indicating the perception of the refugees as outlaws.
    • 杀其男丁，虏其子女 - Phrase meaning 'killed the men and captured the women and children,' indicating the brutal treatment of the refugees.
    • 脚手共械，颈身掘坑，埋筑至腰 - Phrase meaning 'chained together by hands and feet, neck and body dug into pits, buried up to the waist,' describing the cruel method of punishment.
    • 洁心专念观世音 - Phrase meaning 'purify the heart and focus on the Bodhisattva Guanyin,' indicating the act of devout prayer.
    • 械忽自破，上得离身 - Phrase meaning 'the chains suddenly broke, allowing them to free themselves,' indicating a miraculous event.
    • 遂得免脱 - Phrase meaning 'thus escaped,' indicating the successful escape.
    • 脚痛 - Phrase meaning 'feeling pain in the feet,' indicating physical discomfort.
    • 目所亲见 - Phrase meaning 'seen with one's own eyes,' indicating firsthand witness.
    • 法苑珠林六十五 - Reference to the 65th volume of the 'Flower Garden of the Dharma Treasury,' a comprehensive collection of Buddhist stories and teachings.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋张崇，京兆杜陵人也 - Refers to Zhang Chong, a person from Duling, Jingzhao (present-day Xi'an, Shaanxi Province), during the Jin dynasty.
    • 晋太元中 - Refers to the Taiyuan period (376-396) of the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 符坚既败 - Refers to the defeat of Fu Jian, the ruler of the Former Qin dynasty, by the Eastern Jin in the Battle of Fei River in 383.
    • 长安百姓有千余家，南走归晋 - Describes the mass migration of people from Chang'an (present-day Xi'an) to the south to rejoin the Eastern Jin dynasty after the fall of the Former Qin.
    • 镇戍所拘，谓为游寇 - Describes the treatment of the refugees by local military forces, who considered them bandits.
    • 杀其男丁，虏其子女 - Describes the brutal treatment of the refugees by the local garrison.
    • 洁心专念观世音 - Refers to the practice of devoutly praying to the Bodhisattva Guanyin, a central figure in Mahayana Buddhism known for compassion and mercy.
    • 法苑珠林六十五 - Refers to the 65th volume of the 'Flower Garden of the Dharma Treasury,' a significant Buddhist text compiled by Daoxuan in the Tang dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 张崇 - Zhang Chong, a historical figure from the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 京兆杜陵 - Duling, a district in Jingzhao (present-day Xi'an, Shaanxi Province).
    • 晋 - The Jin dynasty, specifically the Eastern Jin.
    • 太元 - The Taiyuan period (376-396) of the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 符坚 - Fu Jian, the ruler of the Former Qin dynasty.
    • 长安 - Chang'an, the capital of the Former Qin and later the Eastern Jin.
    • 观世音 - Guanyin, the Bodhisattva of compassion in Mahayana Buddhism.
    • 法苑珠林六十五 - The 65th volume of the 'Flower Garden of the Dharma Treasury,' a significant Buddhist text compiled by Daoxuan in the Tang dynasty.

    Zhang Chong of Jin, a native of Duling, Jingzhao. When he was young, he was devoted to the law. During the Taiyuan period of the Eastern Jin, after Fu Jian was defeated, thousands of families from Chang'an fled south to return to the Jin dynasty. They were detained by the local garrison and considered as bandits. The men were killed, and the women and children were captured. Zhang Chong and four others were chained together by hands and feet, neck and body dug into pits, and buried up to the waist, each separated by twenty steps. The next day, they were to be chased and shot with arrows for entertainment. Zhang Chong, feeling desperate, focused solely on the Bodhisattva Guanyin. At night, the chains suddenly broke, allowing them to free themselves. They then escaped and managed to escape. Zhang Chong, now in pain from his feet, continued on the road with his companions, passing by a temple. There, he again called out the name of the Bodhisattva Guanyin, prostrated himself with sincere devotion, placed a stone in front of him, and made a vow, saying that he now intended to cross to the east of the river, to appeal to the Jin emperor about the chaos, to seek justice for these wronged spirits, and to save his wife and children. If his wish was granted, the stone would split into two. After Zhang Chong finished his prostration, the stone indeed split. Zhang Chong then arrived in the capital, reported to the White Tiger Hall, and detailed the list of wronged individuals. The emperor then pardoned them all. Those who had been sold as slaves were registered as commoners. The monk Zhi Sheng, who witnessed this with his own eyes, recorded it in the 65th volume of the "Flower Garden of the Dharma Treasury." [60]

    晋王懿，字仲德，太原人也。守车骑将军。世信奉法。父苗，符坚时为中山太守，为丁零所害。仲德与兄元德，携母南归。登陟峭崄，饥疲绝粮。无复余计，惟归心三宝。忽见一童子，牵青牛，见懿等饥，各乞一饭。因忽不见。时积雨大水，懿前望浩然，不知何处为浅，可得揭蹑。俄有一白狼，旋绕其前，过水而反，似若引导。如此者三。于是逐狼而渡，水才至膝。俄得陆路，南归晋帝。后自五兵尚书，为徐州刺史。尝欲设斋：宿昔洒扫，敷陈香华，盛列经像。忽闻法堂有经呗声，清婉流畅。懿遽往观：见有五沙门在佛坐前，威容伟异，神仪秀出。懿知非凡僧，心甚欢敬。沙门回相瞻?，意若依然。音旨未交，忽而竦身飞空而去。亲表宾僚，见者甚众。咸悉欣跃，倍增信悟。珠林六十五 [61]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [61]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 晋王懿，字仲德 - Standard biographical formula introducing a person's name and courtesy name.
    • 守车骑将军 - Indicates a military title, specifically 'Guardian of the Cavalry General.'
    • 世信奉法 - Means 'the family has always believed in and followed the law,' implying a tradition of legalism or Confucian governance.
    • 携母南归 - Means 'took his mother southward,' referring to a journey or migration.
    • 登陟峭崄 - Literally 'ascended steep and dangerous terrain,' used metaphorically for overcoming hardships.
    • 饥疲绝粮 - Means 'starving, exhausted, and without food.'
    • 归心三宝 - Refers to dedicating oneself to the Three Jewels (Buddha, Dharma, Sangha) of Buddhism.
    • 忽见一童子，牵青牛 - Narrative detail describing a sudden appearance of a boy leading a green ox.
    • 乞一饭 - Means 'asked for a single meal.'
    • 积雨大水 - Refers to heavy rain and flooding.
    • 不知何处为浅，可得揭蹑 - Means 'did not know where the shallow part was, where one could wade across.'
    • 旋绕其前 - Means 'circled in front of him.'
    • 过水而反 - Means 'crossed the water and returned.'
    • 似若引导 - Means 'as if guiding.'
    • 如此者三 - Means 'this happened three times.'
    • 逐狼而渡 - Means 'followed the wolf and crossed.'
    • 水才至膝 - Means 'the water only reached the knees.'
    • 俄得陆路 - Means 'soon found a land route.'
    • 南归晋帝 - Means 'returned south to the Jin Emperor.'
    • 自五兵尚书，为徐州刺史 - Indicates a career progression from a military position to a civil administrative role.
    • 尝欲设斋 - Means 'once intended to set up a vegetarian meal (for a religious ceremony).'
    • 宿昔洒扫，敷陈香华，盛列经像 - Describes preparations for the ceremony, including cleaning, arranging incense and flowers, and displaying scriptures and images.
    • 忽闻法堂有经呗声，清婉流畅 - Means 'suddenly heard chanting in the lecture hall, clear, graceful, and flowing.'
    • 遽往观 - Means 'hurriedly went to see.'
    • 威容伟异，神仪秀出 - Describes the monks as having majestic and extraordinary appearance and demeanor.
    • 懿知非凡僧，心甚欢敬 - Means 'Yi knew they were not ordinary monks and was very happy and respectful.'
    • 沙门回相瞻?，意若依然 - Means 'the monks looked back at each other, as if they recognized something.'
    • 音旨未交，忽而竦身飞空而去 - Means 'before they could exchange words, they suddenly rose up and flew away into the sky.'
    • 亲表宾僚，见者甚众 - Means 'relatives, friends, and guests, many of whom witnessed this.'
    • 咸悉欣跃，倍增信悟 - Means 'all were delighted and greatly increased their faith and understanding.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋王懿 - Refers to Yi, a historical figure during the Jin Dynasty.
    • 符坚 - Refers to Fu Jian, the ruler of the Former Qin Dynasty.
    • 中山太守 - Refers to the governor of Zhongshan, a region in northern China.
    • 丁零 - Refers to the Tiele, a nomadic group that invaded and caused harm to the region.
    • 三宝 - Refers to the Three Jewels of Buddhism: Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha.
    • 法堂 - Refers to a lecture hall in a Buddhist temple.
    • 经呗声 - Refers to the chanting of Buddhist scriptures.
    • 珠林六十五 - Refers to the 'Jeweled Forest' (a collection of Buddhist stories), specifically Chapter 65.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • The sentence '登陟峭崄，饥疲绝粮' describes both physical and emotional states, which must be clearly conveyed in English.
    • The sequence of events in '忽见一童子，牵青牛，见懿等饥，各乞一饭' must be clearly separated to avoid confusion.
    • The repeated actions of the wolf in '俄有一白狼，旋绕其前，过水而反，似若引导' must be clearly described to maintain the narrative flow.
    • The phrase '如此者三' indicates repetition and must be translated to reflect the number of occurrences.
    • The description of the sound in '忽闻法堂有经呗声，清婉流畅' must be rendered to capture its quality and effect.
    • The sudden action of the monks flying away in '音旨未交，忽而竦身飞空而去' must be translated to convey the miraculous nature of the event.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋王懿 - Yi, a historical figure.
    • 符坚 - Fu Jian, the ruler of the Former Qin Dynasty.
    • 中山 - Zhongshan, a region in northern China.
    • 丁零 - The Tiele, a nomadic group.
    • 三宝 - The Three Jewels of Buddhism.
    • 法堂 - A lecture hall in a Buddhist temple.
    • 珠林六十五 - Chapter 65 of the 'Jeweled Forest.'

    晋王懿，字仲德，太原人也。守车骑将军。世信奉法。父苗，符坚时为中山太守，为丁零所害。仲德与兄元德，携母南归。登陟峭崄，饥疲绝粮。无复余计，惟归心三宝。忽见一童子，牵青牛，见懿等饥，各乞一饭。因忽不见。时积雨大水，懿前望浩然，不知何处为浅，可得揭蹑。俄有一白狼，旋绕其前，过水而反，似若引导。如此者三。于是逐狼而渡，水才至膝。俄得陆路，南归晋帝。后自五兵尚书，为徐州刺史。尝欲设斋：宿昔洒扫，敷陈香华，盛列经像。忽闻法堂有经呗声，清婉流畅。懿遽往观：见有五沙门在佛坐前，威容伟异，神仪秀出。懿知非凡僧，心甚欢敬。沙门回相瞻?，意若依然。音旨未交，忽而竦身飞空而去。亲表宾僚，见者甚众。咸悉欣跃，倍增信悟。珠林六十五

    Final Translation:

    Jin Wang Yi, courtesy name Zhongde, was from Taiyuan. He served as the Guardian of the Cavalry General. His family had always believed in and followed the law. His father, Miao, was the governor of Zhongshan during the reign of Fu Jian, but was killed by the Tiele. Zhongde and his elder brother Yuande took their mother southward. They ascended steep and dangerous terrain, starving, exhausted, and without food. With no other options, they dedicated themselves to the Three Jewels of Buddhism. Suddenly, they saw a boy leading a green ox, who saw their hunger and asked for a single meal for each of them. The boy then vanished. At that time, there was heavy rain and flooding. Yi looked ahead and saw the vast waters, unsure where the shallow part was where one could wade across. Soon, a white wolf circled in front of him, crossed the water, and returned, as if guiding them. This happened three times. Following the wolf, they crossed the water, which only reached their knees. Soon, they found a land route and returned south to the Jin Emperor. Later, he served as a military official and became the governor of Xuzhou. Once, he intended to set up a vegetarian meal: he cleaned and prepared the space, arranged incense and flowers, and displayed scriptures and images. Suddenly, he heard chanting in the lecture hall, clear, graceful, and flowing. Yi hurried to see and found five monks before the Buddha's seat, with majestic and extraordinary appearance and demeanor. Yi knew they were not ordinary monks and was very happy and respectful. The monks looked back at each other, as if they recognized something. Before they could exchange words, they suddenly rose up and flew away into the sky. Relatives, friends, and guests, many of whom witnessed this, were all delighted and greatly increased their faith and understanding. Chapter 65 of the "Jeweled Forest." [61]

    晋程道惠，字文和，武昌人也。世奉五升米道，不信有佛。常云：“古来正道，莫踰李老。何乃信惑胡言，以为胜教。”太元十五年，病死。心下尚暖，家不殡殓。数日得稣。说初死时，见十许人缚录将去。逢一比丘，云此人宿福，未可缚也。乃解其缚，散驱而去。道路修平，而两边棘刺森然，略不容足。驱诸罪人，驰走其中。肉随着刺，号呻聒耳。见惠行在平路，皆叹羡曰：“佛弟子行路，复胜人也？”惠曰：“我不奉法。”其人笑曰：“君忘之耳。 [62]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [62]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 莫踰李老 - Means 'no other philosophy surpasses Daoism,' emphasizing the supremacy of Daoism.
    • 胡言 - Refers to foreign or non-Chinese ideas, often with a negative connotation, here referring to Buddhist teachings.
    • 未可缚也 - Means 'cannot be bound,' indicating that the person has not yet reached a stage where they can be judged or punished.
    • 散驱而去 - Means 'scattered and driven away,' suggesting that the sinners were sent away without further punishment.
    • 肉随着刺 - Literally 'flesh sticking to thorns,' metaphorically describing the painful experience of the sinners.
    • 号呻聒耳 - Means 'cries and groans fill the ears,' emphasizing the distressing sounds of the suffering sinners.
    • 君忘之耳 - Means 'you have just forgotten,' indicating that the person had previously known but now seems to have forgotten something important.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 五升米道 - Refers to a Daoist sect that practiced a ritual involving the consumption of five sheng of rice, symbolizing purity and longevity.
    • 李老 - Laozi, the legendary founder of Daoism, whose teachings are recorded in the *Daodejing*.
    • 佛 - Buddhism, which was introduced to China from India and became a major religion alongside Daoism and Confucianism.
    • 太元十五年 - The 15th year of the Taiyuan era, which corresponds to 390 AD during the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 比丘 - A term for a Buddhist monk, indicating that the person who helped the deceased was a Buddhist practitioner.

    Proper Nouns

    • 程道惠 - The name of the main character.
    • 李老 - Laozi, the founder of Daoism.
    • 太元十五年 - The 15th year of the Taiyuan era (390 AD).
    • 比丘 - A Buddhist monk.

    Jin Cheng Daohui, courtesy name Wenhua, was from Wuchang. His family had long practiced the Five Sheng Daoist sect and did not believe in Buddhism. He often said, "Since ancient times, the true path has never surpassed that of Laozi. Why believe in and be misled by foreign words, thinking they are superior teachings?" In the 15th year of Taiyuan (390 AD), he fell ill and died. His heart was still warm, and his family did not perform the burial rites immediately. After several days, he revived and recounted his experience. He said that when he first died, he saw about ten people binding and leading him away. He encountered a monk who said that this person had accumulated good karma and should not be bound. The monk then released him and sent him away. The road was smooth, but on both sides were dense thorns, barely allowing passage. The sinners were forced to run through this thorny path, their flesh sticking to the thorns, and their cries and groans filled the air. Seeing Daohui walking on a peaceful road, they all admired and said, "The path of the Buddhist disciple is indeed better than ours!" Daohui replied, "I do not follow the Dharma." The monk smiled and said, "You have just forgotten." [62]

    ”惠因自忆先身奉佛，已经五生五死。忘失本志。今生在世，幼遇恶人，未达邪正，乃惑邪道。既至大城，迳进听事。见一人，年可四五十，南面而坐。见惠，惊问曰：“君不应来。”有一人，着单衣帻，持簿书对曰：“此人伐社，杀人罪，应来此。”向所逢比丘亦随惠入，申理甚至。云伐社非罪也。此人宿福甚多，杀人虽重，报未至也。南面坐者曰，可罚所录人，命惠就坐，谢曰：“小鬼谬滥，枉相录来。亦由君忘失宿命，不知奉大正法教也。”将遣惠还，乃使暂兼覆校将军。 [63]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [63]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 惠因自憶先身奉佛 - Hui recalls his past life serving the Buddha.
    • 五生五死 - Metaphor for repeated cycles of rebirth and death in Buddhist contexts.
    • 忘失本志 - Forgetting one's original aspiration or purpose.
    • 幼遇惡人 - Encountering evil people at a young age.
    • 未達邪正 - Not distinguishing between right and wrong.
    • 惑邪道 - Being misled by false teachings.
    • 徑進聽事 - Proceeding directly to the hearing chamber.
    • 南面而坐 - Sitting facing south, a traditional position of authority.
    • 君不應來 - You should not be here, an expression of surprise or disapproval.
    • 着單衣帻 - Wearing a plain robe and headband, describing the attire of a low-ranking official.
    • 持簿書 - Holding records, referring to documents or ledgers.
    • 此人伐社，殺人罪，應來此 - This person cut down a sacred tree and committed murder, so he should be here.
    • 向所逢比丘亦隨惠入 - The monk whom Hui encountered earlier also followed him inside.
    • 申理甚至 - Argued persuasively, indicating a strong defense.
    • 非罪也 - It is not a crime, a legal or moral judgment.
    • 此人宿福甚多 - This person has accumulated much merit in past lives.
    • 報未至也 - The retribution has not yet arrived.
    • 可罰所錄人 - It is possible to punish the person who recorded this.
    • 命惠就坐 - Ordered Hui to sit.
    • 謝曰 - Said in apology.
    • 小鬼謬濫 - The minor demon made a mistake.
    • 枉相錄來 - Wrongfully brought here.
    • 亦由君忘失宿命 - Also because you forgot your past life.
    • 不知奉大正法教也 - Did not know to follow the great orthodox teachings.
    • 將遣惠還 - Was about to send Hui back.
    • 乃使暫兼覆校將軍 - Was then appointed temporarily as a deputy inspector general.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 先身奉佛 - Buddhist concept of a previous life dedicated to serving the Buddha.
    • 五生五死 - Metaphor for the cyclical nature of rebirth and death in Buddhist cosmology.
    • 忘失本志 - Common Buddhist phrase indicating a loss of spiritual resolve.
    • 幼遇惡人 - Reflects the influence of negative external forces on a person's moral development.
    • 未達邪正 - Philosophical or moral dilemma, where one cannot distinguish between right and wrong.
    • 惑邪道 - Reference to being led astray by false or harmful teachings.
    • 徑進聽事 - Legal or administrative term, indicating a place where official business is conducted.
    • 南面而坐 - Traditional Chinese custom, where the south-facing position symbolizes authority.
    • 君不應來 - Formal expression of surprise or disapproval in legal or administrative contexts.
    • 着單衣帻 - Describes the attire of a low-ranking official or attendant.
    • 持簿書 - Refers to the records or documents used in legal or administrative proceedings.
    • 此人伐社，殺人罪，應來此 - Act of cutting down a sacred tree, considered a serious offense in ancient China.
    • 向所逢比丘亦隨惠入 - Reference to a Buddhist monk, indicating the presence of religious figures.
    • 申理甚至 - Legal or moral argument, indicating a persuasive defense.
    • 非罪也 - Legal or moral judgment, indicating that an action is not considered a crime.
    • 此人宿福甚多 - Buddhist concept of accumulated good karma from past lives.
    • 報未至也 - Buddhist concept of consequences that have not yet manifested.
    • 可罰所錄人 - Legal or administrative term, indicating the possibility of punishing the recorder.
    • 命惠就坐 - Formal command, indicating that Hui was ordered to sit.
    • 謝曰 - Formal expression of apology or gratitude.
    • 小鬼謬濫 - Reference to a low-ranking spirit or demon in Chinese folklore.
    • 枉相錄來 - Indicates that Hui was wrongfully summoned.
    • 亦由君忘失宿命 - Buddhist concept, where forgetting one's past life can lead to confusion.
    • 不知奉大正法教也 - Reference to the true and correct Buddhist doctrine.
    • 將遣惠還 - Indicates the intention to release Hui.
    • 乃使暫兼覆校將軍 - Reference to a high-ranking military official, indicating a promotion.

    Proper Nouns

    • 惠 - Name of the protagonist, Hui.
    • 比丘 - Buddhist monk.
    • 大城 - Large city, likely referring to a significant urban center in ancient China.
    • 覆校將軍 - High-ranking military official responsible for oversight.

    Hui recalled his past life serving the Buddha, having undergone five cycles of rebirth and death. He had forgotten his original aspiration. In this life, at a young age, he encountered evil people, failed to distinguish between right and wrong, and was misled by false teachings. Upon arriving in a large city, he proceeded directly to the hearing chamber. There, he saw a man, around forty or fifty years old, sitting facing south. The man was surprised and asked, “You should not be here.” Another person, wearing a plain robe and headband, holding records, replied, “This person cut down a sacred tree and committed murder, so he should be here.” The monk whom Hui had encountered earlier also followed him inside and argued persuasively. He claimed that cutting down the tree was not a crime. This person had accumulated much merit in past lives, and though the act of murder was grave, the retribution had not yet arrived. The man sitting facing south said, “It is possible to punish the person who recorded this,” and ordered Hui to sit. He then apologized, saying, “The minor demon made a mistake, wrongfully bringing you here. It is also because you forgot your past life, not knowing to follow the great orthodox teachings.” He was about to send Hui back, and was then appointed temporarily as a deputy inspector general. [63]

    历观地狱。惠欣然辞出。导从而行。行至诸城，城城皆是地狱。人众巨亿，悉受罪报。见有掣狗，啮人百节，肌肉散落，流血蔽地。又有群鸟，其喙如锋，飞来甚速，鸩然血至，入人口中，表里贯洞；其人宛转呼叫，筋骨碎落。其余经见，与赵泰屑荷，大抵粗同，不复具载。唯此二条为异，故详记之。观历既遍，乃遣惠还。复见向所逢比丘，与惠一铜物，形如小铃，曰：“君还至家，可弃此门外，勿以入室。某年月日，君当有厄。诫慎过此，寿延九十。 [64]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [64]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 历观 - Classical phrase meaning 'to observe or view sequentially'; used here to describe the journey through the hells.
    • 惠欣然辞出 - 'Hui happily declined and left,' where '欣然' indicates a cheerful or willing departure.
    • 导从而行 - 'Guided and followed,' suggesting that the guide led Hui through the hells.
    • 掣狗 - 'Tugging dogs' or 'pulling dogs,' which likely refers to dogs pulling or dragging people, causing harm.
    • 肌肉散落，流血蔽地 - 'Muscles scattered, blood covering the ground,' a vivid description of the gruesome scene.
    • 群鸟，其喙如锋 - 'A flock of birds, their beaks like blades,' indicating the birds' sharp and dangerous beaks.
    • 鸩然血至，入人口中，表里贯洞 - 'Blood flows in, like poison, into people's mouths, from inside out, through and through,' a detailed description of the birds' actions.
    • 其人宛转呼叫，筋骨碎落 - 'The people twist and call out, bones and tendons broken and falling,' a vivid description of the victims' suffering.
    • 其余经见，与赵泰屑荷，大抵粗同，不复具载 - 'Other things seen, compared to Zhao Tai's experiences, generally similar, not fully recorded,' a summary of additional scenes.
    • 唯此二条为异，故详记之 - 'Only these two are different, therefore, they are recorded in detail,' highlighting the unique aspects.
    • 观历既遍，乃遣惠还 - 'After observing all the hells, then sent Hui back,' indicating the completion of the tour.
    • 复见向所逢比丘，与惠一铜物，形如小铃 - 'Again saw the monk he had met before, gave Hui a copper object, shaped like a small bell,' a description of the encounter and the gift.
    • 君还至家，可弃此门外，勿以入室 - 'When you return home, you can throw this outside the door, do not bring it into the house,' providing instructions.
    • 某年月日，君当有厄 - 'On a certain day in the year, you will face danger,' indicating a warning.
    • 诫慎过此，寿延九十 - 'Be cautious beyond this point, your life will extend to ninety years,' providing a caution and a promise of longevity.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 地狱 - 'Hells,' referring to the Buddhist concept of hells where sinners are punished.
    • 赵泰 - A historical figure whose experiences in the hells are mentioned for comparison.
    • 比丘 - 'Monk,' a term for a male Buddhist monk.
    • 铜物 - 'Copper object,' a gift from the monk.
    • 小铃 - 'Small bell,' a specific type of object given to Hui.
    • 厄 - 'Danger' or 'misfortune,' indicating a future event.
    • 诫慎 - 'Be cautious,' a warning to take care.
    • 寿延九十 - 'Life extending to ninety years,' indicating a promise of longevity.

    Proper Nouns

    • 惠 - The name of the person who observes the hells.
    • 赵泰 - A historical figure whose experiences in the hells are mentioned for comparison.
    • 比丘 - 'Monk,' a term for a male Buddhist monk.
    • 铜物 - 'Copper object,' a gift from the monk.
    • 小铃 - 'Small bell,' a specific type of object given to Hui.
    • 厄 - 'Danger' or 'misfortune,' indicating a future event.
    • 诫慎 - 'Be cautious,' a warning to take care.
    • 寿延九十 - 'Life extending to ninety years,' indicating a promise of longevity.

    After observing the hells, Hui happily declined and left, guided and followed. They traveled through various cities, each of which was a hell. There were countless people, all suffering punishments. They saw tugging dogs, biting people's joints, causing muscles to scatter and blood to cover the ground. There were also flocks of birds with beaks like blades, flying swiftly, their blood flowing into people's mouths, from inside out, through and through. The people twisted and called out, their bones and tendons broken and falling. Other things seen were generally similar to Zhao Tai's experiences, not fully recorded. Only these two scenes were different, so they are recorded in detail. After observing all the hells, Hui was then sent back. Again, he saw the monk he had met before, who gave him a copper object shaped like a small bell. The monk said, "When you return home, you can throw this outside the door, do not bring it into the house. On a certain day in the year, you will face danger. Be cautious beyond this point, your life will extend to ninety years." [64]

    ”时道惠家于京师大街南，自见来还。达皂荚桥，见亲表三人，住车共语，悼惠之亡。至门，见婢行哭而市。彼人及婢，咸弗见也。惠将入门，置向铜物门外树上，光明舒散，流飞属天。良久还小，奄尔而灭。至户，闻尸臭惆怅恶之。时宾亲奔吊，突惠者多，不得徘徊。因进入尸，忽然而稣。说所逢车人及市婢，咸皆符同。惠后为廷尉，预西堂听讼，未及就列，欻然烦闷，不识人，半日乃愈。计其时日，即道人所戒之期。顷之，迁为广州刺史。元嘉六年卒，六十九矣。珠林五十五 [65]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [65]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 時道惠家於京師大街南 - "時" indicates a specific time or occasion; "家於" means to reside or live in; "京師大街南" refers to a location in the southern part of the capital city's main street.
    • 達皂荚桥 - "達" means to reach or arrive at; "皂荚桥" is a proper noun referring to a bridge named after the soapberry tree.
    • 見親表三人，住車共语，悼惠之亡 - "見" means to meet or encounter; "親表" refers to relatives or close acquaintances; "住車共语" means to stop the carriage and talk together; "悼惠之亡" means to mourn the death of Dao Hui.
    • 至門，見婢行哭而市 - "至門" means to reach the door; "見婢行哭而市" means to see a maid walking and crying while going to market.
    • 彼人及婢，咸弗見也 - "彼人及婢" means those people and the maid; "咸弗見也" means they were not seen by anyone.
    • 惠將入门，置向铜物门外树上，光明舒散，流飛属天 - "置向铜物门外树上" means to place copper objects outside the door on the tree; "光明舒散，流飛属天" describes a scene where light spreads and rises into the sky.
    • 良久還小，奄爾而滅 - "良久還小" means after a long time it becomes smaller; "奄爾而滅" means it suddenly disappears.
    • 至戶，聞尸臭惆怅惡之 - "至戶" means to reach the door; "聞尸臭" means to smell the corpse's stench; "惆怅惡之" means to feel sad and disgusted.
    • 時賓親奔吊，突惠者多，不得徘徊 - "時賓親奔吊" means guests and relatives came to mourn; "突惠者多" means many people rushed to Dao Hui; "不得徘徊" means he couldn't linger.
    • 因進入尸，忽然而蘇 - "因進入尸" means because he entered the room with the body; "忽然而蘇" means he suddenly revived.
    • 說所逢車人及市婢，咸皆符同 - "說所逢車人及市婢" means he described the people he met in the carriage and the maid at the market; "咸皆符同" means they all matched what he had seen.
    • 惠後為廷尉，預西堂聽訟，未及就列，欻然煩悶，不識人，半日乃愈 - "預西堂聽訟" means he participated in hearing cases in the west hall; "未及就列" means he had not yet taken his place; "欻然煩悶，不識人" means he suddenly felt dizzy and disoriented; "半日乃愈" means he recovered after half a day.
    • 計其時日，即道人所戒之期 - "計其時日" means calculating the time; "即道人所戒之期" means it was the period Dao Hui had been warned about.
    • 頃之，遷為廣州刺史 - "頃之" means shortly afterward; "遷為廣州刺史" means he was promoted to the position of Governor of Guangzhou.
    • 元嘉六年卒，六十九矣 - "元嘉六年" means the sixth year of the Yüan Jia era; "卒" means he died; "六十九矣" means he was sixty-nine years old.
    • 珠林五十五 - "珠林五十五" refers to a specific volume or chapter in a collection of stories or records.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 京師大街南 - Refers to a location in the southern part of the capital city, likely referring to the ancient Chinese capital.
    • 皂荚桥 - A proper noun referring to a bridge named after the soapberry tree, which may have historical or cultural significance.
    • 廷尉 - An official title in ancient China, equivalent to a judge or prosecutor.
    • 西堂 - Refers to a hall or room in a government building where legal cases were heard.
    • 元嘉 - The reign title of Emperor Wen of Liu Song, indicating the historical period in which the events took place.
    • 珠林 - Likely refers to a collection of stories or records, possibly a literary or historical text.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • The sentence is elliptical and requires the reader to infer that Dao Hui had left and returned.
    • The sequence of actions (reaching the bridge, meeting relatives, stopping the carriage, talking, and mourning) is compressed and must be read as a continuous narrative.
    • The sentence is elliptical and requires the reader to infer that the maid was seen by Dao Hui but not by others.
    • The description of the copper objects and the light spreading into the sky is metaphorical and requires careful interpretation.
    • The sentence is elliptical and requires the reader to infer that the light gradually diminished and disappeared.
    • The sentence is elliptical and requires the reader to infer that Dao Hui felt disgust upon entering the room.
    • The sentence is elliptical and requires the reader to infer that Dao Hui was overwhelmed by the crowd.
    • The sentence is elliptical and requires the reader to infer that Dao Hui revived after entering the room with the body.
    • The sentence is elliptical and requires the reader to infer that Dao Hui's description matched the reality.
