    • The sentence is elliptical and requires the reader to infer that Dao Hui experienced a sudden illness during his duties.
    • The sentence is elliptical and requires the reader to infer that the timing of the event matched a previous warning.
    • The sentence is elliptical and requires the reader to infer that Dao Hui was promoted shortly after the event.
    • The sentence is elliptical and requires the reader to infer that Dao Hui died at the age of sixty-nine during the sixth year of the Yüan Jia era.

    Proper Nouns

    • 道惠 - The name of the main character, Dao Hui.
    • 京師 - The ancient Chinese capital city.
    • 皂荚桥 - A proper noun referring to a bridge named after the soapberry tree.
    • 廷尉 - An official title in ancient China.
    • 西堂 - A hall or room in a government building.
    • 元嘉 - The reign title of Emperor Wen of Liu Song.
    • 珠林 - Likely refers to a collection of stories or records.

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    Dao Hui once lived in the southern part of the capital city's main street. One day, he returned home and reached the soapberry tree bridge. There, he encountered three relatives who stopped their carriage to talk and mourn his death. When he arrived at the door, he saw a maid walking and crying while going to market. Neither the people nor the maid were seen by anyone else. As Dao Hui was about to enter, he placed copper objects outside the door on a tree. The light spread and rose into the sky, gradually becoming smaller and suddenly disappearing. When he reached the door, he smelled the stench of the corpse and felt sad and disgusted. At that time, guests and relatives came to mourn, and many people rushed to Dao Hui, preventing him from lingering. Because he entered the room with the body, he suddenly revived. He described the people he met in the carriage and the maid at the market, and they all matched what he had seen. Later, Dao Hui became a prosecutor and participated in hearing cases in the west hall. Before he could take his place, he suddenly felt dizzy and disoriented, not recognizing anyone, and recovered after half a day. Calculating the time, it was the period Dao Hui had been warned about. Shortly afterward, he was promoted to the position of Governor of Guangzhou. He died in the sixth year of the Yüan Jia era, at the age of sixty-nine. This story is recorded in Zhu Lin Volume 55. [65]

    晋沙门慧达，姓刘名萨荷，西河离石人也。未出家时：长于军旅；不闻佛法；尚气武；好畋猎。年三十一，暴病而死。体尚温柔。家未殓。至七日而稣。说云将尽之时，见有两人执缚将去。向西北行。行路转高，稍得平衢。两边列树。见有一人，执弓带剑，当衢而立。指语两人，将荷西行。见屋舍甚多。白壁赤柱。荷入一家，有女子美容服。荷就乞食。空中声言，勿与之也。有人从地踊出，执铁杵，将欲击之。荷遽走，历入十许家皆然，遂无所得。复西北行，见一妪乘车，与荷一卷书。荷受之。西至一家，馆宇华整。有妪坐于户外，口中虎牙。屋内床帐光丽，竹席青几。复有女子处之。问荷，“得书来不？”荷以书卷与之。女取余书比之。俄见两沙门，谓荷，“汝识我不？”荷答：“不识。”沙门曰：“今宜归命释迦文佛。”荷如言发念，因随沙门俱行。遥见一城，类长安城，而色甚黑，盖铁城也。见人身甚长大肤黑如漆，头发曳地。 [66]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [66]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 晋沙门慧达 - Title and name, '晋' (Jin) refers to the Jin Dynasty, '沙门' (sāmén) means Buddhist monk, and '慧达' (Huìdá) is the name of the monk.
    • 姓刘名萨荷 - Formal way of stating that the monk's surname is Liu and his given name is Sàhé.
    • 长于军旅 - Literally 'long in military service,' meaning he was experienced in military affairs.
    • 不闻佛法 - 'Not having heard of Buddhist teachings.'
    • 尚气武 - 'Valuing martial spirit,' indicating a preference for martial virtues.
    • 好畋猎 - 'Loving hunting,' showing a fondness for hunting.
    • 暴病而死 - 'Dying suddenly from an illness.'
    • 体尚温柔 - 'Body still warm,' indicating that the body had not yet cooled down.
    • 家未殓 - 'Family had not yet performed the burial rites.'
    • 至七日而稣 - 'Revived after seven days,' indicating a miraculous recovery.
    • 见有两人执缚将去 - 'Saw two people binding and taking him away,' suggesting a supernatural encounter.
    • 向西北行 - 'Heading northwest,' indicating a specific direction.
    • 行路转高，稍得平衢 - 'The path became higher, gradually reaching a flat road,' describing the terrain.
    • 两边列树 - 'Trees lined both sides,' indicating a well-ordered path.
    • 执弓带剑 - 'Holding a bow and wearing a sword,' describing a warrior or guardian.
    • 当衢而立 - 'Standing in the middle of the road,' indicating a central position.
    • 指语两人 - 'Pointing to two people,' suggesting a gesture or command.
    • 将荷西行 - 'Taking Huìdá to the west,' indicating a direction.
    • 屋舍甚多 - 'Many houses,' indicating a large number of buildings.
    • 白壁赤柱 - 'White walls and red columns,' describing the architecture.
    • 入一家 - 'Entering a house,' indicating a specific location.
    • 美容服 - 'Beautifully dressed,' describing the appearance of the woman.
    • 就乞食 - 'Asking for food,' indicating a request for sustenance.
    • 空中声言 - 'A voice from the air said,' indicating a supernatural communication.
    • 勿与之也 - 'Do not give it to him,' a command.
    • 有人从地踊出 - 'Someone emerged from the ground,' indicating a supernatural event.
    • 执铁杵 - 'Holding an iron pestle,' describing a tool or weapon.
    • 将欲击之 - 'About to strike him,' indicating an imminent threat.
    • 遽走 - 'Ran quickly,' indicating a sudden action.
    • 历入十许家 - 'Entered ten or so houses,' indicating a series of locations.
    • 遂无所得 - 'Finally got nothing,' indicating a lack of success.
    • 见一妪乘车 - 'Saw an old woman riding in a carriage,' indicating a specific encounter.
    • 与荷一卷书 - 'Gave Huìdá a scroll,' indicating a gift or message.
    • 西至一家 - 'Reached a house to the west,' indicating a specific location.
    • 馆宇华整 - 'Elegant and well-ordered buildings,' describing the architecture.
    • 坐于户外 - 'Sitting outside the house,' indicating a specific location.
    • 口中虎牙 - 'Having tiger teeth in her mouth,' indicating a supernatural or mythical feature.
    • 床帐光丽 - 'Beautifully decorated beds and curtains,' describing the interior.
    • 竹席青几 - 'Bamboo mat and green table,' describing the furniture.
    • 复有女子处之 - 'There was another woman there,' indicating a specific location.
    • 问荷，得书来不 - 'Asked Huìdá, 'Did you receive the book?',' indicating a question.
    • 荷以书卷与之 - 'Huìdá gave the scroll to her,' indicating an action.
    • 女取余书比之 - 'The woman took the remaining books and compared them,' indicating an action.
    • 俄见两沙门 - 'Soon saw two monks,' indicating a specific encounter.
    • 谓荷，汝识我不 - 'Said to Huìdá, 'Do you recognize us?',' indicating a question.
    • 荷答，不识 - 'Huìdá replied, 'I do not recognize you',' indicating a response.
    • 今宜归命释迦文佛 - 'Now it is appropriate to devote oneself to the Buddha Śākyamuni,' indicating a religious instruction.
    • 荷如言发念 - 'Huìdá followed the words and made a vow,' indicating an action.
    • 因随沙门俱行 - 'Followed the monks together,' indicating an action.
    • 遥见一城 - 'Saw a city in the distance,' indicating a specific location.
    • 类长安城 - 'Similar to the city of Chang'an,' indicating a comparison.
    • 而色甚黑 - 'But the color was very dark,' indicating a description.
    • 盖铁城也 - 'It was a city of iron,' indicating a specific type of city.
    • 见人身甚长大肤黑如漆 - 'Saw people with very tall and large bodies, their skin black like lacquer,' indicating a description.
    • 头发曳地 - 'Hair dragging on the ground,' indicating a specific physical feature.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋沙门慧达 - The monk Huìdá, who lived during the Jin Dynasty.
    • 姓刘名萨荷 - The monk's full name, Liu Sàhé.
    • 长于军旅 - Indicates that the monk had experience in military service, which was common for monks during the period.
    • 不闻佛法 - Suggests that the monk had not yet been exposed to Buddhist teachings.
    • 尚气武 - Reflects the cultural value placed on martial virtues in the Jin Dynasty.
    • 好畋猎 - Hunting was a popular pastime among the elite during the Jin Dynasty.
    • 暴病而死 - A common theme in Buddhist texts, where sudden death is often a turning point for spiritual transformation.
    • 体尚温柔 - Indicates that the body had not yet cooled down, suggesting a recent death.
    • 家未殓 - The family had not yet performed the burial rites, which was a significant social and religious obligation.
    • 至七日而稣 - Reviving after seven days is a miraculous event, often associated with divine intervention.
    • 见有两人执缚将去 - Suggests a supernatural encounter, possibly a test or a journey to the afterlife.
    • 向西北行 - The direction northwest may have symbolic significance in Buddhist cosmology.
    • 行路转高，稍得平衢 - Describes a journey through a well-ordered path, possibly symbolizing a spiritual journey.
    • 两边列树 - Trees lining both sides may symbolize a path of righteousness or a journey through a sacred space.
    • 执弓带剑 - A warrior or guardian figure, possibly a protector of the spiritual realm.
    • 当衢而立 - Standing in the middle of the road may symbolize a central role or a pivotal moment.
    • 指语两人 - A gesture or command, possibly guiding the monk on his journey.
    • 将荷西行 - Taking the monk to the west, which may symbolize a journey toward enlightenment.
    • 屋舍甚多 - Many houses may symbolize a bustling or populated spiritual realm.
    • 白壁赤柱 - White walls and red columns are common in traditional Chinese architecture, symbolizing purity and strength.
    • 入一家 - Entering a house may symbolize entering a new phase or realm.
    • 美容服 - Beautifully dressed may symbolize a state of grace or purity.
    • 就乞食 - Asking for food may symbolize a humble or needy state.
    • 空中声言 - A voice from the air may symbolize divine guidance or communication.
    • 勿与之也 - Do not give it to him, may be a test of the monk's virtue or a command from a higher power.
    • 有人从地踊出 - Emerging from the ground may symbolize a supernatural or divine appearance.
    • 执铁杵 - An iron pestle may symbolize a tool of transformation or a weapon.
    • 将欲击之 - About to strike him, may symbolize a test of the monk's resolve or a threat to his safety.
    • 遽走 - Running quickly may symbolize a sudden realization or a need to escape.
    • 历入十许家 - Entering ten or so houses may symbolize a series of trials or experiences.
    • 遂无所得 - Finally getting nothing may symbolize a lack of material gain or a need for spiritual growth.
    • 见一妪乘车 - Seeing an old woman riding in a carriage may symbolize a wise or guiding figure.
    • 与荷一卷书 - Giving the monk a scroll may symbolize a gift or message from a higher power.
    • 西至一家 - Reached a house to the west, which may symbolize a journey toward enlightenment.
    • 馆宇华整 - Elegant and well-ordered buildings may symbolize a well-ordered spiritual realm.
    • 坐于户外 - Sitting outside the house may symbolize a state of openness or exposure.
    • 口中虎牙 - Having tiger teeth in her mouth may symbolize a supernatural or mythical feature.
    • 床帐光丽 - Beautifully decorated beds and curtains may symbolize a luxurious or sacred space.
    • 竹席青几 - Bamboo mat and green table may symbolize a simple or natural setting.
    • 复有女子处之 - There was another woman there, which may symbolize a guide or a helper.
    • 问荷，得书来不 - Asked Huìdá, 'Did you receive the book?', which may symbolize a test or a question of faith.
    • 荷以书卷与之 - Huìdá gave the scroll to her, which may symbolize a transfer of knowledge or a gift.
    • 女取余书比之 - The woman took the remaining books and compared them, which may symbolize a review or a comparison of knowledge.
    • 俄见两沙门 - Soon saw two monks, which may symbolize a meeting with spiritual guides.
    • 谓荷，汝识我不 - Said to Huìdá, 'Do you recognize us?', which may symbolize a test of recognition or a question of identity.
    • 荷答，不识 - Huìdá replied, 'I do not recognize you', which may symbolize a state of ignorance or a lack of recognition.
    • 今宜归命释迦文佛 - Now it is appropriate to devote oneself to the Buddha Śākyamuni, which may symbolize a call to spiritual devotion.
    • 荷如言发念 - Huìdá followed the words and made a vow, which may symbolize a commitment to spiritual practice.
    • 因随沙门俱行 - Followed the monks together, which may symbolize a journey with spiritual guides.
    • 遥见一城 - Saw a city in the distance, which may symbolize a vision of a spiritual realm.
    • 类长安城 - Similar to the city of Chang'an, which may symbolize a comparison to a well-known spiritual or cultural center.
    • 而色甚黑 - But the color was very dark, which may symbolize a state of darkness or ignorance.
    • 盖铁城也 - It was a city of iron, which may symbolize a strong or unyielding spiritual realm.
    • 见人身甚长大肤黑如漆 - Saw people with very tall and large bodies, their skin black like lacquer, which may symbolize a description of a supernatural or divine being.
    • 头发曳地 - Hair dragging on the ground, which may symbolize a state of humility or submission.

    During the Jin Dynasty, there was a Buddhist monk named Huìdá, whose surname was Liu and given name was Sàhé. He was from Líshí, Xīhé. Before he became a monk, he was experienced in military service; he had not yet heard of Buddhist teachings; he valued martial virtues; and he loved hunting. At the age of thirty-one, he suddenly died from an illness. His body was still warm, and his family had not yet performed the burial rites. Seven days later, he revived. He recounted that as he was nearing death, he saw two people binding and taking him away. They headed northwest. The path became higher, gradually reaching a flat road. Trees lined both sides. He saw a person holding a bow and wearing a sword, standing in the middle of the road. The person pointed to two others and took Huìdá to the west. He saw many houses with white walls and red columns. He entered one house where a beautifully dressed woman was. He asked for food. A voice from the air said, "Do not give it to him." Someone emerged from the ground, holding an iron pestle, and was about to strike him. Huìdá ran quickly, entering ten or so houses, but finally got nothing. He then saw an old woman riding in a carriage, who gave him a scroll. He accepted it. He reached a house to the west, with elegant and well-ordered buildings. An old woman was sitting outside the house, with tiger teeth in her mouth. The interior had beautifully decorated beds and curtains, a bamboo mat, and a green table. There was another woman there. She asked Huìdá, "Did you receive the book?" He gave the scroll to her. The woman took the remaining books and compared them. Soon, he saw two monks, who said to Huìdá, "Do you recognize us?" He replied, "I do not recognize you." The monks said, "Now it is appropriate to devote oneself to the Buddha Śākyamuni." Huìdá followed the words and made a vow, and then followed the monks together. He saw a city in the distance, similar to the city of Chang'an, but the color was very dark, and it was a city of iron. He saw people with very tall and large bodies, their skin black like lacquer, and their hair dragging on the ground. [66]

    沙门曰：“此狱中鬼也。”其处甚寒。有冰如席，飞散着人。着头，头断；着脚，脚断。二沙门云：“此寒冰狱也。”荷便识宿命，知两沙门往维卫佛时，并其师也。作沙弥时，以犯俗罪，不得受戒。世虽有佛，竟不得见从。再得人身：一生羌中，今生晋中。又见从伯，在此狱里。谓荷曰：“昔在邺时，不知事佛。见人灌像，聊试学之；而不肯还直。今故受罪。犹有灌福，幸得生天。”次见刀山地狱。次第经历，观见甚多。狱狱异城，不相杂厕。人数如沙，不可称计。楚毒科法，略与经说相符。自荷履践地狱，示有光景。俄而忽见金色，晖明皎然。见人长二丈许，相好严华，体黄金色。左右并曰：观世大士也。皆起迎礼。有二沙门，形质相类，并行而东。荷作礼毕。菩萨具为说法，可千余言，末云：“凡为亡人设福，若父母兄弟，爰至七世，姻媾亲戚，朋友路人，或在精舍，或在家中，亡者受苦，即得免脱。七月望日，沙门受腊；此时设供，弥为胜也。 [67]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [67]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 沙門曰 - Formal expression meaning 'the monk said.'
    • 此獄中鬼也 - Statement identifying the entity as a ghost from the underworld.
    • 其处甚寒 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'the place is very cold.'
    • 有冰如席，飞散着人 - Vivid description of ice shards flying and hitting people.
    • 着头，头断；着脚，脚断 - Hyperbolic expression describing the severe consequences of being hit by the ice.
    • 二沙门云 - Formal expression meaning 'the two monks said.'
    • 此寒冰狱也 - Statement identifying the place as the Cold Ice Hell.
    • 荷便识宿命 - Phrase meaning 'the monk immediately recognized his past life.'
    • 知两沙门往维卫佛时，并其师也 - Statement indicating that the two monks were disciples of the same teacher during the time of the Buddha Vipasyin.
    • 作沙弥时，以犯俗罪，不得受戒 - Statement describing the monk's past as a novice who committed a lay sin and thus could not receive ordination.
    • 世虽有佛，竟不得见从 - Statement indicating that despite the presence of the Buddha, the monk never had the opportunity to follow him.
    • 再得人身：一生羌中，今生晋中 - Statement indicating that the monk has been reborn twice: once in Qiang and now in Jin.
    • 又见从伯，在此狱里 - Statement indicating that the monk sees his uncle in this hell.
    • 谓荷曰 - Formal expression meaning 'the uncle said to the monk.'
    • 昔在邺时，不知事佛 - Statement indicating that the uncle did not know about Buddhism in the past when he was in Ye.
    • 见人灌像，聊试学之 - Statement indicating that the uncle saw someone pouring water over a statue and tried it himself.
    • 而不肯还直 - Statement indicating that the uncle did not return the water.
    • 今故受罪 - Statement indicating that the uncle is now suffering punishment.
    • 犹有灌福，幸得生天 - Statement indicating that the uncle still has the merit from pouring water and will be reborn in heaven.
    • 次见刀山地狱 - Statement indicating that the monk next sees the Blade Mountain Hell.
    • 次第经历，观见甚多 - Statement indicating that the monk experiences various hells in sequence and sees many things.
    • 狱狱异城，不相杂厕 - Statement indicating that each hell is in a different city and does not mix with others.
    • 人数如沙，不可称计 - Statement indicating that the number of people in the hells is as numerous as sand and cannot be counted.
    • 楚毒科法，略与经说相符 - Statement indicating that the punishments and rules in the hells are roughly consistent with what is said in the scriptures.
    • 自荷履践地狱 - Statement indicating that the monk experiences the hells firsthand.
    • 示有光景 - Statement indicating that there is a glimmer of light.
    • 俄而忽见金色，晖明皎然 - Statement indicating that suddenly the monk sees a golden light that is bright and clear.
    • 见人长二丈许，相好严华，体黄金色 - Description of a person who is about two zhang tall, with a beautiful appearance and a golden body.
    • 左右并曰 - Formal expression meaning 'those around him said.'
    • 观世大士也 - Identification of the person as the Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara.
    • 皆起迎礼 - Statement indicating that everyone rises to greet and pay respects.
    • 有二沙门，形质相类，并行而东 - Description of two monks who look similar and walk east together.
    • 菩萨具为说法，可千余言 - Statement indicating that the Bodhisattva gives a detailed explanation, speaking for more than a thousand words.
    • 凡为亡人设福，若父母兄弟，爰至七世，姻媾亲戚，朋友路人 - Statement indicating that setting up merit for the deceased, whether they are parents, siblings, or even friends and passersby, is beneficial.
    • 或在精舍，或在家中 - Statement indicating that the merit can be set up in a temple or at home.
    • 亡者受苦，即得免脱 - Statement indicating that the deceased can be freed from suffering.
    • 七月望日，沙门受腊 - Statement indicating that on the 15th day of the seventh month, monks receive their year-end offerings.
    • 此时设供，弥为胜也 - Statement indicating that setting up offerings at this time is especially meritorious.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 沙门 - Refers to Buddhist monks.
    • 维卫佛 - Refers to the Buddha Vipasyin, one of the twenty-four Buddhas in the past.
    • 邺 - Refers to Ye, an ancient city in China.
    • 羌 - Refers to Qiang, a region in ancient China.
    • 晋 - Refers to Jin, a dynasty in Chinese history.
    • 观世大士 - Refers to the Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara, also known as Guanyin.
    • 七月望日 - Refers to the 15th day of the seventh month in the lunar calendar, a significant date in Buddhist practice.
    • 沙门受腊 - Refers to the ceremony where monks receive their year-end offerings.

    Proper Nouns

    • 沙门 - Buddhist monks.
    • 维卫佛 - Buddha Vipasyin.
    • 邺 - Ancient city in China.
    • 羌 - Region in ancient China.
    • 晋 - Dynasty in Chinese history.
    • 观世大士 - Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara.
    • 七月望日 - 15th day of the seventh month in the lunar calendar.
    • 沙门受腊 - Ceremony where monks receive their year-end offerings.

    The monk said, "This is a ghost from the underworld." The place was extremely cold. There were ice shards as large as mats, flying and scattering onto people. When they hit someone's head, the head would break off; when they hit someone's feet, the feet would break off. The two monks said, "This is the Cold Ice Hell." The monk immediately recognized his past life, knowing that the two monks were disciples of the same teacher during the time of the Buddha Vipasyin. As a novice, he had committed a lay sin and thus could not receive ordination. Despite the presence of the Buddha, he never had the opportunity to follow him. He has been reborn twice: once in Qiang and now in Jin. He also saw his uncle in this hell. The uncle said to the monk, "In the past, when I was in Ye, I did not know about Buddhism. I saw someone pouring water over a statue and tried it myself; but I did not return the water. Now I am suffering punishment. I still have the merit from pouring water and will be reborn in heaven." Next, the monk saw the Blade Mountain Hell. He experienced various hells in sequence, seeing many things. Each hell was in a different city and did not mix with others. The number of people in the hells was as numerous as sand and could not be counted. The punishments and rules in the hells were roughly consistent with what is said in the scriptures. As the monk experienced the hells firsthand, there was a glimmer of light. Suddenly, he saw a golden light that was bright and clear. He saw a person who was about two zhang tall, with a beautiful appearance and a golden body. Those around him said, "This is the Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara." Everyone rose to greet and pay respects. There were two monks who looked similar and walked east together. After the monk paid his respects, the Bodhisattva gave a detailed explanation, speaking for more than a thousand words, concluding, "In setting up merit for the deceased, whether they are parents, siblings, or even friends and passersby, it is beneficial. Whether in a temple or at home, the deceased can be freed from suffering. On the 15th day of the seventh month, when monks receive their year-end offerings, setting up offerings at this time is especially meritorious." [67]

    若制器物，以充供养，器器摽题，言为某人亲奉上三宝，福施弥多，其庆逾速。沙门白衣，见身为过，及宿世之罪，种种恶业，能于众中尽自发露，不失事条，勤诚忏悔者，罪即消灭。如其弱颜羞惭，耻于大众露其过者，可在屏处，默自记说，不失事者，罪亦除灭。若有所遗漏，非故隐蔽，虽不获免，受报稍轻。若不能悔，无惭愧心，此名执过不反，命终之后，克坠地狱。又他造塔及与堂殿，虽复一土一木，若染若碧，率诚供助，获福甚多。 [68]

    If one makes objects to fulfill offerings, each object should be marked, stating that someone personally presents it to the Three Treasures. The merit of giving is even greater, and the blessings come even faster. Monks and laypeople who see their own bodies as faults, as well as the sins of past lives and various evil deeds, can openly confess in the assembly without losing the proper order, and those who sincerely repent will have their sins eradicated. If one is shy and ashamed, and is reluctant to reveal their faults in front of the assembly, they can do so in private, silently remembering and confessing, and still have their sins eradicated. If there are any omissions, not deliberately hidden, even if they cannot be completely avoided, the retribution will be slightly lighter. If one cannot repent and lacks a heart of shame or remorse, this is called clinging to faults without turning back, and after death, they will inevitably fall into hell. If someone else builds a pagoda or a hall, even if it is just one piece of soil or one piece of wood, whether dyed or painted, with sincere offerings and support, they will gain much merit. [68]

    若见塔殿，或有草秽，不加耘除，蹈之而行，礼拜功德，功随即尽矣。”又曰：“经者尊典，化导之津。波罗密经，功德最胜。首楞严亦其次也。若有善人，读诵经处，其地皆为金刚，但肉眼众生，不能见耳。能勤讽持，不坠地狱。般若定本，及如来钵，后当东至汉地。能立一善，于此经钵，受报生天，倍得功德。”所说甚广，略要载之。荷临辞去，谓曰：“汝应历劫，备受罪报。以尝闻经法，生欢喜心，今当见受轻报。一过便免。汝得济活，可作沙门。 [69]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [69]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 若见塔殿 - If you see the pagoda or temple hall.
    • 或有草秽 - If there is grass or weeds.
    • 不加耘除 - If it is not weeded or cleared.
    • 蹈之而行 - If you step on it and walk.
    • 礼拜功德 - If you perform acts of worship or merit.
    • 功随即尽矣 - The merit will immediately be exhausted.
    • 经者尊典 - The scriptures are revered classics.
    • 化导之津 - The means of transformation and guidance.
    • 波罗密经 - The Perfection of Wisdom Sutra.
    • 功德最胜 - The merit is supreme.
    • 首楞严亦其次也 - Is also second-best.
    • 若有善人 - If there is a good person.
    • 读诵经处 - Where they read and recite the scriptures.
    • 其地皆为金刚 - The ground becomes diamond-like.
    • 但肉眼众生 - But ordinary beings with physical eyes.
    • 不能见耳 - Cannot see it.
    • 能勤讽持 - If they can diligently recite and uphold.
    • 不坠地狱 - Will not fall into hell.
    • 般若定本 - The definitive edition of the Prajnaparamita Sutra.
    • 及如来钵 - And the Buddha's alms bowl.
    • 后当东至汉地 - Will later come to the Han region (China).
    • 能立一善 - If one performs a good deed.
    • 于此经钵 - In connection with this scripture and alms bowl.
    • 受报生天 - Will receive a reward and be reborn in heaven.
    • 倍得功德 - Will gain double the merit.
    • 所说甚广 - What was said is extensive.
    • 略要载之 - A brief summary is provided.
    • 荷临辞去 - The speaker leaves.
    • 谓曰 - Says to someone.
    • 汝应历劫 - You should endure many lifetimes.
    • 备受罪报 - Suffer severe karmic consequences.
    • 以尝闻经法 - Because you have heard the teachings of the scriptures.
    • 生欢喜心 - Have developed a joyful heart.
    • 今当见受轻报 - Now you will experience a lighter karmic consequence.
    • 一过便免 - After one cycle, you will be free.
    • 汝得济活 - You will be saved and live.
    • 可作沙门 - Can become a monk.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 塔殿 - Refers to pagodas and temple halls, which are important structures in Buddhist architecture.
    • 草秽 - Refers to grass and weeds, symbolizing neglect and disorder.
    • 波罗密经 - The Perfection of Wisdom Sutra, a key text in Mahayana Buddhism.
    • 首楞严 - The Sutra of the Lion's Roar, another significant Mahayana text.
    • 般若定本 - The definitive edition of the Prajnaparamita Sutra, a collection of texts on the perfection of wisdom.
    • 如来钵 - The Buddha's alms bowl, a sacred object in Buddhism.
    • 汉地 - Refers to the Han region, which historically corresponds to China.
    • 沙门 - Refers to Buddhist monks, who renounce worldly life to pursue spiritual enlightenment.

    Proper Nouns

    • 塔殿 - Pagodas and temple halls.
    • 波罗密经 - The Perfection of Wisdom Sutra.
    • 首楞严 - The Sutra of the Lion's Roar.
    • 般若定本 - The definitive edition of the Prajnaparamita Sutra.
    • 如来钵 - The Buddha's alms bowl.
    • 汉地 - The Han region (China).
    • 沙门 - Buddhist monks.

    If you see a pagoda or temple hall, and there is grass or weeds, and it is not weeded or cleared, and you step on it and walk, and perform acts of worship or merit, the merit will immediately be exhausted." He then said, "The scriptures are revered classics, the means of transformation and guidance. The Perfection of Wisdom Sutra has the supreme merit. The Sutra of the Lion's Roar is second-best. If there is a good person, where they read and recite the scriptures, the ground becomes diamond-like, but ordinary beings with physical eyes cannot see it. If they can diligently recite and uphold, they will not fall into hell. The definitive edition of the Prajnaparamita Sutra, and the Buddha's alms bowl, will later come to the Han region (China). If one performs a good deed in connection with this scripture and alms bowl, they will receive a reward and be reborn in heaven, gaining double the merit." What was said is extensive, but a brief summary is provided. The speaker leaves and says, "You should endure many lifetimes, suffering severe karmic consequences. Because you have heard the teachings of the scriptures and developed a joyful heart, now you will experience a lighter karmic consequence. After one cycle, you will be free. You will be saved and live, and can become a monk. [69]

    洛阳、临淄、建业、鄮阴、成都五处并有阿育王塔。又吴中两石像，育王所使鬼神造也。颇得真相。能往礼拜者，不堕地狱。”语已东行。荷作礼而别。出南大道，广百余步。道上行者，不可称计。道边有高座高数十丈，有沙门坐之。左右僧众，列倚甚多。有人执笔，北面而立，谓荷曰：“在襄阳时，何故杀鹿？”跪答曰：“他人射鹿，我加创耳。又不啖肉，何缘受报？”时即见襄阳杀鹿之地，草树山涧，忽然满目。所乘黑马，并皆能言。悉证荷杀鹿年月时日。 [70]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [70]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 並有 - Means 'all have' or 'all possess'.
    • 所使 - Means 'caused to be done' or 'commanded'.
    • 不墮 - Means 'not fall into' or 'not descend into'.
    • 廣 - Means 'wide' or 'broad'.
    • 不可稱計 - Means 'cannot be counted' or 'incalculable'.
    • 高座 - Means 'high seat' or 'pulpit'.
    • 列倚 - Means 'lined up' or 'arranged'.
    • 何故 - Means 'why' or 'for what reason'.
    • 加創 - Means 'added a wound' or 'inflicted an injury'.
    • 何緣受報 - Means 'why should I receive punishment' or 'why should I be punished'.
    • 忽然滿目 - Means 'suddenly fill my vision'.
    • 悉證 - Means 'all confirm' or 'all testify'.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 洛陽 - Historical city in China, capital during the Han Dynasty.
    • 臨淄 - Historical city in China, capital of the State of Qi.
    • 建業 - Historical city in China, capital of the Eastern Wu Kingdom.
    • 鄮陰 - Place name, possibly referring to a region in modern-day Zhejiang.
    • 成都 - Major city in the Sichuan region.
    • 阿育王塔 - Stupas built by Emperor Ashoka, a Mauryan emperor known for promoting Buddhism.
    • 襄陽 - City in Hubei Province, known for its historical significance.
    • 鹿 - Deer, often symbolizing purity and grace in Chinese culture.
    • 高座 - High seat or pulpit, often used in religious contexts.
    • 僧眾 - Monks or Buddhist practitioners.
    • 鬼神 - Ghosts and spirits, often associated with supernatural beings in Chinese folklore and religious beliefs.

    Proper Nouns

    • 洛陽 - Rendered as Luoyang.
    • 臨淄 - Rendered as Linzi.
    • 建業 - Rendered as Jianye.
    • 鄮陰 - Rendered as Maoyin.
    • 成都 - Rendered as Chengdu.
    • 阿育王 - Rendered as Emperor Ashoka.
    • 襄陽 - Rendered as Xiangyang.
    • 鹿 - Rendered as Deer.
    • 高座 - Rendered as High seat.
    • 僧眾 - Rendered as Monks.
    • 鬼神 - Rendered as Ghosts and spirits.
    • 荷 - Rendered as He (a personal name).

    There are five places that all have the Ashoka Stupa: Luoyang, Linzi, Jianye, Maoyin, and Chengdu. Additionally, there are two stone statues in Wu, created by the ghosts and spirits commanded by Emperor Ashoka. These statues are quite true to life. Those who go to worship them will not fall into hell.

    He then traveled eastward. He made a bow and left. The southern main road was wide over a hundred steps. The number of people walking on the road was incalculable. On both sides of the road, many monks were lined up. A person holding a brush stood facing north and said to He, "When you were in Xiangyang, why did you kill the deer?" He knelt and answered, "Someone else shot the deer, and I just added a wound. I didn't eat the meat, so why should I be punished?" At that moment, He saw the place in Xiangyang where the deer was killed, with grass, trees, mountains, and ravines suddenly filling his vision. The black horse he was riding could also speak, and all confirmed the year, month, day, and hour when He killed the deer. [70]

    荷惧然无对。须臾，有人以??之，投镬汤中。自视四体，溃然烂碎。有风吹身，聚小岸边，忽然不觉还复全形。执笔者复问：“汝又射雉，亦尝杀雁。”言已，?投镬汤，如前烂法。受此报已，乃遣荷去。入一大城，有人居焉。谓荷曰：“汝受轻罪，又得还生，是福力所扶。而今以后，复作罪不？”乃遣人送荷。遥见故身，意不欲还。送人推引，久久乃附形，而得稣活。奉法精勤，遂即出家。字曰慧达。太元末，尚在京师。后往许昌，不知所终。珠林八十六 [71]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [71]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 荷惧然无对 - Phrase meaning 'the man was greatly frightened and had no response.'
    • 须臾，有人以??之 - Phrase meaning 'after a moment, someone brought something (unclear) to him.'
    • 投镬汤中 - Phrase meaning 'threw him into a cauldron of boiling water.'
    • 自视四体，溃然烂碎 - Phrase meaning 'he looked at his body, which was rotten and shattered.'
    • 有风吹身，聚小岸边 - Phrase meaning 'there was a wind that blew him, gathering him at a small shore.'
    • 忽然不觉还复全形 - Phrase meaning 'suddenly, without realizing it, he returned to his complete form.'
    • 执笔者复问 - Phrase meaning 'the recorder asked again.'
    • 汝又射雉，亦尝杀雁 - Phrase meaning 'you also shot a pheasant, and also killed a goose.'
    • 言已，?投镬汤，如前烂法 - Phrase meaning 'after he finished speaking, he was thrown into the cauldron of boiling water again, as before, in the same way of rotting.'
    • 受此报已，乃遣荷去 - Phrase meaning 'after receiving this retribution, they sent him away.'
    • 入一大城，有人居焉 - Phrase meaning 'he entered a large city where people lived.'
    • 谓荷曰 - Phrase meaning 'they told him.'
    • 汝受轻罪，又得还生，是福力所扶 - Phrase meaning 'you have received a light punishment, and have been reborn, this is due to the power of good fortune.'
    • 而今以后，复作罪不 - Phrase meaning 'from now on, will you commit more sins?'
    • 乃遣人送荷 - Phrase meaning 'they then sent someone to send him.'
    • 遥见故身，意不欲还 - Phrase meaning 'he saw his old body from afar, and did not want to return.'
    • 送人推引，久久乃附形，而得稣活 - Phrase meaning 'the person who was sending him pushed and pulled, and after a long time, he attached to his form and revived.'
    • 奉法精勤，遂即出家 - Phrase meaning 'he diligently followed the law, and then immediately left home to become a monk.'
    • 字曰慧达 - Phrase meaning 'his name was Huidat.'
    • 太元末，尚在京师 - Phrase meaning 'at the end of the Taoyuan period, he was still in the capital.'
    • 后往许昌，不知所终 - Phrase meaning 'later, he went to Xuchang, and no one knows what happened to him.'
    • 珠林八十六 - Phrase meaning 'Zhu Lin 86.'

    The man was greatly frightened and had no response. After a moment, someone brought something (unclear) to him and threw him into a cauldron of boiling water. He looked at his body, which was rotten and shattered. There was a wind that blew him, gathering him at a small shore. Suddenly, without realizing it, he returned to his complete form. The recorder asked again: "You also shot a pheasant, and also killed a goose." After he finished speaking, he was thrown into the cauldron of boiling water again, as before, in the same way of rotting. After receiving this retribution, they sent him away. He entered a large city where people lived. They told him: "You have received a light punishment, and have been reborn, this is due to the power of good fortune. From now on, will you commit more sins?" They then sent someone to send him. He saw his old body from afar and did not want to return. The person who was sending him pushed and pulled, and after a long time, he attached to his form and revived. He diligently followed the law and then immediately left home to become a monk. His name was Huidat. At the end of the Taoyuan period, he was still in the capital. Later, he went to Xuchang, and no one knows what happened to him. Zhu Lin 86. [71]

    晋沙门竺法纯，山阴显义寺主也。晋元兴中，起寺行墙，至兰上买材。路经湖道。材主是妇人，而应共至材所，准许价直。遂与同船俱行。既入大湖，日?暴风，波浪如山。纯船小水入，命在瞬息。念值行无福，忽遇斯灾。又与妇人俱行，其以罔惧。乃一心诵观世音经。俄有大舟，泛流趣纯。适时既入夜，行旅已绝。纯自惟念，不应有此流船，疑是神力。既而共渡乘之。而此小船，应时即没。大舟随波鼓荡，俄得达其岸耳。珠林十七 [72]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [72]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 念值行无福 - Means 'thinking that he had no good fortune,' indicating the monk's belief that his misfortune was due to a lack of merit.
    • 疑是神力 - Means 'suspecting it was a divine power,' indicating the monk's belief that the boat's appearance was supernatural.
    • 命在瞬息 - Means 'life was in danger at any moment,' emphasizing the perilous situation.
    • 波浪如山 - Means 'waves like mountains,' emphasizing the intensity of the storm.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋朝 - Refers to the Jin dynasty, a period in Chinese history (266-420 CE) known for its cultural and literary achievements.
    • 佛教 - Buddhism, which was introduced to China during the Han dynasty and became more widespread during the Jin dynasty.
    • 观世音菩萨 - Guanyin, a bodhisattva in Mahayana Buddhism known for compassion and the ability to save beings from suffering.
    • 大湖 - Refers to a large lake, possibly Tai Lake (Taihu) or another significant body of water in the region.
    • 山阴 - Shanyin, a historical name for the area around modern-day Shaoxing, Zhejiang, known for its cultural and literary significance.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋 - The Jin dynasty.
    • 竺法纯 - Zhi Fachuang, the monk.
    • 山阴 - Shanyin, the location.
    • 显义寺 - Xianyi Temple.
    • 元兴 - The Yuanxing period.
    • 兰 - The location where the wood was purchased.
    • 观世音 - Guanyin, the bodhisattva.
    • 珠林 - The *Jade Forest* (Zhu Lin), the source of the story.

    During the Yuanxing period of the Jin dynasty, there was a monk named Zhi Fachuang, who served as the abbot of Xianyi Temple in Shanyin. While constructing a temple wall, he went to buy wood on the shore of Lan. On the way, they passed through a lake area, and the owner of the wood was a woman. They agreed to meet at the wood site and set the price. So, they went together on the same boat. Once they entered the large lake, a violent storm struck at dusk, with waves like mountains. The monk's small boat was flooded, and his life was in danger at any moment. He thought that he had no good fortune and suddenly encountered this disaster. Additionally, he was traveling with the woman, which greatly increased his fear. He then recited the Sutra of the Bodhisattva Guanyin with all his heart. Soon, a large boat appeared, floating toward him. At that time, it was already night, and there were no other travelers. Reflecting to himself, the monk wondered why there should have been such a flowing boat, suspecting it was a divine power. They then crossed together and boarded it. But the small boat immediately sank. The large boat followed the waves and moved, and soon reached the shore. This story is recorded in the Jade Forest, volume seventeen. [72]

    晋沙门释开达，隆安二年，登垄采甘草，为羌所执。时年大饥，羌胡相啖。乃至达栅中，将食之。先在栅者，有十余人；羌日夕亨俎，唯达尚存。自达被执，便潜诵观世音经，不懈乎心。及明日当见啖，其晨始曙，忽有大虎，遥逼群羌。奋怒号吼。羌各骇怖迸走。虎乃前啮栅木，得成小阕，可容人过。已而徐去。达初见虎啮栅，必谓见害。既栅穿而不入，心疑其异。将是观音力。计度诸羌，未应便反，即穿栅逃走；夜行昼伏，遂得免脱。珠林十七 [73]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [73]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 登垄采甘草 - Literally 'climbing a ridge to gather licorice,' a common activity in rural areas.
    • 为羌所执 - Captured by the Qiang people.
    • 大饥 - A severe famine.
    • 羌胡相啖 - The Qiang and Hu people eating each other, indicating extreme desperation.
    • 栅中 - In a stockade or enclosure.
    • 亨俎 - To prepare and cook food, often used in a sacrificial context.
    • 潜诵观世音经 - Secretly reciting the *Guanyin Sutra*.
    • 不懈乎心 - Without ceasing in his heart, indicating continuous devotion.
    • 明日当见啖 - Scheduled to be eaten the next day.
    • 忽有大虎 - Suddenly a large tiger appeared.
    • 奋怒号吼 - Roaring angrily.
    • 骇怖迸走 - Scattered in fear and ran away.
    • 啮栅木 - Biting the wooden fence.
    • 成小阕 - Creating a small opening.
    • 容人过 - Large enough for a person to pass through.
    • 徐去 - Slowly left.
    • 栅穿而不入 - The fence was breached but the tiger did not enter.
    • 计度诸羌 - Calculated that the Qiang people would not return immediately.
    • 穿栅逃走 - Broke through the fence and fled.
    • 夜行昼伏 - Traveling at night and hiding during the day.
    • 遂得免脱 - Ultimately escaped.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋 - The Eastern Jin Dynasty (317-420 CE).
    • 沙门 - A Buddhist monk.
    • 释开达 - The name of the monk, which may be a historical figure or a fictional character.
    • 隆安二年 - The second year of the Long'an era (398 CE).
    • 羌 - The Qiang people, a nomadic ethnic group in ancient China.
    • 胡 - A general term for non-Han Chinese peoples, often used in a derogatory sense.
    • 观世音经 - The *Guanyin Sutra*, a Buddhist scripture dedicated to the bodhisattva Guanyin.
    • 珠林十七 - Likely refers to a specific section in a larger Buddhist text or historical record, possibly the *Zhu Lin* (Jewel Grove) collection.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋 - The Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 释开达 - The name of the monk.
    • 隆安二年 - The second year of the Long'an era (398 CE).
    • 羌 - The Qiang people.
    • 胡 - Non-Han Chinese peoples.
    • 观世音经 - The *Guanyin Sutra*.
    • 珠林十七 - A specific section or chapter in a larger text.

    A Buddhist monk named Sishikaikai from the Eastern Jin Dynasty, during the second year of the Long'an era, climbed a ridge to gather licorice when he was captured by the Qiang people. That year was marked by a severe famine, and the Qiang and Hu people were so desperate that they resorted to eating each other. He was taken into a stockade, where he was to be eaten along with ten or so others who were already confined there. The Qiang people prepared meals daily, but only Sishikaikai survived. From the moment he was captured, he secretly recited the Guanyin Sutra without ceasing in his heart. On the day he was scheduled to be eaten, just as dawn began to break, a large tiger suddenly appeared, roaring angrily at the Qiang people. Terrified, they scattered and fled. The tiger then approached the wooden fence, biting it until a small opening was created, large enough for a person to pass through. Afterward, the tiger slowly left. Initially, Sishikaikai thought the tiger would attack him, but when the fence was breached and the tiger did not enter, he began to suspect that something unusual was happening. He believed it was the power of Guanyin. Calculating that the Qiang people would not return immediately, he broke through the fence and fled, traveling at night and hiding during the day, and ultimately escaped. This account is recorded in the Zhu Lin (Jewel Grove), chapter seventeen. [73]

    晋潘道秀，吴郡人。年二十余，为军纠主北为征固。案此句有讹广记引作尝随军北征既而军小失利。秀窜逸被掠。经数处作奴。俘虏异域，欲归无因。少信佛法，恒志心念观世音。每梦寐，辄见像。广记引有像字后既南奔，迷不知道；于穷山中，忽睹真形，如今行像。因作礼。礼竟，豁然不觉失之。二句广记引作恰然不觉安行乃得还路，遂归本土。后精进弥笃。年垂六十而亡。珠林十七广记一百十
    　　晋栾苟，广记引并作荀不知何许人也，少奉法，尝作福富平令，先从征庐循，值小失利，船舫遭火垂尽，贼亦交逼：正在中江，风浪骇目，苟恐怖分尽，犹诵念观世音。俄见江中有一人挺然孤立，腰与水齐，苟心知祈念有感，火贼己切，广记引无此句便投水就之。身既浮涌，脚以履地寻而大军遣船迎接败者，遂得免济。珠林十七广记一百十 [74]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [74]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 为军纠主北为征固 - Ambiguous phrase requiring further research; corrected version is '尝随军北征既而军小失利', meaning 'he once followed the army north and the army suffered a small defeat.'
    • 如今行像 - Unclear meaning; describes encountering a true image of Guanyin in a remote mountain.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋潘道秀 - Historical figure Pan Daoxiu from the Jin dynasty.
    • 吴郡 - Region of Wu County in the state of Jin.
    • 观世音 - Bodhisattva of compassion in Buddhism, Guanyin.
    • 珠林十七广记一百十 - Sources where the story is recorded, likely Buddhist or historical texts.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋潘道秀 - Historical figure Pan Daoxiu from the Jin dynasty.
    • 吴郡 - Region of Wu County in the state of Jin.
    • 观世音 - Bodhisattva of compassion in Buddhism, Guanyin.
    • 珠林十七广记一百十 - Sources where the story is recorded.

    Pan Daoxiu of Wu County, during the Jin dynasty, was in his twenties when he served as a military officer and was dispatched to the north. According to the Guangji (Miscellaneous Records), the original text may have an error, and the correct version is: "He once followed the army north and the army suffered a small defeat." Pan Daoxiu fled and was captured, and he was enslaved in several places. Captured in a foreign land, he had no way to return home. He had faith in Buddhism and constantly prayed to Guanyin. In his dreams, he often saw images of Guanyin. Later, he fled south and became lost. In a remote mountain, he suddenly encountered a true image of Guanyin, as if it were a moving statue. He performed a ritual of worship. After the ritual was completed, he suddenly realized that the image had disappeared. According to the Guangji, an alternative reading is: "He suddenly realized that he was safe and found his way home, thus returning to his native land." After this, he became even more devout. He died at nearly sixty years old. Recorded in Zhulin 17 and Guangji 110.

    Luang Gou, according to the Guangji, whose name is also written as Luan Gou, was an unknown person who, in his youth, practiced the Dharma. He once served as the magistrate of Fuping and was dispatched to the south to suppress Lu Xun. He suffered a small defeat, and his ships were nearly destroyed by fire, while the enemy was also closing in. In the middle of the river, the wind and waves were terrifying, and Luang Gou was filled with fear. He continued to recite the name of Guanyin. Suddenly, he saw a person standing alone in the middle of the river, waist-deep in water. Luang Gou knew that his prayers had been answered. The fire and the enemy were now at bay. He jumped into the water to join the person. His body floated and rose, and his feet touched the ground. Soon after, the main army sent ships to rescue the defeated soldiers, and he was saved. Recorded in Zhulin 17 and Guangji 110. [74]

    晋沙门释法智为白衣时，常独行，至大泽中，忽遇猛火，四方俱起，走路已绝，便至心礼诵观世音；俄然火过，一泽之草，无有遗茎者，唯智所处容身不烧。于是始乃敬奉大法。后为姚兴将，从征索虏，军退，失马，落在围里；乃隐沟边荆棘丛中，得蔽头，复念观世音，心甚勤至。隔沟人遥唤后军，指令煞之，而军过搜觅辄无见者，遥得免济。后遂出家。珠林十七广记一百十
    　　晋南宫子敖，始平人也，戍新平城，为佛佛虏儿长乐公所破，合城数千人皆被诛害。子敖虽分必死，而犹至心念观世音。既而次至子敖，群刃交下，或高或僻，持刀之人，或疲懈四支不随。尔时长乐公亲自临刑，惊问之，子敖聊尔答云：“能作马鞍。”乃令原释。子敖亦不知所作此言。时后遂得遁逸。造小形像，广记引作乃造一观音小像贮以香函，行则顶戴也。珠林十七广记一百十 [75]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [75]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 為之一空 - Set phrase meaning completely emptied; fields were left barren.
    • 至心礼诵观世音 - To sincerely recite and chant the name of Guanyin; a religious practice involving devotion and prayer.
    • 俄然火过 - Suddenly the fire passed by; a vivid description of the fire subsiding quickly.
    • 一泽之草，无有遗茎者 - All the grass in the marsh was completely burned; emphasizes the extent of the destruction.
    • 唯智所处容身不烧 - Only where Fa Zhi was standing was not burned; highlights the miraculous nature of the event.
    • 敬奉大法 - To revere and follow the great law (Dharma); indicates a commitment to Buddhist teachings.
    • 从征索虏 - Following to fight against the Xianbei (索虏); refers to military campaigns against the Xianbei people.
    • 隐沟边荆棘丛中 - Hiding in a thorny bush by a ditch; describes a place of concealment.
    • 念观世音 - To recite the name of Guanyin; another instance of the religious practice.
    • 心甚勤至 - With a very sincere and earnest heart; emphasizes the intensity of the devotion.
    • 隔沟人遥唤后军 - People on the other side of the ditch called out to the rear army; describes a situation where someone is trying to alert others.
    • 军过搜觅辄无见者 - When the army passed and searched, they could not find him; indicates the successful concealment.
    • 遥得免济 - He was saved from afar; suggests that he was rescued or spared.
    • 遂出家 - He then became a monk; marks a significant change in his life.
    • 造小形像 - To make a small statue; refers to the creation of a religious artifact.
    • 广记引作乃造一观音小像贮以香函，行则顶戴也 - According to the *Guangji* (Extensive Records), he made a small statue of Guanyin and kept it in a fragrant box, carrying it on his head when traveling; provides additional details about the statue and its significance.
    • 晋南宫子敖 - A layperson named Nan Gong Ziao from the Jin dynasty.
    • 始平人也 - From Shiping; indicates his place of origin.
    • 戍新平城 - Garrisoning at Xiping City; referring to his military service.
    • 为佛佛虏儿长乐公所破 - Was defeated by the Longle Prince of the Fofo (Xianbei) tribe; describes a military defeat.
    • 合城数千人皆被诛害 - Thousands of people in the city were all killed; emphasizes the severity of the defeat.
    • 子敖虽分必死 - Nan Gong Ziao, although knowing he would surely die; indicates his awareness of the dire situation.
    • 而犹至心念观世音 - Still sincerely recited the name of Guanyin; shows his continued devotion.
    • 既而次至子敖 - Later, it was Nan Gong Ziao's turn; indicates the sequence of events.
    • 群刃交下 - Multiple swords came down; describes a violent attack.
    • 或高或僻 - Some high, some low, or some off-target; describes the varied nature of the attacks.
    • 持刀之人，或疲懈四支不随 - The person holding the knife was tired and his limbs did not follow his commands; describes the physical and mental state of the attacker.
    • 长乐公亲自临刑 - The Longle Prince personally oversaw the execution; indicates the high level of authority involved.
    • 惊问之 - Asked him in surprise; shows the unexpected nature of the event.
    • 子敖聊尔答云 - Nan Gong Ziao casually replied; indicates a nonchalant response.
    • 能作马鞍 - Can make a saddle; is an unexpected and seemingly unrelated response.
    • 乃令原释 - Then he was spared; indicates the outcome of the interaction.
    • 子敖亦不知所作此言 - Nan Gong Ziao did not know why he said this; suggests a lack of understanding or intention behind his words.
    • 时后遂得遁逸 - Later, he managed to escape; indicates the successful evasion.
    • 造小形像 - To make a small statue; refers to the creation of a religious artifact.
    • 广记引作乃造一观音小像贮以香函，行则顶戴也 - According to the *Guangji* (Extensive Records), he made a small statue of Guanyin and kept it in a fragrant box, carrying it on his head when traveling; provides additional details about the statue and its significance.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋 - The Jin dynasty, a period in Chinese history.
    • 姚兴 - Emperor Yao Xing, a ruler of the Later Qin dynasty.
    • 索虏 - A derogatory term for the Xianbei people, a nomadic group that ruled parts of northern China.
    • 观世音 - Guanyin, the bodhisattva of compassion in Mahayana Buddhism.
    • 佛佛虏儿 - A term used to refer to the Xianbei people, who were often seen as barbarians by the Chinese.
    • 长乐公 - A title given to a prominent Xianbei leader.
    • 广记 - *Guangji* (Extensive Records), a collection of stories and anecdotes from various sources.
    • 珠林 - *Zhulin* (Jade Forest), another collection of stories and anecdotes.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋 - The Jin dynasty.
    • 姚兴 - Emperor Yao Xing.
    • 索虏 - The Xianbei people.
    • 观世音 - Guanyin.
    • 佛佛虏儿 - The Xianbei people.
    • 长乐公 - A title given to a prominent Xianbei leader.
    • 广记 - *Guangji* (Extensive Records).

    During the Jin dynasty, there was a monk named Fa Zhi, who was once a layperson. He often traveled alone and one day found himself in a marsh, where he suddenly encountered a fierce fire that erupted from all directions, blocking all escape routes. In his desperate situation, he sincerely recited and chanted the name of Guanyin. Suddenly, the fire passed, and all the grass in the marsh was completely burned, except for the spot where Fa Zhi stood, which remained unscathed. Thus, he began to revere and follow the great law (Dharma). Later, he became a general under Emperor Yao Xing and followed him to fight against the Xianbei. When the army retreated, he lost his horse and was captured. He hid in a thorny bush by a ditch, managed to cover his head, and continued to recite the name of Guanyin with a very sincere and earnest heart. People on the other side of the ditch called out to the rear army to execute him, but when the army passed and searched, they could not find him. He was saved from afar. Later, he became a monk. According to the Zhulin (Jade Forest) and Guangji (Extensive Records), volume 17, page 110.

    In the Jin dynasty, there was a layperson named Nan Gong Ziao from Shiping. He was garrisoning at Xiping City and was defeated by the Longle Prince of the Fofo (Xianbei) tribe. Thousands of people in the city were all killed. Although Nan Gong Ziao knew he would surely die, he still sincerely recited the name of Guanyin. Later, it was his turn, and multiple swords came down, some high, some low, or some off-target. The person holding the knife was tired and his limbs did not follow his commands. At that time, the Longle Prince personally oversaw the execution and asked him in surprise. Nan Gong Ziao casually replied, "Can make a saddle." Then he was spared. Nan Gong Ziao did not know why he said this. Later, he managed to escape. He made a small statue of Guanyin and kept it in a fragrant box, carrying it on his head when traveling. According to the Guangji (Extensive Records), volume 17, page 110. [75]

    晋刘度，平原辽城人也乡里有一千余家，并奉大法，造立形像，供养僧尼。值虏主木未时此县尝有逋逃，未大怒，欲尽灭一城。众并凶惧，分必殄尽。度乃洁诚率众归命观世音。顷之，未见物从空中下，绕其所住屋柱；惊视，乃观世音经。使人读之，广记引无此句未大欢喜，用省刑戮。于是此城即得免害。珠林十一广记一百十
    　　晋郭宣之，太原人也，义熙四年，为杨思平梁州府司马。杨以辄害范元之等被法，宣亦同执在狱，唯一心归向观世音菩萨。后夕将眠之际，忽亲睹菩萨光明照狱，宣瞻觌礼拜，祈请誓愿，久之乃没。俄而宣之独被恩赦。既释，依所见形，制造图像，又立精舍焉。后历零陵衡阳，卒官。珠林十七 [76]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [76]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 奉大法 - To follow or adhere to the great law or principle, often referring to religious or moral teachings.
    • 造立形像 - To create or establish images, likely referring to the creation of religious statues or icons.
    • 供养僧尼 - To provide sustenance and support for monks and nuns.
    • 值虏主木未时 - A phrase that is somewhat ambiguous. '虏主' likely refers to a foreign ruler or invader, and '木未时' could be a specific time or period, possibly a typo or corruption of a proper name or title.
    • 未大怒 - The ruler '未' becomes extremely angry.
    • 分必殄尽 - To be divided and inevitably destroyed.
    • 归命观世音 - To dedicate oneself to the Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara.
    • 从空中下 - To descend from the sky.
    • 绕其所住屋柱 - To circle around the pillar of the house where they reside.
    • 亲睹菩萨光明照狱 - To personally witness the light of the Bodhisattva illuminating the prison.
    • 瞻觌礼拜 - To gaze upon, worship, and pay respects.
    • 祈请誓愿 - To pray, make requests, and vow.
    • 独被恩赦 - To be uniquely granted amnesty.
    • 依所见形，制造图像 - To create images based on what was seen.
    • 立精舍焉 - To establish a meditation hall or monastery.
    • 历零陵衡阳，卒官 - To serve in various places such as Lingling and Hengyang, and to die in office.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋刘度 - Liu Du, a person from the Jin dynasty.
    • 平原辽城人也 - From Liaocheng in the Plain (a region in modern-day Shandong).
    • 乡里有一千余家，并奉大法 - A village with over a thousand households, all following the great law.
    • 虏主木未时 - A foreign ruler during the 'Mumu' period (possibly a typo or corruption).
    • 尽灭一城 - To completely destroy a city.
    • 观世音菩萨 - The Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara, a key figure in Mahayana Buddhism.
    • 晋郭宣之 - Guo Xuanzhi, a person from the Jin dynasty.
    • 义熙四年 - The fourth year of the Yixi era (408 AD) during the Jin dynasty.
    • 杨思平梁州府司马 - Yang Siping, the Military Governor of Liangzhou.
    • 辄害范元之等被法 - Accused of harming Fan Yuanzhi and others and thus subjected to the law.
    • 珠林十一广记一百十 - References to the 'Jewel Treasury' (珠林) and 'Broad Record' (广记), likely sources or collections of stories.
    • 珠林十七 - Reference to the 'Jewel Treasury,' likely a source or collection of stories.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋刘度 - Liu Du, a person from the Jin dynasty.
    • 平原辽城 - Liaocheng in the Plain (a region in modern-day Shandong).
    • 虏主木未时 - A foreign ruler during the 'Mumu' period (possibly a typo or corruption).
    • 未 - The ruler '未' (possibly a typo or corruption of a proper name or title).
    • 观世音菩萨 - The Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara.
    • 晋郭宣之 - Guo Xuanzhi, a person from the Jin dynasty.
    • 太原 - Taiyuan, a city in Shanxi.
    • 义熙四年 - The fourth year of the Yixi era (408 AD) during the Jin dynasty.
    • 杨思平 - Yang Siping, the Military Governor of Liangzhou.
    • 范元之 - Fan Yuanzhi, a person mentioned in the text.
    • 零陵衡阳 - Lingling and Hengyang, cities in Hunan.

    Jin Dynasty Liu Du, from Liaocheng in the Plain (a region in modern-day Shandong), lived in a village with over a thousand households, all of whom followed the great law, created images, and provided support for monks and nuns. During the time of the foreign ruler "Mumu," this county had some fugitives, and the ruler "未" became extremely angry, wanting to completely destroy the city. The people were all terrified and believed they would inevitably be destroyed. Liu Du then sincerely led the people to dedicate themselves to the Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara. Soon, something descended from the sky and circled around the pillar of the house where they resided. They were surprised and saw it was the Sutra of Avalokiteshvara. They had someone read it, and it was found that the widely recorded text did not contain this sentence. The ruler "未" was greatly pleased and thus spared the punishment. Thus, this city was spared from harm. (From the "Jewel Treasury" Volume 11, "Broad Record" Volume 110)

    Jin Dynasty Guo Xuanzhi, from Taiyuan, served as the Military Governor of Liangzhou in the fourth year of the Yixi era (408 AD). Yang Siping was accused of harming Fan Yuanzhi and others and thus subjected to the law, and Guo Xuanzhi was also imprisoned with him. He dedicated himself solely to the Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara. One night, as he was about to sleep, he suddenly witnessed the light of the Bodhisattva illuminating the prison. He gazed upon, worshipped, and prayed, vowing for a long time until the light disappeared. Soon, Guo Xuanzhi was uniquely granted amnesty. After being released, he created images based on what he had seen and established a meditation hall. Later, he served in various places such as Lingling and Hengyang, and died in office. (From the "Jewel Treasury" Volume 17) [76]

    晋新野庾绍之，小字道覆，晋湘东太守，与南阳宋协中表昆弟，情好绸缪。绍元兴卒，病卒，义熙中，忽见形诣协，形貌衣服，具如平生，而两脚着械。既至，脱械置地而坐。协问：“何由得顾？”答云：“蹔蒙假归，与卿亲好，故相过也。”协问：鬼神之事，绍辄漫略，不甚谐对。唯云：“宜勤精进，不可杀生；若不能都断，可勿宰牛，食肉之时，无啖物心。”协云：“五脏与肉，乃复异耶？”答曰：“心者，善神之宅也，其罪尤重。”具问亲戚，因谈世事，末复求酒。协时时饵茱萸酒，因为设之。酒至，对杯不饮，云有茱萸气。协曰：“为恶之耶？”答云：“下官皆畏之，非独我也。”绍为人语声高壮，此言论时不异恒日。有顷，协儿邃之来，绍闻屐声，极有惧色，谓协曰：“生气见陵，不复得住；与卿三年别耳！”因贯械而起，出户便灭。协后为正员郎，果三年而卒珠林九十四广记三百二十四 [77]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [77]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 小字 - A nickname or pet name given to someone, often in childhood.
    • 情好绸缪 - A phrase meaning 'deep affection and close relationship,' emphasizing the strong bond between the two individuals.
    • 蹔蒙假归 - A euphemistic expression meaning 'temporarily allowed to return,' often used in the context of the afterlife.
    • 宜勤精进 - A phrase encouraging diligence and spiritual advancement.
    • 不可杀生 - A Buddhist or Daoist principle of not taking life, emphasizing compassion and non-violence.
    • 食肉之时，无啖物心 - A specific instruction to avoid eating the heart of animals, possibly due to its symbolic significance in Chinese culture.
    • 五脏与肉，乃复异耶 - A rhetorical question asking whether the heart is different from other parts of the body, highlighting its special status.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 庾绍之 - A historical figure, specifically a governor of Xiangdong during the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 宋协 - Another historical figure, a relative of庾绍之, with whom he had a close relationship.
    • 元兴 - The reign title of Emperor An of Jin, indicating the period when庾绍之 died.
    • 义熙 - The reign title of Emperor An of Jin, indicating the period when庾绍之's spirit visited宋协.
    • 鬼神之事 - Refers to matters related to spirits and the supernatural, a common theme in Chinese folklore and literature.
    • 假归 - A term used in the afterlife to describe a temporary return to the world of the living, often with a specific purpose.

    Proper Nouns

    • 庾绍之 - A historical figure, governor of Xiangdong during the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 宋协 - A historical figure, a relative of庾绍之, with whom he had a close relationship.
    • 元兴 - The reign title of Emperor An of Jin.
    • 义熙 - The reign title of Emperor An of Jin.
    • 珠林九十四广记三百二十四 - References to specific chapters in the 'Jade Forest' and 'Record of the Marvelous,' indicating the source of the story.

    Jin Xinjiang's Yu Shaozhi, with the nickname Dao Fu, was the governor of Xiangdong during the Eastern Jin Dynasty. He had a close relationship with NanYang's Song Xie, who was his cousin and brother-in-law, and their affection was deep and enduring. Yu Shaozhi died in the first year of Yuanxing due to illness. In the first year of Yixi, he suddenly appeared in the form of a spirit to visit Song Xie. His appearance and attire were exactly as they had been in life, but his feet were bound with shackles. Upon arrival, he removed the shackles and placed them on the ground before sitting down. Song Xie asked, "Why have you come to see me?" Yu Shaozhi replied, "I have been temporarily allowed to return, and because of our close relationship, I have come to visit you." Song Xie asked about matters related to spirits and the supernatural, but Yu Shaozhi spoke vaguely and did not provide detailed answers. He only said, "You should strive for spiritual advancement, not kill living beings; if you cannot completely abstain, do not slaughter cows, and when eating meat, do not consume the heart." Song Xie asked, "Is the heart different from other parts of the body?" Yu Shaozhi replied, "The heart is the dwelling place of the benevolent spirit, and its sin is particularly heavy." He then inquired about Song Xie's relatives and discussed worldly affairs. At the end, he requested wine. Song Xie often drank wine with wormwood, so he prepared it for Yu Shaozhi. When the wine was served, Yu Shaozhi did not drink, saying there was a scent of wormwood. Song Xie said, "Is it because it is bad?" Yu Shaozhi replied, "All of us fear it, not just me." Yu Shaozhi had a loud and strong voice, and during this conversation, it was no different from his usual self. After a while, Song Xie's son Shuo Zhi came, and Yu Shaozhi heard the sound of his footsteps and became extremely frightened. He told Song Xie, "I am being oppressed by living energy and cannot stay here anymore; I will be separated from you for three years!" He then put on the shackles and stood up, and upon exiting the door, he disappeared. Song Xie later became a regular official, and indeed, he died three years later. (From Jade Forest 94 and Record of the Marvelous 324) [77]

    晋沙门释法安者，庐山之僧远法师弟子也，义熙末，阳新县虎暴甚盛，县有大社树，下有筑神庙，左右民居以百数。遭虎死者夕必一两，法安尝游其县，?投此屯，民以惧虎，早闭门闾，且不识法安，不肯受之。法安遥之树下，坐禅通夜，向晓，有虎负人而至，投树之北，见安，如喜如跳，伏安前，安为说法授戒，虎据地不动，有顷而去。至旦，屯人追死者至树下，见安大惊，谓其神人，故虎不害。自兹以后，而虎患遂息。众益敬异，一县士庶，略皆奉法。后欲画像山壁，不能得空青，欲用铜青，而又无铜。夜梦人迳其床前云：“此中有两铜钟，便可取之。”安明即掘得，遂以成像。后远法师铸像，安送一劝助；余一，武昌太守熊无患借观之，遂留不改。法苑珠林十九 [78]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [78]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 坐禅通夜 - Meditated through the night, a common practice among Buddhist monks.
    • 授戒 - Bestowed precepts, a ritual where a teacher imparts moral and spiritual guidelines to disciples.
    • 画像山壁 - Painting an image on the mountain wall, a form of religious art.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋 - The Jin Dynasty (266–420 CE), a period in Chinese history.
    • 庐山之僧远法师 - Far Master, a famous monk who lived on Mount Lu (Lushan), known for his teachings and influence.
    • 义熙末 - The end of the Yìxī era, a specific period in the Liu Song dynasty.
    • 阳新县 - Yangxin County, a place in Hubei Province, known for its natural beauty and historical significance.
    • 大社树 - A sacred tree often found in local temples or shrines.
    • 神庙 - Temples dedicated to local deities or spirits.
    • 虎暴甚盛 - Tigers were very rampant, indicating a period of frequent tiger attacks.
    • 法苑珠林 - A collection of Buddhist stories and teachings, compiled by Daoxuan in the Tang Dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 释法安 - Monk Fa'an.
    • 武昌太守熊无患 - Xiong Wuhuan, the governor of Wuchang.

    A Buddhist monk named Fa'an, a disciple of Master Yuan of Mount Lu, was traveling to Yangxin County during the end of the Yìxī era. At the time, tigers were rampant in the area, and there was a large sacred tree in the county with a temple built beneath it. The surrounding settlements housed hundreds of families. Every evening, a few people were killed by tigers. One day, Fa'an arrived in the county and stayed in a settlement. The people were afraid of the tigers and closed their doors early, and not knowing Fa'an, they did not accept him. Fa'an sat in meditation under the tree through the night. Early in the morning, a tiger carrying a person arrived and approached the tree. Seeing Fa'an, the tiger jumped around excitedly, lay down in front of him, and Fa'an taught it precepts. The tiger stood still and then left. By dawn, the people of the settlement followed the dead to the tree and saw Fa'an, greatly surprised. They thought he was a divine being, so the tiger did not harm them. From then on, the tiger problem ceased. The people became more respectful and amazed, and almost all of them followed the law (or religious precepts). Later, Fa'an wanted to paint an image on the mountain wall but could not find blue stone, so he wanted to use green stone, but there was no copper. He dreamed of someone passing by his bed and saying, "There are two bronze bells here, you can take them." In the morning, Fa'an dug them up and used them to complete the image. Later, when Master Yuan cast an image, Fa'an contributed one. The other one was borrowed by the governor of Wuchang, Xiong Wuhuan, to view and remained with him. [78]

    汉案当作晋珠林误题沙门竺昙盖秦郡人也，真确有苦行，持钵振锡，取给四辈。居于蒋山。常行般舟，尤善神祝，多有应验。司马元显甚敬奉之，卫将军刘毅闻其精苦，招来姑孰，深相爱遇。义兴案当作义熙五年，大旱，陂湖竭涸，苗稼焦枯，祈祭山川，累旬无应；毅乃请僧设斋，盖亦在焉。斋毕，躬乘露桁，浮泛川溪，文武士庶，倾州悉行。盖于中流，焚香礼拜，至诚慷慨，乃读海龙王经；造卷发音，云气便起，转读将半，沛泽四合，才及释轴，洪雨滂注，畦湖毕满，其年以登。刘敬叔时为毅国郎中令，亲豫此集，自所睹见。珠林六十三 [79]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [79]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 汉案当作晋珠林误题 - Indicates a textual error, suggesting that the original text should be corrected from 'Han' to 'Jin' and '珠林' (Jade Forest) is mistakenly attributed.
    • 真确有苦行 - Describes someone who is truly virtuous and practices asceticism.
    • 持钵振锡 - Refers to a monk carrying a begging bowl and a staff, symbolizing the monastic life.
    • 取给四辈 - Means to provide for the four classes of people (monks, nuns, laymen, and laywomen).
    • 般舟 - Refers to a specific type of meditation practice.
    • 尤善神祝 - Means particularly skilled in divination or exorcism.
    • 多有应验 - Indicates that many of his predictions or rituals have come true.
    • 甚敬奉之 - Means to hold someone in high respect and venerate them.
    • 招来姑孰 - Refers to inviting someone to a place called Gusu (present-day Ma'anshan, Anhui).
    • 深相爱遇 - Means to deeply love and cherish someone.
    • 义兴案当作义熙五年 - Indicates a textual error, suggesting that '义兴' should be corrected to '义熙' (405 AD).
    • 大旱 - Refers to a severe drought.
    • 陂湖竭涸 - Means that ponds and lakes have dried up.
    • 苗稼焦枯 - Describes crops withering and dying.
    • 祈祭山川 - Refers to praying and sacrificing to mountains and rivers.
    • 累旬无应 - Means that after many days, there was no response.
    • 请僧设斋 - Refers to inviting monks to perform a ritual meal.
    • 躬乘露桁 - Means to personally ride in a carriage.
    • 浮泛川溪 - Describes floating on rivers and streams.
    • 文武士庶 - Refers to literati, soldiers, and commoners.
    • 倾州悉行 - Means that the entire state participated.
    • 焚香礼拜 - Refers to burning incense and performing worship.
    • 至诚慷慨 - Means to be sincere and enthusiastic.
    • 海龙王经 - Refers to the *Sutra of the Dragon King of the Sea*.
    • 造卷发音 - Means to read aloud from a scroll.
    • 云气便起 - Describes clouds forming.
    • 转读将半 - Means to read halfway through.
    • 沛泽四合 - Describes rain falling in all directions.
    • 才及释轴 - Means to just finish reading the scroll.
    • 洪雨滂注 - Describes heavy rain pouring down.
    • 畦湖毕满 - Means that all ponds and fields are full.
    • 其年以登 - Indicates a good harvest that year.
    • 亲豫此集 - Means to personally attend this gathering.
    • 自所睹见 - Refers to what was personally witnessed.
    • 珠林六十三 - Refers to the *Jade Forest* (a Buddhist text) chapter 63.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 汉案当作晋珠林误题 - Indicates a textual error in the original manuscript.
    • 真确有苦行 - Describes a monk who practices asceticism.
    • 持钵振锡 - Symbolizes the monastic life.
    • 取给四辈 - Refers to the four classes of people in Buddhist monastic society.
    • 般舟 - A specific type of meditation practice.
    • 尤善神祝 - Indicates skill in divination or exorcism.
    • 司马元显 - A historical figure, a regent during the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 卫将军刘毅 - A historical figure, a general during the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 义熙五年 - 405 AD, a year in the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 大旱 - A severe drought.
    • 陂湖竭涸 - Drought conditions affecting water bodies.
    • 苗稼焦枯 - Crop failure due to drought.
    • 祈祭山川 - Traditional Chinese rituals to appease natural elements.
    • 累旬无应 - No response to prayers and sacrifices.
    • 请僧设斋 - Inviting monks to perform a ritual meal.
    • 躬乘露桁 - Personal participation in a ritual.
    • 浮泛川溪 - Symbolic of spiritual journeying.
    • 文武士庶 - Refers to different social classes.
    • 倾州悉行 - Large-scale participation in a ritual.
    • 焚香礼拜 - Traditional Buddhist worship practices.
    • 至诚慷慨 - Sincerity and enthusiasm in religious practice.
    • 海龙王经 - A Buddhist sutra.
    • 造卷发音 - Reading a religious text aloud.
    • 云气便起 - Symbolic of divine intervention.
    • 转读将半 - Progress in reading a religious text.
    • 沛泽四合 - Rain falling in all directions.
    • 才及释轴 - Finishing a religious text.
    • 洪雨滂注 - Heavy rain.
    • 畦湖毕满 - Water filling up ponds and fields.
    • 其年以登 - Good harvest.
    • 亲豫此集 - Personal attendance at a significant event.
    • 自所睹见 - Personal observation.
    • 珠林六十三 - Chapter 63 of the *Jade Forest* (a Buddhist text).

    Proper Nouns

    • 汉 - Han Dynasty.
    • 晋 - Jin Dynasty.
    • 珠林 - *Jade Forest* (a Buddhist text).
    • 真确 - A name or title of the monk.
    • 司马元显 - A historical figure, a regent during the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 卫将军刘毅 - A historical figure, a general during the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 义熙五年 - 405 AD, a year in the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 蒋山 - A mountain in Nanjing.
    • 海龙王经 - *Sutra of the Dragon King of the Sea*.
    • 刘敬叔 - A historical figure, a government official during the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 珠林六十三 - Chapter 63 of the *Jade Forest* (a Buddhist text).

    A Monk Named Zhi Tan

    During the Jin Dynasty, a monk named Zhi Tan, from the Qin Prefecture, was known for his true virtue and ascetic practices. He carried a begging bowl and a staff, providing for the four classes of people (monks, nuns, laymen, and laywomen). He resided on Jiangshan Mountain. He often practiced the "Banzhou" meditation and was particularly skilled in divination and exorcism, with many of his predictions and rituals proving accurate. Emperor Yuanxian of the Jin Dynasty held him in high respect and venerated him. General Liu Yi, upon hearing of his rigorous practices, invited him to Gusu, where he was deeply loved and cherished. In the fifth year of the Yixi era (405 AD), a severe drought struck, causing ponds and lakes to dry up and crops to wither and die. Despite repeated prayers and sacrifices to mountains and rivers, there was no response for many days. General Yi then invited monks to perform a ritual meal, and Zhi Tan was among them. After the meal, he personally rode in a carriage, floating on rivers and streams. Literati, soldiers, and commoners from the entire state participated in the ritual. Zhi Tan, in the middle of the river, burned incense and performed worship with sincerity and enthusiasm. He then read aloud from the Sutra of the Dragon King of the Sea. As he began to read, clouds formed. Halfway through, rain fell in all directions. Just as he finished reading the scroll, heavy rain poured down, filling all ponds and fields. That year, there was a bountiful harvest. Liu Jingshu, who was serving as a Langzhongling under General Yi, personally attended this gathering and witnessed these events. This account is recorded in Chapter 63 of the Jade Forest. [79]

    晋向靖，字奉仁，河内人也，在吴兴郡丧数岁女。四字广记引作有一女数岁而亡女始病时，弄小刀子，母夺取不与，伤母手。丧后一年，母又产一女，女年四岁，谓母曰：“前时刀子何在？”母曰：“无也。”女曰：“昔争刀子，故伤母手，云何无耶？”母甚惊怪，具以告靖，靖曰：“先刀子犹在不？”母曰：“痛念前女，故不录之。”靖曰：“可更觅数个刀子，合置一处，令女自择。”女见大喜，即取先者曰：“此是儿许。”父母大小乃知前女审其先身。珠林二十六广记三百八十七引至即取先者 [80]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [80]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 四字廣記引作 - A phrase indicating that the text is cited or referenced in the Four-Character Notes (四字廣記), a collection of short notes or anecdotes.
    • 弄小刀子 - A literal phrase meaning 'playing with a small knife,' which can be interpreted as a child's innocent behavior.
    • 母奪取不與 - A phrase describing the mother taking the knife away from the child, emphasizing the child's persistence.
    • 傷母手 - A phrase indicating that the child's actions caused the mother to get hurt.
    • 喪後一年 - A phrase indicating a period of one year after the death of the child.
    • 前時刀子何在 - A question asking where the previous knife is, suggesting a connection to the past.
    • 無也 - A simple negative response meaning 'no' or 'not.'
    • 昔爭刀子 - A phrase referring to a past argument or struggle over the knife.
    • 故傷母手 - A phrase explaining the reason for the mother's injury, linking it to the past event.
    • 可更觅数个刀子 - A suggestion to find more knives, implying a need to replace or find the original one.
    • 合置一处 - A phrase meaning 'to put together in one place,' suggesting a collection of items.
    • 令女自择 - A phrase instructing the girl to choose among the items.
    • 此是儿许 - A phrase indicating that the girl recognizes the knife as belonging to her, possibly from a past life.
    • 父母大小乃知前女审其先身 - A phrase indicating that the parents and others now understand that the deceased daughter had a clear memory of her past life.
    • 珠林二十六廣記三百八十七引至即取先者 - A citation indicating that the text is referenced in Zhu Lin (珠林) volume 26 and Guang Ji (廣記) volume 387, specifically up to the point where the girl takes the knife.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 向靖 - A historical figure, likely a prominent person in the context of the story.
    • 字奉仁 - The courtesy name (字) of 向靖, which is a common practice in Chinese culture to give a person a secondary name for social and respectful use.
    • 河内人也 - Indicates that 向靖 is from the region of Hebei, a historical and geographical area in China.
    • 吴兴郡 - A specific region in China, likely referring to a place where the events took place.
    • 珠林二十六廣記三百八十七 - References to specific volumes in Zhu Lin and Guang Ji, which are collections of historical and anecdotal records.

    Proper Nouns

    • 向靖 - The main character of the story.
    • 吴兴郡 - The specific region where the events take place.
    • 珠林二十六廣記三百八十七 - Specific volumes in the Zhu Lin and Guang Ji collections that reference the story.

    During the Jin dynasty, Xiang Jing, courtesy name Fengren, was a man from Hedong. He had a daughter in Wuxing who died at a young age. The Four-Character Notes cite a story about a girl who died at a young age. When she first fell ill, she played with a small knife, and her mother took it away from her, causing the child to injure her mother's hand. A year after her death, the mother gave birth to another daughter, who was four years old. The girl asked her mother, "Where is the knife from before?" The mother replied, "It is not here." The girl said, "We argued over the knife before, so I hurt your hand. How can it not be here?" Her mother was greatly surprised and informed Xiang Jing about this. Xiang Jing asked, "Is the previous knife still around?" The mother replied, "I am too heartbroken to remember the past, so I did not keep it." Xiang Jing suggested, "Find several knives, put them together in one place, and let the girl choose." The girl saw them and was very happy, immediately picking up the original one and saying, "This is mine." The parents and others then realized that the deceased daughter had a clear memory of her past life. This story is cited in Zhu Lin volume 26 and Guang Ji volume 387, specifically up to the point where the girl takes the knife. [80]

    赵石长和者，赵国高邑人也，年十九时，病一月余日亡。家贫，未能及时得殡敛，经四日而稣。说：初死时，东南行，见二人治道，在和前五十步，和行有迟疾，二人治道亦随缓速，常五十步。而道之两边，棘刺森然，皆如鹰爪，见人甚众，群走棘中，身体伤裂，地皆流血。见和独行平道，俱叹息曰：“佛子独行大道中。”前至，见瓦屋采楼，可数千闲，有屋甚高，上有一人，形面壮大，着皂袍四缝，临窗而坐。和拜之，广记引作升之阁上人曰：“石君来耶？一别二千余年。”长和尔时意中便若忆此别时也，和相识有马牧孟丞夫妻，先死已积年岁，阁上人曰：“君识孟丞不？”长和曰：“识。”阁上人曰：“孟丞生时不能精进，今恒为我司埽除之役；孟丞妻精进，居处甚乐。 [81]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [81]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 為之一空 - Set phrase meaning completely emptied; fields were left barren.
    • 趙石長和者 - Formal introduction of the subject, meaning 'Zhaoshi Changhe is.'
    • 病一月余日亡 - Literally means 'sick for more than a month and died,' but in Classical Chinese, this can be interpreted as 'died after being sick for more than a month.'
    • 未能及时得殡敛 - Means 'unable to get timely burial and preparation.'
    • 经四日而稣 - Literally means 'after four days and revived,' but in Classical Chinese, this can be interpreted as 'revived after four days.'
    • 初死时，东南行 - Means 'at the time of his initial death, he traveled southeast.'
    • 治道 - Means 'repairing the road,' but in this context, it likely refers to a metaphorical path or journey.
    • 棘刺森然 - Describes 'thorns and brambles standing densely,' symbolizing obstacles or dangers.
    • 皆如鹰爪 - Means 'all like eagle claws,' emphasizing the sharpness and danger of the thorns.
    • 见人甚众，群走棘中，身体伤裂，地皆流血 - Describes a scene where many people rush into the thorns, resulting in bodily injuries and bloodshed.
    • 佛子独行大道中 - Means 'the Buddha's child walks alone on the great road,' suggesting a spiritual or enlightened path.
    • 瓦屋采楼 - Refers to 'tile-roofed houses and colorful towers,' describing a grand and elaborate setting.
    • 可数千闲 - Means 'can be counted in thousands,' indicating a large number of buildings.
    • 着皂袍四缝 - Describes a person wearing a black robe with four seams, likely a formal or ceremonial garment.
    • 临窗而坐 - Means 'sitting by the window,' suggesting a position of observation or contemplation.
    • 一别二千余年 - Means 'separated for more than two thousand years,' emphasizing a long period of time.
    • 马牧孟丞夫妻 - Refers to 'Ma Mu Shengcheng and his wife,' indicating a couple.
    • 司埽除之役 - Means 'serving as a cleaner or sweeper,' suggesting a menial task.
    • 精进 - Means 'diligent and striving,' indicating a state of spiritual or moral effort.
    • 居处甚乐 - Means 'living very happily,' suggesting a contented state of existence.
    • 當以銜勒制之 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'should be controlled by the bit and bridle,' suggesting the need for discipline and restraint.
    • 勿使流亂軌躅 - Means 'do not let it flow and disrupt the tracks,' emphasizing the importance of maintaining proper form and structure.
    • 放意填坑岸 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'let the mind run wild and fill the pits and banks,' suggesting the dangers of losing control and straying from the path.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 趙國高邑人 - Refers to 'a person from Gaoyi in Zhao,' indicating a specific geographical and historical context.
    • 佛子 - Refers to 'the Buddha's child,' a term used in Buddhism to denote a follower or disciple.
    • 瓦屋采楼 - Describes a grand and elaborate architectural setting, likely a temple or palace.
    • 着皂袍四缝 - Describes a formal or ceremonial garment, likely worn by a religious or official figure.
    • 马牧孟丞夫妻 - Refers to a couple, likely known figures or ancestors in the context of the story.
    • 司埽除之役 - Refers to a menial task, likely a metaphor for a lowly position in the afterlife.
    • 精进 - Refers to a state of spiritual or moral effort, a key concept in Buddhism.
    • 居处甚乐 - Refers to a contented state of existence, likely a reward for spiritual effort.
    • 銜勒 - Refers to the bit and bridle of a horse, a common metaphor for control and discipline in classical Chinese literature.
    • 軌躅 - Refers to the tracks or path, symbolizing the proper way or method.
    • 填坑岸 - Refers to filling pits and banks, a metaphor for straying from the path and causing disorder.

    Proper Nouns

    • 趙石長和 - The name of the subject.
    • 趙國高邑 - The place of origin.
    • 佛子 - A term used in Buddhism.
    • 瓦屋采楼 - A grand and elaborate architectural setting.
    • 着皂袍四缝 - A formal or ceremonial garment.
    • 馬牧孟丞 - A known figure or ancestor.
    • 精進 - A state of spiritual or moral effort.
    • 居處甚樂 - A contented state of existence.
