    Zhaoshi Changhe was a man from Gaoyi in Zhao. At the age of nineteen, he fell ill for more than a month and died. Due to poverty, he could not be buried in time, and after four days, he revived. He recounted: "At the time of my initial death, I traveled southeast and saw two people repairing the road, fifty steps ahead of me. As I walked at varying speeds, the two people also adjusted their pace to match, always maintaining a distance of fifty steps. The path on either side was lined with dense thorns, all like eagle claws. Many people rushed into the thorns, suffering severe injuries, and blood flowed everywhere. Seeing me walking alone on a flat path, they all sighed and said, 'The Buddha's child walks alone on the great road.' As I continued, I came to a place with tile-roofed houses and colorful towers, numbering in the thousands. There was a particularly tall house, and on top of it, a man with a large and imposing appearance was sitting by the window, wearing a black robe with four seams. I bowed to him, and the man on the upper floor said, 'Is it you, Stone? We have been separated for more than two thousand years.' At that moment, I felt as if I remembered this separation. I knew a couple named Ma Mu Shengcheng and his wife, who had died many years ago. The man on the upper floor asked, 'Do you recognize Shengcheng?' I replied, 'I do.' He said, 'Shengcheng did not strive diligently in life and now serves as a cleaner for me; his wife, however, was diligent and lives very happily.' [81]

    ”举手指西南一房曰：“孟妻在此也。”孟妻开窗见和，厚相慰问，遍访其家中大小安不消息，曰：“石君还时，可更见过，当因附书也。”俄见孟丞执?提箕，自阁西来，亦问家消息。阁上人曰：“闻鱼龙超精进为信，尔何所修行？”长和曰：“不食鱼肉，酒不经口，恒转尊经，救诸疾痛。”阁上人曰？“所传不妄也。”语久之闲，阁上人问都录主者：“审案石君名录，勿谬滥也。”主者案录云：“余三十年命在。”阁上人曰：“君欲归不？”和对曰：“愿归。”乃敕主者，以车骑两吏送之。长和拜辞，上车而归。前所行道，更有传馆吏民饮食储跱之具。倏忽至家，恶其尸臭，不欲附之，于尸头立；见其家亡妹于后推之，踣尸面上，因得稣活。道人支法山时未出家，闻和所说，遂定入道之志。法山者，咸和时人也。珠林七广记三百八十三 [82]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [82]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 举手指西南一房曰 - Literal expression meaning 'pointed to a room in the southwest and said.'
    • 孟妻在此也 - Statement indicating 'Meng's wife is here.'
    • 厚相慰问 - Polite expression meaning 'gave heartfelt condolences.'
    • 遍访其家中大小安不消息 - Phrase meaning 'inquired about the well-being of all members of the family.'
    • 可更见过，当因附书也 - Polite request meaning 'you can visit again, and I will send a letter.'
    • 俄见孟丞执?提箕，自阁西来 - Description meaning 'soon saw Meng the Chancellor carrying a basket, coming from the west of the hall.'
    • 亦问家消息 - Phrase meaning 'also asked about the family news.'
    • 阁上人曰 - Phrase meaning 'the person on the veranda said.'
    • 闻鱼龙超精进为信，尔何所修行 - Question meaning 'Have you heard that Fish and Dragon have advanced spiritually? What have you practiced?'
    • 长和曰 - Phrase meaning 'Chang He replied.'
    • 不食鱼肉，酒不经口，恒转尊经，救诸疾痛 - Statement meaning 'I do not eat fish or meat, do not drink alcohol, always recite the scriptures, and help those in pain.'
    • 阁上人曰 - Phrase meaning 'the person on the veranda said.'
    • 所传不妄也 - Statement meaning 'What has been said is not false.'
    • 语久之闲 - Phrase meaning 'after talking for a while.'
    • 阁上人问都录主者 - Phrase meaning 'the person on the veranda asked the chief recorder.'
    • 审案石君名录，勿谬滥也 - Phrase meaning 'check the list of Master Shi, do not make mistakes.'
    • 主者案录云 - Phrase meaning 'the chief recorder checked the list and said.'
    • 余三十年命在 - Statement meaning 'I have thirty years of life left.'
    • 阁上人曰 - Phrase meaning 'the person on the veranda said.'
    • 君欲归不 - Question meaning 'Do you want to return?'
    • 和对曰 - Phrase meaning 'He replied.'
    • 愿归 - Statement meaning 'I wish to return.'
    • 乃敕主者，以车骑两吏送之 - Phrase meaning 'then ordered the chief recorder to send two officials by carriage to escort him.'
    • 长和拜辞，上车而归 - Phrase meaning 'Chang He bowed and took leave, then got on the carriage and returned.'
    • 前所行道，更有传馆吏民饮食储跱之具 - Phrase meaning 'on the way, there were more relay stations with officials and civilians storing food and supplies.'
    • 倏忽至家，恶其尸臭，不欲附之，于尸头立 - Phrase meaning 'suddenly arrived home, disliked the smell of the corpse, did not want to touch it, and stood at the head of the corpse.'
    • 见其家亡妹于后推之，踣尸面上，因得稣活 - Phrase meaning 'saw his deceased sister-in-law push him from behind, causing him to fall on the face of the corpse, and thus he revived.'
    • 道人支法山时未出家，闻和所说，遂定入道之志 - Phrase meaning 'Monk Zhi Fashan, who had not yet entered the monastic life, heard what He said and thus decided to enter the path of the Dharma.'
    • 法山者，咸和时人也 - Phrase meaning 'Fashan was a person of the Xianhe period.'
    • 珠林七广记三百八十三 - Phrase meaning 'Jewel Forest, Volume Seven, General Records, Chapter Three Hundred and Eighty-Three.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 孟妻 - Refers to Meng's wife, a character in the story.
    • 孟丞 - Refers to Meng the Chancellor, a character in the story.
    • 鱼龙超精进为信 - A reference to spiritual advancement, possibly alluding to Buddhist or Taoist practices.
    • 尊经 - Refers to the scriptures, likely Buddhist or Taoist texts.
    • 石君 - Refers to Master Shi, a character in the story.
    • 道人支法山 - Refers to Monk Zhi Fashan, a historical figure who later became a monk.
    • 咸和 - Refers to the Xianhe period, a time during the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 珠林七广记三百八十三 - Refers to a specific chapter in the 'Jewel Forest,' a historical text.

    Proper Nouns

    • 孟妻 - Meng's wife.
    • 孟丞 - Meng the Chancellor.
    • 鱼龙 - Fish and Dragon, likely a metaphor for spiritual beings.
    • 长和 - Chang He.
    • 石君 - Master Shi.
    • 道人支法山 - Monk Zhi Fashan.
    • 咸和 - Xianhe period.
    • 珠林七广记三百八十三 - Jewel Forest, Volume Seven, General Records, Chapter Three Hundred and Eighty-Three.

    He pointed to a room in the southwest and said, "Meng's wife is here." Meng's wife opened the window and saw Chang He, and gave heartfelt condolences, inquiring about the well-being of all members of his family. She said, "When Master Shi returns, you can visit again, and I will send a letter." Soon, she saw Meng the Chancellor carrying a basket, coming from the west of the hall, and also asked about the family news. The person on the veranda said, "Have you heard that Fish and Dragon have advanced spiritually? What have you practiced?" Chang He replied, "I do not eat fish or meat, do not drink alcohol, always recite the scriptures, and help those in pain." The person on the veranda said, "What has been said is not false." After talking for a while, the person on the veranda asked the chief recorder, "Check the list of Master Shi, do not make mistakes." The chief recorder checked the list and said, "I have thirty years of life left." The person on the veranda said, "Do you want to return?" Chang He replied, "I wish to return." Then, he ordered the chief recorder to send two officials by carriage to escort him. Chang He bowed and took leave, then got on the carriage and returned. On the way, there were more relay stations with officials and civilians storing food and supplies. Suddenly, he arrived home, disliked the smell of the corpse, did not want to touch it, and stood at the head of the corpse. He saw his deceased sister-in-law push him from behind, causing him to fall on the face of the corpse, and thus he revived. Monk Zhi Fashan, who had not yet entered the monastic life, heard what He said and thus decided to enter the path of the Dharma. Fashan was a person of the Xianhe period. Jewel Forest, Volume Seven, General Records, Chapter Three Hundred and Eighty-Three. [82]

    赵沙门单，或作善，字道开，不知何许人也。别传高僧传在佛调之止舟云，炖煌人，本姓孟，少出家，欲穷栖岩谷，故先断谷食。初进?，三年后服练松脂，三十年后唯时吞小石子，石子下，辄复断酒脯杂果。体畏风寒，唯啖椒姜，气力微弱，而肤色润泽，行步如飞。山神数试，未曾倾动，仙人恒来，意亦不耐，每啮蒜以却之。端坐静念，昼夜不眠。久住抱罕，石虎建武二年，自西平迎来，至邺下，不乘舟车，日行七百余里。 [83]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [83]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 欲穷栖岩谷 - Wants to live in remote mountains and valleys.
    • 端坐静念，昼夜不眠 - Sits quietly and meditates, never sleeping day or night.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 别传高僧传 - A separate biography in the *High Monks' Transmission*.
    • 佛调之止舟云 - A story about Buddha adjusting his boat.
    • 炖煌 - Dunhuang, a city in Gansu Province.
    • 抱罕 - A place where the monk stayed.
    • 石虎建武二年 - The second year of Jianwu reign of Shi Hu.
    • 西平 - Xiping, a place from which the monk was brought.
    • 邺下 - Ye, the capital of the Later Zhao.

    Proper Nouns

    • 赵沙门单 - The name of the monk.
    • 孟 - The original surname of the monk.

    A Buddhist monk named Shan, sometimes written as Shan, with the courtesy name Dao Kai, whose origin is unknown. In a separate biography in the High Monks' Transmission and the story of Buddha adjusting his boat, it is said that he was from Dunhuang and originally surnamed Meng. He became a monk at a young age and wanted to live in remote mountains and valleys, so he first stopped eating grains. Initially, he took some unspecified substance, and three years later, he began to take pine resin. Thirty years later, he only occasionally swallowed small stones. After the stones passed, he stopped drinking alcohol and eating meat and fruits. His body fears cold and wind, and he only eats pepper and ginger. His strength is weak, but his skin is smooth and lustrous, and he walks like a flying bird. Mountain gods frequently tested him, but he never wavered. Immortals often came to him, but he also did not tolerate them. He would chew garlic to repel them. He sat quietly and meditated, never sleeping day or night. He stayed for a long time in Bao Han. In the second year of Jianwu reign of Shi Hu, he was brought from Xiping and arrived at Ye. He did not ride in boats or carriages and traveled over seven hundred miles a day. [83]

    过南安，度一童子为沙弥，年十三四，行亦及开。既至，居于昭德佛图，服缕粗弊，背?恒袒。于屋内作棚阁，高八九尺，上织菅为帐，禅于其中。绝谷七载，常御杂药，药有松脂伏苓之气。善能治目疾，常周行墟野，救疗百姓，王公远近，赠遗累积，皆受而施散，一毫无余。石虎之末，逆知其乱，乃与弟子南之许昌。升平三年，来至建业，复适番禺，住罗浮山，荫卧林薄，邈然自怡。以其年七月卒，遗言露尸林里，弟子从之。陈郡袁彦伯，兴宁元年，为南海太守，与弟颖叔登游此岳，致敬其骸，烧香作礼。珠林二十七 [84]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [84]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 過南安 - Literally 'pass through Nan'an,' indicating a journey or transit through a place.
    • 度一童子為沙彌 - 'Initiate a young boy as a novice monk,' a ritual act of ordination.
    • 行亦及開 - 'His behavior was also mature,' describing the boy's demeanor.
    • 居於昭德佛圖 - 'Reside in the Zhaode Pagoda,' indicating a place of religious practice.
    • 服繡粗弊，背?恒袒 - 'Wear coarse, tattered robes, often exposing his back,' describing his ascetic lifestyle.
    • 于屋内作棚閣，高八九尺 - 'Build a small pavilion inside the house, about eight or nine feet high,' describing a specific structure.
    • 上織菅為帳 - 'Weave reeds into a canopy,' detailing the construction of the pavilion.
    • 禪于其中 - 'Meditate inside it,' indicating his practice of meditation.
    • 絕谷七載 - 'Abstain from food for seven years,' describing his extreme asceticism.
    • 常御雜藥，藥有松脂伏苓之氣 - 'Regularly take various medicines, which have the scent of pine resin and poria,' describing his medicinal regimen.
    • 善能治目疾 - 'Skilled in treating eye diseases,' indicating his medical expertise.
    • 常周行墟野，救療百姓 - 'Frequently travel through the countryside, healing the common people,' describing his charitable work.
    • 王公遠近，贈遺累積，皆受而施散，一無餘 - 'Both local and distant nobles offered gifts, which he accepted and distributed, leaving nothing for himself,' describing his generosity.
    • 石虎之末，逆知其亂，乃與弟子南之許昌 - 'At the end of Shitou's reign, he foresaw the chaos and moved south to Xuchang with his disciples,' indicating his foresight and action.
    • 升平三年，來至建業，復適番禺，住羅浮山 - 'In the third year of Shengping, he came to Jianye, then traveled to Panyu, residing on Luofu Mountain,' describing his travels.
    • 陰卧林薄，邈然自怡 - 'Rest in the shade of the forest, finding joy in solitude,' describing his lifestyle.
    • 以其年七月卒，遺言露尸林里，弟子從之 - 'He died in July of that year, leaving a final instruction to be buried in the woods, which his disciples followed,' describing his final wishes.
    • 陳郡袁彦伯，興寧元年，為南海太守，與弟颖叔登游此岳，致敬其骸，燒香作禮 - 'In the first year of Xingning, Chen Jun's Yuan Yanbo, as the governor of Nanhai, climbed this mountain with his brother Ying Shu to pay respects to his remains, burning incense and performing rituals,' describing a later visit.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 南安 - A place name, likely referring to a region in ancient China.
    • 沙彌 - A novice monk in Buddhism, indicating the religious context.
    • 昭德佛圖 - A pagoda or temple named Zhaode, a place of Buddhist practice.
    • 絕谷 - Abstaining from food, a practice of extreme asceticism in Buddhism.
    • 松脂伏苓 - Ingredients in traditional Chinese medicine, indicating his medical regimen.
    • 石虎 - A historical figure, likely referring to Shi Hu, a ruler of the Later Zhao dynasty.
    • 升平 - A reign title of Emperor Mu of Jin.
    • 建業 - An ancient name for Nanjing, a major city in Chinese history.
    • 番禺 - An ancient name for Guangzhou, a major city in southern China.
    • 羅浮山 - A famous mountain in Guangdong, known for its religious and cultural significance.
    • 陳郡袁彦伯 - A historical figure, likely referring to Yuan Yanbo, a governor of Nanhai.
    • 興寧 - A reign title of Emperor Mu of Jin.
    • 南海 - Refers to the region of Nanhai, likely referring to Guangdong or nearby areas.
    • 珠林二十七 - Likely a reference to a specific text or chapter in a Buddhist canon, indicating the source of the passage.

    Proper Nouns

    • 南安 - A place name.
    • 沙彌 - A religious term.
    • 昭德佛圖 - A place name.
    • 石虎 - A historical figure.
    • 建業 - A place name.
    • 番禺 - A place name.
    • 羅浮山 - A place name.
    • 陳郡袁彦伯 - A historical figure.
    • 興寧 - A reign title.
    • 南海 - A place name.
    • 珠林二十七 - A reference to a specific text or chapter.

    A Man of Asceticism and Benevolence

    Passing through Nan'an, he initiated a young boy, aged thirteen or fourteen, as a novice monk. The boy's behavior was also mature. Upon arrival, he resided in the Zhaode Pagoda, wearing coarse and tattered robes, often exposing his back. Inside the house, he built a small pavilion, about eight or nine feet high, and wove reeds into a canopy, meditating within it. He abstained from food for seven years, regularly taking various medicines, which had the scent of pine resin and poria. Skilled in treating eye diseases, he frequently traveled through the countryside, healing the common people. Both local and distant nobles offered gifts, which he accepted and distributed, leaving nothing for himself. At the end of Shi Hu's reign, foreseeing the chaos, he moved south to Xuchang with his disciples. In the third year of Shengping, he came to Jianye, then traveled to Panyu, residing on Luofu Mountain, resting in the shade of the forest, finding joy in solitude. He died in July of that year, leaving a final instruction to be buried in the woods, which his disciples followed. In the first year of Xingning, Chen Jun's Yuan Yanbo, as the governor of Nanhai, climbed this mountain with his brother Ying Shu to pay respects to his remains, burning incense and performing rituals. Jewel Forest, Chapter 27. [84]

    秦徐义者，高陆人也，少奉法，为苻坚尚书。坚末，兵革蜂起，贼获义，将加戮害，乃埋其两足，编发于树。夜中专念观世音，有顷得眠，梦人谓之曰：“今事亟矣，何暇眠乎？”义便惊起，见守防之士，并疲而寝；乃试自奋动，手发既解，足亦得脱，因而遁去。百余步，隐小丛草，便闻追者交驰，火炬星陈，互绕此丛，而竟无见者。天明，贼散，归投邺寺，遂得免之。珠林十七广记一百十
    　　秦毕览，东平人也，少奉法，随慕容垂北征，没虏，单马逃窜。虏追骑将及，览至心诵念观世音；既得免脱，因入深山，迷惑失道，又专心归念，中夜，见一道人，法服持锡，示以途径，遂得还路，安隐至家。珠林十七广记一百十
    　　宋沙门法称，临终曰：“有松山人告我；‘江东刘将军应受天命。’并以三十二璧一饼金为信。”宋祖闻之，命僧惠义往松山，七日七夜行道，梦有一长○翁指示；及觉，分明忆所在，掘而得之。广记二百七十六 [85]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [85]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 兵革蜂起 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'armies rose up like bees,' describing widespread military uprisings.
    • 迷惑失道 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'lost one's way and became confused.'
    • 安隐至家 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'arrived home safely and quietly.'
    • 专念观世音 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'devoted oneself to chanting the name of Guanyin.'
    • 秦徐义者，高陆人也 - Formal introduction to Xu Yi, a man from Gaolü.
    • 少奉法 - Means 'when young, he served the law,' indicating his early involvement in legal matters.
    • 为苻坚尚书 - Means 'served as a high official under Fu Jian,' where Fu Jian was the ruler of the Former Qin dynasty.
    • 贼获义，将加戮害 - Means 'the rebels captured Xu Yi and intended to kill him.'
    • 乃埋其两足，编发于树 - Describes a cruel punishment where Xu Yi's feet were buried and his hair tied to a tree.
    • 夜中专念观世音 - Means 'he devoted himself to chanting the name of Guanyin at night.'
    • 有顷得眠 - Means 'after a while, he fell asleep.'
    • 梦人谓之曰 - Means 'a person in a dream told him.'
    • 今事亟矣，何暇眠乎 - Means 'the situation is urgent, why should you sleep?'
    • 便惊起 - Means 'he woke up suddenly.'
    • 见守防之士，并疲而寝 - Means 'he saw the guards, all tired and asleep.'
    • 乃试自奋动 - Means 'he then tried to move himself.'
    • 手发既解，足亦得脱 - Means 'his hands were freed, and his feet also escaped.'
    • 因而遁去 - Means 'and thus he fled.'
    • 百余步，隐小丛草 - Means 'after a hundred steps, he hid in a small patch of grass.'
    • 便闻追者交驰，火炬星陈，互绕此丛，而竟无见者 - Describes how the pursuers passed by without seeing him.
    • 天明，贼散，归投邺寺，遂得免之 - Means 'when dawn came, the rebels dispersed, and he returned to the temple in Ye, thus escaping.'
    • 珠林十七广记一百十 - Refers to the source of the story, a collection of anecdotes.
    • 秦毕览，东平人也 - Formal introduction to Bi Luan, a man from Dongping.
    • 少奉法，随慕容垂北征，没虏，单马逃窜 - Means 'when young, he served the law, followed Murong Chui on a northern campaign, was captured by the enemy, and fled alone on horseback.'
    • 虏追骑将及 - Means 'the enemy's cavalry was about to catch up.'
    • 览至心诵念观世音 - Means 'Bi Luan devoted himself to chanting the name of Guanyin.'
    • 既得免脱，因入深山，迷惑失道 - Means 'after escaping, he entered a deep mountain and lost his way.'
    • 又专心归念 - Means 'he then focused his mind on returning.'
    • 中夜，见一道人，法服持锡，示以途径 - Means 'in the middle of the night, he saw a monk in monastic robes holding a staff, showing him the way.'
    • 遂得还路，安隐至家 - Means 'and thus he found his way back, arriving safely home.'
    • 珠林十七广记一百十 - Refers to the source of the story, a collection of anecdotes.
    • 宋沙门法称，临终曰 - Formal introduction to Fa Chen, a monk from the Song dynasty, who said on his deathbed.
    • 有松山人告我 - Means 'a man from Songshan told me.'
    • 江东刘将军应受天命 - Means 'the General Liu in Jiangdong should receive the mandate of heaven.'
    • 并以三十二璧一饼金为信 - Means 'and gave me thirty-two jade disks and a piece of gold as proof.'
    • 宋祖闻之，命僧惠义往松山，七日七夜行道 - Means 'Emperor Song heard this and ordered the monk Hu Yi to go to Songshan, where he practiced for seven days and seven nights.'
    • 梦有一长○翁指示 - Means 'he dreamed of an old man with a long beard who pointed the way.'
    • 及觉，分明忆所在，掘而得之 - Means 'when he woke up, he clearly remembered the location and dug it up.'
    • 广记二百七十六 - Refers to the source of the story, a collection of anecdotes.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 苻坚 - The ruler of the Former Qin dynasty.
    • 尚书 - A high-ranking official in ancient China.
    • 观世音 - The bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara, known for compassion and protection.
    • 邺寺 - A temple in Ye, an important city in ancient China.
    • 慕容垂 - A prominent figure in the Later Yan dynasty.
    • 宋祖 - Emperor Song, the founder of the Song dynasty.
    • 松山 - A mountain in China, often associated with spiritual and historical significance.

    Proper Nouns

    • 徐义 - Xu Yi, a historical figure.
    • 苻坚 - Fu Jian, the ruler of the Former Qin dynasty.
    • 观世音 - Guanyin, the bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara.
    • 邺寺 - A temple in Ye.
    • 慕容垂 - Murong Chui, a prominent figure in the Later Yan dynasty.
    • 宋祖 - Emperor Song, the founder of the Song dynasty.
    • 松山 - Songshan, a mountain in China.
    • 法称 - Fa Chen, a monk from the Song dynasty.
    • 惠义 - Hu Yi, a monk who followed Fa Chen's instructions.

    In the Qin state, there was a man named Xu Yi from Gaolü. When he was young, he served the law and became a high official under Fu Jian. During the later years of Fu Jian's reign, widespread military uprisings broke out, and Xu Yi was captured by the rebels, who intended to kill him. They buried his feet and tied his hair to a tree. At night, Xu Yi devoted himself to chanting the name of Guanyin (Avalokiteshvara). After a while, he fell asleep and had a dream where a person told him, "The situation is urgent, why should you sleep?" Xu Yi woke up suddenly and saw that the guards were all tired and asleep. He then tried to move himself, and his hands were freed, and his feet also escaped. Thus, he fled. After a hundred steps, he hid in a small patch of grass and heard the pursuers passing by, their torches arranged in a star-like formation, circling around the patch of grass, but they never saw him. When dawn came, the rebels dispersed, and Xu Yi returned to the temple in Ye, thus escaping. This story is recorded in Zhulin (Jade Forest) Volume 17, Chapter 110.

    In the Qin state, there was another man named Bi Luan from Dongping. When he was young, he served the law and followed Murong Chui on a northern campaign, where he was captured by the enemy and fled alone on horseback. The enemy's cavalry was about to catch up, so Bi Luan devoted himself to chanting the name of Guanyin. After escaping, he entered a deep mountain and lost his way. He then focused his mind on returning, and in the middle of the night, he saw a monk in monastic robes holding a staff, who showed him the way. Thus, he found his way back and arrived safely home. This story is also recorded in Zhulin (Jade Forest) Volume 17, Chapter 110.

    In the Song dynasty, there was a monk named Fa Chen. On his deathbed, he said, "A man from Songshan told me, 'The General Liu in Jiangdong should receive the mandate of heaven.' He also gave me thirty-two jade disks and a piece of gold as proof." Emperor Song heard this and ordered the monk Hu Yi to go to Songshan, where he practiced for seven days and seven nights. Hu Yi dreamed of an old man with a long beard who pointed the way. When he woke up, he clearly remembered the location and dug it up. This story is recorded in Guangji (Extensive Records) Volume 276. [85]

    宋仇那跋摩者，此言功德，高僧传云此言功德铠案拔摩是铠记误也种罽宾王子也，幼而出家，号三藏法师。宋初，来游中国，宣译至典甚众。律行精高，莫与为比。惠高僧传惠作慧观沙门钦其风德，要来京师，居于只洹寺。当时来诣者，疑非凡人，而神味深密，莫能测焉。尝赴请于钟山定林寺，时诸道俗多采众华，布僧席下，验求真人；诸僧所坐，华同萎悴，而跋摩席华，鲜荣若初，于是京师歙然增加敬意。至元嘉八年九月十八日卒，都无痾患，但结跏趺坐，敛衽?手，乃经信宿，容色不变。于时或谓深禅，既而得遗书于筵下，云获沙门二果，乃知其终。弟子侍侧，普闻馨烟。京师赴会二百余人，其夕转经，户外集听盈阶。将晓，而西南上有云气勃然，俄有一物，长将一?，绕尸而去，同集咸睹云。跋未亡时，作三十偈，以付弟子曰：“可送示天竺僧也。”法苑珠林四十二 [86]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [86]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 此言功德 - This means merit or This is called merit.
    • 高僧传云此言功德铠案拔摩是铠记误也 - The Biographies of Eminent Monks states that this means 'merit armor,' but the record is in error.
    • 当时来诣者，疑非凡人，而神味深密，莫能测焉 - At that time, those who came to see him suspected that he was not an ordinary person, but his spiritual essence was profound and mysterious, beyond comprehension.
    • 于时或谓深禅，既而得遗书于筵下，云获沙门二果，乃知其终 - At that time, some thought he was in deep meditation, but later a note was found under the seat saying he had attained the second level of arhatship, thus it was known that he had passed away.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 仇那跋摩 - A Buddhist monk who lived during the Southern and Northern Dynasties period.
    • 高僧传 - The Biographies of Eminent Monks, a historical record of notable Buddhist monks.
    • 罽宾 - An ancient kingdom in the northwest of India, also known as Kāpiśa or Kāśmīra.
    • 三藏法师 - A title given to a monk who has mastered the three baskets of Buddhist scriptures: Vinaya (monastic rules), Sutra (discourses), and Abhidharma (philosophical treatises).
    • 只洹寺 - The Jetavana Monastery, a famous Buddhist monastery in ancient India.
    • 钟山定林寺 - Dinglin Temple on Zhongshan Mountain, a significant Buddhist temple in China.
    • 元嘉八年 - The 8th year of the Yuanjia era (431 AD) during the Liu Song dynasty.
    • 法苑珠林 - The Jewel Forest of the Dharma Realm, a compendium of Buddhist stories and teachings.

    Proper Nouns

    • 紹興 - Era name of the Southern Song; rendered as Shaoxing.
    • 惠高僧传惠作慧观沙门钦其风德，要来京师 - The Biographies of Eminent Monks records that Master Huiguan, a monk, admired his virtue and invited him to the capital.
    • 京师赴会二百余人，其夕转经，户外集听盈阶 - Over 200 people from the capital came to the gathering, and that evening, as they chanted scriptures, people gathered outside the door, filling the steps.
    • 将晓，而西南上有云气勃然，俄有一物，长将一?，绕尸而去，同集咸睹云 - As dawn approached, a dense cloud appeared in the southwest, and soon a creature, about a foot long, circled his body and then disappeared. All present saw this.

    The Translator of Merit: Master Kao Na

    This is called the Translator of Merit. According to the Biographies of Eminent Monks, it means "merit armor," but the record is in error. He was a prince of Kāpiśa (Kāśmīra) who, at a young age, entered the monastic life and was known as a Tripiṭaka Master. In the early Song dynasty, he came to China, where he translated many important Buddhist scriptures. His observance of precepts was extremely high, unmatched by others. Master Huiguan, a monk, admired his virtue and invited him to the capital, where he lived at the Jetavana Monastery.

    At that time, those who came to see him suspected that he was not an ordinary person, but his spiritual essence was profound and mysterious, beyond comprehension. He once attended a gathering at Dinglin Temple on Zhongshan Mountain. At that time, many laypeople and monks picked various flowers and placed them under the monk's seat to test for a true sage. The flowers under the other monks' seats withered, but the flowers under Master Kao's seat remained fresh and vibrant. Thus, the capital city's respect for him increased.

    He passed away on the 18th day of the 9th month in the 8th year of the Yuanjia era (431 AD). He died without any illness, just sitting in the full lotus position, with his sleeves folded and hands together, for two nights, with his appearance unchanged. At that time, some thought he was in deep meditation, but later a note was found under the seat saying he had attained the second level of arhatship, thus it was known that he had passed away. His disciples who were by his side smelled a fragrant smoke. Over 200 people from the capital came to the gathering, and that evening, as they chanted scriptures, people gathered outside the door, filling the steps.

    As dawn approached, a dense cloud appeared in the southwest, and soon a creature, about a foot long, circled his body and then disappeared. All present saw this. Before he passed away, he composed thirty verses and said to his disciples, "You can show these to monks from India." This account is found in Chapter 42 of The Jewel Forest of the Dharma Realm. [86]

    宋陈安居者，襄阳县人也，伯父少事巫俗，鼓舞祭祀，神影庙宇，充满其宅；父独敬信释法，旦夕斋戒。后伯父亡，无子，父以安居绍焉。安居虽即伯舍，而理行精求，淫飨之事，废不复设。于是遂得笃病，而发则为歌神之曲，迷闷惛僻，如此者弥岁，而执心愈固。常誓曰：“若我不杀之志，遂当亏夺者，必先自脔截四体，乃就其事。”家人并谏之，安居不听。经积二年，永初元年，病发，遂绝，但心下微暖，家人不敛；至七日夜，守视之者，觉尸足闲如有风来飘衣动衾，于是而稣有声，家人初惧尸蹶，并走避之，既而稍能转动，末求饮浆，家人喜之，问从何来？安居乃具说所经见云：初有人若使者；将刀数十，呼将去。从者欲缚之，使者曰：“此人有福，未可缚也。 [87]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [87]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 宋陈安居者，襄阳县人也 - Formal introduction of a person, 'Cao Chen Anju, a native of Xiangyang County in the state of Song.'
    • 伯父少事巫俗，鼓舞祭祀，神影庙宇，充满其宅 - Describes the elder brother's involvement in shamanistic practices, including dancing and sacrificing, with temple images and structures filling his house.
    • 父独敬信释法，旦夕斋戒 - Indicates the father's solitary reverence and trust in Buddhist law, practicing daily fasting and purification.
    • 后伯父亡，无子，父以安居绍焉 - States that after the elder brother died without an heir, the father designated Anju as his successor.
    • 安居虽即伯舍，而理行精求，淫飨之事，废不复设 - Suggests that although Anju inherited the elder brother's house, he pursued a more refined and disciplined life, abandoning excessive feasting and sacrificial rituals.
    • 发则为歌神之曲，迷闷惛僻，如此者弥岁，而执心愈固 - Describes Anju's episodes where he would sing songs to the gods, becoming confused and dazed, persisting for a year, with his resolve growing stronger.
    • 若我不杀之志，遂当亏夺者，必先自脔截四体，乃就其事 - Anju's vow that if his determination to kill (possibly referring to himself) were to be thwarted, he would first cut off his limbs before proceeding.
    • 经积二年，永初元年，病发，遂绝，但心下微暖，家人不敛 - Indicates that after two years, in the first year of Yongchu, Anju fell ill, seemingly died, but with warmth still in his chest, so the family did not bury him.
    • 至七日夜，守视之者，觉尸足闲如有风来飘衣动衾，于是而稣有声，家人初惧尸蹶，并走避之，既而稍能转动，末求饮浆 - Describes the event where, on the seventh night, those watching noticed the corpse's feet moving as if wind was blowing, clothes and bedding fluttering, and then Anju made a sound of revival. Initially, the family feared the corpse was coming back to life and fled, but later, he began to move slightly and eventually asked for water.
    • 安居乃具说所经见云 - Anju then detailed what he had experienced.
    • 初有人若使者；将刀数十，呼将去 - Describes an initial encounter with someone resembling a messenger who carried many knives and called for Anju to follow.
    • 从者欲缚之，使者曰：“此人有福，未可缚也” - Indicates that followers wanted to bind him, but the messenger said, 'This person has good fortune, do not bind him.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋 - Refers to the state of Song, one of the warring states during the Warring States period.
    • 襄阳县 - A specific location in the state of Song, indicating the geographical context.
    • 巫俗 - Refers to shamanistic customs, which were prevalent in ancient China.
    • 鼓舞祭祀 - Describes the practice of dance and sacrifice, common in shamanistic rituals.
    • 神影庙宇 - Refers to temple images and structures, which were central to shamanistic worship.
    • 释法 - Refers to Buddhist law, indicating the religious context of the father's beliefs.
    • 斋戒 - Refers to fasting and purification, common practices in Buddhism.
    • 永初元年 - The first year of the Yongchu era, a specific time period in Chinese history.
    • 使者 - Refers to a messenger or envoy, often associated with divine or supernatural beings in classical texts.
    • 刀 - Refers to knives, which can symbolize punishment or execution in this context.

    Proper Nouns

    • 宋 - The state of Song.
    • 襄阳县 - Xiangyang County.
    • 永初元年 - The first year of the Yongchu era.

    A Man Named Chen Anju from Xiangyang County

    Chen Anju was a native of Xiangyang County in the state of Song. His elder uncle was involved in shamanistic customs, performing dances and sacrifices, with temple images and structures filling his house. His father, on the other hand, alone revered and trusted Buddhist law, practicing daily fasting and purification. After the elder uncle died without an heir, the father designated Anju as his successor. Although Anju inherited the elder uncle's house, he pursued a more refined and disciplined life, abandoning excessive feasting and sacrificial rituals. As a result, he fell ill severely, and during his episodes, he would sing songs to the gods, becoming confused and dazed. This persisted for a year, and his resolve grew stronger. He often vowed, "If my determination to kill (possibly referring to himself) were to be thwarted, I would first cut off my limbs before proceeding." His family tried to dissuade him, but Anju refused to listen. After two years, in the first year of Yongchu, his illness flared up, and he seemingly died, but with warmth still in his chest, so the family did not bury him. On the seventh night, those watching noticed the corpse's feet moving as if wind was blowing, clothes and bedding fluttering, and then Anju made a sound of revival. Initially, the family feared the corpse was coming back to life and fled, but later, he began to move slightly and eventually asked for water. The family was delighted and asked where he had come from. Anju then detailed what he had experienced: initially, there was someone resembling a messenger; carrying many knives, he called for Anju to follow. The followers wanted to bind him, but the messenger said, "This person has good fortune, do not bind him." [87]

    ”行三百许里，至一城府，楼宇甚整，使者将至数处，如局司所居，末有人授纸笔与安居曰：“可疏二十四通死名。”安居即如言疏名成数通，有一侍从内出，扬声大呼曰：“安居可入。”既入，称有教付刺奸狱，吏两人，一云：“与大械。”一云：“此人颇有福，可止三尺械。”疑论不判，乃共视文书，久之，遂与三尺械。有顷，见有贵人，翼从数十，形貌都雅，谓安居曰：“汝那得来？”安居具陈所由，贵人曰：“汝伯有罪，但宜录治，以先植小福，故蹔得游散，乃敢告诉？吾与汝父，幼少有旧，见汝依然可随我共游观也。”狱吏不肯释械，曰：“府君无教，不敢专辄。”贵人曰：“但付我，不使走逸也。”乃释之。贵人将安居遍至诸地狱，备观众苦，略与经文相符。游历未竟有传教来云：“府君唤安居。”安居茫惧然求救于贵人，贵人曰：“汝自无罪，但以实对，必无忧也。 [88]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [88]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 行三百许里 - A measure of distance, meaning 'traveled about three hundred li.'
    • 至一城府 - Means 'arrived at a city or administrative center.'
    • 楼宇甚整 - Means 'the buildings were very well-organized or orderly.'
    • 使者将至数处 - Means 'the messenger went to several places.'
    • 如局司所居 - Means 'as if to the residence of the bureau director.'
    • 末有人授纸笔与安居曰 - Means 'finally, someone gave paper and ink to Anju and said.'
    • 可疏二十四通死名 - Means 'you can write down twenty-four names of the dead.'
    • 扬声大呼曰 - Means 'shouted loudly and called out.'
    • 安居可入 - Means 'Anju can enter.'
    • 称有教付刺奸狱 - Means 'said there was an order to hand over to the prison for investigating crimes.'
    • 一云 - Means 'one said.'
    • 此人颇有福 - Means 'this person has quite a bit of fortune.'
    • 可止三尺械 - Means 'can stop at a three-foot chain.'
    • 疑论不判 - Means 'doubtful discussion without a decision.'
    • 乃共视文书 - Means 'they then all looked at the documents.'
    • 遂与三尺械 - Means 'so they gave him a three-foot chain.'
    • 有顷 - Means 'after a while.'
    • 翼从数十 - Means 'with several attendants.'
    • 形貌都雅 - Means 'appearance was very elegant.'
    • 汝那得来 - Means 'how did you come here?'
    • 汝伯有罪 - Means 'your uncle has a crime.'
    • 但宜录治 - Means 'should be recorded and punished.'
    • 以先植小福 - Means 'because of a small amount of previous good fortune.'
    • 故蹔得游散 - Means 'so temporarily got to wander around.'
    • 乃敢告诉 - Means 'so dared to report.'
    • 吾与汝父 - Means 'I and your father.'
    • 幼少有旧 - Means 'had old acquaintances when young.'
    • 见汝依然可随我共游观也 - Means 'seeing you still, can follow me to tour together.'
    • 狱吏不肯释械 - Means 'the prison guard did not release the chain.'
    • 府君无教 - Means 'the lord has no instruction.'
    • 不敢专辄 - Means 'dare not act on my own.'
    • 但付我 - Means 'just give it to me.'
    • 不使走逸也 - Means 'so he will not escape.'
    • 乃释之 - Means 'so they released him.'
    • 贵人将安居遍至诸地狱 - Means 'the noble person took Anju to all the hells.'
    • 备观众苦 - Means 'to see all the sufferings.'
    • 略与经文相符 - Means 'roughly matching the scriptures.'
    • 游历未竟有传教来云 - Means 'while touring, a messenger came and said.'
    • 府君唤安居 - Means 'the lord called Anju.'
    • 茫惧然求救于贵人 - Means 'feeling confused and fearful, sought help from the noble person.'
    • 汝自无罪 - Means 'you yourself have no crime.'
    • 但以实对 - Means 'just answer truthfully.'
    • 必无忧也 - Means 'there will be no worry.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 使者 - Refers to a messenger or envoy.
    • 局司 - Refers to the bureau director or a high-ranking official.
    • 刺奸狱 - Refers to the prison for investigating crimes.
    • 三尺械 - Refers to a three-foot chain, a form of restraint.
    • 贵人 - Refers to a noble person or a high-ranking official.
    • 地狱 - Refers to hells or places of punishment in Buddhist cosmology.
    • 经文 - Refers to the scriptures, likely Buddhist or Taoist texts.
    • 府君 - Refers to a high-ranking official or a lord.

    Proper Nouns

    • 安ju - The name of the main character.
    • 局司 - The bureau director or a high-ranking official.
    • 刺奸狱 - The prison for investigating crimes.
    • 贵人 - The noble person or high-ranking official.
    • 地狱 - The hells or places of punishment in Buddhist cosmology.
    • 经文 - The scriptures, likely Buddhist or Taoist texts.
    • 府君 - The high-ranking official or lord.

    Traveled about three hundred li, Anju arrived at a city or administrative center where the buildings were very well-organized. The messenger went to several places, including the residence of the bureau director. Finally, someone gave paper and ink to Anju and said, "You can write down twenty-four names of the dead." Anju wrote down the names as instructed. A servant then came out and shouted loudly, "Anju, you may enter." Upon entering, it was said there was an order to hand over to the prison for investigating crimes. Two officials debated, one saying, "Use the large chain," and the other, "This person has quite a bit of fortune; a three-foot chain will suffice." Unable to reach a decision, they all looked at the documents for a long time and eventually decided to give him a three-foot chain. After a while, a noble person appeared with several attendants, his appearance very elegant. He asked Anju, "How did you come here?" Anju explained his situation. The noble person said, "Your uncle has a crime, but he should be recorded and punished. Because of a small amount of previous good fortune, you temporarily got to wander around, so you dared to report. I and your father had old acquaintances when young. Seeing you still, I can take you on a tour." The prison guard did not release the chain, saying, "The lord has no instruction; I dare not act on my own." The noble person replied, "Just give it to me; I will not let him escape." So they released him. The noble person took Anju to all the hells, showing him all the sufferings, which roughly matched the scriptures. While touring, a messenger came and said, "The lord calls Anju." Feeling confused and fearful, Anju sought help from the noble person. The noble person reassured him, "You yourself have no crime; just answer truthfully, and there will be no worry." [88]

    ”安居至合，见有钳梏者数百，一时俱进，安居在第三，既至阶下，一人服冠冕，立于囚前，读诸罪簿：其第一者云，昔娶妻之始，夫妇为誓，有子无子，终不相弃，而其人本是祭酒，妻亦奉道，共化导徒众，得士女弟子，因而奸之，遂弃本妻，妻常冤诉。府君曰：“汝夫妇违誓大义，不罪二，终罪一也；师资义着在三，而奸之，是父子相淫，无以异也。付法局详刑！”次读第二女人辞牒，忘其姓名，云家在南阳冠军县黄水里，家安爨器于福灶口，而此妇眠重，婴儿于灶上匍匐走行，粪污爨器中，此妇寤，已即请谢神，祗盥洗精熟，而其舅乃骂詈此妇，言无有天道鬼神，置此女人，得行污秽，司令闻知此，录送之。府君曰：“眠重非过，小儿无知，又已请谢神明，是无罪也；舅骂詈言无道，诬谤幽灵，可录之来。”须臾而到，赤索捉至。 [89]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [89]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 安居至合 - A proper name or title, possibly referring to a person or a place, but its exact meaning is unclear.
    • 钳梏者数百 - Refers to individuals who are shackled or restrained, and '数百' means 'hundreds.' Describes a large group of prisoners.
    • 一时俱进 - Means 'all advanced at once,' indicating that the prisoners were brought forward together.
    • 在第三 - Indicates the position or order, meaning 'in the third place.'
    • 既至阶下 - Means 'once they reached the steps below,' describing the location where the prisoners arrived.
    • 一人服冠冕 - Describes a person wearing a crown or hat, likely a judge or official.
    • 读诸罪簿 - Means 'read the records of various crimes,' referring to the official documentation of the prisoners' offenses.
    • 其第一者云 - Indicates the first entry in the records, '云' is an archaic particle used to introduce a statement.
    • 昔娶妻之始，夫妇为誓，有子无子，终不相弃 - This is a vow made by the couple at the time of their marriage, promising to stay together regardless of whether they had children or not.
    • 而其人本是祭酒，妻亦奉道，共化导徒众，得士女弟子，因而奸之，遂弃本妻 - The man was originally a *zhijiu* (a high-ranking official in a religious or scholarly organization), and his wife also followed the same path. They together led and educated many disciples, but he committed adultery and abandoned his original wife.
    • 妻常冤诉 - The wife frequently complained of her injustice.
    • 府君曰 - '府君' is a respectful term for a local official or judge.
    • 汝夫妇违誓大义，不罪二，终罪一也 - The judge says, 'You both violated the great principle of your vow, and you are not guilty of two crimes, but one.'
    • 师资义着在三，而奸之，是父子相淫，无以异也 - The relationship between teacher and disciple is considered sacred, and committing adultery is akin to incest, which is a grave sin.
    • 付法局详刑 - '付法局' refers to the legal department, and '详刑' means to determine the punishment.
    • 次读第二女人辞牒 - The judge then reads the petition of the second woman.
    • 忘其姓名 - The name of the woman is forgotten.
    • 云家在南阳冠军县黄水里 - She says her home is in Huangshui Village, Junxian County, Nanyang.
    • 家安爨器于福灶口 - She placed cooking utensils at the mouth of the 'Fuzao' (a special stove).
    • 而此妇眠重，婴儿于灶上匍匐走行，粪污爨器中 - The woman slept heavily, and her baby crawled on the stove, defecating in the cooking utensils.
    • 此妇寤，已即请谢神，祗盥洗精熟 - Upon waking, the woman immediately apologized to the gods and washed the utensils thoroughly.
    • 而其舅乃骂詈此妇，言无有天道鬼神，置此女人，得行污秽 - Her brother-in-law scolded her, saying there are no gods or spirits, and that she allowed such filth to occur.
    • 司令闻知此，录送之 - The officials heard about this and took her to be punished.
    • 眠重非过，小儿无知，又已请谢神明，是无罪也 - The judge says that sleeping heavily is not a fault, and the baby was innocent. The woman had already apologized to the gods, so she is not guilty.
    • 舅骂詈言无道，诬谤幽灵，可录之来 - The brother-in-law's scolding is unrighteous, and he is spreading false accusations against the spirits. He can be brought in for punishment.
    • 须臾而到，赤索捉至 - Shortly after, he was brought in with a red rope around his neck.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 祭酒 - A high-ranking official in a religious or scholarly organization, often translated as 'chief priest' or 'scholar-official.'
    • 福灶 - A special stove or hearth, often associated with religious or ritual practices.
    • 爨器 - Cooking utensils.
    • 法局 - The legal department or court.
    • 详刑 - Determining the appropriate punishment.
    • 南阳冠军县黄水里 - A specific location, indicating the woman's hometown.
    • 舅 - Brother-in-law.
    • 赤索 - A red rope, often used as a symbol of restraint or punishment in ancient China.

    Proper Nouns

    • 安居 - A proper name or title, possibly referring to a person or a place.
    • 祭酒 - A high-ranking official in a religious or scholarly organization.
    • 法局 - The legal department or court.
    • 南阳冠军县黄水里 - A specific location, indicating the woman's hometown.
    • 赤索 - A red rope, often used as a symbol of restraint or punishment in ancient China.

    Translation:

    At the assembly, there were hundreds of shackled prisoners who were brought forward all at once, and "Anju" was in the third position. Once they reached the steps below, a man wearing a crown stood before the prisoners and read the records of their crimes. The first entry stated that when the man married, he and his wife took an oath: whether they had children or not, they would never abandon each other. However, the man was originally a high-ranking official in a religious or scholarly organization, and his wife also followed the same path. Together, they led and educated many disciples, but he committed adultery and abandoned his original wife. The wife frequently complained of her injustice. The judge said, "You both violated the great principle of your vow, and you are not guilty of two crimes, but one; the relationship between teacher and disciple is sacred, and committing adultery is akin to incest, which is a grave sin. Hand this case over to the legal department for sentencing!"

    Next, the judge read the petition of the second woman. Her name is forgotten, but she said her home was in Huangshui Village, Junxian County, Nanyang. She had placed cooking utensils at the mouth of a special stove. One day, the woman slept heavily, and her baby crawled on the stove, defecating in the cooking utensils. Upon waking, the woman immediately apologized to the gods and washed the utensils thoroughly. However, her brother-in-law scolded her, saying there were no gods or spirits, and that she allowed such filth to occur. The officials heard about this and took her to be punished. The judge said, "Sleeping heavily is not a fault, and the baby was innocent. The woman had already apologized to the gods, so she is not guilty; your brother-in-law's scolding is unrighteous, and he is spreading false accusations against the spirits. He can be brought in for punishment." Shortly after, he was brought in with a red rope around his neck. [89]

    安居阶下人具读名牒，为伯所诉云云，府君曰：“此人事佛，大德人也！其伯杀害无辜，訾诳百姓，罪宜穷治；以昔有小福，故未加罪，伯今复谤诉无辜？”教催录取，未及至，而府君遣安居还，云：“若可还去，善成胜业，可寿九十三，努力勉之！勿复更来也。”安居出至合，局司云：“君可拔郤死名。”于是安居以次抽名既毕，而欲向游贵人所贵人亦至，云：“知汝无他，得还甚善，努力修功德；吾身福微，不办生天受报，于此辅佐府君，亦优游富乐神道之美。吾家在宛，姓某名某，还为吾致意：深尽奉法，勿犯佛禁，可具以所见示语之也。 [90]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [90]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 安居階下人具讀名牒 - Formal expression meaning 'Anju, the person at the lower level, reads the list of names.'
    • 為伯所訴云云 - Means 'complained by his elder brother,' with '云云' being an ellipsis for unspecified details.
    • 大德人也 - Means 'a great virtuous person,' often used to praise someone.
    • 其伯殺害無辜，訾謗百姓 - Means 'his elder brother killed the innocent and slandered the people,' where '訾謗' means to defame or slander.
    • 罪宜窮治 - Means 'the crime should be thoroughly investigated.'
    • 以昔有小福，故未加罪 - Means 'because he had a little good fortune in the past, he was not punished.'
    • 伯今復謗訴無辜 - Means 'the elder brother now again defames the innocent.'
    • 教催取錄 - Means 'the instruction to take him in.'
    • 若可還去，善成勝業 - Means 'if you can return, good deeds will be accomplished.'
    • 可壽九十三，努力勉之 - Means 'you can live to be ninety-three years old, strive hard.'
    • 勿復更來也 - Means 'do not come again.'
    • 局司云 - Means 'the official said,' where '局司' refers to an official or administrator.
    • 君可拔郤死名 - Means 'you can withdraw your name from the list of the dead,' where '拔郤' means to remove or withdraw.
    • 游貴人所 - Means 'visited the noble person,' where '貴人' refers to a noble or influential person.
    • 知汝無他，得還甚善 - Means 'knowing you have no other intentions, it is very good that you can return.'
    • 吾身福微，不辦生天受報 - Means 'my own virtue is slight, I cannot ascend to heaven to receive reward,' where '不辦' means unable to or cannot.
    • 於此輔佐府君 - Means 'here to assist the lord,' where '府君' refers to a local official or lord.
    • 亦優游富樂神道之美 - Means 'also enjoy the beauty of leisurely and wealthy spiritual life.'
    • 吾家在宛，姓某名某 - Means 'my home is in Wan, my surname is [surname] and my name is [name].'
    • 還為吾致意 - Means 'when you return, convey my greetings,' where '致意' means to convey greetings or messages.
    • 深盡奉法，勿犯佛禁 - Means 'deeply adhere to the law, do not violate the Buddhist precepts.'
    • 可具以所見示語之 - Means 'you can fully tell them what you have seen,' where '示語' means to show or tell.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 府君 - Refers to a local official or lord, often used in the context of the afterlife or in official documents.
    • 名牒 - Refers to a list of names, often used in official records or registries.
    • 伯 - Refers to an elder brother, often used in familial or social contexts.
    • 大德人 - A term used to praise someone for their virtue or moral excellence.
    • 無辜 - Means innocent, often used in the context of legal or moral judgments.
    • 訾謗 - Means to defame or slander, often used in the context of moral or social criticism.
    • 小福 - Refers to a small amount of good fortune or virtue.
    • 局司 - Refers to an official or administrator, often used in the context of government or bureaucracy.
    • 拔郤 - Means to remove or withdraw, often used in the context of official actions or decisions.
    • 貴人 - Refers to a noble or influential person, often used in the context of social or political hierarchies.
    • 神道 - Refers to the spiritual or religious path, often used in the context of Buddhism or other religions.
    • 宛 - Refers to a place, often used in the context of geographical or historical locations.
    • 奉法 - Means to adhere to the law, often used in the context of legal or moral obligations.
    • 佛禁 - Refers to the precepts or rules of Buddhism, often used in the context of religious practice.

    Proper Nouns

    • 安居 - The name of the person in the story.
    • 伯 - The elder brother of Anju.
    • 府君 - The local official or lord.
    • 局司 - The official or administrator.
    • 貴人 - The noble person.
    • 宛 - The place where the noble person's home is located.

    Anju, the person at the lower level, read the list of names, and was complained about by his elder brother. The lord said, "This person worships the Buddha, a great virtuous person! His elder brother killed the innocent and slandered the people, and should be thoroughly investigated; because he had a little good fortune in the past, he was not punished. The elder brother now again defames the innocent?" The instruction was given to take him in, but before they could arrive, the lord sent Anju back, saying, "If you can return, good deeds will be accomplished, you can live to be ninety-three years old, strive hard! Do not come again." Anju left and went to the hall, the official said, "You can withdraw your name from the list of the dead." Thus, Anju removed his name one by one, and then decided to visit the noble person. The noble person also arrived, saying, "Knowing you have no other intentions, it is very good that you can return, strive hard to cultivate virtue; my own virtue is slight, I cannot ascend to heaven to receive reward, here to assist the lord, also enjoy the beauty of leisurely and wealthy spiritual life. My home is in Wan, my surname is [surname] and my name is [name], when you return, convey my greetings: deeply adhere to the law, do not violate the Buddhist precepts, you can fully tell them what you have seen." [90]

    ”乃以三人送安居出门，数步，有专使送符与安居，谓曰：“君可持此符，经过戍逻以示之，勿辄偷过，偷过有徒?也。若有水碍，可以此符投水中，即得过也。”安居受符而归行久之，阻大江，不得渡，安居依言投符，蒙然如眩，乃是其家屋前中方地也。正闻家中号恸哭泣，所送之人，劝还就身，安居云：“身已臭秽吾不复能归。”此人乃强排之，踣于尸脚上。安居既愈，欲验黄水妇人，故往冠军县寻问；果有此妇，相见依然，如有曩旧，云：己死得生，舅即以某日而亡，说所闻见，与安居悉同。受五戒师字僧昊，襄阳人也，末居长沙，本与安居同里，闻其口说。安居之终，亦亲睹，果九十三焉。珠林六十二 [91]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [91]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 乃以三人送安居出门 - 乃 means 'then' or 'thus,' and 以三人送 means 'with three people accompanying.' The phrase indicates a specific action taken by the narrator.
    • 数步 - Literally 'several steps,' indicating a short distance.
    • 专使送符与安居 - 专使 means 'special envoy' or 'dedicated messenger,' and 送符 means 'delivering a talisman.'
    • 君可持此符，经过戍逻以示之 - 君可持此符 means 'you can hold this talisman,' and 经过戍逻以示之 means 'when passing through the checkpoints, show it to them.'
    • 勿辄偷过，偷过有徒也 - 勿辄偷过 means 'do not pass through secretly,' and 偷过有徒也 means 'if you do, you will be caught.'
    • 若有水碍，可以此符投水中，即得过也 - 若有水碍 means 'if there is water blocking the way,' and 可以此符投水中，即得过也 means 'you can throw this talisman into the water and you will be able to cross.'
    • 蒙然如眩 - 蒙然 means 'dazed' or 'confused,' and 如眩 means 'like being dizzy.' Together, they describe a disoriented state.
    • 乃是其家屋前中方地也 - 乃是 means 'it turns out to be,' 其家屋前 means 'in front of his house,' and 中方地 means 'the central courtyard.'
    • 正闻家中号恸哭泣 - 正闻 means 'just heard,' and 家中号恸哭泣 means 'the family was wailing and crying.'
    • 所送之人，劝还就身 - 所送之人 means 'the people who accompanied him,' and 劝还就身 means 'they urged him to return and join them.'
    • 身已臭秽吾不复能归 - 身已臭秽 means 'my body is already foul-smelling,' and 吾不复能归 means 'I cannot return.'
    • 此人乃强排之，踣于尸脚上 - 此人乃强排之 means 'these people forcibly pushed him,' and 踣于尸脚上 means 'he fell on top of the corpse's feet.'
    • 安居既愈，欲验黄水妇人 - 安居既愈 means 'after Anju had recovered,' and 欲验黄水妇人 means 'he wanted to verify the story of the woman from the Yellow River.'
    • 果有此妇，相见依然，如有曩旧 - 果有此妇 means 'there really was this woman,' and 相见依然，如有曩旧 means 'they met again, as if they had known each other before.'
    • 云：己死得生，舅即以某日而亡 - 云 means 'said,' 己死得生 means 'I died and was reborn,' and 舅即以某日而亡 means 'my uncle died on a certain day.'
    • 说所闻见，与安居悉同 - 说所闻见 means 'what she heard and saw,' and 与安居悉同 means 'was exactly the same as what Anju had experienced.'
    • 受五戒师字僧昊，襄阳人也，末居长沙，本与安居同里 - 受五戒师 means 'a monk who had taken the Five Precepts,' 字僧昊 means 'his name was Seng Hao,' 襄阳人也 means 'he was from Xiangyang,' 末居长沙 means 'he later settled in Changsha,' and 本与安居同里 means 'he originally lived in the same neighborhood as Anju.'
    • 闻其口说。安居之终，亦亲睹，果九十三焉 - 闻其口说 means 'he heard it from his own mouth,' 安居之终，亦亲睹 means 'he also personally witnessed Anju's death,' and 果九十三焉 means 'he was indeed ninety-three years old.'
    • 珠林六十二 - 珠林 means 'Jade Forest,' and 六十二 means 'Chapter 62.' This is likely a reference to a specific chapter in a text.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 安居 - A person's name, possibly a historical figure or a fictional character.
    • 黄水妇人 - The woman from the Yellow River, referring to a supernatural or mystical figure.
    • 五戒 - The Five Precepts, a set of ethical guidelines in Buddhism.
    • 僧昊 - A monk named Seng Hao, possibly a historical figure.
    • 襄阳 - A city in Hubei Province, China.
    • 长沙 - A city in Hunan Province, China.
    • 珠林 - Jade Forest, likely a reference to a specific text or collection of stories.

    Proper Nouns

    • 安居 - A person's name.
    • 黄水妇人 - A supernatural or mystical figure.
    • 五戒 - The Five Precepts in Buddhism.
    • 僧昊 - A monk named Seng Hao.
    • 襄阳 - A city in Hubei Province, China.
    • 长沙 - A city in Hunan Province, China.
    • 珠林 - Jade Forest, likely a reference to a specific text or collection of stories.
    • 冠军县 - A county, possibly a fictional or historical location.

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    Example 3

    Source: 凡為文章，猶乘騏驥，雖有逸氣，當以銜勒制之，勿使流亂軌躅，放意填坑岸也。

    Translation: In all writing, it is like riding a swift steed — though it may possess a wild and unrestrained spirit, one must control it with bit and bridle, and must not let it run wild, straying from the proper path or indulging in aimless wanderings.

    Example 4

    Source: 自古明王聖帝，猶須勤學，況凡庶乎！此事遍於經史，吾亦不能鄭重，聊舉近世切要，以啟寤汝耳。

    Translation: From ancient times, wise kings and sage emperors still required diligent study; how much more so, then, for common people! This matter is found throughout the classics and histories. I cannot elaborate further, but I will briefly mention a few essential points of recent times in order to enlighten you.

    Example 5

    Source: 夫文章者，原出五經：詔命策檄，生於書者也；序述論議，生於易者也；歌詠賦頌，生於詩者也；祭祀哀誄，生於禮者也；書奏箴銘，生於春秋者也。朝廷憲章，軍旅誓誥，敷顯仁義，發明功德，牧民建國，不可暫無。至於陶冶性靈，從容諷諫，入其滋味，亦樂事也。行有餘力，則可習之。

    Translation: As for writings, their origin lies in the Five Classics: edicts, memorials, and military proclamations arise from the Shujing (Book of Documents); expository and argumentative writings arise from the Yijing (Book of Changes); songs, odes, and praises arise from the Shijing (Book of Poetry); ritual sacrifices, mourning, and elegies arise from the Liji (Book of Rites); memorials, admonitions, and inscriptions arise from the Chunqiu (Spring and Autumn Annals). Court regulations, military oaths and decrees, the proclamation of benevolence and righteousness, the elucidation of virtue and merit, governing the people and establishing the state—all are indispensable. As for cultivating the mind and spirit, offering gentle admonition in a relaxed manner, and savoring the flavor of such writings, these too are pleasant pursuits. When one has spare energy, then one may study them.

    Source text:
    ”乃以三人送安居出门，数步，有专使送符与安居，谓曰：“君可持此符，经过戍逻以示之，勿辄偷过，偷过有徒?也。若有水碍，可以此符投水中，即得过也。”安居受符而归行久之，阻大江，不得渡，安居依言投符，蒙然如眩，乃是其家屋前中方地也。正闻家中号恸哭泣，所送之人，劝还就身，安居云：“身已臭秽吾不复能归。”此人乃强排之，踣于尸脚上。安居既愈，欲验黄水妇人，故往冠军县寻问；果有此妇，相见依然，如有曩旧，云：己死得生，舅即以某日而亡，说所闻见，与安居悉同。受五戒师字僧昊，襄阳人也，末居长沙，本与安居同里，闻其口说。安居之终，亦亲睹，果九十三焉。珠林六十二

    Provide only the final translation with no additional commentary. [91]

    宋沙门僧规者，武当寺僧也，时京兆张瑜于此县，常请僧规在家供养。永初元年十二月五日，无痾忽暴死，二日而稣愈。自说云：五日夜五更中，闻门巷闲哓哓有声，须臾，见有五人，炳炬火，执信旛，迳来入屋，叱咀僧规因顿卧恍然，五人便以赤绳缚将去。行至一山，都无草木，土色坚黑，有类石铁；山侧左右，白骨填积，山数十里，至三岐路有一人，甚长壮，被铠执仗，问五人：“有几人来？”答曰：“政一人耳！”五人又将规入一道中，俄至一城外，有屋数十，筑壤为之，屋前有立木，长十余丈，上有铁梁，形如桔槔，左右有匮，贮土，土有品数，或有十斛形，亦如五升大者。 [92]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [92]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 宋沙门僧规者，武当寺僧也 - Formal introduction of the subject, 'Cao Shamen Senggui, a monk of Wudang Temple.'
    • 京兆张瑜于此县，常请僧规在家供养 - 'Jingzhao Zhang Yu in this county often invited Senggui to his home for support,' where '供养' can mean both 'support' (as in financial support) and 'nourishment' (spiritual or material).
    • 无痾忽暴死，二日而稣愈 - 'Without any illness, he suddenly died, but after two days, he revived.' '无痾' means 'without any illness,' and '稣愈' refers to revival or resurrection.
    • 闻门巷闲哓哓有声 - 'He heard a clamor in the street,' where '哓哓' describes a noisy, clamorous sound.
    • 炳炬火，执信旛 - 'Carrying torches and holding banners,' where '炳炬火' refers to bright torches, and '执信旛' means holding banners or flags.
    • 迳来入屋 - 'They came straight into the house,' where '迳' means 'directly' or 'straight.'
    • 叱咀僧规因顿卧恍然 - 'They shouted at Senggui, causing him to fall into a trance-like state,' where '叱咀' means 'shouting' or 'yelling,' and '顿卧恍然' describes a sudden, trance-like state.
    • 赤绳缚将去 - 'They bound him with a red rope and took him away,' where '赤绳' is a red rope, often associated with binding or leading.
    • 山数十里，至三岐路 - 'A mountain several miles wide, leading to a three-way fork,' where '岐路' refers to a fork in the road.
    • 甚长壮，被铠执仗 - 'Very tall and strong, wearing armor and holding weapons,' where '被铠' means 'wearing armor,' and '执仗' means 'holding weapons.'
    • 问五人：“有几人来？” - 'He asked the five people, 'How many of you have come?'' where '几人' means 'how many people.'
    • 政一人耳！ - 'Only one person!' where '政' is an archaic form of '只' (only), and '耳' is a particle emphasizing the statement.
    • 一道中 - 'In one of the paths,' where '道' means 'path' or 'way.'
    • 俄至一城外 - 'Soon they arrived at the outskirts of a city,' where '俄' means 'soon' or 'suddenly.'
    • 屋数十，筑壤为之 - 'There were dozens of houses, built from earth,' where '筑壤' means 'built from earth.'
    • 屋前有立木，长十余丈 - 'There was a standing tree in front of the house, over ten zhang tall,' where '长十余丈' means 'over ten zhang tall' (one zhang is about 3.3 meters).
    • 上有铁梁，形如桔槔 - 'With an iron beam on top, shaped like a seesaw,' where '桔槔' refers to a seesaw or balance.
    • 左右有匮，贮土 - 'On either side were bins, storing earth,' where '匮' means 'bin' or 'chest,' and '贮土' means 'storing earth.'
    • 土有品数，或有十斛形，亦如五升大者 - 'The earth had different sizes, some as large as ten dou, others as small as five dou,' where '品数' means 'different sizes,' and '十斛' and '五升' refer to units of measurement (one dou is about 10 liters).

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋 - Refers to the Southern Song Dynasty, a historical period in China.
    • 沙门 - A term for Buddhist monks, indicating the religious background of the subject.
    • 武当寺 - Refers to Wudang Temple, a specific temple in the region.
    • 京兆 - Refers to the capital region, likely referring to the capital of the Southern Song Dynasty.
    • 张瑜 - A historical figure, likely a local official or wealthy patron.
    • 僧规 - The name of the monk, whose story is being recounted.
    • 暴死 - A sudden death, which was often interpreted as a supernatural event in classical Chinese literature.
    • 稣愈 - Revival or resurrection, which could be interpreted as a miraculous event.
    • 门巷闲哓哓有声 - A noisy, clamorous sound, often associated with supernatural or ghostly phenomena.
    • 炳炬火，执信旛 - Carrying torches and banners, often associated with processions or rituals.
    • 赤绳 - A red rope, often associated with binding or leading, sometimes used in Buddhist or Taoist rituals.
    • 三岐路 - A three-way fork in the road, often used as a metaphor for a choice or a crossroads.
    • 被铠执仗 - Wearing armor and holding weapons, often associated with warriors or guardians.
    • 铁梁，形如桔槔 - An iron beam shaped like a seesaw, which could be a metaphor for balance or judgment.
    • 匮，贮土 - Bins storing earth, which could be a metaphor for storing or containing something.

    Proper Nouns

    • 宋 - The Southern Song Dynasty.
    • 沙门 - Buddhist monks.
    • 武当寺 - Wudang Temple.
    • 京兆 - The capital region.
    • 张瑜 - A historical figure.
    • 僧规 - The name of the monk.
    • 三岐路 - A three-way fork in the road.
    • 铁梁 - An iron beam.
    • 桔槔 - A seesaw or balance.
    • 匮 - Bins or chests.

    During the Southern Song Dynasty, there was a monk named Senggui from Wudang Temple. At that time, Zhang Yu from Jingzhao often invited Senggui to his home for support. In the first year of Yongchou, on the fifth day of the twelfth month, Senggui died suddenly without any illness, but he revived after two days. He recounted what happened: On the fifth night of the fifth watch, he heard a clamor in the street. Soon after, he saw five people carrying torches and holding banners, coming straight into the house. They shouted at Senggui, causing him to fall into a trance-like state, and the five people then bound him with a red rope and took him away. They led him to a mountain with no grass or trees, the soil was hard and black, like stone or iron. On both sides of the mountain, there were piles of white bones, stretching for dozens of miles. They reached a three-way fork where there was a very tall and strong man, wearing armor and holding weapons. He asked the five people, "How many of you have come?" They replied, "Only one person!" The five then took Senggui into one of the paths, and soon they arrived at the outskirts of a city. There were dozens of houses, built from earth, with a standing tree in front of the house, over ten zhang tall. On top of the tree was an iron beam shaped like a seesaw, with bins on either side, storing earth. The earth had different sizes, some as large as ten dou, others as small as five dou. [92]

    有一人，衣帻并赤，语规曰：“汝生世时，有何罪福？依实说之，勿妄言也。”规惶怖未答，赤衣人如局吏云：“可开簿检其罪福也。”有顷，吏至长木下，提一匮土，县铁梁上称之，如觉低昂，吏谓规曰：“此种量罪福之秤也。汝福少罪多，应先受罚。”俄有一人，衣冠长者，谓规曰：“汝沙门也，何不念佛？我闻悔过可度八难。
    ”规于是一心称佛，衣冠人谓吏曰：“可更为此人称之，既是佛弟子，幸可度脱。”吏乃复上匮称之，称乃正平。既而将规至监官前辩之，监执笔观簿，迟疑久之；又有一人，朱衣玄冠，佩印绶，执玉板，来，曰：“算簿上未有此人名也。”监官愕然，命左右收录去，须臾，见反缚向五人来，监官曰：“杀鬼，何以滥将人来？乃鞭之。少顷有使者称：“天帝唤道人来。”既至帝宫，经见践历略，皆金宝精光，晃昱不得凝视。 [93]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [93]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 衣帻并赤 - Literally 'robe and cap both red,' likely referring to a specific attire or role.
    • 依实说之，勿妄言也 - Means 'speak the truth, do not speak falsely.'
    • 惶怖未答 - Means 'in great fear and unable to answer.'
    • 赤衣人如局吏 - Literally 'the red-clothed person like an official,' referring to someone in an official capacity.
    • 可开簿检其罪福也 - Means 'can open the ledger to check their merits and faults.'
    • 量罪福之秤 - Literally 'the scale for measuring merits and faults,' a metaphorical device.
    • 悔过可度八难 - Means 'repentance can help cross the eight difficulties.'
    • 一心称佛 - Means 'to sincerely recite Buddha's name.'
    • 监官前辩之 - Means 'to debate in front of the supervisor.'
    • 算簿上未有此人名也 - Means 'there is no name of this person on the ledger.'
    • 反缚向五人来 - Literally 'five people coming with hands tied behind their backs,' likely referring to executioners or enforcers.
    • 鞭之 - Means 'to whip them.'
    • 天帝唤道人来 - Means 'the Heavenly Emperor calls the Daoist priest.'
    • 经见践历略 - Literally 'what is seen and experienced,' referring to the journey or experience.
    • 金宝精光，晃昱不得凝视 - Literally 'golden treasures with brilliant light, dazzling to the point of not being able to look directly.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 沙门 - Refers to a Buddhist monk or ascetic.
    • 念佛 - Means 'to recite Buddha's name,' a central practice in Buddhism.
    • 八难 - Refers to the eight obstacles or difficulties in Buddhist cosmology.
    • 监官 - Refers to a supervisor or overseer, likely in a bureaucratic or spiritual context.
    • 算簿 - Refers to a ledger or record book, often used in both bureaucratic and spiritual contexts.
    • 天帝 - Refers to the Heavenly Emperor, a central figure in Daoist and Chinese cosmology.
    • 道人 - Refers to a Daoist priest or practitioner.
    • 帝宫 - Refers to the palace of the Heavenly Emperor, a celestial realm in Chinese mythology.
    • 金宝精光 - Refers to the brilliant light of golden treasures, symbolizing divine or celestial splendor.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • The sentence introduces a red-clothed person who speaks to the monk, requiring careful parsing of the dialogue.
    • The question and instruction must be rendered clearly to maintain the interrogative tone.
    • The metaphorical comparison of the red-clothed person to an official must be preserved.
    • The conditional clause must be rendered clearly to maintain the logical flow.
    • The sequence of events must be rendered clearly to maintain the narrative flow.
    • The statement of absence from the ledger must be rendered clearly to maintain the logical flow.
    • The description of the five bound individuals must be rendered clearly to maintain the narrative flow.
    • The call from the Heavenly Emperor must be rendered clearly to maintain the narrative flow.
    • The description of the journey or experience must be rendered clearly to maintain the narrative flow.
    • The description of the brilliant light must be rendered clearly to maintain the narrative flow.

    Proper Nouns

    • 规 - The name of the monk, likely a specific individual.
    • 监官 - The title of the supervisor or overseer.
    • 天帝 - The title of the Heavenly Emperor.
    • 道人 - The title of the Daoist priest or practitioner.

    A man appeared, dressed entirely in red, and spoke to the monk Guī: "When you were born, what merits and faults did you have? Speak the truth, do not speak falsely." Guī was in great fear and unable to answer. The red-clothed person, acting like an official, said, "Can we open the ledger to check their merits and faults?" After a while, an official arrived at the long wooden stand, lifted a small chest of earth, and hung it on an iron beam to weigh it. As if sensing the imbalance, the official told Guī, "This is the scale for measuring merits and faults. Your merits are few and your faults many; you should be punished first." Soon, a man dressed in formal attire approached and said to Guī, "You are a monk, why don't you recite Buddha's name? I have heard that repentance can help cross the eight difficulties."

    Guī then sincerely recited Buddha's name. The formally dressed man told the official, "Can you weigh this person again? Since he is a disciple of Buddha, it is fortunate that he can be saved." The official then reweighed the chest, and the scale became perfectly balanced. They then took Guī to the supervisor's presence to debate the matter. The supervisor held a brush and looked at the ledger, hesitating for a long time. Another man, dressed in red with a black hat, wearing seals and holding a jade tablet, came and said, "There is no name of this person on the ledger." The supervisor was startled and ordered his attendants to take Guī away. Soon, five men appeared with their hands tied behind their backs. The supervisor said, "Kill the ghosts, why did you bring people indiscriminately? They were whipped." Shortly after, a messenger announced, "The Heavenly Emperor calls the Daoist priest." Upon arriving at the Heavenly Emperor's palace, Guī saw the journey and experiences, all adorned with golden treasures and brilliant light, dazzling to the point of not being able to look directly. [93]

    帝左右朱衣宝冠，饰以华珍，帝曰：“汝是沙门，何不勤业而为小鬼，横收捕也？”规稽首诸佛，祈恩请福，帝曰：“汝命未尽，今当还生；宜勤精进，勿屡游白衣家！杀鬼取人，亦多枉滥，如汝比也。”规曰：“横滥之厄，当以何方而济免之？”帝曰：“广设福业，最为善也；若不办，尔可作八关斋；生免横祸，死离地狱，亦其次也。”语毕，遣规去。行还未久，见一精舍，大有沙门，见武当寺主白法师，弟子慧进，皆在焉，居宇宏整，资待自然，规请欲居之，有一沙门曰：“此是福地，非君所得处也。”使者将规还至瑜家而去。珠林八十三 [94]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [94]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 帝左右朱衣宝冠，饰以华珍 - Describes the emperor wearing red robes and a jeweled crown, symbolizing his divine or imperial status, adorned with precious treasures.
    • 汝是沙门，何不勤业而为小鬼，横收捕也 - A rhetorical question asking the monk why he is not diligently practicing his monastic duties but instead being captured as a demon.
    • 规稽首诸佛，祈恩请福 - The monk paid homage to the Buddhas, praying for blessings, indicating a religious act of devotion.
    • 命未尽，今当还生 - Your life is not yet over, and you will be reborn, suggesting a continuation of the monk's existence.
    • 宜勤精进，勿屡游白衣家 - Advises the monk to diligently strive and not frequently visit the homes of laypeople, implying a warning against worldly distractions.
    • 杀鬼取人，亦多枉滥，如汝比也 - Killing demons and taking people is often unjust and excessive, like you, criticizing the practice of indiscriminate killing.
    • 广设福业，最为善也 - Widely establishing good deeds is the best, emphasizing the importance of virtuous actions.
    • 八关斋 - An eight-precept observance, a form of Buddhist fasting and discipline.
    • 生免横祸，死离地狱，亦其次也 - To avoid misfortune in life and to be reborn in heaven after death, which is also good, indicating a secondary benefit of the eight-precept observance.
    • 遣规去 - The emperor sent the monk away, signaling the end of the interaction.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 帝 - Refers to the emperor, likely a Buddhist deity or a ruler in a Buddhist context.
    • 沙门 - Refers to a Buddhist monk, indicating a religious figure.
    • 小鬼 - Refers to a demon or a lesser spirit, often associated with negative or harmful actions.
    • 诸佛 - Refers to the Buddhas, the enlightened beings in Buddhism.
    • 八关斋 - A specific Buddhist practice involving eight precepts, often observed on special days.
    • 武当寺主白法师，弟子慧进 - Refers to the head of the Wudang Temple, Master Bai, and his disciple Huixin, indicating a specific Buddhist community.
    • 珠林八十三 - Refers to the 'Jewel Forest' (a collection of Buddhist texts) in volume 83, providing a historical and textual context.

    Proper Nouns

    • 帝 - The emperor, likely a Buddhist deity or a ruler in a Buddhist context.
    • 沙门 - A Buddhist monk.
    • 小鬼 - A demon or a lesser spirit.
    • 诸佛 - The Buddhas.
    • 八关斋 - An eight-precept observance in Buddhism.
    • 武当寺主白法师，弟子慧进 - The head of the Wudang Temple, Master Bai, and his disciple Huixin.
    • 珠林八十三 - The 'Jewel Forest' in volume 83, a collection of Buddhist texts.

    The emperor, adorned with red robes and a jeweled crown, surrounded by precious treasures, said to the monk, "You are a monk, why are you not diligently practicing your monastic duties but instead being captured as a demon?" The monk bowed to the Buddhas, praying for blessings. The emperor then said, "Your life is not yet over, and you will be reborn. You should diligently strive and not frequently visit the homes of laypeople! Killing demons and taking people is often unjust and excessive, like you." The monk asked, "What can I do to avoid such misfortune?" The emperor replied, "Widely establishing good deeds is the best; if you cannot, you can observe the eight-precept observance. This will help you avoid misfortune in life and be reborn in heaven after death, which is also good." After speaking, the emperor sent the monk away. Shortly after, the monk saw a monastery with many monks, including Master Bai, the head of the Wudang Temple, and his disciple Huixin. The monastery was grand and orderly, and the monk requested to stay there. One of the monks said, "This is a blessed place, not suitable for you to stay." The messenger then took the monk back to the house of Yu. [94]

    何澹之，东海人，宋大司农，不信经法，多行残害。永初中，得病，见一鬼，形甚长壮，牛头人身，手执铁?，昼夜守之。忧布屏营，使道家作章符印录，备诸禳绝，而犹见如故。相识沙门慧义，闻其病往○；澹之为说所见，慧义曰：“此是牛头阿旁也，”罪福不昧，唯人所招；君能转心向法，则此鬼自消。”澹之迷很不革，顷之遂死。珠林八十三
    　　宋沙门竺慧炽，新野人，住在江陵四层寺，永初二年，卒，弟子为设七日会。其日将夕烧香竟，道贤沙门因往视炽弟子，至房前，忽暧暧若人形，详视，乃慧炽也，容貌衣服，不异生时。谓贤：“君旦食肉，美不？”贤曰：“美。”炽曰：“我坐食肉，今生饿狗地狱道。”贤惧詟未及得答，炽复言：“汝若不信，试看我背后。”乃回背示贤，见三黄狗，形半似驴，眼甚赤，光照户内，状欲啮炽而复止。贤骇怖闷绝，良久乃稣。具说其事。珠林九十四 [95]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [95]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 不信經法 - Does not believe in the teachings of the scriptures or does not follow religious doctrines.
    • 多行殘害 - Often engages in cruel and harmful acts.
    • 日夜守之 - Guards him day and night.
    • 使道家作章符印錄 - Has Taoists create talismans and inscriptions to ward off evil.
    • 而猶見如故 - But still sees it as before.
    • 聞其病往○ - Hears of his illness and visits him.
    • 為說所見 - Tells him about what he has seen.
    • 君能轉心向法 - If you can change your heart and turn to the law (or religion).
    • 則此鬼自消 - Then this ghost will naturally disappear.
    • 迷很不革 - Remains stubborn and unrepentant.
    • 頃之遂死 - Soon after, he dies.
    • 弟子為設七日會 - His disciples set up a seven-day memorial service.
    • 將夕燒香竟 - As evening approached, after burning incense.
    • 因往視熾弟子 - Went to visit Zhuhui Chi's disciples.
    • 至房前 - Arrived at the front of the room.
    • 忽暧暧若人形 - Suddenly, it appeared as if a human figure.
    • 詳視 - Looked closely.
    • 乃慧熾也 - It was Zhuhui Chi.
    • 容貌衣服，不異生時 - His appearance and clothing were no different from when he was alive.
    • 謂賢 - Said to Daoxian.
    • 君旦食肉，美不 - Did you enjoy the meat you ate this morning?
    • 我坐食肉，今生餓狗地獄道 - I am suffering in the hell of hungry dogs because I ate meat.
    • 懼詟未及得答 - Daoxian was terrified and did not get a chance to answer.
    • 熾復言 - Zhuhui Chi spoke again.
    • 汝若不信，試看我背後 - If you don't believe me, look at my back.
    • 乃回背示賢 - He turned his back to show Daoxian.
    • 見三黃狗，形半似驢，眼甚赤，光照戶內 - Daoxian saw three yellow dogs, half the shape of donkeys, with very red eyes that illuminated the room.
    • 狀欲啮熾而復止 - They seemed about to bite Zhuhui Chi but then stopped.
    • 駭怖悶絕 - Daoxian was terrified and fainted.
    • 良久乃蘇 - After a long time, he regained consciousness.
    • 具說其事 - He told the whole story.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 永初中 - A specific period in Chinese history, likely during the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 牛頭阿旁 - A type of ghost or demon in Chinese folklore, often associated with punishment for sins.
    • 道家 - Refers to Taoist practitioners or Taoism.
    • 沙門 - Refers to Buddhist monks.
    • 珠林 - Refers to the 'Zhu Lin' (Jewel Forest), a collection of Buddhist stories and parables.
    • 宋 - Refers to the Song dynasty.
    • 新野 - A place name, likely referring to a specific region in China.
    • 江陵 - A place name, likely referring to a specific city in China.
    • 四層寺 - Refers to a specific temple, possibly known for its four-tiered structure.

    Proper Nouns

    • 何澹之 - A historical figure, though specific details about him are not provided in the text.
    • 宋沙門竺慧熾 - A monk named Zhuhui Chi from the Song dynasty.
    • 新野人 - From Xinye, a place name.
    • 住在江陵四層寺 - Lives in the Four-Tiered Temple in Jiangling.
    • 永初二年 - The second year of the Yongchun era.
    • 卒 - Died.
    • 弟子 - His disciples.
    • 道賢沙門 - A monk named Daoxian.

    He Danzhi, a man from Donghai, served as the Grand Agricultural Minister during the Song dynasty. He did not believe in religious teachings and often engaged in cruel and harmful acts. During the Yongchun era, he fell ill and saw a ghost. The ghost had a very tall and strong form, with a bull's head and a human body, holding an iron tool, and guarded him day and night. Feeling distressed and restless, he had Taoists create talismans and inscriptions to ward off evil, but still saw the ghost as before. A monk named Huiyi, whom he knew, heard of his illness and visited him. Danzhi told him about what he had seen. Huiyi said, "This is the 'Niu Tou A Pang' ghost." He explained that retribution for sins and blessings is not obscured, and it is only what one has brought upon oneself. "If you can change your heart and turn to the law (or religion), then this ghost will naturally disappear." Danzhi remained stubborn and unrepentant, and soon after, he died. This story is recorded in the "Zhu Lin" (Jewel Forest), entry 83.

    Monk Zhuhui Chi, a native of Xinye, lived in the Four-Tiered Temple in Jiangling. In the second year of the Yongchun era, he died. His disciples set up a seven-day memorial service. On that day, as evening approached and after burning incense, a monk named Daoxian went to visit Zhuhui Chi's disciples. Arriving at the front of the room, he suddenly saw a figure that appeared human. Looking closely, it was Zhuhui Chi. His appearance and clothing were no different from when he was alive. He said to Daoxian, "Did you enjoy the meat you ate this morning?" Daoxian replied, "Yes, it was enjoyable." Zhuhui Chi said, "I am suffering in the hell of hungry dogs because I ate meat." Daoxian was terrified and did not get a chance to answer. Zhuhui Chi spoke again, "If you don't believe me, look at my back." He turned his back to show Daoxian, who saw three yellow dogs, half the shape of donkeys, with very red eyes that illuminated the room. They seemed about to bite Zhuhui Chi but then stopped. Daoxian was terrified and fainted. After a long time, he regained consciousness and told the whole story. This account is recorded in the "Zhu Lin" (Jewel Forest), entry 94. [95]

    晋王练，字玄明，琅琊人也，宋侍中。父玟，字季琰，晋中书令；相识有一梵沙门，每瞻玟风釆，甚敬悦之辄语同学云：“若我后生得为此人作子，于近愿亦足矣。玟闻而戏之曰：“法师才行，正可为弟子子耳！”顷之，沙门病亡，亡后岁余，而练生焉。始能言，便解外国语及绝国之奇珍锒器珠贝；生所不见，未闻其名，即而名之，识其产出，又自然亲爱诸梵过于汉人。咸谓沙门审其先身，故玟字之曰阿练，遂为大名云云。珠林二十六　广记三百八十七　辩正论八陈子良注引冥祥记云琅琊王玟其妻无子常祈观音乞儿玟后行路逢一胡僧其意极甚悦之其胡僧曰我死当为君子少时道人果亡三月间玟妻有妊及生能语即解西域十六国音大聪明有器度即晋尚书王渊明身也故小名阿练前生时事有验 [96]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [96]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 甚敬悦之 - Very respectful and delighted, expressing deep respect and joy.
    • 若我后生得为此人作子 - If I were to be reborn as a son of this person, a statement about a wish for rebirth.
    • 正可为弟子子耳 - Just suitable to be a disciple's child, a humorous response to the monk's wish.
    • 于近愿亦足矣 - It would be enough for my near wishes, expressing satisfaction with a modest goal.
    • 生所不见 - Things he had never seen, indicating unfamiliarity.
    • 未闻其名 - Had not heard their names, indicating lack of knowledge.
    • 即而名之 - And immediately named them, indicating quick recognition.
    • 识其产出 - Knew their place of origin, indicating knowledge of the source.
    • 自然亲爱诸梵 - Naturally loved all the monks, expressing a deep affection.
    • 过于汉人 - More than Han people, indicating a stronger bond.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋王练 - Jin Wang Lian, a historical figure known for his unique abilities and background.
    • 宋侍中 - Song Shizhong, a high-ranking official in the Song dynasty, indicating a prestigious position.
    • 晋中书令 - Jin Zhongshu Ling, a high-ranking official in the Jin dynasty, indicating a significant role in government.
    • 梵沙门 - Vajra monk, a term for a Buddhist monk.
    • 观音 - Guanyin, the Bodhisattva of compassion, a central figure in Buddhism.
    • 胡僧 - Hu Seng, a term for a foreign monk, often referring to those from Central Asia or India.
    • 西域十六国音 - The languages of the sixteen kingdoms of the Western Regions, indicating knowledge of multiple languages from Central Asia.
    • 珠林二十六 - Zhu Lin 26, a reference to a specific text or chapter in the 'Zhu Lin' (Treasury of Pearls).
    • 广记三百八十七 - Guang Ji 387, a reference to a specific text or chapter in the 'Guang Ji' (Comprehensive Records).
    • 辩正论八陈子良注引冥祥记 - Bian Zheng Lun 8 Chen Ziliang annotation, citing the 'Míng Xiáng Jì' (Record of Auspicious Signs), indicating a scholarly reference.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋王练 - Jin Wang Lian, a historical figure.
    • 字玄明 - Zi Xuanming, the courtesy name of Jin Wang Lian.
    • 琅琊 - Langya, a place of origin.
    • 宋侍中 - Song Shizhong, an official title.
    • 父玟 - Father Wen, the name of Jin Wang Lian's father.
    • 字季琰 - Zi Jiyan, the courtesy name of Jin Wang Lian's father.
    • 晋中书令 - Jin Zhongshu Ling, an official title.
    • 梵沙门 - Vajra monk, a term for a Buddhist monk.
    • 观音 - Guanyin, the Bodhisattva of compassion.
