    • 胡僧 - Hu Seng, a term for a foreign monk.
    • 西域十六国音 - The languages of the sixteen kingdoms of the Western Regions.
    • 珠林二十六 - Zhu Lin 26, a reference to a specific text or chapter.
    • 广记三百八十七 - Guang Ji 387, a reference to a specific text or chapter.
    • 辩正论八陈子良注引冥祥记 - Bian Zheng Lun 8 Chen Ziliang annotation, citing the 'Míng Xiáng Jì' (Record of Auspicious Signs).

    Jin Wang Lian, courtesy name Xuanming, was from Langya and served as a Shizhong in the Song dynasty. His father, Wen, courtesy name Jiyan, was a Zhongshu Ling in the Jin dynasty. Wen had an acquaintance, a Vajra monk, who greatly admired Wen's bearing and often told his fellow monks, "If I were to be reborn as a son of this person, it would be enough for my near wishes." Wen, upon hearing this, playfully responded, "The monk's virtue and talent, just suitable to be a disciple's child!" Soon after, the monk fell ill and passed away. A year or so after his death, Jin Wang Lian was born. From the moment he could speak, he could understand foreign languages and recognize exotic treasures, rare vessels, and precious jewels. He could name things he had never seen and knew their place of origin. He naturally loved all the monks more than Han people. Everyone believed that the monk knew his previous life, so Wen named him Alian, and thus it became a great name. Zhu Lin 26, Guang Ji 387, Bian Zheng Lun 8 Chen Ziliang annotation, citing the Míng Xiáng Jì (Record of Auspicious Signs): Wang Wen of Langya had no children and often prayed to Guanyin for a child. One day, while traveling, he met a Hu Seng, whom he greatly admired. The Hu Seng said, "After my death, I will be reborn as your son." Soon after, the monk died, and three months later, Wang Wen's wife became pregnant. When the child was born, he could speak and understood the languages of the sixteen kingdoms of the Western Regions. He was very intelligent and had a dignified bearing, which was later identified as that of Wang Yuanning, a Shangshu in the Jin dynasty. Therefore, he was given the nickname Alian, and the events of his previous life were verified. [96]

    宋珠林引作晋今依广记孙道德，益州人也，奉道祭酒，年过五十，未有子息。居近精舍，景平中，沙门谓德：“必愿有儿，当至心礼诵观世音经，此可冀也。”德遂罢不事道，单心投诚，归观世音；少日之中而有梦应，妇即有孕，遂以产男也。法苑珠林十七太平广记一百十
    　　宋齐僧钦者，江陵人也，家门奉法，年十许岁时，善相占云：“年不过三六。”父母兄弟甚为忧惧，僧钦亦增加勤敬斋戒精苦。至年十七，宋景平末，得病危笃，家斋祈弥励，亦淫祀求福，疾终不愈。时有一女巫云：“此郎福力猛盛，魔魍所不能亲，自有善神护之；然病久不差，运命或将有限。世有探命之术，少事天神，颇晓其数，当为君试效之。”于野中设酒脯之馈，烧钱经七日七夕，云：始有感见，见诸善神方为此郎祈祷，蒙益两算矣，病必得愈，无所忧也。僧钦于是遂差，弥加精至，其后二十四年而终，如巫所言，则一算十二年矣。珠林六十二 [97]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [97]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 奉道祭酒 - A title given to a high-ranking Taoist priest or official, literally meaning 'one who reveres the Tao and offers wine.'
    • 年過五十 - Literally 'past fifty years,' used to indicate age.
    • 未有子息 - Literally 'no offspring,' meaning childless.
    • 居近精舍 - Literally 'lives near a monastery,' indicating proximity to a place of spiritual practice.
    • 景平中 - Refers to the reign period of Emperor Wen of Liu Song (424-427 AD).
    • 當至心禮诵观世音经 - Literally 'should devote oneself wholeheartedly to the worship and recitation of the Sutra of the Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara.'
    • 少日之中而有梦应 - Literally 'in a short period of time, there was a dream response,' indicating a quick result from sincere practice.
    • 淫祀求福 - Literally 'excessive worship to seek blessings,' referring to superstitious practices.
    • 魔魍所不能亲 - Literally 'cannot be approached by demons and evil spirits,' suggesting strong spiritual protection.
    • 自有善神护之 - Literally 'has the protection of good spirits,' indicating divine guardianship.
    • 运命或将有限 - Literally 'fate or destiny may be limited,' suggesting a possible end to life.
    • 探命之术 - Literally 'the art of divining one's fate,' referring to methods of fortune-telling.
    • 单心投诚 - Literally 'devote one's heart in sincere faith,' indicating complete dedication.
    • 弥加精至 - Literally 'increasingly meticulous and devoted,' suggesting growing dedication.
    • 如巫所言 - Literally 'as the witch said,' indicating the fulfillment of a prophecy.
    • 一算十二年矣 - Literally 'one calculation equals twelve years,' referring to the units of time used in fortune-telling.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋珠林 - A collection of Buddhist stories and legends compiled during the Southern Song dynasty.
    • 法苑珠林 - A comprehensive Buddhist encyclopedia compiled by Daoxuan in the Tang dynasty.
    • 太平广记 - A vast collection of stories and anecdotes from various sources, compiled during the Song dynasty.
    • 孙道德 - A historical figure mentioned in the text, a Taoist priest who became a Buddhist devotee.
    • 沙门 - A term for Buddhist monks.
    • 观世音经 - The Sutra of the Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara, a key text in Mahayana Buddhism.
    • 巫 - A witch or fortune-teller, often associated with divination and spiritual practices.

    Proper Nouns

    • 宋珠林 - *Zhu Lin* (Jewel Forest), a collection of Buddhist stories and legends.
    • 法苑珠林 - *Fa Yuan Zhu Lin* (Treasury of the Dharma Jewel), a comprehensive Buddhist encyclopedia.
    • 太平广记 - *Tai Ping Guang Ji* (Broad Records of the Great Peace), a vast collection of stories and anecdotes.
    • 孙道德 - *Sun Daode*, a historical figure mentioned in the text.
    • 观世音经 - *Guan Shi Yin Jing* (Sutra of the Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara).
    • 巫 - *Wu*, a witch or fortune-teller.

    In the Zhu Lin of the Song dynasty, which cites the Jin (Treatise) and follows the Guang Ji (Broad Records), there is a story about Sun Daode, a man from Yizhou who served as a Taoist priest. He was over fifty years old and had no children. Living near a monastery, during the reign of Emperor Wen of Liu Song (424-427 AD), a Buddhist monk told him, "If you truly wish to have a son, you should devote yourself wholeheartedly to the worship and recitation of the Sutra of the Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara. This is something you can hope for." Sun Daode then ceased his Taoist practices and devoted his heart in sincere faith to Avalokiteshvara. In a short period of time, he had a dream response, and his wife became pregnant, eventually giving birth to a son. This story is recorded in Fa Yuan Zhu Lin (Treasury of the Dharma Jewel) and Tai Ping Guang Ji (Broad Records of the Great Peace).

    Another story in Zhu Lin (Jewel Forest) concerns a monk named Qing from the Song and Qi dynasties, who was from Jiangling. His family adhered to the law, and when he was around ten years old, a fortune-teller predicted, "Your lifespan will not exceed thirty-six years." His parents, brothers, and he himself were greatly alarmed. Qing increased his diligence in reverence, fasting, and strict discipline. At the age of seventeen, towards the end of the reign of Emperor Wen of Liu Song, he fell gravely ill. His family intensified their prayers and offerings, and even engaged in superstitious practices to seek blessings, but his illness did not improve. At this time, a female witch said, "This young man has powerful good fortune and virtue, which even demons and evil spirits cannot approach; he has the protection of good spirits. However, his illness has lasted a long time, and his fate or destiny may be limited. There is a method to divining one's fate. I have served the heavenly deities and understand this technique. I will try it for you." She set up an offering of wine and meat in the wild, burned money, and performed rituals over seven days and seven nights during the seventh day of the seventh month. She said, "I have now seen a response. I saw various good spirits praying for this young man, and he has been granted two additional years of life. His illness will certainly be cured, so there is nothing to worry about." Qing then recovered, and his devotion became even more meticulous. He lived for twenty-four more years after this, as the witch had predicted, with each calculation representing twelve years. This story is recorded in Zhu Lin (Jewel Forest). [97]

    宋魏世子者，梁郡人也，奉法精进，儿子遵修，唯妇迷闭，不信释教。元嘉初，女年十四病死，七日而苏。云可安施高座，并无量寿经。世子即为具设经座，女先虽斋戒礼拜，而未尝看经，今广记引有今字即升座转读，声句清利，下启父言：“儿死便往无量寿国，见父兄及己三人，池中已有芙蓉大华，后当化生其中；唯母独无，不胜此苦，乃心，故归启报。”语绝，复绝，母于是乃敬信法教。信字教字据广记引补　珠林十五太平广记一百十四 [98]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [98]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 奉法精进 - Refers to diligently following the law or religious precepts.
    • 儿子遵修 - Means 'sons who follow and practice' (the religious or legal precepts).
    • 唯妇迷闭 - Means 'only the wife is confused and closed-minded.'
    • 不信释教 - Means 'does not believe in the Buddhist teachings.'
    • 病死 - Means 'died of illness.'
    • 七日而苏 - Means 'revived after seven days.'
    • 可安施高座 - Means 'can be placed on a high seat' (likely referring to a Buddhist teaching seat).
    • 无量寿经 - Refers to the *Infinite Life Sutra*, a Buddhist scripture.
    • 斋戒礼拜 - Means 'fasting, abstaining, and prostrating' (religious practices).
    • 转读 - Means 'reciting or chanting' (likely referring to chanting scriptures).
    • 声句清利 - Means 'the voice and phrases are clear and fluent.'
    • 下启父言 - Means 'she then spoke to her father.'
    • 儿死便往无量寿国 - Means 'when the child died, she went to the Land of Infinite Life' (a Buddhist paradise).
    • 见父兄及己三人 - Means 'she saw her father, elder brother, and herself' (in the afterlife).
    • 池中已有芙蓉大华 - Means 'there were already large lotus flowers in the pond.'
    • 后当化生其中 - Means 'she will be reborn there later.'
    • 唯母独无 - Means 'only the mother was not there.'
    • 不胜此苦 - Means 'she could not bear this suffering.'
    • 乃心，故归启报 - Means 'she was determined, so she returned to report' (to her father).
    • 语绝，复绝 - Means 'her words ended, and she died again.'
    • 母于是乃敬信法教 - Means 'the mother then began to respect and believe in the teachings.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋魏世子 - Refers to the crown prince of the Wei state during the Song dynasty.
    • 梁郡 - Refers to a historical region in China.
    • 元嘉初 - Refers to the early years of the Yüanjia era (424–453 CE) during the Liu Song dynasty.
    • 无量寿国 - Refers to the Pure Land of Infinite Life, a Buddhist paradise.
    • 芙蓉大华 - Refers to lotus flowers, which are symbolic in Buddhism.
    • 化生 - Refers to rebirth in a spiritual realm, a concept in Buddhism.
    • 珠林 - Refers to a Buddhist text collection.
    • 太平广记 - Refers to a vast collection of Chinese stories and anecdotes from the Song dynasty.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • This phrase introduces the subject and his origin, but the structure is elliptical and requires clarification.
    • This sequence describes the family's religious practices and the wife's disbelief, but the syntax is compact and requires careful parsing.
    • This clause describes the daughter's death and revival, but the temporal sequence must be clearly rendered.
    • This long sequence describes the daughter's experience in the afterlife, requiring careful segmentation.
    • This clause explains the daughter's motivation for returning to report, but the syntax is elliptical and requires clarification.

    Proper Nouns

    • 宋魏世子 - The crown prince of the Wei state during the Song dynasty.
    • 梁郡 - A historical region in China.
    • 元嘉初 - The early years of the Yüanjia era (424–453 CE) during the Liu Song dynasty.
    • 无量寿国 - The Pure Land of Infinite Life, a Buddhist paradise.
    • 芙蓉大华 - Lotus flowers, symbolic in Buddhism.
    • 化生 - Rebirth in a spiritual realm, a concept in Buddhism.
    • 珠林 - A Buddhist text collection.
    • 太平广记 - A vast collection of Chinese stories and anecdotes from the Song dynasty.

    The Crown Prince of Wei, a native of Liang Commandery, diligently followed the law, and his sons also adhered to it. However, his wife was confused and closed-minded, and she did not believe in the Buddhist teachings. In the early years of the Yüanjia era, their daughter, who was fourteen years old, died of illness. Seven days later, she revived and said that a high seat and the Infinite Life Sutra could be placed there. The Crown Prince immediately arranged for the sutra and the seat. Although the daughter had previously fasted, abstained, and prostrated, she had never read the sutra before. Now, with the addition of the characters "广记引" (from the Zhu Lin and Taiping Guangji), she ascended the seat and recited the sutra fluently. She then spoke to her father, saying, "When I died, I went to the Land of Infinite Life and saw my father, elder brother, and myself. There were already large lotus flowers in the pond, and I will be reborn there later. However, my mother was not there, and I could not bear this suffering. I was determined, so I returned to report." Her words ended, and she died again. Her mother then began to respect and believe in the teachings. [98]

    宋张兴者，新兴人也，颇信佛法，尝从沙门僧融昙翼时受八戒。兴常为劫所引，夫得走逃，妻坐系狱，掠笞积日。时县失火，出囚路侧，会融翼同行，经过囚边；妻惊呼：“阇梨何以赐救？”融曰：“贫道力弱，无救如何？唯宜勤念观世音，庶获免耳。”妻便昼夜祈念，经十许日于夜，梦一沙门，以脚蹈之广记引作以足蹑之曰：“咄咄可起！”妻即惊起，钳锁桎梏，忽然俱解。便走趣户，户时犹闭，警防殊严；既无由出，虑有觉者，乃复着械广记引作乃却自械寻复得眠，又梦向沙门曰：“户已开矣！”妻觉而驰出，守备者并已惛睡，妻安步而去。时夜甚闇，行可数里，卒值一人；妻惧躄地，已而相讯，乃其夫也。相扶悲喜，夜投僧翼，翼藏匿之，遂得免。时元嘉初也。珠林十七广记一百十 [99]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [99]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 宋張興者 - Nominal phrase introducing the subject; '者' marks a nominal clause.
    • 從沙門僧融 - '從' means 'to follow' or 'to learn from'.
    • 時受八戒 - '時' refers to a specific occasion or moment.
    • 劫所引 - '劫' can mean 'robbers' or 'thieves'.
    • 夫得走逃 - '夫' is the subject, and '得' indicates success.
    • 妻坐系獄 - '坐' means 'to be punished' or 'to be held accountable'.
    • 掠笞積日 - '掠笞' refers to beating and flogging, and '積日' means 'for several days'.
    • 縣失火 - '縣' is a county or local government.
    • 出囚路側 - '出' means 'to release,' and '路側' means 'the side of the road'.
    • 會融翼同行 - '會' means 'to happen to be,' and '同行' means 'to walk together'.
    • 經過囚邊 - '經過' means 'to pass by,' and '囚邊' means 'near the prisoners'.
    • 驚呼 - '驚' means 'surprised,' and '呼' means 'to shout'.
    • 貧道力弱 - '貧道' is a humble self-reference for a monk, and '力弱' means 'weak in strength'.
    • 無救如何 - '無救' means 'no help,' and '如何' means 'how'.
    • 勤念觀世音 - '勤念' means 'diligently recite,' and '觀世音' is the name of the Bodhisattva Guanyin.
    • 庶獲免耳 - '庶' means 'perhaps,' '獲免' means 'be spared,' and '耳' is a particle indicating a slight tone.
    • 錐鎖桎梏 - '錐鎖' means 'shackles,' and '桎梏' means 'fetters'.
    • 忽然俱解 - '忽然' means 'suddenly,' and '俱解' means 'all were undone'.
    • 走趣戶 - '走' means 'to run,' and '趣戶' means 'towards the door'.
    • 戶時猶閉 - '戶' means 'door,' and '時猶閉' means 'still closed'.
    • 警防殊嚴 - '警防' means 'guards,' and '殊嚴' means 'very strict'.
    • 無由出 - '無由' means 'no way,' and '出' means 'to escape'.
    • 慮有覺者 - '慮' means 'to worry,' '有' means 'someone,' and '覺者' means 'who might wake up'.
    • 乃復著械 - '乃' means 'then,' '復' means 'again,' and '著械' means 'put the shackles on'.
    • 尋復得眠 - '尋' means 'soon,' and '復得眠' means 'fell asleep again'.
    • 戶已開矣 - '戶' means 'door,' '已開' means 'is open,' and '矣' is a particle indicating a completed action.
    • 守備者並已惛睡 - '守備者' means 'the guards,' '並已' means 'all were,' and '惛睡' means 'asleep'.
    • 安步而去 - '安步' means 'to walk calmly,' and '而去' means 'and left'.
    • 卒值一人 - '卒' means 'suddenly,' and '值' means 'to meet'.
    • 相扶悲喜 - '相扶' means 'supported each other,' and '悲喜' means 'joy and sorrow'.
    • 夜投僧翼 - '夜' means 'at night,' '投' means 'to go to,' and '僧翼' is the name of the monk.
    • 翼藏匿之 - '藏匿' means 'to hide'.
    • 遂得免 - '遂' means 'thus,' and '得免' means 'escaped'.
    • 時元嘉初也 - '時' means 'at that time,' '元嘉' is the era name, and '初也' means 'at the beginning'.
    • 珠林十七 - '珠林' is a collection of stories, and '十七' means 'Volume 17'.
    • 廣記一百十 - '廣記' is another collection of stories, and '一百十' means 'Volume 110'.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋 - The Song dynasty, a period in Chinese history.
    • 張興 - A person's name.
    • 沙門僧融 - A monk named Seng Rong.
    • 八戒 - The Eight Precepts, a set of Buddhist precepts.
    • 劫 - Robbers or thieves.
    • 元嘉 - An era name of the Song dynasty, specifically the reign of Emperor Wen of Song.
    • 珠林 - A collection of stories, possibly referring to the 'Book of Jewels'.
    • 廣記 - Another collection of stories, possibly referring to the 'Comprehensive Records'.

    Proper Nouns

    • 宋 - The Song dynasty.
    • 張興 - A person's name.
    • 沙門僧融 - A monk named Seng Rong.
    • 八戒 - The Eight Precepts.

    Cao Xing of the Song dynasty was a native of Xingning and had a strong belief in Buddhism. He once followed the monk Seng Rong (Tanyi) and received the Eight Precepts at that time. Cao Xing was often led by robbers, and his husband managed to escape, but his wife was imprisoned and beaten for several days. At one point, the county experienced a fire, and the prisoners were released to the side of the road. Coincidentally, Seng Rong happened to be walking with them and passed by the prisoners. The wife shouted in surprise, "Reverend, how can you save me?" Seng Rong replied, "I am weak in spirit and cannot help you. You should diligently recite the name of Guanyin, perhaps you may be spared." The wife then began to recite the name of Guanyin day and night. After about ten days, she had a dream in which a monk stepped on her feet and said, "Hurry up and get up!" The wife woke up in shock, and suddenly all her shackles and fetters were undone. She ran towards the door, but it was still closed, and the guards were very strict. There was no way to escape, and she worried that someone might wake up. So, she put the shackles back on and soon fell asleep again. In another dream, the same monk told her, "The door is open now!" The wife woke up and ran out, finding the guards all asleep. She walked calmly away. It was very dark at night, and she walked for several miles before suddenly encountering someone. The wife was afraid and fell to the ground, but when she looked up, it was her husband. They supported each other in joy and sorrow, and at night, they went to Seng Rong. Seng Rong hid them, and they thus escaped. This was at the beginning of the Yuanjia era. The story is recorded in Volume 17 of the "Book of Jewels" and Volume 110 of the "Comprehensive Records." [99]

    宋元嘉初，中有黄龙沙弥昙无竭者，诵观世音经，净修苦行。与诸徒属五十二人往寻佛国，备经荒险，贞志弥坚。既达天竺舍卫，路逢山象一群，竭赍经诵念，称名归命，有师子从林中出，象惊奔走。后有野牛一群，鸣吼而来，将欲加害，竭又如初归命，有大鹫飞来，牛便惊散。遂得克免。法苑珠林七十五 [100]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [100]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 宋元嘉初 - Temporal marker meaning 'in the early years of the Yuanjia period of the Song dynasty.'
    • 净修苦行 - Engaged in pure cultivation and austere practices, indicating a life of spiritual discipline and hardship.
    • 备经荒险，贞志弥坚 - Endured all kinds of dangers and hardships, yet his resolve became even stronger.
    • 既达天竺舍卫 - After arriving at Shāwèi in Tianzhu (India).
    • 竭赍经诵念，称名归命 - Monk Tan Wujie offered the sutra, chanted the name, and devoted himself to the Buddha.
    • 遂得克免 - Thus, he was able to escape unharmed.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋元嘉初 - The early years of the Yuanjia period (424-453 CE) of the Southern Song dynasty, a time of relative peace and cultural flourishing.
    • 黄龙沙弥昙无竭者 - Tan Wujie, a historical figure known for his pilgrimage to India, which is documented in the *Biography of Tan Wujie* (《昙无竭传》).
    • 观世音经 - Likely refers to the *Lotus Sutra* or another sutra dedicated to the Bodhisattva Guanyin, a central figure in Mahayana Buddhism.
    • 天竺舍卫 - Shāwèi in Tianzhu (India), specifically referring to the city of Shravasti, a major center of Buddhist activity.
    • 法苑珠林 - A large-scale Buddhist encyclopedic work compiled by Daoxuan during the Tang dynasty, containing various stories and teachings.

    Proper Nouns

    • 宋元嘉初 - The early years of the Yuanjia period of the Song dynasty.
    • 黄龙沙弥昙无竭者 - The novice monk Tan Wujie, known as Huanglong.
    • 观世音经 - The *Guanshiyin Sutra* or another sutra dedicated to the Bodhisattva Guanyin.
    • 天竺舍卫 - Shāwèi in Tianzhu (India), specifically the city of Shravasti.
    • 法苑珠林七十五 - Chapter 75 of the *Fa Yuan Zhu Lin*.

    In the early years of the Yuanjia period of the Song dynasty, there was a novice monk named Tan Wujie, known as Huanglong, who chanted the Guanshiyin Sutra and engaged in pure cultivation and austere practices. Together with his disciples, fifty-two in number, he went to seek the land of the Buddha. They endured all kinds of dangers and hardships, yet his resolve became even stronger. After arriving at Shāwèi in Tianzhu (India), he encountered a group of wild elephants on the road. Monk Tan Wujie offered the sutra, chanted the name, and devoted himself to the Buddha. A lion emerged from the forest, and the elephants were frightened and fled. Later, a herd of wild cattle, roaring, came to attack. Monk Tan Wujie again recited the name and dedicated himself to the Buddha as before. A large vulture flew over, and the cattle were frightened and scattered. Thus, he was able to escape unharmed. (Chapter 75 of the Fa Yuan Zhu Lin) [100]

    宋唐文伯，东海戆榆人也，弟好蒲博，家资都尽；屯中有寺，经过人或以钱上佛，弟屡窃取。久后病癞，卜者云：“祟由盗佛钱。”父怒曰：“佛是何神，乃令我儿致此？吾当试更虏夺，若复能病，可也。”前县令何欣之妇上织成宝盖带四枚，乃盗取之，以为腰带。不盈百日，复得恶病，发疮之始，起腰带处。世时在元嘉年初尔。珠林七十九广记一百十六
    　　宋沙门释道冏，扶风好畤人也，本姓马氏，学业淳粹，弱龄有声。元嘉二年九月，在洛阳为人作普贤斋，道俗四十许人，已经七日，正就中食，忽有一人，裤褶乘马，入至堂前，下马礼佛；冏谓常人，不加礼异，此人登马挥鞭，忽失所在，便见赤光，赫然竟天，良久而灭。后三年十二月，在白衣家复作普贤斋，将竟之日，有二沙门，容服如凡，直来礼佛；泉中谓是庸僧，不甚尊仰，聊问何居？答曰：“住在前屯。 [101]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [101]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 宋唐文伯 - A person named Wen Bo from the Tang dynasty.
    • 东海戆榆人也 - Describes Wen Bo as being from the region of Dangyu in the East Sea, an archaic way of specifying his place of origin.
    • 弟好蒲博 - Refers to Wen Bo's brother who enjoys playing dice or gambling.
    • 家资都尽 - Means that the brother has squandered all their family wealth.
    • 屯中有寺 - Indicates that there is a temple within a military camp or garrison.
    • 经过人或以钱上佛 - Refers to people occasionally offering money to the Buddha when passing by the temple.
    • 弟屡窃取 - Means that the brother repeatedly stole the offerings.
    • 久后病癞 - Describes the brother falling ill with leprosy after a long time.
    • 卜者云：“祟由盗佛钱。” - A diviner says that the illness is caused by stealing the offerings to the Buddha.
    • 父怒曰 - The father becomes angry and speaks.
    • 佛是何神 - The father questions the nature of the Buddha as a deity.
    • 乃令我儿致此 - The father wonders why the Buddha would cause his son to fall ill.
    • 吾当试更虏夺 - The father decides to test whether he can also fall ill by stealing the offerings.
    • 若复能病，可也 - If he too falls ill, then the diviner's words are true.
    • 前县令何欣之妇上织成宝盖带四枚 - Refers to the wife of a former county magistrate named He Xin who offered four brocade belts as a canopy.
    • 乃盗取之，以为腰带 - The father steals these belts and uses them as a belt for himself.
    • 不盈百日，复得恶病 - Within less than a hundred days, the father also falls ill.
    • 发疮之始，起腰带处 - The illness begins at the place where the belt was worn.
    • 世时在元嘉年初尔 - This event took place during the early years of the reign of Emperor Yuan of Liu Song.
    • 珠林七十九广记一百十六 - References to the *Zhu Lin* (Pearl Forest) and *Guang Ji* (Comprehensive Records), which are collections of anecdotes and stories.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋 - Refers to the Liu Song dynasty, a period in Chinese history.
    • 元嘉 - The era name of Emperor Yuan of Liu Song, indicating the specific time frame.
    • 沙门释道冏 - A Buddhist monk named Dao Jiong, who is the main subject of the second part of the text.
    • 扶风好畤人也 - Describes Dao Jiong's place of origin, which is a county in the region of Fufeng.
    • 本姓马氏 - Indicates that Dao Jiong originally had the surname Ma.
    • 学业淳粹 - Means that Dao Jiong had pure and refined learning.
    • 弱龄有声 - Refers to Dao Jiong being well-known for his scholarly achievements at a young age.
    • 元嘉二年九月 - Specifies the date of the event, which is the ninth month of the second year of the reign of Emperor Yuan.
    • 洛阳 - The capital city of the Liu Song dynasty, where the event took place.
    • 普贤斋 - A type of Buddhist ritual or gathering dedicated to the Bodhisattva Samantabhadra.
    • 道俗四十许人 - Refers to about forty people, including both monks and laypeople.
    • 已经七日 - Indicates that the ritual has been ongoing for seven days.
    • 中食 - Refers to the midday meal during the ritual.
    • 裤褶乘马 - Describes a person wearing trousers and a jacket riding a horse.
    • 直来礼佛 - The person comes directly to pay respects to the Buddha.
    • 冏谓常人 - Dao Jiong considers the person to be an ordinary person.
    • 赤光，赫然竟天 - Describes a bright red light that spreads across the sky.
    • 后三年十二月 - Specifies the date of the second event, which is the twelfth month of the third year after the first event.
    • 白衣家 - Refers to a layperson's home.
    • 将竟之日 - Indicates the day when the ritual is about to end.
    • 二沙门 - Refers to two monks.
    • 容服如凡 - Describes the monks as appearing like ordinary people.
    • 直来礼佛 - The monks come directly to pay respects to the Buddha.
    • 泉中 - Refers to Dao Jiong, who is called 'Quan Zhong' in this context.
    • 住在前屯 - Indicates that the monks live in a previous garrison or camp.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • This section addresses the syntactic structure of the text, focusing on the clarity and coherence of sentences. It highlights potential ambiguities that may arise due to the complex sentence structures and the use of classical Chinese, which can sometimes lead to multiple interpretations. The goal is to ensure that the translation maintains the original meaning while being clear and understandable in the target language.

    Proper Nouns

    • 宋唐文伯 - A person named Wen Bo from the Tang dynasty.
    • 东海戆榆 - A region in the East Sea.
    • 弟 - Wen Bo's brother.
    • 蒲博 - Gambling or dice games.
    • 家资 - Family wealth.
    • 屯 - A military camp or garrison.
    • 寺 - A temple.
    • 经过人 - People passing by.
    • 佛 - The Buddha.
    • 卜者 - A diviner.
    • 祟 - An evil spirit or curse.
    • 父 - The father.
    • 何欣 - A former county magistrate.
    • 织成宝盖带 - Brocade belts used as a canopy.
    • 世时 - The time frame.
    • 元嘉 - The era name of Emperor Yuan of Liu Song.
    • 珠林七十九 - The *Zhu Lin* (Pearl Forest), a collection of anecdotes.
    • 广记一百十六 - The *Guang Ji* (Comprehensive Records), another collection of anecdotes.
    • 宋沙门释道冏 - A Buddhist monk named Dao Jiong.
    • 扶风好畤 - A county in the region of Fufeng.
    • 马氏 - The original surname of Dao Jiong.
    • 洛阳 - The capital city of the Liu Song dynasty.
    • 普贤斋 - A type of Buddhist ritual dedicated to the Bodhisattva Samantabhadra.
    • 道俗 - Monks and laypeople.
    • 裤褶 - Trousers and a jacket.
    • 赤光 - A bright red light.
    • 白衣家 - A layperson's home.

    During the Tang dynasty, there was a man named Wen Bo from the region of Dangyu in the East Sea. His younger brother enjoyed gambling and eventually squandered all their family wealth. There was a temple within a military camp, and sometimes people passing by would offer money to the Buddha. The brother frequently stole these offerings. After a long time, he fell ill with leprosy. A diviner said, "The illness is caused by stealing the offerings to the Buddha." The father became angry and said, "What kind of god is the Buddha, causing my son to fall ill? I will try to steal the offerings myself. If I too fall ill, then the diviner's words are true." He stole four brocade belts that the wife of a former county magistrate named He Xin had offered as a canopy and used them as a belt. Within less than a hundred days, the father also fell ill, and the illness began at the place where the belt was worn. This event took place during the early years of the reign of Emperor Yuan of Liu Song. It is recorded in the Zhu Lin (Pearl Forest) and Guang Ji (Comprehensive Records).

    In the Liu Song dynasty, there was a Buddhist monk named Dao Jiong from the county of Haozhi in the region of Fufeng. Originally, he had the surname Ma and had pure and refined learning, being well-known for his scholarly achievements at a young age. In the ninth month of the second year of the reign of Emperor Yuan, he was in Luoyang, the capital city, conducting a ritual dedicated to the Bodhisattva Samantabhadra for someone. About forty people, including both monks and laypeople, had already been participating in the ritual for seven days. On the day of the midday meal, a person suddenly appeared, wearing trousers and a jacket and riding a horse, entering the hall and dismounting to pay respects to the Buddha. Dao Jiong considered the person to be an ordinary person and did not give him special attention. The person then mounted his horse, waved his whip, and suddenly disappeared. A bright red light spread across the sky, lasting for a long time before fading away. Three years later, in the twelfth month, Dao Jiong was again in a layperson's home, conducting a ritual dedicated to the Bodhisattva Samantabhadra. On the day the ritual was about to end, two monks appeared, dressed in ordinary clothing, and came directly to pay respects to the Buddha. Dao Jiong thought they were common monks and did not show much respect. He casually asked where they lived, and they replied, "We live in a previous garrison." [101]

    ”时众白衣有张道，觉其有异，至心礼拜，沙门出门，行可数十步，忽有飞尘，直上冲天，追目此僧，不复知所。冏以七年与同学来游京师，时司空何尚之始构南涧精舍，冏寓居焉。夜中忽见四人乘一新车，从四人，传教来在屋内，呼与共载道，冏惊其夜至，疑而未言，因眼闭，不觉升车。俄而至郡后沈桥，见一贵人，着?被笺布单衣，坐床焘伞，形似华盖，卤簿从卫可数百人，悉服黄衣，见冏惊曰：“行般舟道人，精心远诣，○欲知其处耳！何故将来？”即遣人引送冏还。至精舍门外，失所送人，门闭如故，扣唤久之，寺内诸僧咸惊相报告，开门内之。视所住房户，犹故关之。珠林十七 [102]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [102]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 白衣 - Refers to ordinary people, as opposed to officials or monks.
    • 沙门 - Refers to a monk or Buddhist practitioner.
    • 般舟道人 - Refers to a traveler on a spiritual journey, possibly alluding to Buddhist or Taoist practices.
    • 卤簿 - Refers to the ceremonial procession and attendants accompanying a noble person.
    • 黄衣 - Refers to yellow clothing, which may symbolize nobility or a specific religious or cultural status.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 司空 - An official title in the Chinese bureaucracy, equivalent to a minister or grand works commissioner.
    • 南涧精舍 - A monastery or retreat, likely named after its location near a stream.
    • 珠林十七 - Refers to a specific volume in a collection of texts, likely a Buddhist or Taoist canon.

    Proper Nouns

    • 张道 - A person's name.
    • 冏 - A person's name.
    • 司空何尚之 - The name of an official, '何尚之' (He Shangzhi).
    • 南涧精舍 - The name of a monastery or retreat.
    • 珠林十七 - A reference to a specific volume in a collection of texts.

    At that time, among the common people, there was a man named Zhang Dao. He felt that the monk was unusual and worshiped him with utmost sincerity. The monk went out and walked about ten steps. Suddenly, flying dust shot straight up to the sky. Looking back at the monk, one could no longer see where he had gone. Jiong came to the capital with his fellow students after seven years. At that time, the Grand Works Commissioner He Shangzhi had just begun to construct the Nanjian Monastery, and Jiong stayed there. At night, he suddenly saw four people in a new carriage, accompanied by four attendants. They came into the house and called Jiong to ride with them. Jiong was surprised that they came at night and hesitated to speak. He closed his eyes and did not feel himself being lifted onto the carriage. Soon, they arrived at the Sheng Bridge behind the county. There, he saw a noble person wearing a robe made of fine cloth, sitting on a bed with an umbrella-like canopy, its shape resembling a canopy. There were hundreds of attendants and guards, all wearing yellow clothes. Seeing Jiong, the noble person was surprised and said, "The traveler on the Ban Zhou path, with sincere heart and far-reaching aspirations, wants to know the place. Why bring him here?" He immediately sent people to escort Jiong back. When they reached the monastery gate, the people who had escorted him were gone, and the gate was still closed. After knocking and calling for a long time, the monks inside the monastery were all startled and reported it. They opened the door and let him in. Looking at the room where he had stayed, it was still locked. Zhu Lin, Volume 17. [102]

    宋李旦，字世则，广陵人也，以孝谨质素着称乡里。元嘉三年，正月十四日，暴病，心下不冷，七日而苏，含以饮粥，○○○○。○有一人，持信旛来至床头，称府君教唤，旦便随去。直北向行，道甚平净。既至，城阁高丽，似今宫阙，遣传教慰劳，问呼：“旦可前”至大厅事上，见有三十人，单衣青帻，列坐森然，一人东坐，披袍隐凡，左右侍卫，可有百余，视旦而语坐人云：“当示以诸狱，令世知也。 [103]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [103]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 以孝谨质素著称乡里 - Renowned in the village for filial piety, diligence, and simplicity.
    • 暴病 - Sudden illness or ailment.
    • 心下不冷 - The heart was not cold, suggesting that Rì Dàn remained warm or alive despite the illness.
    • 七日而苏 - Revived after seven days, indicating a miraculous recovery.
    • 含以饮粥 - Literally means 'to drink porridge while holding it,' but in this context, it likely means 'to be fed porridge.'
    • 持信幡来至床头 - Carrying a banner of faith came to the bedside, suggesting a messenger or guide from the afterlife.
    • 府君教唤 - The lord of the prefecture called, referring to a higher authority or deity.
    • 直北向行 - Went directly north, indicating the direction of the journey.
    • 道甚平净 - The road was very flat and clean, describing the path taken.
    • 城阁高丽 - The city gates and towers were tall and beautiful, describing the appearance of the city.
    • 遣传教慰劳 - Sent a messenger to comfort and console, indicating a gesture of care.
    • 见有三十人，单衣青帻 - Saw thirty people, wearing plain clothes and blue headbands, describing the appearance of the group.
    • 列坐森然 - Sitting in an orderly and solemn manner, describing the arrangement of the group.
    • 披袍隐凡 - Wearing a robe and hiding in the mundane, suggesting a figure of authority or divinity.
    • 左右侍卫，可有百余 - With about a hundred attendants on either side, describing the entourage.
    • 视旦而语坐人云 - Looked at Dàn and spoke to the seated people, saying, indicating the speaker's address.
    • 当示以诸狱 - Will show you the various prisons, indicating the purpose of the visit.
    • 令世知也 - To make the world know, indicating the broader message or lesson.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋李旦，字世则，广陵人也 - Refers to Lǐ Dàn, a man from Guangling (modern-day Yangzhou) during the Song dynasty. Guangling was an important city in southern China.
    • 元嘉三年，正月十四日 - Refers to the third year of the Yuán Jiā era during the reign of Emperor Wu of Liu Song (426 CE). The Yuán Jiā era was a period of peace and prosperity.
    • 府君 - Refers to the local lord or magistrate, often used in folk tales and religious contexts to denote a higher authority or deity.
    • 信幡 - A banner or flag used in religious or ceremonial contexts, often associated with spiritual guidance.
    • 城阁高丽 - Describes the grandeur and beauty of the city, which may be a metaphor for the afterlife or a heavenly realm.
    • 单衣青帻 - Describes the attire of the people, which may symbolize their status or role in the afterlife.
    • 披袍隐凡 - Describes a figure of authority or divinity, possibly a deity or a high-ranking official in the afterlife.
    • 诸狱 - Refers to various prisons or realms of punishment, often found in Chinese mythology and folklore.

    Proper Nouns

    • 宋李旦，字世则 - Lǐ Dàn, a historical or fictional figure from the Song dynasty.
    • 广陵 - Guangling, a city in modern-day Yangzhou, Jiangsu Province.
    • 元嘉三年 - The third year of the Yuán Jiā era (426 CE) during the reign of Emperor Wu of Liu Song.
    • 府君 - A title for a local lord or magistrate, often used in folk tales and religious contexts.
    • 信幡 - A banner or flag used in religious or ceremonial contexts.
    • 城阁 - City gates and towers, often used to describe grand structures.
    • 诸狱 - Various prisons or realms of punishment, often found in Chinese mythology and folklore.

    Lǐ Dàn, courtesy name Shìzé, was a man from Guangling, renowned in his village for his filial piety, diligence, and simplicity. In the third year of the Yuán Jiā era, on the fourteenth day of the first month, he suddenly fell ill, but his heart remained warm, and he revived after seven days, being fed porridge. One day, a person carrying a banner of faith came to his bedside, saying that the lord of the prefecture had called for him. Lǐ Dàn immediately followed the messenger. They traveled directly north, the road being very flat and clean. Upon arrival, they saw city gates and towers that were tall and beautiful, resembling the palaces of today. A messenger was sent to comfort and console him, and he was called to come forward to the main hall. There, he saw thirty people, all wearing plain clothes and blue headbands, sitting in an orderly and solemn manner. One person sat on the east side, wearing a robe and hiding in the mundane, with about a hundred attendants on either side. The person looked at Lǐ Dàn and spoke to the seated people, saying, "We will show you the various prisons, to make the world know." [103]

    ”旦闻言已，举头四视，都失向处，乃是地狱中。见群罪人，受诸苦报，呻吟号呼，不可忍视。寻有传教，称：“府君信君，可还去，当更相迎。”因此而还。至六年正月复死，七日又活，述所见事，较略如先。或有罪因寄语报家，道生时犯罪，使为作福，称说姓字，亲识乡伍，旦依言寻求皆得之。又云：“甲申年当行疾疠，杀诸恶人，佛家弟子，作八关斋，心修善行，可得免也。”旦本作道家祭酒，即欲弃箓本法道民谏制故遂两事，而常劝化，作八关斋。法苑珠林六 [104]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [104]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 旦聞言已 - Literally 'Dawn heard the words,' likely referring to a person named 'Dawn' or a metaphorical expression.
    • 舉頭四視 - 'Raised his head to look all around,' indicating a moment of realization or shock.
    • 都失向處 - 'Completely lost the previous place,' suggesting a sudden change of location or state.
    • 乃是地獄中 - 'It is in the underworld,' indicating a shift to a supernatural or afterlife setting.
    • 群罪人，受諸苦報 - 'A crowd of sinners, suffering various punishments,' describing the torment of the damned.
    • 呻吟號呼 - 'Groaning and shouting,' depicting the sounds of suffering.
    • 不可忍視 - 'Cannot bear to look at,' emphasizing the intensity of the scene.
    • 傳教 - 'Messenger' or 'teacher,' likely referring to a spiritual guide or intermediary.
    • 府君信君 - 'The Lord of the Mansion believes in you,' indicating a divine or authoritative figure.
    • 可還去 - 'Can return and go,' suggesting permission to leave.
    • 當更相迎 - 'Will come to greet again,' indicating a future meeting.
    • 因此而還 - 'Therefore returned,' marking the end of the experience.
    • 至六年正月復死 - 'In the sixth year of January, he died again,' indicating a cycle of death and resurrection.
    • 七日又活 - 'Seven days later, he came back to life,' describing a miraculous event.
    • 述所見事 - 'Described what he saw,' indicating a recounting of the experience.
    • 較略如先 - 'Similar in detail to before,' suggesting consistency in the narrative.
    • 或有罪因寄語報家 - 'Some of the sins were reported by a messenger to the family,' indicating a supernatural communication.
    • 道生時犯罪 - 'When he was born, he committed a sin,' suggesting a predestined or inherited guilt.
    • 使為作福 - 'Ordered to perform good deeds,' indicating a divine command.
    • 稱說姓字 - 'Called out names and surnames,' suggesting identification.
    • 親識鄉伍 - 'Familiar faces and fellow villagers,' indicating community recognition.
    • 旦依言尋求皆得之 - 'Dawn followed the words and found them all,' suggesting successful fulfillment of instructions.
    • 又云 - 'Also said,' introducing additional information.
    • 甲申年當行疾疠 - 'In the year Jia Shen, a plague will spread,' indicating a prediction.
    • 殺諸惡人 - 'Killing all evil people,' suggesting divine retribution.
    • 佛家弟子 - 'Buddhist disciples,' indicating followers of Buddhism.
    • 作八关斋 - 'Performing the Eight Precepts,' referring to a Buddhist practice.
    • 心修善行 - 'Practicing good deeds with the heart,' indicating moral cultivation.
    • 可得免也 - 'Can be exempted,' suggesting salvation or protection.
    • 旦本作道家祭酒 - 'Dawn originally practiced Daoism as a priest,' indicating a religious background.
    • 即欲棄箓本法 - 'Wanted to abandon the Daoist scriptures,' suggesting a change in belief.
    • 道民谏制 - 'Daoist followers advised and controlled,' indicating external influence.
    • 而常劝化 - 'And often preached,' suggesting active religious propagation.
    • 法苑珠林六 - 'The Sixth Chapter of the Treasure Forest of Dharma,' indicating the source of the text.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 地獄 - 'Underworld,' a concept in Chinese religion and mythology.
    • 府君 - 'Lord of the Mansion,' a title for a deity or authoritative figure in the underworld.
    • 八关斋 - 'The Eight Precepts,' a Buddhist practice of fasting and moral observance.
    • 法苑珠林 - 'The Treasure Forest of Dharma,' a Buddhist encyclopedia.
    • 甲申年 - 'The year Jia Shen,' a specific year in the Chinese zodiac calendar.

    Proper Nouns

    • 旦 - Likely a proper name or title, possibly referring to a person named 'Dawn.'
    • 府君 - A title for a deity or authoritative figure in the underworld.
    • 法苑珠林 - The title of a Buddhist encyclopedia.
    • 甲申年 - A specific year in the Chinese zodiac calendar.

    Dawn heard the words and raised his head to look all around, completely losing the previous place, realizing he was in the underworld. He saw a crowd of sinners suffering various punishments, groaning and shouting, a sight that was unbearable to look at. Soon, a messenger appeared and said, "The Lord of the Mansion believes in you; you can return and go, and we will come to greet you again." Therefore, he returned. In the sixth year of January, he died again, but seven days later, he came back to life. He described what he saw, which was similar in detail to before. Some of the sins were reported by a messenger to the family, saying that when he was born, he committed a sin and was ordered to perform good deeds. The messenger called out names and surnames, and familiar faces and fellow villagers were found by Dawn following the words. He also said, "In the year Jia Shen, a plague will spread, killing all evil people. Buddhist disciples, by performing the Eight Precepts and practicing good deeds with the heart, can be exempted." Dawn originally practiced Daoism as a priest, but he wanted to abandon the Daoist scriptures due to the advice and control of Daoist followers, so he engaged in both practices and often preached, performing the Eight Precepts. The Sixth Chapter of the Treasure Forest of Dharma. [104]

    宋尚书仆射荥阳郑鲜之，元嘉四年，从大驾巡京至都，夕暴亡，乃灵语着人曰：“吾寿命久尽，早应过世，赖比岁来敬信佛法，放生布施，以此功德，延驰数年耳。夫幽显报应，有若影响，宜放落俗务，崇心大教。”于时胜贵多皆闻云。珠林六
    　　宋周宗者，广陵肥如人也，元嘉七年，随刘彦之北伐，王师失利，与同邑六人逃窜。闲行于彭城北，遇一空寺，无有僧徒，中有形像，以水精为相，因共窃取，出屯贸食。其一人羸病，等辈轻之，独不得分。既各还家，三四年中，宗等五人，相继病癞而死，不得分者，独获全免。珠林七十九广记一百十六 [105]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [105]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 宋尚书仆射荥阳郑鲜之 - Title and name of a high-ranking official during the Liu Song dynasty.
    • 元嘉四年 - The fourth year of the Yuanjia era, a reign period of Emperor Wu of Liu Song.
    • 从大驾巡京至都 - Following the imperial procession to the capital city.
    • 夕暴亡 - Died suddenly in the evening.
    • 灵语着人曰 - The spirit spoke to someone saying.
    • 寿命久尽 - Life span has long ended.
    • 早应过世 - Should have passed away long ago.
    • 赖比岁来敬信佛法 - Due to the recent years of respecting and believing in Buddhism.
    • 放生布施 - Releasing animals and making charitable donations.
    • 以此功德 - By this merit.
    • 延驰数年耳 - Extended by several years.
    • 幽显报应 - Retribution in the visible and invisible worlds.
    • 有若影响 - As if it were an echo or shadow.
    • 宜放落俗务 - Should abandon worldly affairs.
    • 崇心大教 - Uphold the great teaching.
    • 宋周宗者 - Zhou Zong of the Song dynasty.
    • 广陵肥如人也 - A person from Feiru, Guangling.
    • 元嘉七年 - The seventh year of the Yuanjia era.
    • 随刘彦之北伐 - Followed Liu Yan to the northern campaign.
    • 王师失利 - The imperial army suffered defeat.
    • 与同邑六人逃窜 - With six fellow townspeople, they fled.
    • 闲行于彭城北 - Wandering north of Pengcheng.
    • 遇一空寺 - Encountered an empty temple.
    • 无有僧徒 - No monks or disciples.
    • 中有形像 - There were statues inside.
    • 以水精为相 - Made of crystal.
    • 因共窃取 - They collectively stole.
    • 出屯贸食 - Sold at the military camp.
    • 其一人羸病 - One of them was weak and ill.
    • 等辈轻之 - The others looked down on him.
    • 独不得分 - Only he did not get a share.
    • 既各还家 - After each returned home.
    • 三四年中 - Within three to four years.
    • 宗等五人 - Zhou Zong and four others.
    • 相继病癞而死 - Died one after another from leprosy.
    • 不得分者 - The one who did not get a share.
    • 独获全免 - Remained unharmed.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋尚书仆射荥阳郑鲜之 - Zheng Xianzhi was a high-ranking official during the Liu Song dynasty.
    • 元嘉 - The Yuanjia era was a reign period of Emperor Wu of Liu Song.
    • 从大驾巡京至都 - This refers to an imperial procession to the capital city, which was a significant event.
    • 灵语着人曰 - This phrase suggests a supernatural or spiritual experience.
    • 佛法 - Buddhism, which was introduced to China during the Han dynasty and became more widespread during the Six Dynasties period.
    • 放生布施 - These are common Buddhist practices of releasing animals and making charitable donations.
    • 幽显报应 - This concept emphasizes the idea of karma and retribution in both the visible and invisible worlds.
    • 宋周宗者 - Zhou Zong was a person from the Song dynasty.
    • 广陵肥如人也 - Feiru was a place in Guangling, which is now part of Jiangsu province.
    • 元嘉七年 - The seventh year of the Yuanjia era.
    • 随刘彦之北伐 - Liu Yan was a general who led a northern campaign.
    • 王师失利 - This refers to the defeat of the imperial army, which was a significant event in the history of the Liu Song dynasty.
    • 与同邑六人逃窜 - This describes a group of people fleeing after a military defeat.
    • 闲行于彭城北 - Pengcheng (modern-day Xuzhou) was an important city in northern China.
    • 遇一空寺 - This describes encountering an empty temple, which is a common motif in Chinese literature.
    • 以水精为相 - Crystal statues were a luxury item in ancient China.
    • 因共窃取 - This describes a group of people stealing, which was a serious crime.
    • 出屯贸食 - This refers to selling goods at a military camp, which was a common practice during times of war.
    • 其一人羸病 - This describes one of the group being weak and ill, which may have influenced the others' actions.
    • 等辈轻之 - This describes the others looking down on the weak and ill person, which reflects social dynamics.
    • 独不得分 - This describes the weak and ill person not getting a share, which may have contributed to their survival.
    • 既各还家 - This describes the group returning home after the event.
    • 三四年中 - This describes the passage of time, which is a common motif in Chinese literature.
    • 宗等五人 - This describes Zhou Zong and four others, who died from leprosy.
    • 相继病癞而死 - This describes the group dying one after another from leprosy, which was a serious disease.
    • 不得分者 - This describes the weak and ill person who did not get a share, which may have contributed to their survival.
    • 独获全免 - This describes the weak and ill person remaining unharmed, which is a common motif in Chinese literature.

    During the reign of Emperor Wu of the Liu Song dynasty, Zheng Xianzhi, a high-ranking official and servant to the emperor, traveled with the imperial procession to the capital city in the fourth year of the Yuanjia era. In the evening, he died suddenly. His spirit then spoke to someone, saying, "My life span has long ended, and I should have passed away long ago. However, due to the recent years of respecting and believing in Buddhism, releasing animals, and making charitable donations, by this merit, my life was extended by several years. Retribution in the visible and invisible worlds is as if it were an echo or shadow. One should abandon worldly affairs and uphold the great teaching." At that time, many noble and wealthy people heard of this. (Zhu Lin, Volume 6)

    Zhou Zong, a person from Feiru, Guangling, followed General Liu Yan to the northern campaign in the seventh year of the Yuanjia era. The imperial army suffered defeat, and Zhou Zong fled with six fellow townspeople. Wandering north of Pengcheng, they encountered an empty temple with no monks or disciples. Inside the temple, there were statues made of crystal. They collectively stole the statues and sold them at the military camp. One of them was weak and ill, and the others looked down on him, so he did not get a share. After each returned home, within three to four years, Zhou Zong and four others died one after another from leprosy, while the one who did not get a share remained unharmed. (Zhu Lin, Volume 79; Guangji, Volume 116) [105]

    宋案当作晋广记引无顺阳郭诠，字仲衡，晋益州刺史，义熙初，以党附桓玄被诛。二句依广记引补亡后三十余载，元嘉八年，忽见形诣女婿南阳刘凝之家，车卫甚盛。谓凝之曰：“仆有谪事，可见为作四十僧会，当得免也。”言终不见。刘谓是魍魉，不以在意。复夕，诠又与女梦言：“吾有谪罚，已告汝婿，令为设会；何以至今四字广记引有不能见矜耶？”女晨起，见诠从户过，怒言：“竟不能相救？今便就罪。”女号踊留之，问当何处设斋？答云：“可归吾舍。”倏然复没。凝之即狼狈供办，会毕，有人称诠信与凝之相闻，言：“感君厚惠，事始获宥。”言已失去，于是而绝。珠林九十一广记三百二十四 [106]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [106]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 宋案当作晋广记引无顺阳郭诠 - This phrase means that the story of Guo Quan should be recorded in the *Jin Guangji* (晋广记) instead of the *Song* (宋) records. '宋案' refers to the *Song* records, and '当作晋广记引' suggests that the story should be included in the *Jin Guangji'.
    • 以党附桓玄被诛 - This phrase means 'because he was associated with and supported Huan Xuan, he was executed.' '党附' means 'to associate with' or 'to support,' and '被诛' means 'to be executed.'
    • 二句依广记引补亡 - This phrase means 'two sentences were added based on the *Jin Guangji* to supplement the missing parts.' '依广记引' means 'based on the *Jin Guangji*,' and '补亡' means 'to supplement the missing parts.'
    • 忽见形诣女婿南阳刘凝之家 - This phrase means 'he suddenly appeared in the form of a ghost to visit his son-in-law Liu Ning's house in Nanyang.' '忽见形' means 'suddenly appeared in the form of a ghost,' and '诣' means 'to visit.'
    • 谓凝之曰 - This phrase means 'he said to Liu Ning.' '谓' means 'to say,' and '凝之' is the name of Liu Ning.
    • 仆有谪事 - This phrase means 'I have a punishment to serve.' '仆' is a humble term for 'I,' and '谪事' means 'punishment to serve.'
    • 可见为作四十僧会 - This phrase means 'you can arrange for forty Buddhist gatherings.' '可见为作' means 'you can arrange for,' and '四十僧会' means 'forty Buddhist gatherings.'
    • 当得免也 - This phrase means 'I will be able to escape it.' '当得免' means 'will be able to escape,' and '也' is a particle that emphasizes the statement.
    • 刘谓是魍魉 - This phrase means 'Liu thought it was a ghost.' '刘' is the name of Liu Ning, '谓' means 'thought,' and '魍魉' means 'ghost.'
    • 不以在意 - This phrase means 'he did not take it seriously.' '不以在意' means 'did not take it seriously.'
    • 复夕 - This phrase means 'the next night.' '复' means 'again,' and '夕' means 'night.'
    • 诠又与女梦言 - This phrase means 'Quan again spoke to his daughter in a dream.' '诠' is the name of Guo Quan, '与女梦言' means 'spoke to his daughter in a dream.'
    • 吾有谪罚 - This phrase means 'I have a punishment to serve.' '吾' means 'I,' and '谪罚' means 'punishment to serve.'
    • 已告汝婿 - This phrase means 'I have already told your husband.' '已告' means 'have already told,' and '汝婿' means 'your husband.'
    • 令为设会 - This phrase means 'to arrange for a gathering.' '令为设会' means 'to arrange for a gathering.'
    • 何以至今不能见矜耶 - This phrase means 'why can't I be spared until now?' '何以' means 'why,' '至今' means 'until now,' '不能见矜' means 'can't be spared,' and '耶' is a particle that emphasizes the question.
    • 女晨起 - This phrase means 'the daughter got up in the morning.' '女' means 'daughter,' and '晨起' means 'got up in the morning.'
    • 见诠从户过 - This phrase means 'she saw Quan passing through the door.' '见' means 'saw,' '诠' is the name of Guo Quan, '从户过' means 'passing through the door.'
    • 怒言 - This phrase means 'she angrily said.' '怒言' means 'angrily said.'
    • 竟不能相救 - This phrase means 'you couldn't save me at all.' '竟' means 'at all,' '不能相救' means 'couldn't save me.'
    • 今便就罪 - This phrase means 'now I will face the punishment.' '今' means 'now,' '便就罪' means 'will face the punishment.'
    • 女号踊留之 - This phrase means 'the daughter cried and tried to stop him.' '女' means 'daughter,' '号踊' means 'cried and tried to stop,' and '留之' means 'stop him.'
    • 问当何处设斋 - This phrase means 'she asked where to set up the gathering.' '问' means 'asked,' '当何处' means 'where,' and '设斋' means 'set up the gathering.'
    • 答云 - This phrase means 'he replied.' '答' means 'replied,' and '云' means 'said.'
    • 可归吾舍 - This phrase means 'you can return to my house.' '可' means 'can,' '归吾舍' means 'return to my house.'
    • 倏然复没 - This phrase means 'he disappeared suddenly.' '倏然' means 'suddenly,' and '复没' means 'disappeared.'
    • 凝之即狼狈供办 - This phrase means 'Liu Ning immediately hurriedly arranged for it.' '凝之' is the name of Liu Ning, '即' means 'immediately,' '狼狈' means 'hurriedly,' and '供办' means 'arranged for it.'
    • 会毕 - This phrase means 'the gathering was completed.' '会' means 'gathering,' and '毕' means 'completed.'
    • 有人称诠信与凝之相闻 - This phrase means 'someone said that Quan had sent a message to Liu Ning.' '有人称' means 'someone said,' '诠信' means 'Quan's message,' and '与凝之相闻' means 'sent to Liu Ning.'
    • 言：‘感君厚惠，事始获宥。’ - This phrase means 'saying, 'I am grateful for your kindness, and I have finally been pardoned.'' '言' means 'saying,' '感君厚惠' means 'I am grateful for your kindness,' and '事始获宥' means 'I have finally been pardoned.'
    • 言已失去，于是而绝 - This phrase means 'the message was lost, and then he disappeared.' '言已失去' means 'the message was lost,' and '于是而绝' means 'then he disappeared.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋广记 - The *Jin Guangji* (晋广记) is a historical record from the Jin dynasty.
    • 义熙初 - This refers to the early years of the reign of Emperor Shao of Liu Song (刘宋少帝), specifically the year 405 AD.
    • 桓玄 - Huan Xuan was a powerful general and regent during the Eastern Jin dynasty who briefly usurped the throne in 403 AD.
    • 元嘉八年 - This refers to the eighth year of the reign of Emperor Wen of Liu Song (刘宋文帝), specifically the year 431 AD.
    • 南阳 - Nanyang is a city in Henan province, known for its historical significance.
    • 珠林九十一 - This refers to the 91st volume of the *Zhu Lin* (珠林), a collection of Buddhist stories.
    • 广记三百二十四 - This refers to the 324th volume of the *Guang Ji* (广记), another collection of stories and legends.

    Proper Nouns

    • 宋 - Refers to the *Song* records.
    • 晋广记 - Refers to the *Jin Guangji*.
    • 顺阳郭诠 - Refers to Guo Quan, a historical figure.
    • 晋益州刺史 - Refers to the position of governor of Yizhou during the Jin dynasty.
    • 义熙初 - Refers to the early years of the reign of Emperor Shao of Liu Song.
    • 桓玄 - Refers to Huan Xuan, a historical figure.
    • 元嘉八年 - Refers to the eighth year of the reign of Emperor Wen of Liu Song.
    • 南阳 - Refers to Nanyang, a city in Henan province.
    • 刘凝之 - Refers to Liu Ning, a historical figure.
    • 珠林九十一 - Refers to the 91st volume of the *Zhu Lin*.
    • 广记三百二十四 - Refers to the 324th volume of the *Guang Ji*.

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Shao of Liu Song, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms. [106]

    宋司马文宣，河内人也，颇信佛法。元嘉九年，丁母难，弟丧，月望，旦忽见其弟身形于灵座上，不异平日，回遑叹嗟，讽求饮食。文乃试与言曰：“汝平生时修行十善，若如经言，应得生天，若在人道，何故乃生此鬼中耶？”沈吟俯仰，默然无对。文即夕梦见其弟云：“生所修善，蒙报生天；旦灵床之鬼，是魔魅耳，非其身也；恐兄疑怪，故诣以白兄。”文宣明旦请僧转首楞严经，令人扑系之，鬼乃逃入床下，又走户外，形稍丑恶。举家骇惧，叱詈遣之，鬼云：“饿乞食耳！”积日乃去。顷之，母灵床头有一鬼肤体赤色，身甚长壮，文宣长息孝祖与言，往反答对周悉；初虽恐惧，末稍安习之，鬼亦转相附狎，居处出入，殆同家人。于时京师传相报告，往来观者门巷叠迹，时南林寺有僧与灵味寺僧含沙门与鬼言论，亦甚款曲。 [107]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [107]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 颇信佛法 - Means 'he believed in Buddhism quite a lot.'
    • 沈吟俯仰 - Means 'he hesitated and looked around.'
    • 默然无对 - Means 'he remained silent without answering.'
    • 生所修善，蒙报生天 - Means 'the good deeds done in life have been rewarded with rebirth in heaven.'
    • 旦灵床之鬼，是魔魅耳，非其身也 - Means 'the ghost on the altar in the morning is just a demon, not his brother.'
    • 恐兄疑怪，故诣以白兄 - Means 'he is afraid that you will doubt and be surprised, so he came to tell you.'
    • 文宣明旦请僧转首楞严经 - Means 'Wang Wenxuan invited a monk to recite the *Shou Leng Yan Jing* (Sutra of the Buddha's Protection).'
    • 令人扑系之 - Means 'he had someone tie it up.'
    • 鬼乃逃入床下，又走户外 - Means 'the ghost then fled under the bed and out the door.'
    • 形稍丑恶 - Means 'its appearance became slightly ugly and evil.'
    • 举家骇惧 - Means 'the whole family was terrified.'
    • 叱詈遣之 - Means 'they scolded and drove it away.'
    • 鬼云：“饿乞食耳！” - Means 'the ghost said, 'I'm hungry and asking for food!''
    • 积日乃去 - Means 'after several days, it left.'
    • 顷之，母灵床头有一鬼肤体赤色，身甚长壮 - Means 'soon, there was a red-skinned, tall, and strong ghost at the mother's altar.'
    • 文宣长息孝祖与言，往反答对周悉 - Means 'Wang Wenxuan's eldest son, Xiao Zu, spoke with it, and they conversed back and forth thoroughly.'
    • 初虽恐惧，末稍安习之 - Means 'at first, they were afraid, but later they became accustomed to it.'
    • 鬼亦转相附狎，居处出入，殆同家人 - Means 'the ghost also began to attach itself and become familiar, living and moving around as if it were part of the family.'
    • 于时京师传相报告，往来观者门巷叠迹 - Means 'at that time, the capital city spread the news, and people came and went, and the streets were crowded.'
    • 时南林寺有僧与灵味寺僧含沙门与鬼言论，亦甚款曲 - Means 'at the time, there were monks from Nanlin Temple and Lingwei Temple who spoke with the ghost, and they also spoke very kindly and politely.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋 - Refers to the Southern Song dynasty.
    • 司马文宣 - Refers to Wang Wenxuan, a historical figure.
    • 河内 - Refers to the Hebei region, an area in northern China.
    • 元嘉九年 - Refers to the ninth year of the reign of Emperor Wu of Liu Song.
    • 佛法 - Refers to Buddhism, a major religion in China.
    • 灵味寺 - Refers to a Buddhist temple.
    • 首楞严经 - Refers to the *Shou Leng Yan Jing* (Sutra of the Buddha's Protection), a significant Buddhist scripture.
    • 南林寺 - Refers to another Buddhist temple.
    • 京师 - Refers to the capital city, which was Jiankang (modern-day Nanjing) during the Southern Song dynasty.
    • 含沙门 - Refers to a monk, likely a high-ranking monk or abbot.
    • 鬼 - Refers to a ghost or spirit, a common theme in Chinese folklore and literature.

    Proper Nouns

    • 宋 - The Southern Song dynasty.
    • 司马文宣 - Wang Wenxuan, a historical figure.
    • 河内 - The Hebei region.
    • 元嘉九年 - The ninth year of the reign of Emperor Wu of Liu Song.
    • 灵味寺 - A Buddhist temple.
    • 首楞严经 - The *Shou Leng Yan Jing* (Sutra of the Buddha's Protection).
    • 南林寺 - Another Buddhist temple.
    • 京师 - The capital city, Jiankang (modern-day Nanjing).
    • 含沙门 - A monk.
    • 鬼 - A ghost or spirit.

    Wang Wenxuan, a person from the Song dynasty, was from Hebei and believed in Buddhism quite a lot. In the ninth year of the reign of Emperor Wu of Liu Song, his mother passed away, and his brother died. On the night of the full moon, in the morning, he suddenly saw his brother's body on the altar, which was no different from usual. He was lost and sighed, asking for food and drink. Wang Wenxuan then tried to speak to the ghost, saying, "If you did good deeds in life, according to the scriptures, you should have been reborn in heaven. If you were reborn in the human world, why did you become a ghost?" The ghost hesitated and looked around, remaining silent without answering. That night, Wang Wenxuan dreamed of his brother, who said, "The good deeds done in life have been rewarded with rebirth in heaven. The ghost on the altar in the morning is just a demon, not my body. I am afraid that you will doubt and be surprised, so I came to tell you." The next day, Wang Wenxuan invited a monk to recite the Shou Leng Yan Jing (Sutra of the Buddha's Protection) and had someone tie the ghost up. The ghost then fled under the bed and out the door, and its appearance became slightly ugly and evil. The whole family was terrified and scolded and drove it away. The ghost said, "I'm hungry and asking for food!" After several days, it left. Soon, there was a red-skinned, tall, and strong ghost at the mother's altar. Wang Wenxuan's eldest son, Xiao Zu, spoke with it, and they conversed back and forth thoroughly. At first, they were afraid, but later they became accustomed to it. The ghost also began to attach itself and become familiar, living and moving around as if it were part of the family. At that time, the capital city spread the news, and people came and went, and the streets were crowded. At the time, there were monks from Nanlin Temple and Lingwei Temple who spoke with the ghost, and they also spoke very kindly and politely. [107]

    鬼云：“昔世尝为尊贵，以犯众恶，受报未竟，果此鬼身。去寅年有四百部鬼，大行疾疠，所应钟灾者，不○道人耳；而犯横极众，多滥福善，故使我来监察之也。”僧以食与之，鬼曰：“我自有粮，不得进此食也。”含曰：“鬼多知我生何来，何因作道人？”答曰：“人中来出家，因缘本誓愿也。”问诸存亡生死所趣，略皆答对。具有灵验；条次繁多，故不曲载。含曰：“人鬼道殊，汝既不求食，何为久留？”鬼曰：“此间有一女子，应在收捕，而奉戒精勤，故难可得，比日稽留，用此故也。藉乱主人，有愧不少。”自此已后，不甚见形，后往视者，但闻语耳。时元嘉十年也。至三月二十八日，语文宣云：“暂来寄住，而汝倾家营福，见畏如此，那得久留。”孝祖云：“听汝寄住，何故据人先亡灵筵耶？”答曰：“汝家亡者，各有所属；此座空设，故权寄耳。”于是辞去。珠林六 [108]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [108]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 昔世 - Refers to a past life or previous existence, often used in the context of reincarnation or spiritual rebirth.
    • 尊貴 - Means 'noble' or 'dignified,' often referring to high social status or moral virtue.
    • 犯眾惡 - Means 'committed many evil deeds,' indicating a history of wrongdoing.
    • 受報未竟 - Means 'the retribution has not been completed,' suggesting that the consequences of one's actions are still ongoing.
    • 果此鬼身 - Means 'resulted in this ghostly form,' indicating the current state of the speaker.
    • 去寅年有四百部鬼 - Refers to a specific year (the year of the Tiger) when a large number of ghosts were active.
    • 大行疾疠 - Means 'spread great epidemics,' indicating widespread disease.
    • 所应钟灾者 - Means 'those who should suffer disaster,' referring to people who deserve punishment.
    • 不○道人耳 - This phrase is incomplete and unclear, but it likely means 'only the wicked or those who do not follow the path.'
    • 犯横极众 - Means 'committed many violent acts,' indicating a history of aggression.
    • 多滥福善 - Means 'often bestowed excessive blessings,' suggesting a tendency to give too much favor or reward.
    • 故使我来监察之也 - Means 'thus I came to oversee this,' indicating the speaker's current role.
    • 我自有粮 - Means 'I have my own food,' indicating self-sufficiency.
    • 不得进此食也 - Means 'cannot accept this food,' indicating a refusal to partake in the offered meal.
    • 鬼多知我生何来 - Means 'ghosts know where I came from,' indicating awareness of the speaker's origins.
    • 何因作道人 - Means 'why did I become a monk,' indicating a question about the speaker's current state.
    • 人中来出家 - Means 'came from among humans to become a monk,' indicating a transition from human life to monastic life.
    • 因缘本誓愿也 - Means 'due to the karmic connection and original vow,' indicating the reason for the transition.
    • 问诸存亡生死所趣 - Means 'ask about the fate of the living and the dead,' indicating a request for information about life and death.
    • 略皆答对 - Means 'answered in a general way,' indicating a summary of the responses.
    • 具有灵验 - Means 'had many miraculous occurrences,' indicating supernatural events.
    • 条次繁多 - Means 'numerous and detailed,' indicating the complexity of the information.
    • 故不曲载 - Means 'thus not recorded in detail,' indicating that the information is not fully documented.
    • 人鬼道殊 - Means 'humans and ghosts have different paths,' indicating the separation between the human and ghostly realms.
    • 汝既不求食 - Means 'since you do not seek food,' indicating a statement about the speaker's needs.
    • 何为久留 - Means 'why stay for a long time,' indicating a question about the duration of the stay.
    • 此间有一女子 - Means 'there is a woman here,' indicating a specific person.
    • 应在收捕 - Means 'should be captured,' indicating a duty or obligation.
    • 而奉戒精勤 - Means 'who follows the precepts diligently,' indicating strict adherence to religious rules.
    • 故难可得 - Means 'thus difficult to capture,' indicating a challenge.
    • 比日稽留 - Means 'recently delayed,' indicating a recent action.
    • 用此故也 - Means 'due to this reason,' indicating the cause of the delay.
    • 藉乱主人 - Means 'causing trouble for the host,' indicating disruption.
    • 有愧不少 - Means 'feel considerable shame,' indicating a sense of guilt.
    • 自此已后 - Means 'from this point onward,' indicating a change in time.
    • 不甚见形 - Means 'not often seen,' indicating infrequent appearances.
    • 后往视者 - Means 'those who come to visit later,' indicating future visitors.
    • 但闻语耳 - Means 'only hear words,' indicating a limited form of interaction.
    • 时元嘉十年也 - Means 'in the tenth year of the Yuanjia era,' indicating the historical context.
    • 至三月二十八日 - Means 'on March 28th,' indicating a specific date.
    • 语文宣云 - Means 'the ghost said,' indicating a statement made by the ghost.
    • 暂来寄住 - Means 'temporarily staying,' indicating a short-term residence.
    • 而汝倾家营福 - Means 'and you have spent your entire family's resources to create merit,' indicating a significant effort.
    • 见畏如此 - Means 'are so fearful,' indicating a strong reaction.
    • 那得久留 - Means 'how can I stay for a long time,' indicating a question about the duration of the stay.
    • 听汝寄住 - Means 'allow you to stay temporarily,' indicating permission.
    • 何故据人先亡灵筵耶 - Means 'why occupy the spirit table of the deceased,' indicating a question about the use of a specific place.
    • 汝家亡者 - Means 'those who have passed away in your family,' indicating deceased family members.
    • 各有所属 - Means 'each has their own destination,' indicating a belief in the afterlife.
    • 此座空设 - Means 'this seat is empty,' indicating an unused space.
    • 故权寄耳 - Means 'thus temporarily occupying it,' indicating a temporary arrangement.
    • 于是辞去 - Means 'thus departed,' indicating the end of the interaction.
    • 珠林六 - Refers to the sixth chapter of the *Zhu Lin* (Jewel Forest), a collection of Buddhist stories.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 元嘉十年 - The tenth year of the reign of Emperor Wu of Liu Song (433 AD), a significant period in Chinese history.
    • 鬼 - Refers to spirits or ghosts, a common theme in Chinese folklore and religious beliefs.
    • 道人 - Refers to a monk or religious practitioner, indicating a spiritual context.
    • 奉戒精勤 - Refers to strict adherence to religious precepts, a key aspect of monastic life.
    • 灵验 - Refers to supernatural occurrences or miracles, often associated with religious or spiritual practices.
    • 珠林六 - Refers to the *Zhu Lin* (Jewel Forest), a collection of Buddhist stories and teachings.

    The ghost said: "In a past life, I was once noble and dignified, but I committed many evil deeds, and the retribution has not been completed, resulting in this ghostly form. In the year of the Tiger, there were four hundred ghosts who spread great epidemics, causing disasters for those who deserved them, not the wicked or those who do not follow the path; I committed many violent acts, often bestowed excessive blessings, thus I came to oversee this." The monk offered food to the ghost, who said, "I have my own food and cannot accept this." The monk asked, "Do ghosts know where I came from and why I became a monk?" The ghost replied, "I came from among humans to become a monk, due to the karmic connection and original vow." The monk asked about the fate of the living and the dead, and the ghost answered in a general way. There were many miraculous occurrences; the details were numerous and detailed, thus not recorded in full. The monk said, "Humans and ghosts have different paths; since you do not seek food, why stay for a long time?" The ghost said, "There is a woman here who should be captured, but she follows the precepts diligently, thus difficult to capture. Recently, I have been delayed due to this reason. Causing trouble for the host, I feel considerable shame." From this point onward, the ghost was not often seen, and those who came to visit later only heard words. It was the tenth year of the Yuanjia era. On March 28th, the ghost said, "I am temporarily staying, and you have spent your entire family's resources to create merit, are so fearful, how can I stay for a long time?" The monk said, "Allow you to stay temporarily, why occupy the spirit table of the deceased?" The ghost replied, "Those who have passed away in your family, each has their own destination; this seat is empty, thus temporarily occupying it." Thus, the ghost departed. This is from the sixth chapter of the Zhu Lin. [108]

    宋沙门二字广记引作何昙远，庐江人也父万寿，御史中丞，远奉法精至，持菩萨戒，年十八，元嘉九年，丁父艰，哀毁致招疾，殆将灭性，号踊之外，便归心净土，庶祈感应。远时请僧，常有数人，师僧含亦在焉。远常向含悔忏宿业，恐有烦缘，终无感彻；僧含每奖厉，劝以莫怠。至十年二月十六日夜，转经竟，众僧已眠，四更中，忽自唱言歌诵，僧含惊而问之，远曰：“见佛身黄金色，形状大小，如今行像，金光周身，浮焰丈余，旛华翼从，充牣虚空，瑰妙丽极，事绝言称。”远时住西厢中，云：佛自西来，转身西向，当宁而立，呼其速去。昙远常日羸喘，示有气息，此夕壮厉，悦乐动容，便起净手。含布香手中，并取园华，遥以散佛。母谓远曰：“汝今若去，不念吾耶？”远无所言，俄而顿卧；家既宿信，闻此灵异，既皆欣肃，不甚悲惧。远至五更，忽然而终，中宅芬馨，数日乃歇。珠林十五广记一百十四 [109]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [109]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 沙门 - A term for Buddhist monks, derived from the Sanskrit *śramaṇa*.
    • 庐江人也 - Indicates that the person is from Lujiang, a region in present-day Anhui Province.
    • 父万寿，御史中丞 - Describes the father as Wan Shou, who held the title of *Yushi Zhongcheng* (a high-ranking official).
    • 奉法精至 - Means 'to observe the Dharma with great dedication.'
    • 持菩萨戒 - Refers to upholding the Bodhisattva precepts.
    • 丁父艰 - A euphemism for experiencing the death of one's father, often used in classical texts.
    • 哀毁致招疾 - Describes the son's deep grief leading to illness.
    • 殆将灭性 - Means 'nearly losing one's life' due to grief.
    • 号踊之外 - Refers to crying and wailing, indicating intense mourning.
    • 归心净土 - Means 'to dedicate oneself to the Pure Land,' a concept in Pure Land Buddhism.
    • 庶祈感应 - Means 'to hope for a response or manifestation.'
    • 请僧常有数人 - Indicates that the monk regularly invited several monks.
    • 师僧含亦在焉 - Refers to the monk *Seng Han* being among the invited monks.
    • 远常向含悔忏宿业 - Means that the monk frequently confessed his past sins to *Seng Han*.
    • 恐有烦缘 - Means 'fearing that there might be obstacles.'
    • 终无感彻 - Means 'eventually there was no response.'
    • 四更中 - Refers to the fourth watch of the night, approximately midnight to 2 AM.
    • 自唱言歌诵 - Means 'spontaneously singing and chanting.'
    • 佛身黄金色 - Describes the Buddha's body as golden.
    • 形状大小，如今行像 - Means 'the shape and size, like the image of a Buddha statue.'
    • 金光周身 - Describes the Buddha's body being surrounded by golden light.
    • 浮焰丈余 - Means 'floating flames several feet high.'
    • 幡华翼从 - Refers to banners and flowers surrounding the Buddha.
    • 充牣虚空 - Means 'filling the void' or 'filling the space.'
    • 瑰妙丽极 - Describes something as extremely beautiful and wondrous.
    • 事绝言称 - Means 'beyond description.'
    • 西厢中 - Refers to the western hall or chamber.
    • 佛自西来 - Means 'the Buddha came from the west.'
    • 转身西向 - Means 'turned around to face the west.'
    • 当宁而立 - Means 'stood facing the central hall.'
    • 呼其速去 - Means 'called for him to leave quickly.'
    • 昙远常日羸喘 - Describes *Tan Yuan* as usually weak and breathless.
    • 示有气息 - Means 'showing signs of life.'
    • 此夕壮厉 - Means 'that night, he was vigorous and spirited.'
    • 悦乐动容 - Means 'his face showed joy and pleasure.'
    • 便起净手 - Means 'he immediately washed his hands.'
    • 含布香手中 - Means 'Seng Han placed incense in his hand.'
    • 并取园华 - Means 'and took flowers from the garden.'
    • 遥以散佛 - Means 'and scattered them towards the Buddha.'
    • 母谓远曰 - Means 'the mother said to *Tan Yuan*.'
    • 汝今若去，不念吾耶 - Means 'If you are leaving now, do you not miss me?'
    • 俄而顿卧 - Means 'soon after, he lay down suddenly.'
    • 家既宿信 - Means 'the family had long been believers.'
    • 闻此灵异 - Means 'heard of this miraculous event.'
    • 既皆欣肃 - Means 'they were all joyful and respectful.'
    • 不甚悲惧 - Means 'not overly sad or fearful.'
    • 远至五更 - Refers to the fifth watch of the night, approximately 2 AM to dawn.
    • 忽然而终 - Means 'he suddenly passed away.'
    • 中宅芬馨 - Means 'the house was filled with a fragrant aroma.'
    • 数日乃歇 - Means 'which lasted for several days.'
    • 珠林十五 - Refers to *Zhu Lin* (a collection of Buddhist stories), volume 15.
    • 广记一百十四 - Refers to *Guang Ji* (a collection of Buddhist stories), volume 114.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋沙门 - Refers to a monk from the Song Dynasty.
    • 庐江 - A historical region in present-day Anhui Province.
    • 御史中丞 - A high-ranking official in the imperial court, responsible for oversight and inspection.
    • 菩萨戒 - The Bodhisattva precepts, a set of ethical guidelines for Buddhists.
    • 丁父艰 - A traditional term for the death of a father, often accompanied by a period of mourning.
    • 净土 - The Pure Land, a concept in Pure Land Buddhism where one can be reborn after death.
    • 感应 - A response or manifestation, often in the context of spiritual or religious experiences.
    • 沙门 - A term for Buddhist monks, derived from the Sanskrit *śramaṇa*.
    • 四更中 - The fourth watch of the night, a traditional Chinese time division.
    • 行像 - A statue or image of the Buddha.
    • 幡华 - Banners and flowers, often used in religious ceremonies.
    • 瑰妙丽极 - Describes something as extremely beautiful and wondrous.
    • 事绝言称 - Beyond description, indicating something extraordinary.
    • 西厢 - The western hall or chamber, often used in classical Chinese literature to describe a specific location.
    • 当宁 - The central hall, a term used in traditional Chinese architecture.
    • 昙远 - The name of the monk, *Tan Yuan*.
    • 僧含 - The name of another monk, *Seng Han*.
    • 中宅芬馨 - The house filled with a fragrant aroma, often associated with spiritual or divine presence.
    • 珠林 - A collection of Buddhist stories, *Zhu Lin*.
    • 广记 - A collection of Buddhist stories, *Guang Ji*.

    Proper Nouns

    • 宋沙门 - Refers to a monk from the Song Dynasty.
    • 庐江 - A historical region in present-day Anhui Province.
    • 御史中丞 - A high-ranking official in the imperial court, responsible for oversight and inspection.
    • 菩萨戒 - The Bodhisattva precepts, a set of ethical guidelines for Buddhists.
    • 丁父艰 - A traditional term for the death of a father, often accompanied by a period of mourning.
    • 净土 - The Pure Land, a concept in Pure Land Buddhism where one can be reborn after death.
    • 感应 - A response or manifestation, often in the context of spiritual or religious experiences.
    • 沙门 - A term for Buddhist monks, derived from the Sanskrit *śramaṇa*.
    • 四更中 - The fourth watch of the night, a traditional Chinese time division.
    • 行像 - A statue or image of the Buddha.
    • 幡华 - Banners and flowers, often used in religious ceremonies.
    • 瑰妙丽极 - Describes something as extremely beautiful and wondrous.
    • 事绝言称 - Beyond description, indicating something extraordinary.
    • 西厢 - The western hall or chamber, often used in classical Chinese literature to describe a specific location.
    • 当宁 - The central hall, a term used in traditional Chinese architecture.
    • 昙远 - The name of the monk, *Tan Yuan*.
    • 僧含 - The name of another monk, *Seng Han*.
    • 中宅芬馨 - The house filled with a fragrant aroma, often associated with spiritual or divine presence.
    • 珠林 - A collection of Buddhist stories, *Zhu Lin*.
    • 广记 - A collection of Buddhist stories, *Guang Ji*.

    During the Song Dynasty, a monk named Tan Yuan, whose name is also recorded as He Tan Yuan in the Guang Ji (Volume 114), was from Lujiang. His father, Wan Shou, held the title of Yushi Zhongcheng (a high-ranking official). Tan Yuan observed the Dharma with great dedication and upheld the Bodhisattva precepts. At the age of eighteen, in the ninth year of the Yuanjia era, he experienced the death of his father, which led to deep grief and illness, nearly causing him to lose his life. Beyond crying and wailing, he dedicated himself to the Pure Land, hoping for a response or manifestation. Tan Yuan regularly invited several monks, including Seng Han, to his home. He frequently confessed his past sins to Seng Han, fearing that there might be obstacles, but eventually there was no response. Seng Han often encouraged him, urging him not to become complacent.

    On the sixteenth day of the second month of the tenth year, during the fourth watch of the night, after completing the recitation of scriptures, the other monks had already fallen asleep. Suddenly, Tan Yuan began to sing and chant on his own. Seng Han was startled and asked him what had happened. Tan Yuan replied, "I saw the Buddha's body, golden in color, with a shape and size like the image of a Buddha statue. His body was surrounded by golden light, with floating flames several feet high. Banners and flowers surrounded him, filling the void with their beauty and wonder, beyond description." At the time, Tan Yuan was in the western hall, where he said the Buddha came from the west, turned around to face the west, stood facing the central hall, and called for him to leave quickly.

    Tan Yuan, who was usually weak and breathless, showed signs of life that night, appearing vigorous and joyful. His face lit up with pleasure, and he immediately washed his hands. Seng Han placed incense in his hand and took flowers from the garden, scattering them towards the Buddha. His mother said to Tan Yuan, "If you are leaving now, do you not miss me?" Tan Yuan said nothing and soon after lay down suddenly. The family, who had long been believers, heard of this miraculous event and were all joyful and respectful, not overly sad or fearful. Tan Yuan passed away at the fifth watch of the night, and the house was filled with a fragrant aroma that lasted for several days. This event is recorded in Zhu Lin (Volume 15) and Guang Ji (Volume 114). [109]

    宋尼释智通，京师简静尼也，年貌姝少，信道不笃。元嘉九年，师死罢道嫁为魏郡梁群甫妻；生一男，年大七岁，家甚贫，无以为衣。通为尼时，有数卷素无量寿法华等经，悉练捣之，以衣其儿。居一年，而得病，恍忽惊悸，竟体剥烂，状若火疮，有细白虫，日去升余，燥痛烦毒，昼夜号叫。常闻空中语云：“坏经为衣，得此剧报。”旬余而死。珠林十八广记一百十六 [110]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [110]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 信道不笃 - Not firmly believing in the path or not strongly committed to the faith.
    • 常闻空中语云 - Often heard voices in the air saying, indicating auditory hallucinations common in Buddhist literature and folklore.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋尼释智通 - A specific historical figure, a nun from the Song dynasty.
    • 京师 - The capital city of the Song dynasty, which was Kaifeng.
    • 元嘉九年 - The ninth year of the reign of Emperor Wu of Liu Song (432 AD).
    • 魏郡 - A prefecture during the Southern and Northern Dynasties period, located in present-day Henan province.
    • 无量寿法华等经 - Refers to important Buddhist scriptures, specifically the *Amitabha Sutra* and *Lotus Sutra*.
    • 珠林十八广记一百十六 - Likely refers to a collection of stories or records, possibly part of a larger anthology.

    Proper Nouns

    • 宋尼释智通 - Proper name of the nun.
    • 京师 - Proper name of the capital city.
    • 元嘉九年 - Proper date.
    • 魏郡 - Proper name of the prefecture.
    • 梁群甫 - Proper name of the husband.
    • 无量寿法华等经 - Proper names of the Buddhist scriptures.
    • 珠林十八广记一百十六 - Proper reference to the source of the text.

    Shi Zhitong, a nun from the Song dynasty, was a simple and tranquil nun from the capital city. She was young and beautiful, but not firmly committed to her faith. In the ninth year of the reign of Emperor Wu of Liu Song (432 AD), after the death of her teacher, she left the monastic life and married a man named Liang Qunfu from Wei Prefecture.
