They had a son who was seven years old. The family was very poor and lacked clothing. Shi Zhitong had several scrolls of the Amitabha Sutra and Lotus Sutra. She washed and pounded these scrolls to make clothes for her son. After a year, the child fell ill, experiencing confusion, fear, and a condition where his body peeled off like a burn, with small white worms emerging. He suffered from severe pain and fever, crying day and night. He often heard voices in the air saying, "Destroying the sutras to make clothes, you receive this severe retribution." The child died after ten days.

    Source: 珠林十八广记一百十六 [110]

    宋仑氏二女，东官曾城人也，是时祖姊妹。元嘉九年。一引作元年姊年十岁，妹年九岁，里越愚蒙，未知经法。忽以二月八日并失所在，三日而归，粗说见佛。九月十五日又失，一旬还，作外国语，诵经及梵书见西域沙门，便相开解。明年正月十五日，忽复失之，田间作人云：见其从风径飘上天。父母号惧，祀神求福。一引作父母哀哭求祷神鬼既而经月乃返，剃头为尼，被服法衣，持发而归。自说；见佛及比丘尼，曰：“汝宿世因缘，应为我弟子。”举手摩头，发因堕落，与其法名；大曰法缘小曰法彩。临遣还，曰：“可作精舍，当与汝经法也。”女既归家，即毁除鬼座，缮立精庐，夜齐诵经，夕中每有五色光明，流泛峰岭，若灯烛。二女自此后容止华雅，音制诠正，上京风调，不能过也。刺史韦朗孔默等三字一引作就里并迎供养，闻其谈说，甚敬异焉。于是溪里皆知奉法。珠林五又二十二 [111]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [111]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 忽以二月八日并失所在 - Suddenly on the eighth day of the second month, they both disappeared.
    • 三日而归 - After three days, they returned.
    • 粗说见佛 - They roughly described seeing a Buddha.
    • 九月十五日又失 - On the fifteenth day of the ninth month, they disappeared again.
    • 一旬还 - After ten days, they returned.
    • 作外国语 - Spoke a foreign language.
    • 诵经及梵书 - Recited scriptures and Sanskrit texts.
    • 见西域沙门 - Met monks from the Western Regions.
    • 便相开解 - They immediately understood each other.
    • 明年正月十五日 - On the fifteenth day of the first month of the following year.
    • 忽复失之 - Suddenly disappeared again.
    • 田间作人云 - Farmers in the fields said.
    • 见其从风径飘上天 - They saw them being carried away by the wind and ascending into the sky.
    • 父母号惧 - Their parents cried out in fear.
    • 祀神求福 - Worshipped gods to seek blessings.
    • 经月乃返 - After a month, they returned.
    • 剃头为尼 - Shaved their heads to become nuns.
    • 被服法衣 - Dressed in monastic robes.
    • 持发而归 - Carried their hair back.
    • 自说 - They said for themselves.
    • 见佛及比丘尼 - Saw a Buddha and nuns.
    • 汝宿世因缘，应为我弟子 - Your past-life karma has led you to be my disciples.
    • 举手摩头 - Raised their hands and touched their heads.
    • 发因堕落 - Their hair fell out.
    • 与其法名 - Gave them Buddhist names.
    • 大曰法缘小曰法彩 - The elder was named Fa Yuan, and the younger was named Fa Cai.
    • 临遣还 - When they were about to be sent back.
    • 可作精舍 - You can build a temple.
    • 当与汝经法也 - I will give you the scriptures and teachings.
    • 既归家 - After returning home.
    • 即毁除鬼座 - Immediately destroyed the altars for spirits.
    • 缮立精庐 - Built a temple.
    • 夜齐诵经 - At night, they chanted the scriptures together.
    • 夕中每有五色光明 - At dusk, there was often a five-colored light.
    • 流泛峰岭 - Flowing over the peaks and ridges.
    • 若灯烛 - Like lamps and candles.
    • 自此后 - From then on.
    • 容止华雅 - Their demeanor became elegant and refined.
    • 音制诠正 - Their speech became well-structured and correct.
    • 上京风调 - The style of the capital.
    • 不能过也 - Could not surpass.
    • 刺史韦朗孔默等 - Governor Wei Lang, Kong Mo, and others.
    • 并迎供养 - All welcomed and supported them.
    • 闻其谈说 - Hearing their discussions.
    • 甚敬异焉 - They greatly respected and admired them.
    • 于是溪里皆知奉法 - Thus, everyone in the village came to respect the law.
    • 珠林五又二十二 - Julu 5, also 22.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋仑氏 - A noble family in ancient China, indicating their social status.
    • 东官曾城 - A specific location in ancient China, providing geographical context.
    • 元嘉九年 - 432 AD, during the reign of Emperor Wen of Liu Song, providing historical context.
    • 佛 - Refers to the Buddha, the central figure in Buddhism.
    • 比丘尼 - Refers to female Buddhist monks, indicating their spiritual role.
    • 西域沙门 - Refers to Buddhist monks from the Western Regions, indicating their connection to Central Asian Buddhism.
    • 精舍 - Refers to a temple or monastery, indicating their religious practice.
    • 法衣 - Refers to monastic robes, indicating their monastic status.
    • 法名 - Refers to Buddhist names, indicating their formal entry into the monastic community.
    • 宿世因缘 - Refers to past-life karma, indicating a spiritual belief.
    • 经法 - Refers to Buddhist scriptures and teachings, indicating their religious focus.
    • 刺史 - Refers to a local governor, indicating their social and political context.

    Proper Nouns

    • 宋仑氏 - A noble family in ancient China, indicating their social status.
    • 东官曾城 - A specific location in ancient China, providing geographical context.
    • 元嘉九年 - 432 AD, during the reign of Emperor Wen of Liu Song, providing historical context.
    • 佛 - Refers to the Buddha, the central figure in Buddhism.
    • 比丘尼 - Refers to female Buddhist monks, indicating their spiritual role.
    • 西域沙门 - Refers to Buddhist monks from the Western Regions, indicating their connection to Central Asian Buddhism.
    • 精舍 - Refers to a temple or monastery, indicating their religious practice.
    • 法衣 - Refers to monastic robes, indicating their monastic status.
    • 法名 - Refers to Buddhist names, indicating their formal entry into the monastic community.
    • 宿世因缘 - Refers to past-life karma, indicating a spiritual belief.
    • 经法 - Refers to Buddhist scriptures and teachings, indicating their religious focus.
    • 刺史 - Refers to a local governor, indicating their social and political context.

    Two daughters of the Song Lun family, residents of Dongguan Zengcheng, were cousins at the time. In the ninth year of the Yanchang era (432 AD), the elder sister was ten years old, and the younger sister was nine. Living in a remote village, they were ignorant of the scriptures and the Dharma. Suddenly, on the eighth day of the second month, both disappeared, and after three days, they returned, roughly describing having seen a Buddha. On the fifteenth day of the ninth month, they disappeared again, returning after ten days, speaking a foreign language, reciting scriptures and Sanskrit texts, and meeting monks from the Western Regions, immediately understanding each other. In the following year, on the fifteenth day of the first month, they suddenly disappeared again. Farmers in the fields reported seeing them being carried away by the wind and ascending into the sky. Their parents cried out in fear and worshipped gods to seek blessings. After a month, they returned, shaved their heads to become nuns, dressed in monastic robes, and carried their hair back. They said for themselves that they had seen a Buddha and nuns, and the Buddha said, "Your past-life karma has led you to be my disciples." He raised his hands and touched their heads, and their hair fell out. He gave them Buddhist names: the elder was named Fa Yuan, and the younger was named Fa Cai. When they were about to be sent back, he said, "You can build a temple, and I will give you the scriptures and teachings." After returning home, they immediately destroyed the altars for spirits, built a temple, and at night, they chanted the scriptures together. At dusk, there was often a five-colored light, flowing over the peaks and ridges, like lamps and candles. From then on, their demeanor became elegant and refined, their speech well-structured and correct, surpassing the style of the capital. Governor Wei Lang, Kong Mo, and others welcomed and supported them, hearing their discussions, greatly respecting and admiring them. Thus, everyone in the village came to respect the law. Julu 5, also 22. [111]

    宋玉球，字叔达，太原人也，为涪陵太守，以元嘉九年于郡失守，系在刑狱，着一重锁，钉鍱坚固。球先精进，既在囹圄，用心尤至。狱中百余人，并多饥饿，球每食，皆分施之。日自持斋至心念观世音。夜梦升高座，见一沙门，以一卷经与之，题云“光明安行品并诸菩萨名”球得而披读，忘第一菩萨名，第二观世音，第三大势至，又见一车轮沙门曰：“此五道轮也。”既觉，锁皆断脱球心知神力弥增专到，因自钉治其锁，经三日而被原宥。珠林二十三 [112]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [112]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 精進 - A Buddhist term meaning 'diligent practice' or 'zealous effort,' often associated with spiritual cultivation.
    • 囹圄 - Refers to a prison or jail, used here to describe Cao Yuqiu's confinement.
    • 念觀世音 - Means 'to recite or meditate on the Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara,' a central figure in Mahayana Buddhism.
    • 高座 - Refers to a raised seat or platform, often associated with a teacher or speaker.
    • 鍱 - A type of lock or chain, often used in a metaphorical sense to describe confinement or restriction.
    • 原宥 - Means 'to pardon' or 'to forgive,' often used in a legal or moral context.
    • 神力 - Refers to supernatural power or divine assistance, often associated with Buddhist practice.
    • 自钉治其锁 - Means 'to nail and fix the lock' himself, indicating self-reliance and determination.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋玉球 - The name of the subject, Cao Yuqiu, a historical figure from the Southern and Northern Dynasties period.
    • 字叔达 - His courtesy name, 'Shuda,' which is a common practice in Chinese culture.
    • 太原人也 - Indicates that Cao Yuqiu was from Taiyuan, a city in Shanxi Province.
    • 涪陵太守 - Refers to his official title as the governor of Fuling, a region in present-day Chongqing.
    • 元嘉九年 - Refers to the ninth year of the Yuanjia era, which was 432 AD, during the Liu Song dynasty.
    • 失守 - Means 'to lose control' or 'to fail to hold,' referring to his loss of the city.
    • 系在刑狱 - Means 'imprisoned in a criminal court,' indicating his legal status.
    • 光明安行品 - Refers to a Buddhist scripture, likely a sutra or chapter of a sutra, though the exact title is not widely known.
    • 珠林二十三 - Refers to the 23rd chapter of the *Jewel Forest* (珠林), a collection of Buddhist stories and teachings.

    Proper Nouns

    • 宋玉球 - The subject of the text, a historical figure.
    • 叔达 - His courtesy name.
    • 太原 - A city in Shanxi Province, his place of origin.
    • 涪陵 - A region in present-day Chongqing, where he served as governor.
    • 元嘉九年 - The year 432 AD, during the Liu Song dynasty.
    • 观世音 - The Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara.
    • 光明安行品 - A Buddhist scripture, likely a sutra or chapter of a sutra.
    • 珠林二十三 - The 23rd chapter of the *Jewel Forest* (珠林).

    Cao Yuqiu, courtesy name Shuda, was from Taiyuan. He served as the governor of Fuling and lost control of the city in the ninth year of the Yuanjia era (432 AD). As a result, he was imprisoned in a criminal court, where he was chained with a heavy lock that was firmly secured. Cao Yuqiu had always been diligent in his practice, and upon being imprisoned, he focused his efforts even more intently. There were over a hundred prisoners in the jail, many of whom were suffering from hunger. Whenever Cao Yuqiu ate, he would share his food with them. Each day, he would fast and meditate on the Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara with great sincerity. One night, he dreamed of ascending to a high seat and encountering a monk who handed him a scroll of scripture, which was inscribed with the title "The Chapter of Enlightened Conduct and the Names of the Bodhisattvas." Cao Yuqiu read through it, but he could not remember the name of the first Bodhisattva, only recognizing the second as Avalokiteshvara and the third as Mahasthamaprapta. In the dream, he also saw another monk holding a wheel, who said, "This is the wheel of the Five Paths." Upon waking, he found that the lock on his chains had broken. Cao Yuqiu realized that his spiritual practice had increased his supernatural power, and he felt a deep sense of determination. He then proceeded to fix the lock himself, and after three days, he was pardoned. This account is recorded in the 23rd chapter of the Jewel Forest. [112]

    宋刘龄者，不知何许人也，居晋陵东路城屯，颇奉法，于宅中立精舍一闲，时设斋集。元嘉九年三月二十七日，父暴病亡。巫祝并云：家当更有三人丧亡。邻家有道士祭酒，姓魏名叵，常为章符，诳化屯里，语龄曰：“君家衰祸未已，由奉胡神故也。若事大道，必蒙福祐，不改意者，将来灭门。”龄遂揭延祭酒，罢不奉法。叵云：“宜焚去经像，灾乃当除耳。”遂闭精舍户，放火焚烧，炎炽移日，而所烧者，唯屋而已，经像旛○，俨然如故，像于中夜，又放火赫然。时诸祭酒有二十许人，亦有惧畏灵验，密委去者。叵等师徒，犹盛意不止；被发偊步，执持刀索，云斥佛还胡国，不得留中夏，为民害也。龄于其夕，如有人殴打之者，顿仆于地，家人扶起，示余气息，遂委挛躄不能行动，道士魏叵，其时体内发疽，日出二升，不过一月，受苦便死。自外同伴，并皆着癞。其邻人东安太守水丘和传于东阳无疑，时亦多有见者。珠林六十二 [113]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [113]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 不知何许人也 - A standard phrase meaning 'does not know where he comes from,' indicating an unknown origin.
    • 君家衰祸未已 - Means 'Your family's misfortune has not ended.'
    • 颇奉法 - Means 'adheres somewhat to the law' or 'follows the law to some extent.'
    • 于宅中立精舍一闲 - Means 'built a small temple in his home.'
    • 时设斋集 - Means 'held periodic vegetarian feasts and gatherings.'
    • 巫祝并云 - Means 'the shamans and diviners all said.'
    • 家当更有三人丧亡 - Means 'your family will have three more deaths.'
    • 邻家有道士祭酒，姓魏名叵 - Refers to a Taoist priest named Wei Po from a neighboring house.
    • 常为章符，诳化屯里 - Means 'often uses talismans and deceives the people in the garrison.'
    • 语龄曰 - Means 'told Liu Ling.'
    • 君家衰祸未已，由奉胡神故也 - Means 'Your family's misfortune has not ended because you worship foreign gods.'
    • 若事大道，必蒙福祐 - Means 'If you serve the great Tao, you will surely receive blessings.'
    • 不改意者，将来灭门 - Means 'If you do not change your mind, your entire family will be destroyed.'
    • 遂揭延祭酒，罢不奉法 - Means 'Liu Ling then invited the Taoist priest and stopped following the law.'
    • 宜焚去经像，灾乃当除耳 - Means 'You should burn the scriptures and statues, and the disaster will be eliminated.'
    • 遂闭精舍户，放火焚烧，炎炽移日，而所烧者，唯屋而已，经像幡○，俨然如故 - Means 'He then closed the temple doors and set fire to them. The fire burned for a whole day. What was burned was only the buildings. The scriptures, statues, and banners remained undamaged.'
    • 像于中夜，又放火赫然 - Means 'In the middle of the night, the statue itself seemed to set fire again.'
    • 时诸祭酒有二十许人 - Means 'At that time, there were about twenty Taoist priests.'
    • 亦有惧畏灵验，密委去者 - Means 'Some were also afraid of the miraculous events and secretly left.'
    • 叵等师徒，犹盛意不止 - Means 'Wei Po and his disciples still persisted in their efforts.'
    • 被发偊步，执持刀索 - Means 'They went around with disheveled hair and weapons.'
    • 云斥佛还胡国，不得留中夏，为民害也 - Means 'They claimed to drive the Buddha back to the foreign land, not allowing him to stay in China, where he would harm the people.'
    • 龄于其夕，如有人殴打之者，顿仆于地，家人扶起，示余气息，遂委挛躄不能行动 - Means 'That night, it seemed as if someone was beating him. He fell to the ground, and his family helped him up, showing that he was still alive. He became paralyzed and could no longer move.'
    • 道士魏叵，其时体内发疽，日出二升，不过一月，受苦便死 - Means 'The Taoist priest Wei Po developed a boil inside his body. It produced two liters of pus daily, and he died within a month.'
    • 自外同伴，并皆着癞 - Means 'His fellow practitioners also contracted leprosy.'
    • 其邻人东安太守水丘和传于东阳无疑，时亦多有见者 - Means 'His neighbor, the governor of Dong'an, Shui Qiuhe, passed this story on to Dongyang Wuyi. Many people also saw these events.'
    • 珠林六十二 - Refers to the source of the story, 'Jade Forest, Volume 62.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋刘龄 - Refers to Liu Ling, a person from the Song dynasty.
    • 晋陵东路城屯 - A specific location in the Song dynasty.
    • 精舍 - A small temple or shrine.
    • 斋集 - Vegetarian feasts and gatherings, often held for religious or spiritual purposes.
    • 巫祝 - Shamans or diviners who perform rituals and provide guidance.
    • 胡神 - Foreign gods, often referring to non-Chinese deities.
    • 大道 - The great Tao, referring to the Daoist concept of the ultimate reality or the way of nature.
    • 章符 - Talismans or magical symbols used in Taoist rituals.
    • 经像 - Scriptures and statues, often associated with Buddhism.
    • 幡○ - Banners, often used in religious ceremonies.
    • 灭门 - The destruction of an entire family, a severe punishment.
    • 中夏 - China, often used to distinguish Chinese culture from foreign cultures.
    • 珠林六十二 - 'Jade Forest, Volume 62,' a historical or literary work containing this story.

    Proper Nouns

    • 宋刘龄 - Liu Ling, the main character.
    • 晋陵东路城屯 - Jinling Eastern Road City Garrison, the location.
    • 精舍 - Small temple.
    • 斋集 - Vegetarian feasts and gatherings.
    • 巫祝 - Shamans and diviners.
    • 胡神 - Foreign gods.
    • 大道 - The great Tao.
    • 章符 - Talismans.
    • 经像 - Scriptures and statues.
    • 幡○ - Banners.
    • 灭门 - Destruction of an entire family.
    • 中夏 - China.
    • 珠林六十二 - Jade Forest, Volume 62.

    A Man Named Liu Ling from the Song Dynasty

    Liu Ling, a man from the Song Dynasty, was of unknown origin and resided in the Jinling Eastern Road City Garrison. He followed the law to some extent and built a small temple in his home, where he held periodic vegetarian feasts and gatherings. On the 27th day of the third month in the ninth year of the Yuanjia era, his father died suddenly. The shamans and diviners all said that his family would have three more deaths. A Taoist priest named Wei Po from a neighboring house, who often used talismans and deceived the people in the garrison, told Liu Ling, "Your family's misfortune has not ended because you worship foreign gods. If you serve the great Tao, you will surely receive blessings. If you do not change your mind, your entire family will be destroyed." Liu Ling then invited the Taoist priest and stopped following the law. Wei Po said, "You should burn the scriptures and statues, and the disaster will be eliminated." Liu Ling then closed the temple doors and set fire to them. The fire burned for a whole day, but what was burned was only the buildings. The scriptures, statues, and banners remained undamaged. In the middle of the night, the statue itself seemed to set fire again.

    At that time, there were about twenty Taoist priests. Some were also afraid of the miraculous events and secretly left. Wei Po and his disciples still persisted in their efforts. They went around with disheveled hair and weapons, claiming to drive the Buddha back to the foreign land, not allowing him to stay in China, where he would harm the people. That night, it seemed as if someone was beating Liu Ling. He fell to the ground, and his family helped him up, showing that he was still alive. He became paralyzed and could no longer move. The Taoist priest Wei Po developed a boil inside his body, producing two liters of pus daily, and he died within a month. His fellow practitioners also contracted leprosy. His neighbor, the governor of Dong'an, Shui Qiuhe, passed this story on to Dongyang Wuyi. Many people also saw these events. This story is recorded in "Jade Forest, Volume 62." [113]

    宋马虔伯，巴西阆中人也，少信佛法，尝作宣汉县宰。以元嘉十二年七月夜，于县得梦：见天际有三人，长二丈余，姿容严丽，临云下观，诸天妓乐盈仞，空中告曰：“汝厄在荆楚，戊寅之年，八月四日，若处山泽，其祸克消，人中斋戒亦可获免，若过此期，当悟道也。”时俯见相识杨暹等八人，并着锁械，又见道士胡辽，半身土中。天中天际神人皆记八人命尽年月，唯语辽曰：“若能修立功德，犹可延长也。”暹等皆如期终亡，辽益惧奉法，山居勤励弥至。 [114]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [114]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 信佛法 - Believing in or practicing Buddhism.
    • 宣漢縣宰 - Magistrate or county official of Xuanhan County.
    • 元嘉十二年 - The year 435 AD, during the reign of Emperor Wen of Liu Song.
    • 天際有三人 - Three people at the edge of the sky, used to describe a vision of three figures in the sky.
    • 姿容嚴麗 - Dignified and beautiful.
    • 臨云下觀 - Descending to observe from the clouds, indicating the figures' descent.
    • 諸天妓樂盈仞 - A multitude of heavenly musicians and dancers filling the space.
    • 空中告曰 - Spoke from the air, indicating a divine or supernatural communication.
    • 汝厄在荆楚 - Your calamity is in Jingchu, referring to a region in central China.
    • 戊寅之年，八月四日 - The specific date of 435 AD, the year of the Earth Tiger.
    • 若处山泽，其祸克消 - If you stay in the mountains and lakes, the calamity will be eliminated.
    • 人中斋戒亦可获免 - If you observe fasting and purification, you can also be saved.
    • 若过此期，当悟道也 - If you miss this period, you will attain enlightenment.
    • 俯见相识杨暹等八人 - He saw eight familiar people, including Yang Xian, looking down.
    • 并着锁械 - All were bound with shackles.
    • 又见道士胡辽 - He also saw the Taoist priest Hu Liao.
    • 半身土中 - Half of his body was in the ground.
    • 天中天际神人皆记八人命尽年月 - The gods in the sky and at the edge of the sky all recorded the years and months when the eight people would die.
    • 唯语辽曰 - Only said to Hu Liao.
    • 若能修立功德，犹可延长也 - If you can perform good deeds, you can extend your life.
    • 暹等皆如期终亡 - Yang Xian and the others all died as predicted.
    • 辽益惧奉法 - Hu Liao became even more fearful and devoted to the law.
    • 山居勤励弥至 - He lived in the mountains and worked diligently.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋 - The Liu Song Dynasty (420-479 AD).
    • 巴西阆中 - Langzhong, a county in modern-day Sichuan Province.
    • 元嘉十二年 - The 12th year of Emperor Wen of Liu Song's reign, 435 AD.
    • 荆楚 - The region of Jingchu, which includes parts of modern-day Hubei and Hunan Provinces.
    • 戊寅之年 - The year of the Earth Tiger in the Chinese zodiac, 435 AD.
    • 山泽 - Mountains and lakes, often associated with natural and spiritual retreats.
    • 斋戒 - Fasting and purification rituals.
    • 悟道 - Attaining enlightenment or spiritual awakening.
    • 道士 - A Taoist priest or practitioner.
    • 修立功德 - Performing good deeds to accumulate merit.

    Proper Nouns

    • 宋 - The Liu Song Dynasty.
    • 马虔伯 - Ma Qianbo, the main character.
    • 巴西阆中 - Langzhong, a county in Sichuan Province.
    • 元嘉十二年 - 435 AD.
    • 荆楚 - The region of Jingchu.
    • 戊寅之年 - 435 AD.
    • 杨暹 - Yang Xian, one of the eight familiar people.
    • 胡辽 - Hu Liao, the Taoist priest.

    Ma Qianbo, from Langzhong, Bashu, was a devout follower of Buddhism. He once served as the magistrate of Xuanhan County. In the twelfth year of the Yuanjia era (435 AD), during the night of July, he had a dream: he saw three figures at the edge of the sky, each over two zhang tall, dignified and beautiful, descending from the clouds to observe. There were many heavenly musicians and dancers filling the space. From the air, they spoke to him: "Your calamity is in Jingchu. On the day of the Earth Tiger, August 4th, if you stay in the mountains and lakes, the calamity will be eliminated. If you observe fasting and purification, you can also be saved. If you miss this period, you will attain enlightenment." He saw eight familiar people, including Yang Xian, all bound with shackles. He also saw the Taoist priest Hu Liao, half of whose body was in the ground. The gods in the sky and at the edge of the sky all recorded the years and months when the eight people would die. They only spoke to Hu Liao: "If you can perform good deeds, you can extend your life." Yang Xian and the others all died as predicted. Hu Liao became even more fearful and devoted to the law, living in the mountains and working diligently. [114]

    虔伯后为梁州西曹掾广记引有掾字州将萧思话也，萧转南蛮，复命为行参军，虔伯耳荆楚之言，心甚惧然，求萧解职，将适衡山，萧苦不许。十五年即戊寅岁也，六月末，得病，至八月四日，危笃守命。其日黄昏后，忽朗然彻视，遥见西面有三人，形长可二丈，前一人衣?垂鬓，顶光圆明，后二人姿质金曜，仪相端备，列于空中，去地数仞。虔伯委悉详视，犹是前所梦者也。顷之不见，余芳移时方歇，同居小大，皆闻香气，因而流汗，病即小瘥。虔伯所居宇卑陋，于时自觉处在殿堂，廊壁环曜，皆是珍宝。于是所患悉以平复。珠林三十二广记一百十三 [115]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [115]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 虔伯后为梁州西曹掾 - Qianbo later served as the Western Cao Assistant of Liangzhou.
    • 复命为行参军 - Was reassigned as a traveling military advisor.
    • 虔伯耳荆楚之言 - Qianbo heard the words of Jingchu.
    • 求萧解职 - Requested Xiao Sihua to resign.
    • 将适衡山 - Planned to go to Mount Heng.
    • 萧苦不许 - Xiao Sihua strongly refused.
    • 十五年即戊寅岁也 - The year was the 15th year of the Wu Yin period.
    • 六月末，得病 - In late June, he fell ill.
    • 至八月四日，危笃守命 - By August 4th, his condition was critical, but he held on.
    • 其日黄昏后，忽朗然彻视 - That evening, he suddenly saw clearly.
    • 遥见西面有三人 - Saw three people far to the west.
    • 形长可二丈 - Tall, about two zhang.
    • 前一人衣?垂鬓，顶光圆明 - The first person was dressed in fine clothes, with hanging hair, and a round, bright halo on top of his head.
    • 后二人姿质金曜，仪相端备 - The next two had golden, radiant features and dignified, complete demeanor.
    • 列于空中，去地数仞 - Standing in the air, several ren away from the ground.
    • 虔伯委悉详视，犹是前所梦者也 - Qianbo carefully observed, and it was still what he had dreamed of before.
    • 顷之不见，余芳移时方歇 - Soon they disappeared, and the lingering fragrance took a long time to fade.
    • 同居小大，皆闻香气 - Everyone living there, regardless of age, smelled the fragrance.
    • 因而流汗，病即小瘥 - As a result, he sweated, and his condition improved slightly.
    • 虔伯所居宇卑陋 - Qianbo's dwelling was humble and shabby.
    • 于时自觉处在殿堂，廊壁环曜，皆是珍宝 - At that moment, he felt himself in a hall, with corridors and walls shining with light, all adorned with treasures.
    • 于是所患悉以平复 - Thus, all his ailments were cured.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 梁州 - Refers to Liangzhou, an administrative region in ancient China.
    • 西曹掾 - A specific administrative position in the government.
    • 萧思话 - A historical figure, likely a governor or high-ranking official.
    • 行参军 - A military advisor who travels with the army.
    • 荆楚 - Refers to the Jingchu region, known for its cultural and historical significance.
    • 衡山 - Mount Heng, a famous mountain in Hunan Province, often associated with Daoist and Buddhist traditions.
    • 戊寅岁 - The year 15th of the Wu Yin period, which corresponds to a specific year in the Chinese calendar.
    • 广记 - A historical or literary source that cites the term 'Cao'.
    • 珠林三十二广记一百十三 - Refers to specific entries in the *Zhu Lin* and *Guang Ji* collections, which are historical and literary compendia.

    Proper Nouns

    • 虔伯 - Qianbo, the main subject of the text.
    • 梁州 - Liangzhou, the administrative region.
    • 西曹掾 - Western Cao Assistant, the position Qianbo held.
    • 萧思话 - Xiao Sihua, the state governor.
    • 行参军 - Traveling military advisor, the position Qianbo was reassigned to.
    • 荆楚 - Jingchu, the region.
    • 衡山 - Mount Heng, the destination.
    • 戊寅岁 - The 15th year of the Wu Yin period.
    • 广记 - Guangji, the historical or literary source.
    • 珠林三十二广记一百十三 - Specific entries in the *Zhu Lin* and *Guang Ji* collections.

    Qianbo later served as the Western Cao Assistant of Liangzhou, as cited in the Guangji with the term 'Cao', referring to the state governor Xiao Sihua. Xiao was reassigned as a traveling military advisor. Qianbo heard the words of Jingchu, and was greatly afraid, requesting Xiao to resign and planning to go to Mount Heng, but Xiao strongly refused. The year was the 15th year of the Wu Yin period. In late June, he fell ill, and by August 4th, his condition was critical, but he held on. That evening, he suddenly saw clearly, far to the west, three people, tall, about two zhang. The first person was dressed in fine clothes, with hanging hair, and a round, bright halo on top of his head. The next two had golden, radiant features and dignified, complete demeanor, standing in the air, several ren away from the ground. Qianbo carefully observed, and it was still what he had dreamed of before. Soon they disappeared, and the lingering fragrance took a long time to fade. Everyone living there, regardless of age, smelled the fragrance, as a result, he sweated, and his condition improved slightly. Qianbo's dwelling was humble and shabby, at that moment, he felt himself in a hall, with corridors and walls shining with light, all adorned with treasures. Thus, all his ailments were cured. Zhu Lin 32, Guang Ji 113. [115]

    宋沙门竺惠庆，广陵人也，经行修明。元嘉十二年，荆扬大水，川陵如一。惠庆将入庐山，船至小，而暴风忽起，同旅已得依浦，唯惠庆船未及得泊；飘扬中江，风疾浪涌，静待沦覆。庆正心端念，诵观世音经，洲际之人，望见其船迎飙截流，如有数十人牵挽之者，迳到上岸，一舫全济。珠林六十五
    　　宋葛济之，句容人，稚川后也。妻同郡纪氏体貌闲雅，甚有妇德。济之世事仙学，纪氏亦同，而心乐佛法，常存诚不替。元嘉十三年，方在机织，忽觉云日开朗，空中清明，因投释筐梭，仰望四表；见西方有如来真形，及宝盖旛幢，蔽映天汉。心独喜曰：“经说无量寿佛，即此者耶？”便头面作礼。济之敬其如此，仍起就之，纪授济手，指示佛所，济亦登见半身及诸旛盖，俄而隐没。于是云日鲜彩，五色烛耀，乡比亲族，颇亦睹见。两三食顷，方稍除歇。自是屯闾多归法者。珠林十五广记一百十四 [116]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [116]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 經行修明 - A compound phrase meaning 'diligent in practice and clear in understanding,' often used to describe someone who is devout and knowledgeable.
    • 川陵如一 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'rivers and mountains are as one,' describing a flood that has submerged the landscape completely.
    • 正心端念 - Means 'to focus the mind and thoughts,' indicating a state of mental concentration and devotion.
    • 投釋筐梭 - Literally 'throw down the shuttle,' meaning to stop weaving, symbolizing a sudden interruption of work.
    • 四表 - Refers to the four directions or the entire world, indicating a broad view.
    • 無量壽佛 - The Buddha of Infinite Life, a central figure in Pure Land Buddhism.
    • 頭面作禮 - A respectful gesture of bowing with the head and face, indicating deep reverence.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋沙門竺惠慶 - A Buddhist monk named Zhi Huaiqing from the Song dynasty, known for his piety and miraculous experiences.
    • 廣陵 - An ancient name for Yangzhou, a city in Jiangsu Province, China.
    • 元嘉十二年 - The twelfth year of the Yuanchi era (435 AD) during the Southern Song dynasty.
    • 荊揚 - Refers to Jingzhou and Yangzhou, two provinces in southern China.
    • 觀世音經 - The *Sutra of the Bodhisattva Ksitigarbha* or *Sutra of the Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara*, a text praising the Bodhisattva of Compassion.
    • 葛濟之 - A figure named Ge Jizhi, a follower of Taoist practices.
    • 稚川後 - Indicates that Ge Jizhi is a descendant of Ge Hong, a famous alchemist and Taoist scholar.
    • 同郡紀氏 - A woman named Ji, from the same province as Ge Jizhi, known for her piety and devotion to Buddhism.
    • 元嘉十三年 - The thirteenth year of the Yuanchi era (436 AD) during the Southern Song dynasty.
    • 機織 - Refers to the act of weaving, indicating a domestic activity.
    • 寶蓋旛幢 - Decorative elements often associated with Buddhist rituals, such as canopies and banners.
    • 天漢 - Refers to the Milky Way, symbolizing the heavens or the divine realm.
    • 珠林 - A collection of Buddhist stories and miracles, often used to illustrate the power of faith and devotion.

    Proper Nouns

    • 宋 - The Southern Song dynasty (420-479 AD).
    • 沙门 - A term for Buddhist monks.
    • 竺惠庆 - A Buddhist monk named Zhi Huaiqing.
    • 廣陵 - An ancient name for Yangzhou.
    • 元嘉十二年 - The twelfth year of the Yuanchi era (435 AD).
    • 荊揚 - Jingzhou and Yangzhou, two provinces in southern China.
    • 廬山 - Mount Lu, a famous mountain in Jiangxi Province, known for its monasteries and natural beauty.
    • 觀世音經 - The *Sutra of the Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara*.
    • 葛濟之 - A figure named Ge Jizhi.
    • 句容 - A city in Jiangsu Province.
    • 稚川 - Ge Hong, a famous alchemist and Taoist scholar.
    • 紀氏 - A woman named Ji, Ge Jizhi's wife.
    • 元嘉十三年 - The thirteenth year of the Yuanchi era (436 AD).
    • 無量壽佛 - The Buddha of Infinite Life.
    • 珠林 - A collection of Buddhist stories and miracles.

    A Buddhist Monk Named Zhi Huaiqing from the Song Dynasty, a native of Yangzhou, was devout and knowledgeable. In the twelfth year of the Yuanchi era (435 AD), there was a great flood in Jingzhou and Yangzhou, submerging the rivers and mountains as one. Zhi Huaiqing was about to enter Mount Lu, and his small boat was caught in a sudden storm while others had already found shelter. His boat was drifting in the middle of the river, facing strong winds and waves, and it seemed destined to sink. Zhi Huaiqing focused his mind and thoughts, reciting the Sutra of the Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara. People on the shore saw his boat defying the wind and currents, as if pulled by dozens of people, and it eventually reached the shore safely, with all aboard unharmed. This story is recorded in Zhu Lin (Chapter 65).

    Ge Jizhi, a native of Jurong, was a descendant of Ge Hong, a famous alchemist and Taoist scholar. His wife, Ji, from the same province, was graceful and virtuous. Ge Jizhi followed Taoist practices, and Ji did the same, but she had a particular interest in Buddhism and remained sincere in her faith. In the thirteenth year of the Yuanchi era (436 AD), while weaving, Ji suddenly felt a sense of clarity and peace. She looked up and saw the Buddha of Infinite Life, along with canopies and banners, overshadowing the Milky Way. She was delighted and thought, "The sutras say the Buddha of Infinite Life, is this him?" She then bowed her head and face in reverence. Ge Jizhi, seeing her devotion, got up and went to her. Ji showed him the direction of the Buddha, and Ge Jizhi also saw the Buddha's half-body and the various canopies and banners, which soon disappeared. The sky and sun became colorful, and many people in the community saw the same phenomenon. After a couple of meals, the event gradually subsided. From then on, many people in the community turned to Buddhism. This story is recorded in Zhu Lin (Chapter 15, Guangji 114). [116]

    宋尼慧木者，姓傅氏，十一出家，受持小戒，居梁郡筑弋屯寺，始读大品，日诵两卷。师慧超，尝建经堂，木往礼拜，辄见屋内东北隅有一沙门，金色黑衣，足不履地。木又于夜中卧而诵习，梦到西方，见一浴池，有芙蓉华，诸化生人，列坐其中；有一大华，独空无人，木欲登华，攀牵用力，不觉诵经，音响高大，木母谓其魇惊，起唤之。木母笃老，口无复齿，木恒嚼哺饴母，为以过中，不得净漱，故年将立，不受大戒。母终亡后，木自除草开坛，请师受戒。 [117]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [117]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 宋尼慧木者 - Nominal phrase meaning 'the nun named Hui Mu of the Song dynasty.'
    • 受持小戒 - Means 'received and upheld the lesser precepts,' referring to the initial monastic vows.
    • 居梁郡筑弋屯寺 - Means 'dwelled in the Zui Tun Temple in Liang Prefecture.'
    • 始读大品 - Means 'began reading the Great Treatise,' likely referring to the *Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra*.
    • 日诵两卷 - Means 'recited two scrolls daily.'
    • 师慧超 - Means 'the teacher named Hui Chao.'
    • 尝建经堂 - Means 'once built a scripture hall.'
    • 辄见屋内东北隅有一沙门 - Means 'often saw a monk in the northeast corner of the room.'
    • 金色黑衣 - Describes the monk as 'golden in complexion and wearing black robes.'
    • 足不履地 - Means 'feet did not touch the ground,' suggesting supernatural or ethereal qualities.
    • 木又于夜中卧而诵习 - Means 'Hui Mu also recited and studied at night while lying down.'
    • 梦到西方 - Means 'dreamed of the Western Paradise.'
    • 见一浴池 - Means 'saw a bathing pool.'
    • 有芙蓉华 - Means 'had lotus flowers.'
    • 诸化生人 - Means 'various beings born through transformation.'
    • 列坐其中 - Means 'seated in rows within it.'
    • 有一大华，独空无人 - Means 'there was a large lotus flower, empty and without anyone.'
    • 木欲登华 - Means 'Hui Mu wanted to climb onto the lotus.'
    • 攀牵用力 - Means 'strained and pulled with effort.'
    • 不觉诵经 - Means 'unconsciously recited the sutra.'
    • 音响高大 - Means 'the sound was loud and resonant.'
    • 木母谓其魇惊 - Means 'Hui Mu's mother thought she was having a nightmare.'
    • 起唤之 - Means 'got up and called to her.'
    • 木母笃老 - Means 'Hui Mu's mother was very old.'
    • 口无复齿 - Means 'had no teeth left.'
    • 木恒嚼哺饴母 - Means 'Hui Mu always chewed food and fed her mother.'
    • 为以过中 - Means 'because it was past noon.'
    • 不得净漱 - Means 'could not perform a clean rinse.'
    • 故年将立 - Means 'therefore, as she approached adulthood.'
    • 不受大戒 - Means 'did not receive the full precepts.'
    • 母终亡后 - Means 'after her mother passed away.'
    • 木自除草开坛 - Means 'Hui Mu cleared the grass and prepared a platform.'
    • 请师受戒 - Means 'invited a teacher to receive the precepts.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋尼 - Refers to a nun from the Song dynasty.
    • 梁郡 - Refers to Liang Prefecture, a historical region in China.
    • 筑弋屯寺 - Refers to the Zui Tun Temple, a specific temple in Liang Prefecture.
    • 大品 - Refers to the *Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra*, a significant Buddhist text.
    • 师慧超 - Refers to a teacher named Hui Chao.
    • 西方 - Refers to the Western Paradise, a concept in Buddhism.
    • 化生人 - Refers to beings born through transformation, often associated with higher spiritual realms.
    • 大戒 - Refers to the full monastic precepts, a significant milestone in Buddhist practice.

    Proper Nouns

    • 宋尼慧木 - The name of the nun.
    • 傅氏 - The surname of the nun.
    • 梁郡 - The name of the prefecture.
    • 筑弋屯寺 - The name of the temple.
    • 大品 - The title of the *Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra*.
    • 师慧超 - The name of the teacher.
    • 西方 - The concept of the Western Paradise.
    • 化生人 - The term for beings born through transformation.
    • 大戒 - The term for the full monastic precepts.

    Nun Hui Mu of the Song dynasty, surnamed Fu, entered the monastic life at the age of eleven, received and upheld the lesser precepts, and dwelled in the Zui Tun Temple in Liang Prefecture. She began reading the Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra and recited two scrolls daily. Her teacher, Hui Chao, once built a scripture hall. When Hui Mu went to pay homage, she often saw a monk in the northeast corner of the room, golden in complexion and wearing black robes, whose feet did not touch the ground. At night, while lying down and reciting and studying, she dreamed of the Western Paradise, where she saw a bathing pool filled with lotus flowers and various beings born through transformation seated in rows. There was a large lotus flower, empty and without anyone. Hui Mu wanted to climb onto it, strained and pulled with effort, and unconsciously recited the sutra. The sound was loud and resonant. Her mother thought she was having a nightmare and got up to call to her. Hui Mu's mother was very old and had no teeth left. Hui Mu always chewed food and fed her mother, but because it was past noon, she could not perform a clean rinse, so she did not receive the full precepts as she approached adulthood. After her mother passed away, Hui Mu cleared the grass and prepared a platform, inviting a teacher to receive the precepts. [117]

    忽于坛所见天地晃然，悉黄金色，仰望西南，见一天人，着○衣，衣色赤黄，去木或近或远，寻没不见。凡见灵异，秘不语人。木兄出家，闻而欲知，乃诳诱之曰：“汝为道积年，竟无所招，比可养发，当访出门。”木闻甚惧，谓当实然，乃粗言所见。唯静称尼闻其道德，称往为狎，方便请问，乃为具说。木后与同等共礼无量寿佛，因伏地不起，咸谓得眠，蹴而问之，木竟不答，静称复独苦求问，木云：“当伏地之时，梦往安养国见佛，为说小品，已得四卷，因被蹴即觉，甚追恨之。”木元嘉十四年时已六十九。珠林十五 [118]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [118]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 忽于坛所见天地晃然，悉黄金色 - Suddenly, at the altar, he saw the heavens and earth shimmering, all in a golden hue.
    • 唯静称尼闻其道德，称往为狎，方便请问，乃为具说 - Only the nun Jingcheng, who heard of his virtue, approached him with familiarity and skillfully asked, so he told her everything.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 坛所 - Refers to the altar where the protagonist had his vision.
    • 天人 - Refers to a celestial being or divine figure.
    • ○衣 - The character '○' is likely a placeholder for a specific type of robe, possibly a Buddhist robe.
    • 衣色赤黄 - Describes the robe's color as red and yellow, which may have symbolic significance in Buddhist contexts.
    • 木 - The protagonist's name, Mu.
    • 静称尼 - The name of the nun who skillfully elicited the protagonist's story.
    • 无量寿佛 - Refers to the Buddha of Infinite Life, a central figure in Pure Land Buddhism.
    • 安养国 - Refers to the Pure Land, a realm of bliss in Buddhist cosmology.
    • 小品 - Refers to a small sutra, a Buddhist scripture.
    • 元嘉十四年 - The 14th year of the Yuanjia era, a period in Chinese history.
    • 珠林十五 - The source of the text, indicating it is from the 15th volume of the 'Jewel Forest.'

    Proper Nouns

    • 木 - The protagonist's name, Mu.
    • 静称尼 - The name of the nun who skillfully elicited the protagonist's story.
    • 无量寿佛 - Refers to the Buddha of Infinite Life, a central figure in Pure Land Buddhism.
    • 安养国 - Refers to the Pure Land, a realm of bliss in Buddhist cosmology.
    • 小品 - Refers to a small sutra, a Buddhist scripture.
    • 元嘉十四年 - The 14th year of the Yuanjia era, a period in Chinese history.
    • 珠林十五 - The source of the text, indicating it is from the 15th volume of the 'Jewel Forest.'

    A Sudden Vision and Its Aftermath

    Suddenly, at the altar, he saw the heavens and earth shimmering, all in a golden hue. Looking up to the southwest, he saw a celestial being dressed in a robe of red and yellow, appearing sometimes near and sometimes far, eventually disappearing. All that he saw of the supernatural, he kept secret and did not speak of to others. Brother Mu, who had become a monk, heard of this and wanted to know more, so he deceived him, saying, "You have practiced the Way for many years, but have achieved nothing. Soon you will be able to grow your hair, and then you should leave the monastery." Mu was very afraid upon hearing this and thought it was true, so he roughly described what he had seen. Only the nun Jingcheng, who heard of his virtue, approached him with familiarity and skillfully asked, so he told her everything. Later, Mu and his peers worshipped the Buddha of Infinite Life, and because he fell to the ground and did not rise, they thought he had fallen asleep. They kicked him to ask, but Mu did not answer. Nun Jingcheng then persistently asked again, and Mu said, "When I was lying on the ground, I dreamed of going to the Pure Land and meeting the Buddha, who spoke to me a small sutra, which I have already received in four volumes. I was awakened by being kicked, and I deeply regret it." Mu was sixty-nine years old in the 14th year of the Yuanjia era. This account is from the 15th volume of the "Jewel Forest." [118]

    宋释僧瑜，吴兴余杭人，本姓周氏，弱冠出家，号为神理，精修苦业，始终不渝。元嘉十五年，游憩庐山，同侣有昙温慧光等，皆厉操贞洁，俱尚幽栖。乃共筑架其山之阳，今招隐精舍是也。瑜常以为：结溺三途，情形故也，情将尽矣，形亦宜损；药王之○，独何云远？于是屡发言誓，始契烧身，四十有四，孝建二年六月三日，将就本志，道俗赴观，车骑填接。瑜率众行道，训授典戒。尔日密云将雨，瑜乃慨然发誓曰：“若我所志克明，天当清朗；如期诚无感，便宜滂澍。使此四辈知神应之无昧也。”言已，顷之，云景明霁。及焚爓交至，合掌端一，有紫气腾空，别表烟外，移晷乃歇。后旬有四日，瑜所住房里，双桐生焉，根枝丰茂，巨细如一，贯榱直竦，遂成鸿树。理识者以为娑罗宝树，克炳泥洹，瑜之庶几，故见斯证。因号曰双桐沙门。吴郡张辩，时为平南长史，亲睹其事，具为传赞云。珠林六十三 [119]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [119]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 弱冠出家 - Refers to someone who became a monk at the age of 20, a traditional age for coming of age in Chinese culture.
    • 号为神理 - Means 'known as a master of spiritual principles,' indicating his reputation for deep understanding of spiritual matters.
    • 精修苦业 - Means 'diligently practiced ascetic practices,' highlighting his dedication to spiritual discipline.
    • 始终不渝 - Means 'remained steadfast throughout his life,' emphasizing his unwavering commitment.
    • 元嘉十五年 - Refers to the year 438 AD during the Southern Song Dynasty.
    • 游憩庐山 - Means 'visited and stayed at Mount Lu,' a famous mountain known for its scenic beauty and religious significance.
    • 同侣有昙温慧光等 - Refers to his fellow monks, including Tan Wen and Hui Guang.
    • 厉操贞洁 - Means 'maintained pure and upright conduct,' indicating their moral integrity.
    • 俱尚幽栖 - Means 'all favored seclusion,' showing their preference for a life away from the world.
    • 筑架其山之阳 - Means 'built a dwelling on the sunny side of the mountain,' describing the location of their retreat.
    • 今招隐精舍是也 - Means 'which is now known as the Zhaoyin Monastery,' identifying the current name of the place.
    • 结溺三途 - Refers to being trapped in the cycle of rebirth, a concept from Buddhist cosmology.
    • 情形故也 - Means 'due to the nature of body and mind,' explaining the cause of the cycle.
    • 情将尽矣，形亦宜损 - Means 'when the mind is exhausted, the body should also be reduced,' suggesting a physical sacrifice to achieve spiritual liberation.
    • 药王之○ - Refers to the practice of self-immolation, similar to the self-sacrifice of a medicine king (a bodhisattva who sacrifices himself to save others).
    • 屡发言誓 - Means 'repeatedly made vows,' indicating his determination.
    • 始契烧身 - Means 'began to agree to self-immolation,' showing his decision.
    • 孝建二年六月三日 - Refers to the date June 3, 455 AD, during the Southern Song Dynasty.
    • 将就本志 - Means 'to fulfill his vow,' indicating his intention to carry out his vow.
    • 道俗赴观 - Means 'monks and laypeople came to witness,' describing the crowd that gathered.
    • 车骑填接 - Means 'carriages and horses filled the roads,' indicating the large number of people.
    • 率众行道 - Means 'led the congregation in chanting,' showing his leadership.
    • 训授典戒 - Means 'taught the precepts,' indicating his role in instructing others.
    • 密云将雨 - Means 'dark clouds were about to bring rain,' setting the weather scene.
    • 慨然发誓 - Means 'made a solemn vow with great emotion,' showing his sincerity.
    • 若我所志克明，天当清朗 - Means 'if my wish is granted, the sky will be clear and bright,' expressing his hope for a favorable sign.
    • 如期诚无感，便宜滂澍 - Means 'if my wish is not granted, it would be better for it to rain,' indicating his acceptance of the outcome.
    • 使此四辈知神应之无昧也 - Means 'so that these four groups of people know that the divine response is not hidden,' showing his desire for divine validation.
    • 言已，顷之，云景明霁 - Means 'after he spoke, soon after, the clouds cleared and the sky became bright,' describing the immediate sign.
    • 及焚爓交至 - Means 'when the flames reached him,' indicating the moment of self-immolation.
    • 合掌端一 - Means 'with hands joined and focused,' showing his state of mind.
    • 有紫气腾空 - Means 'a purple aura rose into the air,' describing a supernatural phenomenon.
    • 别表烟外 - Means 'separately visible outside the smoke,' indicating the distinct nature of the aura.
    • 移晷乃歇 - Means 'it lasted for a while before disappearing,' showing the duration of the phenomenon.
    • 后旬有四日 - Refers to 14 days later.
    • 瑜所住房里，双桐生焉 - Means 'in the room where he lived, two cedars grew,' describing the miraculous growth.
    • 根枝丰茂，巨细如一 - Means 'the roots and branches were lush and uniform in size,' describing the appearance of the trees.
    • 贯榱直竦 - Means 'straight and tall, piercing the rafters,' indicating the trees' height and straightness.
    • 遂成鸿树 - Means 'thus became great trees,' describing the final state of the trees.
    • 理识者以为娑罗宝树 - Means 'those who understand the meaning believe they are the sacred sala trees,' showing the interpretation of the trees.
    • 克炳泥洹 - Means 'to shine brightly in Nirvana,' indicating the spiritual significance.
    • 瑜之庶几，故见斯证 - Means 'thus, this is a sign of his near attainment,' showing the connection between the miracle and his spiritual status.
    • 因号曰双桐沙门 - Means 'he was then called the Double Cedar Monk,' giving him a new title.
    • 吴郡张辩 - Refers to Zhang Bian, a person from Wu Prefecture.
    • 时为平南长史 - Means 'at the time, he was the Chief Clerk of the Southern Pacification Department,' indicating his official position.
    • 亲睹其事 - Means 'witnessed the event personally,' showing his firsthand account.
    • 具为传赞云 - Means 'he wrote a detailed account and praise,' indicating the source of the text.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋释僧瑜 - Refers to a Buddhist monk named Seng Yu from the Southern Song Dynasty.
    • 吴兴余杭人 - Indicates that he was from Yuhang, Wu Prefecture, in the southern part of China.
    • 本姓周氏 - Means 'originally surnamed Zhou,' indicating his family name before becoming a monk.
    • 庐山 - A famous mountain in Jiangxi Province, known for its natural beauty and religious significance.
    • 昙温慧光等 - Names of his fellow monks, indicating the community of ascetics.
    • 厉操贞洁 - Refers to the high moral standards expected of monks.
    • 俱尚幽栖 - Indicates the preference for a secluded life, common among Buddhist monks.
    • 筑架其山之阳 - Describes the construction of a dwelling on the sunny side of the mountain, a common practice for monasteries.
    • 今招隐精舍是也 - Identifies the current name of the monastery, showing the historical continuity.
    • 结溺三途 - Refers to the Buddhist concept of the cycle of rebirth, including the three realms of desire, form, and formlessness.
    • 情形故也 - Explains the cause of the cycle, emphasizing the interplay between body and mind.
    • 情将尽矣，形亦宜损 - Suggests a physical sacrifice to break the cycle, a practice found in some Buddhist traditions.
    • 药王之○ - Refers to the self-sacrifice of a medicine king, a bodhisattva who sacrifices himself to save others.
    • 屡发言誓 - Indicates his repeated vows, showing his determination.
    • 始契烧身 - Describes his decision to self-immolate, a rare and extreme act of devotion.
    • 孝建二年六月三日 - Refers to a specific date in the Southern Song Dynasty, providing a historical context.
    • 将就本志 - Indicates his intention to fulfill his vow, showing his resolve.
    • 道俗赴观 - Describes the gathering of both monks and laypeople, showing the impact of his decision.
    • 车骑填接 - Indicates the large crowd, showing the public interest.
    • 率众行道 - Shows his leadership in leading the congregation in chanting.
    • 训授典戒 - Indicates his role in teaching the precepts, showing his authority.
    • 密云将雨 - Sets the weather scene, adding to the dramatic atmosphere.
    • 慨然发誓 - Shows his emotional state, indicating his sincerity.
    • 若我所志克明，天当清朗 - Expresses his hope for a favorable sign, showing his faith.
    • 如期诚无感，便宜滂澍 - Indicates his acceptance of the outcome, showing his humility.
    • 使此四辈知神应之无昧也 - Shows his desire for divine validation, indicating his belief in the supernatural.
    • 言已，顷之，云景明霁 - Describes the immediate sign, showing the divine response.
    • 及焚爓交至 - Describes the moment of self-immolation, showing the climax of the event.
    • 合掌端一 - Means 'with hands joined and focused,' showing his state of mind.
    • 有紫气腾空 - Means 'a purple aura rose into the air,' describing a supernatural phenomenon.
    • 别表烟外 - Means 'separately visible outside the smoke,' indicating the distinct nature of the aura.
    • 移晷乃歇 - Means 'it lasted for a while before disappearing,' showing the duration of the phenomenon.
    • 后旬有四日 - Refers to 14 days later.
    • 瑜所住房里，双桐生焉 - Means 'in the room where he lived, two cedars grew,' describing the miraculous growth.
    • 根枝丰茂，巨细如一 - Means 'the roots and branches were lush and uniform in size,' describing the appearance of the trees.
    • 贯榱直竦 - Means 'straight and tall, piercing the rafters,' indicating the trees' height and straightness.
    • 遂成鸿树 - Means 'thus became great trees,' describing the final state of the trees.
    • 理识者以为娑罗宝树 - Means 'those who understand the meaning believe they are the sacred sala trees,' showing the interpretation of the trees.
    • 克炳泥洹 - Means 'to shine brightly in Nirvana,' indicating the spiritual significance.
    • 瑜之庶几，故见斯证 - Means 'thus, this is a sign of his near attainment,' showing the connection between the miracle and his spiritual status.
    • 因号曰双桐沙门 - Means 'he was then called the Double Cedar Monk,' giving him a new title.
    • 吴郡张辩 - Refers to Zhang Bian, a person from Wu Prefecture.
    • 时为平南长史 - Means 'at the time, he was the Chief Clerk of the Southern Pacification Department,' indicating his official position.
    • 亲睹其事 - Means 'witnessed the event personally,' showing his firsthand account.
    • 具为传赞云 - Means 'he wrote a detailed account and praise,' indicating the source of the text.

    A Monk Named Seng Yu of the Song Dynasty

    Seng Yu was a monk from Yuhang, Wu Prefecture, originally surnamed Zhou. At the age of twenty, he became a monk and was known for his mastery of spiritual principles. He diligently practiced ascetic practices and remained steadfast throughout his life. In the fifteenth year of the Yuanjia era (438 AD), he visited and stayed at Mount Lu, where he met fellow monks such as Tan Wen and Hui Guang, all of whom maintained pure and upright conduct and favored seclusion. Together, they built a dwelling on the sunny side of the mountain, which is now known as the Zhaoyin Monastery.

    Seng Yu often believed that being trapped in the cycle of rebirth was due to the nature of body and mind. When the mind is exhausted, the body should also be reduced. He thought that the practice of self-immolation, similar to the self-sacrifice of a medicine king, was not far-fetched. Therefore, he repeatedly made vows and began to agree to self-immolation. On the third day of the sixth month in the second year of the Xiaojian era (455 AD), he intended to fulfill his vow. Monks and laypeople came to witness the event, and carriages and horses filled the roads. Seng Yu led the congregation in chanting and taught the precepts. On that day, dark clouds were about to bring rain. Seng Yu made a solemn vow with great emotion: "If my wish is granted, the sky will be clear and bright; if my wish is not granted, it would be better for it to rain. So that these four groups of people know that the divine response is not hidden." After he spoke, soon after, the clouds cleared and the sky became bright. When the flames reached him, he had his hands joined and focused, and a purple aura rose into the air, separately visible outside the smoke, lasting for a while before disappearing.

    Fourteen days later, in the room where he lived, two cedars grew. The roots and branches were lush and uniform in size, straight and tall, piercing the rafters, thus becoming great trees. Those who understand the meaning believe they are the sacred sala trees, shining brightly in Nirvana, a sign of his near attainment. He was then called the Double Cedar Monk. Zhang Bian of Wu Prefecture, at the time the Chief Clerk of the Southern Pacification Department, witnessed the event personally and wrote a detailed account and praise. [119]

    宋阮稚宗者，河东人也，元嘉十六年，随钟离太守阮愔在郡。愔便与稚宗行至远屯，郡吏盖○边定随焉。行达民家，恍忽如眠，便不复寤；民以为死，举出门外，方营殡具，经夕能言。说：初有一百许人，缚稚宗去，行数十里，至一佛图，僧众供养，不异于世。有一僧曰：“汝好渔猎，今应受报。 [120]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [120]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 恍忽如眠 - Describes a state of being dazed or half-asleep.
    • 不复寤 - Means 'no longer awake' or 'no longer conscious.'
    • 缚稚宗去 - Means 'to bind and take away.'
    • 经夕能言 - Means 'after a night, able to speak.'
    • 行数十里 - Means 'to walk dozens of miles.'
    • 佛图 - A transliteration of 'Buddhist temple' or 'pagoda.'
    • 僧众供养 - Means 'the monks' offerings and worship.'
    • 不异于世 - Means 'no different from the world.'
    • 汝好渔猎 - Means 'you like fishing and hunting.'
    • 今应受报 - Means 'now you should receive your retribution.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋阮稚宗 - Refers to a person named Ruan Zizong from the Song dynasty.
    • 河东 - Indicates that Ruan Zizong was from Hedong, a region in present-day Shanxi province.
    • 元嘉十六年 - A specific year in the Yuanjia era, which helps place the event historically.
    • 钟离太守阮愔 - Refers to a governor named Ruan Yin who was in charge of Zhongli (a place).
    • 远屯 - Refers to a remote military post or settlement.
    • 郡吏盖边定 - Refers to a local official named Gai Bianting who accompanied Ruan Zizong.
    • 渔猎 - Refers to fishing and hunting, which were common activities in ancient China.

    Proper Nouns

    • 宋阮稚宗 - The name of the protagonist, Ruan Zizong, from the Song dynasty.
    • 河东 - The region where Ruan Zizong was from, now part of Shanxi province.
    • 元嘉十六年 - The specific year of the event, 16 of the Yuanjia era.
    • 钟离 - The place where Ruan Yin was the governor.
    • 阮愔 - The name of the governor, Ruan Yin.
    • 远屯 - The remote military post or settlement they visited.
    • 郡吏盖边定 - The name of the local official, Gai Bianting.
    • 佛图 - The Buddhist temple or pagoda they encountered.
    • 僧众 - The monks who were present at the temple.

    In the sixteenth year of the Yuanjia era, a man named Ruan Zizong from Hedong followed the governor of Zhongli, Ruan Yin, to a remote military post. Gai Bianting, a local official, accompanied them. They reached a house where Ruan Zizong suddenly fell into a dazed state, as if he were asleep, and did not regain consciousness. The locals thought he was dead and carried him outside to prepare for burial. However, after a night, Ruan Zizong was able to speak again.

    He recounted that he had been taken by about a hundred people, who bound him and led him away for dozens of miles until they reached a Buddhist temple. The monks there were engaged in offerings and worship, just as they would in the world. One monk said to him, "You liked fishing and hunting in the past, and now you should receive your retribution." [120]

    ”便取稚宗皮，剥脔截具，如治诸牲兽之法，复纳于深水，钩口出之，剖破解切，若为脍状，又镬煮炉炙，初悉糜烂，随以还复，痛恼苦毒，至三乃止。问欲活不？稚宗便叩头请命。道人令其蹲地，以水灌之，云一灌除罪五百。稚宗苦求多灌，沙门曰：“唯三足矣。”见有蚁数头，道人指曰：“此虽微物，亦不可杀，无论复巨此者也。鱼肉自死，此可啖耳。斋会之日，悉着新衣，无新，可浣也。”稚宗因问：“我行旅有三，而独婴苦，何也？”道人曰：“彼二人自知罪福，知而故犯；唯尔愚蒙，不识缘报，故以相诫。”因尔便稣，数日能起。由是遂断渔○耳。珠林六十四 [121]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [121]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 便取稚宗皮 - A direct and abrupt command indicating an immediate action without delay.
    • 剥脔截具 - Describes the process of cutting and preparing the skin, specific to butchery or skinning.
    • 如治诸牲兽之法 - Refers to the method used for treating various animals, implying a standard procedure.
    • 复纳于深水 - Indicates the action of placing something back into deep water, unusual in butchery.
    • 钩口出之 - Describes the action of pulling something out with a hook, likely retrieving the prepared item from the water.
    • 剖破解切，若为脍状 - Describes slicing and cutting the item into pieces resembling sashimi or finely cut meat.
    • 又镬煮炉炙 - Refers to cooking methods involving boiling in a large pot and roasting over a fire.
    • 初悉糜烂 - Means that initially everything was completely ruined or destroyed.
    • 随以还复 - Indicates a restoration or recovery of the original state.
    • 痛恼苦毒 - Describes intense physical and emotional suffering.
    • 至三乃止 - Means that the suffering lasted for three times before stopping.
    • 问欲活不 - A colloquial way of asking if the person wants to live, with a tone of urgency.
    • 稚宗便叩头请命 - Describes the act of kneeling and begging for mercy.
    • 道人令其蹲地，以水灌之 - Indicates the action of the monk making the person squat and pouring water over them.
    • 云一灌除罪五百 - Explains that each pour of water removes 500 sins.
    • 稚宗苦求多灌 - Describes the person's desperate request for more water to be poured.
    • 沙门曰：“唯三足矣。” - The monk responds, limiting the number of times the water can be poured to three.
    • 见有蚁数头 - Describes seeing several ants.
    • 此虽微物，亦不可杀 - Emphasizes that even small creatures should not be killed.
    • 无论复巨此者也 - Extends the principle to larger creatures as well.
    • 鱼肉自死，此可啖耳 - Suggests that fish and meat should only be eaten if they die naturally.
    • 斋会之日，悉着新衣，无新，可浣也 - Recommends wearing new clothes on religious gatherings, or washing old clothes if new ones are not available.
    • 我行旅有三，而独婴苦，何也 - Asks why among the three travelers, only one was affected by the suffering.
    • 道人曰：“彼二人自知罪福，知而故犯；唯尔愚蒙，不识缘报，故以相诫。” - Explains that the other two travelers knew their sins and rewards but deliberately committed them, while the third was ignorant and thus warned.
    • 因尔便稣，数日能起 - Describes the person recovering and being able to stand up after several days.
    • 由是遂断渔耳 - Indicates that the person stopped fishing as a result of the experience.
    • 珠林六十四 - Refers to the source of the text, possibly a collection of stories or teachings.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 稚宗 - Likely a name, possibly a historical or fictional figure.
    • 道人 - Refers to a monk or a religious figure.
    • 沙门 - Another term for a monk, often used in Buddhist contexts.
    • 斋会 - Refers to a religious gathering or a vegetarian meal.
    • 缘报 - Refers to the concept of karma or the consequences of one's actions.
    • 珠林六十四 - Likely refers to a specific text or collection, possibly a Buddhist scripture or a collection of moral stories.

    Proper Nouns

    • 稚宗 - A name, possibly a historical or fictional figure.
    • 道人 - A religious figure, likely a monk.
    • 沙门 - Another term for a monk, often used in Buddhist contexts.
    • 斋会 - A religious gathering or a vegetarian meal.
    • 缘报 - The concept of karma or the consequences of one's actions.
    • 珠林六十四 - A specific text or collection, possibly a Buddhist scripture or a collection of moral stories.

    A monk took the skin of Zhi Zong, cut and prepared it as if treating various beasts, then placed it back into deep water, pulled it out with a hook, sliced and chopped it into pieces resembling sashimi, boiled it in a large pot and roasted it over a fire. Initially, everything was completely ruined, but then it was restored, causing intense physical and emotional suffering that lasted for three times before stopping. The monk asked if Zhi Zong wanted to live, and Zhi Zong knelt and begged for mercy. The monk made him squat and poured water over him, saying that each pour would remove 500 sins. Zhi Zong desperately requested more water to be poured, and the monk replied, "Only three times is enough." Seeing several ants, the monk pointed and said, "These are small creatures, but they should not be killed, let alone larger ones. Fish and meat should only be eaten if they die naturally. On days of religious gatherings, wear new clothes, or wash old clothes if new ones are not available." Zhi Zong then asked, "Among the three travelers, why was only I affected by this suffering?" The monk replied, "The other two knew their sins and rewards but deliberately committed them; you, being ignorant, did not understand the consequences, so I warned you." As a result, Zhi Zong recovered and was able to stand up after several days. From then on, he stopped fishing. This story is from Zhulin 64. [121]

    宋邢怀明，河间人，宋大将军参军，尝随南郡太守朱修之北伐，俱见陷没。于是伺○闲隙，俱得遁归，夜行昼伏，已经三日；犹惧追捕，乃遣人前觇虏○，即数日不还。一夕，将雨阴闇，所遣人将晓忽至。至乃惊曰：“向遥见火光甚明，故来投之，那得至而反闇？”修等怪愕。怀明先奉法，自征后，头上恒载观世音经，转读不废；尔夕亦暗诵，咸疑是经神力。于是常共祈心，遂以得免，居于京师。元嘉十七年，有沙门诣怀明云：“贫道见此巷中及君家，殊有血气，宜移避之。”语毕便去。怀明追而目之，出门便没，意甚恶之。经二旬，邻人张景秀伤父，及杀父妾，怀明以为血气之征，庶得无事。时与刘斌刘景文比门连接，同在一巷；其年，并以刘湛之党，同被诛夷云。珠林二十三 [122]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [122]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 伺○闲隙 - To wait for an opportunity or to spy for a chance.
    • 遁归 - To flee and return or to escape back.
    • 夜行昼伏 - To travel at night and hide during the day.
    • 头恒载观世音经 - Always carrying the *Guanshiyin Sutra* on the head.
    • 转读不废 - To recite without stopping.
    • 殊有血气 - Extremely full of vitality.
    • 移避之 - To move away from it or to avoid it.
    • 意甚恶之 - Very much disliked it.
    • 同被诛夷 - Both were killed or both were executed.
    • 珠林二十三 - Refers to a specific chapter or section in a text, likely a Buddhist or historical text.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋邢怀明 - A historical figure, likely a military general or official during the Southern Song Dynasty.
    • 河间 - A place name, likely referring to a region in northern China.
    • 宋大将军参军 - A high-ranking military official, likely a general or a military advisor.
    • 南郡太守朱修之 - The governor of Nanchang, and a historical figure, likely a military leader.
    • 北伐 - A military campaign to reclaim lost territories in the north, likely during the Southern Song Dynasty.
    • 观世音经 - The *Guanshiyin Sutra*, a Buddhist scripture dedicated to the Bodhisattva Guanyin.
    • 元嘉十七年 - The 17th year of the Yuanjia era, corresponding to 440 CE.
    • 沙门 - A Buddhist monk or ascetic.
    • 刘湛之党 - The political faction associated with Liu Zhan.

    Proper Nouns

    • 宋邢怀明 - A historical figure, likely a military general or official during the Southern Song Dynasty.
    • 河间 - A place name, likely referring to a region in northern China.
    • 宋大将军参军 - A title for a high-ranking military official, likely a general or a military advisor.
    • 南郡太守朱修之 - The governor of Nanchang, and a historical figure, likely a military leader.
    • 观世音经 - The *Guanshiyin Sutra*, a Buddhist scripture dedicated to the Bodhisattva Guanyin.
    • 元嘉十七年 - The 17th year of the Yuanjia era, corresponding to 440 CE.
    • 沙门 - A Buddhist monk or ascetic.
    • 刘湛之党 - The political faction associated with Liu Zhan.
    • 珠林二十三 - A specific chapter or section in a text, likely a Buddhist or historical text.

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms. [122]

    宋程德度，武昌人，父道惠，广州刺史，度为卫军临川王行参军。时在寻阳，屋有?窠，夜见屋里忽然自明，有一小儿，从窠而出，长可尺余，洁净分明，至度床前曰：“君却后二年，当得长生之道。”儵然而灭。德度甚秘异之。元嘉十七年，随王镇广陵，遇禅师释道恭，因就学禅，甚有解分。到十九年春，其家武昌空斋，忽有殊香芬馥，达于衢路。阖境往观，三日乃歇。珠林二十八 [123]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [123]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 长生之道 - The path to immortality, a common theme in Chinese Daoist and Buddhist texts.
    • 甚秘异之 - Very mysterious and extraordinary.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋 - The Southern Song Dynasty (420-479 AD).
    • 元嘉 - The reign title of Emperor Wen of Song (r. 424-453 AD).
    • 随王 - The Prince of Linchuan, a title given to a member of the royal family.
    • 广陵 - An ancient name for Yangzhou, a city in Jiangsu Province.
    • 禅师 - A Zen Buddhist master.
    • 释道恭 - The name of the Zen master Shi Daogong.
    • 殊香芬馥 - A particularly fragrant aroma, often associated with divine or supernatural occurrences.
    • 阖境 - The entire region or area.
    • 珠林 - A collection of stories and anecdotes, often used to illustrate moral or spiritual lessons.

    Proper Nouns

    • 宋 - The Southern Song Dynasty.
    • 程德度 - Cheng Dedu, a historical figure.
    • 武昌 - Modern-day Wuhan.
    • 道惠 - Cheng Dedu's father, Dao Hui.
    • 广州 - Modern-day Guangzhou.
    • 卫军临川王 - The Prince of Linchuan, a title given to a member of the royal family.
    • 行参军 - An assistant military officer.
    • 释道恭 - The Zen master Shi Daogong.
    • 元嘉 - The reign title of Emperor Wen of Song.
    • 广陵 - Modern-day Yangzhou.
    • 珠林 - A collection of stories and anecdotes.

    Cheng Dedu of the Song Dynasty was from Wuchang. His father, Dao Hui, was the governor of Guangzhou, and Cheng Dedu served as an assistant military officer under the Prince of Linchuan. At the time, he was in Xunyang, where he had a nest in his house. One night, the inside of the house suddenly became illuminated. A small child emerged from the nest, about a foot tall, clean and distinct. The child walked up to Cheng Dedu's bed and said, "In two years, you will attain the way of immortality." The child then suddenly disappeared. Cheng Dedu found this event very mysterious and extraordinary. In the 17th year of the Yuanchang era (440 AD), he followed the Prince of Linchuan to Guangling, where he met the Zen master Shi Daogong. He began to study Zen Buddhism and gained significant understanding. By the spring of the 19th year (442 AD), in a vacant room in his family's home in Wuchang, a particularly fragrant aroma suddenly appeared and spread throughout the streets. People from the entire region came to see the phenomenon, and the fragrance lasted for three days before dissipating. This event is recorded in the 28th chapter of the Jade Forest. [123]

    宋刘琛之，沛郡人也，曾在广陵逢一沙门，谓琛之曰：“君有病气，然当不死，可作一二百钱食，饭饴众僧，则免斯患。”琛之素不信法，心起忿慢，沙门曰：“当加祗信，勿用为怒。”相去二十步，忽不复见。琛之经七日，便病时气，危顿殆死；至九日方昼。如梦非梦，见有五层佛图在其心上，有二十许僧绕塔作礼，因此而寤，即得大利，病乃稍愈。后在京师住，忽有沙门，先不相识，直来入户，曰：“君有法，缘何不精进？”琛之因说先所逢遇，答曰“此宾头卢也。”语已，便去，不知所向。琛之以元嘉十七年夏，于广陵遥见惠汪精舍前，旛盖甚众而无形像；驰往观之，比及到门，奄然都灭。珠林三十六 [124]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [124]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 饭饴众僧 - Feeding the monks, a common act of merit in Buddhism.
    • 法缘 - Karmic connection with the Dharma.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋 - The Southern Song Dynasty (420-479 AD).
    • 沛郡 - An ancient commandery in present-day Anhui Province.
    • 广陵 - An ancient city, now part of Yangzhou, Jiangsu Province.
    • 沙门 - Refers to Buddhist monks.
    • 病气 - A term for illness in classical Chinese.
    • 宾头卢 - Venerable Punahala, one of the Four Heavenly Kings in Buddhism.
    • 元嘉十七年 - The 17th year of the Yuanjia era (440 AD), a period in the Southern Song Dynasty.
    • 惠汪精舍 - A Buddhist monastery.
    • 珠林三十六 - The *Zhu Lin* (Jewel Forest), a collection of Buddhist stories.

    Proper Nouns

    • 刘琛之 - Liu Chenzhi.
    • 沙门 - Buddhist monk.
    • 宾头卢 - Venerable Punahala.
    • 元嘉十七年 - The 17th year of the Yuanjia era (440 AD).
    • 惠汪精舍 - Huaiwang Monastery.
    • 珠林三十六 - The *Zhu Lin* (Jewel Forest).

    Liu Chenzhi, a native of Pei Commandery in the Song Dynasty, once encountered a monk in Guangling. The monk told Chenzhi, "You have an illness, but you will not die. You can prepare a meal worth one or two hundred coins to feed the monks, and then you will be free from this illness." Chenzhi always disbelieved in the Dharma and had a rebellious heart. The monk said, "You should add faith, do not be angry." They were twenty steps apart, and suddenly the monk disappeared.

    Chenzhi suffered from a severe illness for seven days and was on the verge of death. On the ninth day, during the day, he saw a five-story pagoda in his heart, as if in a dream but not quite. There were about twenty monks circling the pagoda in reverence. Because of this, he woke up and gained great benefit, and his illness gradually improved.

    Later, while living in the capital, a monk suddenly appeared, whom he did not recognize, and entered directly. He said, "You have a karmic connection with the Dharma, why are you not diligent?" Chenzhi then recounted his previous encounter, and the monk replied, "This is Venerable Punahala." After speaking, the monk left, and his destination was unknown.

    In the summer of the 17th year of the Yuanjia era, Chenzhi saw from afar at the entrance of the Huaiwang Monastery in Guangling a large number of banners and canopies but no visible images. He rushed to see it, but by the time he reached the gate, everything had vanished. This story is recorded in the Zhu Lin (Jewel Forest), volume thirty-six. [124]

    宋伏万寿，平昌人也。元嘉十九年，在广陵为卫府行参军，假讫返舟，四更初过江。初济之时，长波安流，中江而风起如箭，时又极暗，莫知所向。万寿先奉法勤至，唯一心归命观世音，念无闲息。俄尔与船中数人同睹北岸有光，状如屯火，相与喜曰：“此必是欧阳火也。”回舳趣之，未旦而至。问彼人，皆云。昨夜无然火者。方悟神力，至设斋会。珠林二十七
    　　宋顾迈，吴郡人也，奉法甚谨，为卫府行参军。元嘉十九年，亦自都还广陵，发石头城，便逆湖，朔风至横决，风势未弭，而舟人务进，既至中江，波浪方壮，迈单船孤征，忧危无计，诵观世音经，得十许遍，风势渐歇，浪亦稍小。既而中流屡闻奇香芬馥不歇，迈心独嘉，故归诵不辍，遂以安济。珠林二十七 [125]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [125]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 元嘉十九年 - Refers to the 19th year of the reign of Emperor Wu of Liu Song (r. 424-453), a specific time marker.
    • 在广陵为卫府行参军 - Indicates the occupation and location of the subject, meaning 'serving as an assistant military officer in the guard department in Guangling.'
    • 假讫返舟 - Means 'after the leave of absence ended, returned by boat.'
    • 四更初过江 - A time reference, meaning 'crossed the river at the beginning of the fourth watch of the night.'
    • 长波安流 - Describes a calm and smooth river flow.
    • 中江而风起如箭 - Means 'a strong wind suddenly arose in the middle of the river, like an arrow.'
    • 时又极暗 - Means 'the night was extremely dark.'
    • 莫知所向 - Means 'no one knew where to go.'
    • 奉法勤至 - Means 'diligently observed the precepts.'
    • 一心归命观世音 - Means 'devoted his mind entirely to the Bodhisattva Guanyin.'
    • 念无闲息 - Means 'without a moment's interruption in his recitation.'
    • 俄尔与船中数人同睹北岸有光 - Means 'soon, he and several people on the boat saw a light on the northern bank.'
    • 状如屯火 - Means 'it looked like a campfire.'
    • 回舳趣之 - Means 'turned the boat towards it.'
    • 未旦而至 - Means 'arrived before dawn.'
    • 昨夜无然火者 - Means 'there was no one who lit a fire last night.'
    • 方悟神力 - Means 'only then did they realize the power of the divine.'
    • 设斋会 - Means 'held a vegetarian feast.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋 - Refers to the Liu Song dynasty, one of the Southern Dynasties.
    • 伏万寿 - The name of the first person mentioned, a devout Buddhist.
    • 平昌 - The place of origin of the first person.
    • 广陵 - An ancient name for Yangzhou, a city in present-day Jiangsu Province.
    • 卫府行参军 - A military administrative position.
    • 观世音 - The Bodhisattva Guanyin, a central figure in Mahayana Buddhism known for compassion and salvation.
    • 珠林二十七 - A reference to the 27th chapter of the *Zhu Lin* (Jewel Forest), a collection of Buddhist stories and legends.

    Proper Nouns

    • 伏万寿 - The name of the first person.
    • 平昌 - The place of origin.
    • 广陵 - The location.
    • 观世音 - The name of the Bodhisattva.
    • 珠林二十七 - The source of the story.

    During the 19th year of the reign of Emperor Wu of Liu Song, Fu Wanshou, a man from Pingchang, was serving as an assistant military officer in the guard department in Guangling. After his leave of absence ended, he returned by boat, crossing the river at the beginning of the fourth watch of the night. At the start of the crossing, the river was calm and smooth, but a strong wind suddenly arose in the middle of the river, like an arrow. The night was extremely dark, and no one knew where to go. Fu Wanshou had been diligently observing the precepts and devoted his mind entirely to the Bodhisattva Guanyin, without a moment's interruption in his recitation. Soon, he and several people on the boat saw a light on the northern bank, which looked like a campfire. They were delighted and said, "This must be the fire of Ouyang!" They turned the boat towards it and arrived before dawn. When they asked the people there, they all said that no one had lit a fire the previous night. Only then did they realize the power of the divine, and they held a vegetarian feast. This story is recorded in the 27th chapter of the Zhu Lin (Jewel Forest).

    During the 19th year of the reign of Emperor Wu of Liu Song, Gu Mai, a man from Wu County, was also serving as an assistant military officer in the guard department. He returned from the capital to Guangling, departing from Shitoucheng and heading upstream along the lake. A northerly wind suddenly arose, causing the river to surge. The wind did not subside, and the boatmen were eager to proceed. When they reached the middle of the river, the waves were becoming increasingly strong. Gu Mai was traveling alone in a single boat, feeling worried and without a plan. He began to recite the Sutra of the Bodhisattva Guanyin, completing it about ten times. The wind gradually calmed, and the waves also became smaller. As they continued down the river, they frequently smelled a strange fragrance that did not dissipate. Gu Mai found this fragrance pleasing and continued to recite the sutra without stopping. Thus, he was able to cross the river safely. This story is also recorded in the 27th chapter of the Zhu Lin (Jewel Forest). [125]

    秦沙门释道冏，乡里氏族，已载前记，秦姚弘始十八年，师道懿遣至河南霍山采钟乳，与同学道朗等四人共行。持炬探穴，入且三里，遇一深流，横木而过。冏最先济，后辈坠木而死，时火又灭，冥然昏闇。冏生念已尽，恸哭而已。犹故一心呼观世音，誓愿若蒙出路，供百人会，表报威神。经一宿而见小光炯然，状若荧火，儵忽之间，穴中尽明。于是见路，得出岩下，由此信悟弥深，屡睹灵异。元嘉十九年，临川康王作镇广陵，请冏供养。其年九月，于西斋中作十日观世音斋，已得九日，夜四更尽，众僧皆眠，冏起礼拜还欲坐禅，忽见四壁有无数沙门，悉半身出见，一佛螺髻，分明了了；有一长人，着平上帻，笺布裤褶，手把长刀，貌极雄异，捻香授道冏，道冏时不肯受，壁中沙门语云：“冏公可为受香，以覆护主人。”俄而霍然无所复见。当尔之时，都不见众会诸僧，唯睹所置释迦文行像而已。珠林六十五 [126]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [126]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 持炬探穴 - Holding a torch to explore a cave.
    • 遇一深流，横木而过 - Encountered a deep stream and crossed it by placing a log across.
    • 冥然昏闇 - A vivid description of darkness.
    • 生念已尽，恸哭而已 - All hope was lost, nothing but weeping.
    • 一心呼观世音 - Called out with all one's heart to Avalokiteshvara.
    • 誓愿若蒙出路，供百人会，表报威神 - Vowed if granted a way out, to offer a meal for a hundred people, to express gratitude to the Bodhisattva's power.
    • 经一宿而见小光炯然 - After one night, saw a faint but clear light.
    • 状若荧火，儵忽之间，穴中尽明 - Shaped like a firefly, in an instant, the entire cave was illuminated.
    • 屡睹灵异 - Frequently saw miraculous phenomena.
    • 众僧皆眠，冏起礼拜还欲坐禅 - The other monks were asleep, Monk Dao Jiong got up to pay respects and was about to resume meditation.
    • 忽见四壁有无数沙门，悉半身出见 - Suddenly saw countless monks on the walls, all appearing with their upper bodies visible.
    • 一佛螺髻，分明了了 - A Buddha with a coiled topknot, clearly visible.
    • 有一长人，着平上帻，笺布裤褶，手把长刀，貌极雄异 - A tall man, wearing a flat-topped hat, robe and trousers, holding a long sword, with an extremely fierce appearance.
    • 捻香授道冏，道冏时不肯受 - Offering incense to Monk Dao Jiong, but Monk Dao Jiong refused to accept it.
    • 壁中沙门语云 - The monks on the wall said.
    • 冏公可为受香，以覆护主人 - Master Jiong, you should accept the incense, to protect the host.
    • 俄而霍然无所复见 - Soon, suddenly, no longer saw anything.
    • 当尔之时，都不见众会诸僧，唯睹所置释迦文行像而已 - At that moment, could not see any of the gathered monks, only saw the statue of Shakyamuni Buddha that was placed there.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 秦沙门释道冏 - A Buddhist monk from the Later Qin dynasty.
    • 姚弘始十八年 - The eighteenth year of Emperor Yao Xing's reign (396 CE).
    • 观世音 - The Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara, a central figure in Mahayana Buddhism.
    • 元嘉十九年 - The nineteenth year of Emperor Wen of Liu Song's reign (442 CE).
    • 临川康王作镇广陵 - A prince who served as governor of Guangling (modern Yangzhou).
    • 西斋中作十日观世音斋 - A fasting observance dedicated to Avalokiteshvara.
    • 释迦文行像 - A statue of Shakyamuni Buddha.
    • 珠林六十五 - The sixty-fifth volume of the 'Jewel Forest' (珠林), a collection of Buddhist stories.

    A Monk from the Qin Region Named Dao Jiong

    The hometown and clan of Monk Dao Jiong are already recorded in previous accounts. In the eighteenth year of Emperor Yao Xing's reign (396 CE), Master Dao Yi sent him to Mount Huo in Henan to collect cave-dwelling snails. He traveled with three fellow students, including Dao Lang. Holding a torch, they explored a cave for about three miles, where they encountered a deep stream and crossed it by placing a log across. Monk Dao Jiong was the first to cross, but his companions fell off the log and drowned. At that moment, the fire went out, plunging them into total darkness. Dao Jiong felt all hope was lost and wept bitterly. Still, he called out with all his heart to Avalokiteshvara, vowing that if he were granted a way out, he would offer a meal for a hundred people to express gratitude to the Bodhisattva's power. After one night, he saw a faint but clear light, shaped like a firefly. In an instant, the entire cave was illuminated. He then saw a path and emerged from the cave. From this experience, his faith and understanding deepened, and he frequently witnessed miraculous phenomena. In the nineteenth year of Emperor Wen's reign (442 CE), Prince of Linchuan, serving as governor of Guangling (modern Yangzhou), invited Dao Jiong to reside and provide for him. In the ninth month of that year, in the western study, a ten-day fasting observance dedicated to Avalokiteshvara was held. After nine days, at the fourth watch of the night, all the monks were asleep, but Dao Jiong got up to pay respects and was about to resume meditation. Suddenly, he saw countless monks on the walls, all appearing with their upper bodies visible. Among them was a Buddha with a coiled topknot, clearly visible. There was also a tall man, wearing a flat-topped hat, a robe, and trousers, holding a long sword, with an extremely fierce appearance. He offered incense to Dao Jiong, but the monk refused to accept it. The monks on the wall said, "Master Jiong, you should accept the incense to protect the host." Soon, the vision disappeared, and at that moment, Dao Jiong could not see any of the gathered monks, only the statue of Shakyamuni Buddha that was placed there. This account is found in the sixty-fifth volume of the "Jewel Forest" (珠林). [126]

    宋尼释昙辉，蜀郡成都人也，本姓青阳，名白玉，年七岁，便乐坐禅。每坐，辄得境界，意未自了，亦谓是梦耳。曾与姊共寝，夜中入定，姊于屏风角得之，身如木石，亦无气息；姊大惊怪，唤告家人，互共抱扶，至晓不觉。奔问巫觋，皆言鬼神所凭。至年十一，有外国禅师?良耶舍者来入蜀，辉请咨所见，耶舍者以辉禅既有分，欲劝化令出家。时辉将嫁，已有定日，法育未展，闻说，其家潜迎还寺，家既知，将逼嫁之；辉遂不肯行，深立言誓：“若我道心不果，遂被限逼者，便当投火饲虎，弃除秽形，愿十方诸佛证见至心。刺史甄法崇，信尚正法，闻辉志业，迎与相见。并召纲佐及有怀沙门互加难问，辉敷演无屈，坐者叹之。崇乃许离夫家，听其入道。元嘉十九年，临川康王延致广陵。珠林二十二 [127]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [127]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 便乐坐禅 - Enjoyed sitting in meditation; describes her early inclination towards meditation.
    • 辄得境界 - Attained a state of mind; refers to the meditative states she experienced.
    • 意未自了 - Thoughts were not fully clear; expresses her uncertainty about the nature of her experiences.
    • 亦谓是梦耳 - Also considered it to be a dream; further expresses her uncertainty.
    • 身如木石 - Body was like wood and stone; describes her meditative state.
    • 亦无气息 - Had no breath; further describes her meditative state.
    • 投火饲虎 - Vow of extreme dedication; indicates a willingness to sacrifice oneself for spiritual goals.
    • 弃除秽形 - Discard impure form; part of her vow to renounce worldly attachments.
