    • 愿十方诸佛证见至心 - Wish that the Buddhas of the ten directions witness my sincere heart; expresses her deep spiritual commitment.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋 - The Song Dynasty (960-1279 AD); historical context.
    • 蜀郡 - Sichuan province; historically significant region in China.
    • 成都 - Capital city of Sichuan; known for its cultural and economic importance.
    • 坐禅 - Form of Buddhist meditation; culturally significant practice.
    • 境界 - Meditative state or realm; spiritual concept.
    • 入定 - Entering a deep meditative state; key Buddhist practice.
    • 巫觋 - Sorcerers or shamans; cultural practitioners in ancient China.
    • 外国禅师 - Foreign monks, often from India or other regions; cultural exchange in Buddhism.
    • 耶舍 - Common name for foreign monks; possibly refers to a specific individual.
    • 法育 - Legal or formal marriage arrangements; cultural and legal context.
    • 十方诸佛 - Buddhas of the ten directions; common Buddhist concept.
    • 刺史 - Local governor; administrative role in ancient China.
    • 甄法崇 - Specific governor who supported the Dharma; historical figure.
    • 临川康王 - Noble title, possibly referring to a specific historical figure; cultural and political context.
    • 广陵 - City in present-day Jiangsu province; known for its cultural significance.

    Proper Nouns

    • 宋尼释昙辉 - Buddhist nun Tanhui from the Song Dynasty; proper noun.
    • 蜀郡 - Sichuan province; geographical location.
    • 成都 - Chengdu, the capital of Sichuan; geographical location.
    • 青阳 - Her original surname; proper noun.
    • 白玉 - Her given name; proper noun.
    • 耶舍 - Foreign monk; proper noun.
    • 甄法崇 - Governor Zhen Fachong; proper noun.
    • 临川康王 - King Kang of Linchuan; proper noun.
    • 广陵 - Guangling, a city in present-day Jiangsu province; geographical location.

    Buddhist Nun Tanhui from the Song Dynasty was from Chengdu, Sichuan, originally surnamed Qingyang and named Baiyu. At the age of seven, she enjoyed sitting in meditation. Whenever she meditated, she would attain a state of mind, but her thoughts were not fully clear, and she also considered it to be a dream. Once she slept with her sister, and at night she entered a deep meditative state. Her sister found her at the corner of the screen, and her body was like wood and stone, with no breath. Her sister was greatly surprised and alarmed, and called her family. They all held and supported her, until dawn when she came to. They ran to consult sorcerers, who all said it was due to spirits. By the age of eleven, a foreign monk named Yeshe came to Sichuan. Tanhui asked for his opinion on what she saw. Yeshe, seeing that she had a natural aptitude for meditation, wanted to persuade her to become a nun. At the time, Tanhui was about to marry, and the wedding date was already set. Her legal marriage was not yet arranged, and upon hearing this, her family secretly brought her back to the temple. When the family found out, they tried to force her into marriage. Tanhui refused to go and made a deep vow: "If my spiritual heart is not fulfilled and I am forced to marry, I will throw myself into the fire to feed tigers, discard my impure form, and wish that the Buddhas of the ten directions witness my sincere heart." Governor Zhen Fachong, who believed and upheld the true Dharma, heard of Tanhui's aspirations and welcomed and met with her. He also summoned his officials and monks who had aspirations to question her. Tanhui spoke eloquently and without being defeated, and those present were impressed. Zhen then allowed her to leave her fiancé's family and enter the path of the Dharma. In the 19th year of the Yuanjia era, King Kang of Linchuan invited her to Guangling. [127]

    时宋淮南赵习，元嘉二十年为卫军府佐，疾病经时，忧必不济，恒至心归佛。夜梦一人，形貌秀异，若神人者，自屋梁上，以小裹物及剃刀授习，云：“服此药，用此刀，病必即愈。”习既惊觉，果得刀药焉，登即服药，疾除出家，名僧秀，年逾八十乃亡。珠林二十二
    　　宋沙门释慧全，凉州禅师也，开训教授，门徒五百。有一弟子，性颇粗暴，全常不齿。后忽自云：得三道果，全以其无行，永不信许。全后有疾，此弟子夜来问讯时，户犹闭如故，全颇惊异，欲复验之。乃语明夕更来。因密塞窗户。加以重关。弟子中宵而至，迳到床前，谓全曰：“阇黎可见信来。”因曰：“阇黎过世，当生婆罗门家。 [128]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [128]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 時宋淮南趙習 - Refers to Zhao Xi, a person from the region of Huainan during the Song dynasty.
    • 元嘉二十年 - A specific year in the reign of Emperor Wen of Song (423 AD).
    • 衛軍府佐 - An official position, likely an assistant or subordinate in a military office.
    • 疾病經時 - Literally 'sickness for a long time,' indicating prolonged illness.
    • 憂必不濟 - Expresses deep concern that the illness might not be cured.
    • 恒至心歸佛 - Means 'always sincerely turned to Buddhism,' indicating a devout commitment to the religion.
    • 夜夢一人 - Describes a dream encounter with a person.
    • 形貌秀異 - Means 'appearance exceptionally beautiful or noble,' suggesting a divine or supernatural figure.
    • 若神人者 - Describes someone as resembling a god or deity.
    • 自屋梁上 - Indicates that the figure appeared from the ceiling.
    • 以小裹物及剃刀授習 - Means 'gave a small wrapped object and a razor to Xi,' suggesting a gift or medicine.
    • 服此藥，用此刀，病必即愈 - A promise that taking the medicine and using the razor will cure the illness immediately.
    • 登即服藥，疾除出家 - Means 'immediately took the medicine, the disease was cured, and he left home to become a monk.'
    • 名僧秀 - Refers to the monk's name, 'Xiu.'
    • 年逾八十乃亡 - Means 'he lived past eighty years old before passing away.'
    • 珠林二十二 - Likely refers to a specific chapter or section in a Buddhist text, possibly 'Jewel Forest' (珠林), a collection of Buddhist stories.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋 - Refers to the Southern Song dynasty (420–479 AD), a period in Chinese history.
    • 元嘉二十年 - A specific year in the reign of Emperor Wen of Song (423 AD).
    • 衛軍府佐 - An official position in the military administration, indicating the social status of Zhao Xi.
    • 佛教 - Refers to Buddhism, which was widely practiced in China during this period.
    • 出家 - Means 'to leave home and become a monk,' a significant cultural and religious practice in Chinese Buddhism.
    • 珠林 - A Buddhist text, possibly a collection of stories or teachings, indicating the source of this account.

    Proper Nouns

    • 趙習 - The name of the protagonist, Zhao Xi.
    • 元嘉二十年 - A specific year in the reign of Emperor Wen of Song.
    • 衛軍府佐 - An official position.
    • 珠林 - A Buddhist text.

    During the reign of Emperor Wen of Song, in the year 423 AD, Zhao Xi, a native of Huainan, served as an assistant in the military office. He suffered from a prolonged illness, deeply concerned that it would not be cured. He always sincerely turned to Buddhism in his heart. One night, he dreamed of a person with an exceptionally beautiful and noble appearance, resembling a deity, who appeared from the ceiling and gave him a small wrapped object and a razor. The figure said, "Take this medicine and use this razor, and your illness will surely be cured immediately." Zhao Xi was startled awake and indeed found the razor and medicine. He immediately took the medicine, and his illness was cured. He then left home to become a monk, named Xiu, and lived past eighty years old before passing away. This story is recorded in the "Jewel Forest" chapter twenty-two.

    In the Song dynasty, there was a monk named Shihuiquan, a Zen master from Liangzhou, who taught and instructed five hundred disciples. One of his disciples had a rather rough and violent temperament, and Shihuiquan often disdained him. Later, the disciple suddenly claimed to have attained the three fruits of enlightenment. Shihuiquan, however, did not believe him due to his lack of virtuous conduct and never accepted his claim. When Shihuiquan later fell ill, the disciple came to visit him at night. The door was still closed as usual, and Shihuiquan was quite surprised and wanted to test him again. He told the disciple to come back the next evening. Shihuiquan then secretly sealed the windows and doors. That night, the disciple arrived in the middle of the night and went directly to the bed, saying to Shihuiquan, "Venerable one, you can believe me now." He then said, "After your passing, you will be reborn into a Brahmin family." [128]

    ”全曰：“我坐禅积业，岂方生彼？”弟子云：“阇黎信道不笃，兼外学未绝，虽有福业，不能超诣；若作一胜会，得饭一圣人，可成道果耳。”全于是设会。弟子又曰：“可以僧伽黎布施，若有须者，勿择长幼。”及会讫施衣，有一沙弥，就全求衣，全谓是其弟子，全云：“吾欲拟奉圣僧，那得与汝？”回忆前言不得择人，便以欢施。他日见此沙弥，问云：“先与汝衣着不大耶？”沙弥曰：“非徒不得衣，亦有缘事，愧不预会。”全方悟先沙弥者，圣所化也。弟子久乃过世，过世之时，无复余异，唯冢四边，时有白光。全元嘉二十年犹存，居在酒泉。珠林十九 [129]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [129]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 全曰 - Formal expression meaning 'Quan said.'
    • 坐禅积业 - Refers to the practice of sitting in meditation to accumulate merit or virtue.
    • 方生彼 - Ambiguous phrase, can be interpreted as 'be born there' or 'arise there.'
    • 阇黎信道不笃 - '阇黎' (Shilaji) is a term for a Buddhist monk or teacher, and '信道不笃' means 'does not firmly believe in the path.'
    • 外学未绝 - Means 'external learning has not been completely abandoned,' referring to studies outside of Buddhist doctrine.
    • 虽有福业，不能超诣 - '虽有福业' means 'even though there is merit or virtue,' and '不能超诣' means 'cannot transcend or reach higher realms.'
    • 作一胜会，得饭一圣人 - '作一胜会' means 'hold a grand assembly,' and '得饭一圣人' means 'offer food to a sage or enlightened being.'
    • 成道果耳 - Means 'to attain enlightenment or fruit of the path.'
    • 设会 - Means 'to hold a gathering or assembly.'
    • 僧伽黎布施 - '僧伽黎' (Sanghali) refers to a type of robe worn by Buddhist monks, and '布施' means 'to make offerings.'
    • 可以僧伽黎布施，若有须者，勿择长幼 - '可以僧伽黎布施' means 'can be offered as a Sanghali robe,' and '若有须者，勿择长幼' means 'if anyone needs it, do not choose based on age.'
    • 会讫施衣 - '会讫' means 'after the gathering,' and '施衣' means 'to give out clothes.'
    • 就全求衣 - Means 'asked Quan for a robe.'
    • 吾欲拟奉圣僧，那得与汝 - '吾欲拟奉圣僧' means 'I intended to offer it to a holy monk,' and '那得与汝' means 'how could I give it to you?'
    • 回忆前言不得择人，便以欢施 - '回忆前言不得择人' means 'recalling my previous words that I should not choose based on people,' and '便以欢施' means 'so I happily gave it away.'
    • 他日见此沙弥，问云 - '他日见此沙弥' means 'on another day, he saw this novice monk,' and '问云' means 'asked, saying.'
    • 先与汝衣着不大耶 - Means 'Wasn't the robe you received not too big?'
    • 非徒不得衣，亦有缘事，愧不预会 - '非徒不得衣' means 'not only did I not receive a robe,' and '亦有缘事，愧不预会' means 'there were also some reasons, and I am ashamed that I was not invited to the gathering.'
    • 全方悟先沙弥者，圣所化也 - '全方悟' means 'Quan finally realized,' '先沙弥者' means 'the previous novice monk,' and '圣所化也' means 'was transformed by a sage.'
    • 弟子久乃过世，过世之时，无复余异，唯冢四边，时有白光 - '弟子久乃过世' means 'the disciple passed away after a long time,' '过世之时' means 'at the time of his passing,' '无复余异' means 'there were no other unusual phenomena,' and '唯冢四边，时有白光' means 'only around the grave, there was sometimes a white light.'
    • 全元嘉二十年犹存，居在酒泉 - '全元嘉二十年' means 'Quan in the 20th year of the Yuanjia era,' '犹存' means 'still alive,' and '居在酒泉' means 'resided in Jiuquan.'
    • 珠林十九 - Refers to the 19th chapter of the *Zhu Lin* (Jewel Grove), a collection of Buddhist stories.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 坐禅 - A central practice in Buddhism involving meditation to achieve enlightenment.
    • 积业 - Refers to accumulating merit or virtue through good deeds and practices.
    • 方生彼 - The phrase suggests a spiritual or metaphysical destination, possibly a higher realm or state of being.
    • 阇黎 - A term for a Buddhist monk or teacher, often used in a respectful context.
    • 信道不笃 - Indicates a lack of strong faith or commitment to the Buddhist path.
    • 外学 - Refers to studies or knowledge outside of Buddhist doctrine, such as Confucianism, Daoism, or secular learning.
    • 福业 - Merit or virtue accumulated through good actions.
    • 超诣 - To transcend or reach higher spiritual realms.
    • 胜会 - A grand assembly or gathering, often held for religious or spiritual purposes.
    • 圣人 - An enlightened being or sage, often a figure of great wisdom and virtue.
    • 僧伽黎 - A type of robe worn by Buddhist monks, symbolizing their renunciation of worldly life.
    • 布施 - The act of making offerings or donations, often to support religious institutions or individuals.
    • 沙弥 - A novice monk, typically a young boy who has entered a monastery.
    • 元嘉 - The reign title of Emperor Wen of Liu Song (宋文帝), a period in Chinese history.
    • 酒泉 - A city in Gansu Province, historically known for its wine production and strategic importance.

    Proper Nouns

    • 全 - The name of the main character in the story.
    • 阇黎 - A term for a Buddhist monk or teacher.
    • 元嘉 - The reign title of Emperor Wen of Liu Song.
    • 酒泉 - A city in Gansu Province.
    • 珠林 - The title of the collection of Buddhist stories, specifically the 19th chapter.

    Quan said, "I practice meditation and accumulate merit; how could I not be reborn in a higher realm?" The disciple replied, "Venerable one, your faith in the path is not firm, and you have not completely abandoned external studies. Even though you have merit, you cannot transcend to higher realms. If you hold a grand assembly and offer food to a sage, you can attain enlightenment." Quan then held the assembly. The disciple further advised, "You can offer the Sanghali robes as alms; if anyone needs one, do not choose based on age." After the assembly, when distributing the robes, a novice monk approached Quan and asked for one. Quan thought he was one of his disciples and said, "I intended to offer it to a holy monk; how could I give it to you?" Recalling his earlier words that he should not choose based on people, Quan happily gave the robe away. On another day, Quan saw this novice monk and asked, "Wasn't the robe you received not too big?" The novice monk replied, "Not only did I not receive a robe, but there were also some reasons, and I am ashamed that I was not invited to the gathering." Quan finally realized that the previous novice monk was a manifestation of a sage. The disciple passed away after a long time; at the time of his passing, there were no other unusual phenomena, only around the grave, there was sometimes a white light. Quan was still alive in the 20th year of the Yuanjia era, residing in Jiuquan. This is recorded in the 19th chapter of the Zhu Lin. [129]

    宋王胡者，长安人也。叔死数载，元嘉二十三年，忽见形还家，责胡以修谨有阙，家事不理，罚胡五杖。傍人及邻里，并闻其语及杖声，又见杖瘢迹，而不睹其形；唯胡犹得亲接。叔谓胡曰：“吾不应死神道须吾算诸鬼录，今大从吏兵，恐惊损墟里，故不将进耳。”胡亦大见众鬼纷闹若屯外。俄然叔辞去，曰：“吾来年七月七日，当复暂还，欲将汝行，游历幽途，使知罪福之报也。不须费设，若意不已，止可茶来耳。”至期果还，语胡家人云：“吾今将胡游观，毕，当使还，不足忧也。”胡即顿卧床上，泯然如尽。叔于是将胡遍观群山，备睹鬼怪，末至嵩高山。诸鬼遇胡，并有馔设，余族味不异世中，唯姜甚脆美。 [130]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [130]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 忽见形还家 - Literally 'suddenly appeared in form and returned home,' meaning the spirit of the deceased suddenly manifested itself in the physical world.
    • 责胡以修谨有阙 - Means 'reproached Hu for lacking in carefulness and propriety,' indicating a critique of Hu's behavior or conduct.
    • 家事不理 - Means 'household affairs were not managed well,' referring to the neglect of domestic responsibilities.
    • 罚胡五杖 - Means 'punished Hu with five strokes,' a form of corporal punishment.
    • 傍人及邻里，并闻其语及杖声 - Means 'people around and neighbors all heard his words and the sound of the strokes,' indicating widespread witness to the event.
    • 又见杖瘢迹 - Means 'and also saw the marks of the strokes,' referring to visible signs of the punishment.
    • 唯胡犹得亲接 - Means 'only Hu could still touch him,' suggesting a special relationship or connection between Hu and the spirit.
    • 吾不应死神道须吾算诸鬼录 - Means 'I should not have died, but the divine way required me to calculate the records of the ghosts,' indicating a supernatural explanation for the spirit's presence.
    • 今大从吏兵 - Means 'now I am accompanied by many officials and soldiers,' suggesting a grand entourage.
    • 恐惊损墟里 - Means 'fearing to frighten or harm the village,' indicating concern for the well-being of the living.
    • 故不将进耳 - Means 'so I did not bring them in,' explaining why the spirit did not enter the house.
    • 吾来年七月七日，当复暂还 - Means 'I will return again next year on the seventh day of the seventh month,' indicating a specific date for the spirit's return.
    • 欲将汝行，游历幽途 - Means 'I want to take you along, to tour the underworld,' suggesting a journey into the afterlife.
    • 使知罪福之报也 - Means 'to make you understand the retribution of sin and virtue,' indicating a moral lesson.
    • 不须费设 - Means 'no need to spend money on preparations,' suggesting simplicity.
    • 若意不已，止可茶来耳 - Means 'if you wish, just bring tea,' indicating a minimal requirement for the visit.
    • 胡即顿卧床上，泯然如尽 - Means 'Hu immediately lay down on the bed, as if he had passed away,' describing a state of unconsciousness.
    • 叔于是将胡遍观群山 - Means 'Uncle then took Hu to see all the mountains,' indicating a journey through various landscapes.
    • 备睹鬼怪 - Means 'to see all kinds of ghosts and monsters,' describing the supernatural sights.
    • 末至嵩高山 - Means 'finally reaching Mount Song,' indicating a specific destination.
    • 诸鬼遇胡，并有馔设 - Means 'various ghosts met Hu, and there were preparations for food,' suggesting hospitality.
    • 余族味不异世中 - Means 'the rest of the flavors were not different from those in the world,' indicating that the food was familiar.
    • 唯姜甚脆美 - Means 'only ginger was very crisp and delicious,' highlighting a particular detail about the food.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋王胡者，长安人也 - Refers to a person named Hu from Chang'an (present-day Xi'an), a major city in ancient China.
    • 元嘉二十三年 - Refers to the 23rd year of the Yuanjia era during the Southern Song Dynasty (447 AD).
    • 神道 - Refers to the divine way or path, often associated with the supernatural or spiritual realm.
    • 鬼录 - Refers to the records of the dead, a concept in Chinese mythology.
    • 七月七日 - Refers to the seventh day of the seventh lunar month, a significant date in Chinese folklore and mythology.
    • 嵩高山 - Refers to Mount Song, one of the Five Great Mountains of China, known for its spiritual and cultural significance.
    • 诸鬼 - Refers to various spirits or ghosts, a common theme in Chinese mythology and folklore.
    • 馔设 - Refers to preparations for food, indicating hospitality in the afterlife.
    • 姜 - Refers to ginger, a common spice in Chinese cuisine, noted here for its crispness and deliciousness.

    Proper Nouns

    • 宋王胡 - The name of the protagonist, Hu.
    • 长安 - The ancient name for present-day Xi'an, a major city in ancient China.
    • 元嘉二十三年 - The 23rd year of the Yuanjia era during the Southern Song Dynasty (447 AD).
    • 嵩高山 - The name of Mount Song, one of the Five Great Mountains of China.
    • 姜 - The name of the spice ginger, noted for its crispness and deliciousness.

    Hu of Song, a native of Chang'an, had a brother who died several years ago. In the 23rd year of the Yuanjia era (447 AD), the spirit of the deceased brother suddenly appeared in the form of a living person and returned home. He reproached Hu for lacking in carefulness and propriety, and for neglecting household affairs, and punished him with five strokes. People around and neighbors all heard his words and the sound of the strokes, and also saw the marks of the strokes, but they did not see his form; only Hu could still touch him. The spirit said to Hu, "I should not have died, but the divine way required me to calculate the records of the ghosts. Now I am accompanied by many officials and soldiers, fearing to frighten or harm the village, so I did not bring them in." Hu also saw many ghosts gathered outside, causing a commotion. Soon after, the spirit said goodbye and left, saying, "I will return again next year on the seventh day of the seventh month, and I want to take you along to tour the underworld, to make you understand the retribution of sin and virtue. No need to spend money on preparations; if you wish, just bring tea." On the appointed day, he indeed returned and told Hu's family, "I will now take Hu to see the sights, and when we are done, I will send him back; there is no need to worry." Hu immediately lay down on the bed, as if he had passed away. The spirit then took Hu to see all the mountains, and they saw all kinds of ghosts and monsters. Finally, they reached Mount Song. Various ghosts met Hu, and there were preparations for food. The rest of the flavors were not different from those in the world, but only the ginger was very crisp and delicious. [130]

    胡欲怀将还，左右人笑胡云：“止可此食，不得将还也。”胡末见一处，屋宇华旷，帐筵精整，有二少僧居焉。胡造之，二僧为设杂果槟榔等。胡游历久之，备见罪福苦乐之报，乃辞归。叔谓胡曰：“汝既已知善之可修，何宜在家？白足阿练，戒行精高，可师事也。”长安道人足白，故时人谓为白足阿练也，甚为魏虏所敬，虏主主事为师。胡既奉此谏于是寺中，遂见嵩山上年少僧者游学众中。胡大惊，与叙乖阔，问何时来？二僧答云：“贫道本住此寺，往日不忆，与君相识。”胡复说嵩高之遇，此僧云：“君谬耳，岂有此耶？”至明日，二僧无何而去。胡乃具告诸沙门，叙说往日嵩山所见；众咸惊怪，即追求二僧，不知所在，乃悟其神人焉。元嘉末，有长安僧释昙爽来游江南，具说如此也。珠林六 [131]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [131]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 胡欲怀将还 - Hu wants to return home with something in mind.
    • 止可此食，不得将还也 - A humorous or dismissive statement, implying that Hu cannot take it with him.
    • 屋宇华旷 - Describes a grand and spacious house, with elegance and splendor.
    • 帐筵精整 - Refers to finely arranged or decorated furnishings, meticulously prepared.
    • 备见罪福苦乐之报 - To fully witness the rewards and punishments of sin and virtue, as well as suffering and joy.
    • 叔谓胡曰 - The uncle said to Hu, introducing a direct speech.
    • 白足阿练 - A monk named Alayan, whose feet were bare, hence the nickname 'barefoot'.
    • 戒行精高 - Strict in precepts and high in moral conduct, describing the monk's discipline and virtue.
    • 可师事也 - Can be served as a teacher, indicating respect and admiration.
    • 长安道人足白 - A monk from Chang'an (modern Xi'an) who was known for his bare feet.
    • 甚为魏虏所敬 - Highly respected by the Wei barbarians, indicating the monk's status.
    • 虏主主事为师 - The barbarian king regarded him as a teacher, emphasizing the monk's authority.
    • 胡既奉此谏于是寺中 - Hu then followed this advice and stayed in the temple.
    • 嵩山上年少僧者游学众中 - The young monk from Mount Song who was among the students.
    • 与叙乖阔 - To talk about how long it has been, indicating a conversation about time apart.
    • 二僧答云 - The two monks replied, introducing their response.
    • 贫道本住此寺 - I originally lived in this temple, indicating the monks' past residence.
    • 往日不忆，与君相识 - I don't remember meeting you in the past, indicating a lack of recollection.
    • 嵩高之遇 - Refers to the encounter with the young monk from Mount Song.
    • 君谬耳，岂有此耶 - You are mistaken; how could there be such a thing?
    • 无何而去 - They left without further ado, indicating a sudden departure.
    • 乃悟其神人焉 - And then realized they were divine beings, indicating a supernatural realization.
    • 元嘉末 - The end of the Yuanjia era (424-453), a period in the Liu Song dynasty.
    • 长安僧释昙爽来游江南 - The monk from Chang'an named Shitan came to tour the south.
    • 珠林六 - Refers to the sixth chapter of the 'Julei', a Buddhist text.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 胡 - Likely refers to a person named Hu, possibly a layperson or a monk.
    • 叔 - Refers to Hu's uncle, who is advising him.
    • 白足阿练 - A specific monk known for his bare feet and high moral standing.
    • 魏虏 - Refers to the people of the Northern Wei dynasty, a non-Han Chinese state.
    • 长安 - The capital of the Northern Wei dynasty, modern-day Xi'an.
    • 嵩山 - A famous mountain in Henan province, known for its Buddhist and Taoist temples.
    • 元嘉 - The reign era of Emperor Wen of the Liu Song dynasty (424-453).
    • 珠林六 - Refers to the sixth chapter of the 'Julei', a Buddhist text.

    Proper Nouns

    • 胡 - The name of the subject, likely a person or monk.
    • 叔 - The name of Hu's uncle, who is advising him.
    • 白足阿练 - The name of a monk known for his bare feet and high moral standing.
    • 魏虏 - Refers to the people of the Northern Wei dynasty.
    • 长安 - The capital of the Northern Wei dynasty, modern-day Xi'an.
    • 嵩山 - A famous mountain in Henan province, known for its Buddhist and Taoist temples.
    • 元嘉 - The reign era of Emperor Wen of the Liu Song dynasty (424-453).
    • 珠林六 - Refers to the sixth chapter of the 'Julei', a Buddhist text.

    Hu wished to return home with something in mind, but those around him laughed at him and said, "You can only eat here; you cannot take it with you." Hu then came upon a place where the house was grand and spacious, the furnishings were finely arranged, and two young monks lived there. Hu visited them, and the monks prepared a variety of fruits and betel nuts for him. After wandering for a long time, Hu fully witnessed the rewards and punishments of sin and virtue, as well as the sufferings and joys of life, and then he bade farewell and returned home. His uncle said to Hu, "Since you have now come to know that virtue can be cultivated, why should you remain at home? Whitefoot Alayan is strict in precepts and high in moral conduct; you can serve him as a teacher." The monk from Chang'an, whose feet were bare, was thus called Whitefoot Alayan, and he was highly respected by the Wei barbarians, who regarded him as a teacher. Hu then followed this advice and stayed in the temple, where he saw the young monk from Mount Song among the students. Hu was greatly surprised and talked about how long it had been since they last met, asking when he had arrived. The two monks replied, "I originally lived in this temple, but I don't remember meeting you in the past." Hu then recounted his encounter with the young monk from Mount Song, but this monk said, "You are mistaken; how could there be such a thing?" The next day, the two monks left without further ado. Hu then told all the monks about what he had seen at Mount Song in the past; they were all astonished and immediately searched for the two monks, but they could not be found. Hu then realized that they were divine beings. At the end of the Yuanjia era, a monk from Chang'an named Shitan came to tour the south and recounted the same story. This is recorded in the sixth chapter of the "Julei." [131]

    宋居士二字广记引有卞悦之，济阴人也。作朝请，居在潮沟。行年五十，未有子息，妇为娶妾，复积载不孕。将祈求继嗣，千遍转观世音经；广记引作发愿诵观世音经千遍其数垂竟，妾便有娠，遂生一男。时广记引有时字元嘉二十八年原夺二字今补己丑岁也。云云珠林五十二广记一百十一
    　　宋沙门释昙典白衣时年三十，忽暴疾而亡，经七日方活。说：初亡时，见二人驱将去，使辇米。伴辇可有数千人，昼夜无休息。见二道人云：“我是汝五戒本师。”来慰问之。师将往诣官主，云是贫道弟子，且无大罪，历算未穷。即见放遣。二道人送典至家，住其屋上，具约示典。可作沙门，勤修道业。言讫下屋，道人推典着尸腋下，于是而稣。后出家，经二十年，以元嘉十四年亡。珠林九十 [132]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [132]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 居士 - A term for a lay follower of Buddhism or a scholar who practices asceticism.
    • 廣記引 - A reference to the *Yingya Shenglan* (Imperial Readings), a historical record.
    • 作朝請 - Refers to holding a court title or office.
    • 祈求繼嗣 - Means 'to pray for descendants' or 'to seek an heir.'
    • 轉觀世音經 - Means 'to recite the *Guanyin Sutra*.'
    • 發願 - Means 'to make a vow' or 'to pledge.'
    • 垂竟 - Means 'nearly completed' or 'almost finished.'
    • 有娠 - Means 'to be pregnant.'
    • 元嘉二十八年 - The 28th year of the reign of Emperor Wen of Song (451 CE).
    • 己丑歲 - The year of the Earth Ox, a specific year in the Chinese zodiac cycle.
    • 沙門 - Refers to a Buddhist monk.
    • 釋 - A title before a monk's name, meaning 'Venerable.'
    • 白衣 - Refers to a layperson or someone who has not taken monastic vows.
    • 暴疾而亡 - Means 'to die suddenly of a severe illness.'
    • 經七日方活 - Means 'after seven days, came back to life.'
    • 初亡時 - Means 'at the moment of death.'
    • 見二人驅將去 - Means 'saw two people leading him away.'
    • 使辇米 - Means 'sent to carry rice.'
    • 伴辇可有数千人 - Means 'there were about several thousand people carrying rice.'
    • 昼夜无休息 - Means 'day and night without rest.'
    • 五戒本师 - Refers to the five precepts' original teachers.
    • 历算未穷 - Means 'the calculation of fate has not run out.'
    • 即见放遣 - Means 'immediately released and sent away.'
    • 二道人送典至家 - Means 'two monks escorted Tan Dian back home.'
    • 住其屋上 - Means 'stayed on the roof of the house.'
    • 具约示典 - Means 'explained everything to Tan Dian.'
    • 可作沙门 - Means 'you can become a monk.'
    • 勤修道业 - Means 'diligently practice the Dharma.'
    • 言讫下屋 - Means 'after finishing speaking, descended from the roof.'
    • 推典着尸腋下 - Means 'pushed Tan Dian onto the body's armpit.'
    • 于是而稣 - Means 'thus he revived.'
    • 元嘉十四年 - The 14th year of the reign of Emperor Wen of Song (437 CE).

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋 - Refers to the Southern Song Dynasty (420-479 CE).
    • 元嘉二十八年 - The 28th year of the reign of Emperor Wen of Song (451 CE).
    • 己丑岁 - The year of the Earth Ox, a specific year in the Chinese zodiac cycle.
    • 沙门 - Refers to a Buddhist monk.
    • 五戒 - The Five Precepts of Buddhism, which include abstaining from killing, stealing, sexual misconduct, false speech, and intoxicants.
    • 元嘉十四年 - The 14th year of the reign of Emperor Wen of Song (437 CE).

    Proper Nouns

    • 宋 - The Southern Song Dynasty (420-479 CE).
    • 卞悦之 - A person's name.
    • 济阴 - A place name.
    • 潮沟 - A place name.
    • 元嘉二十八年 - The 28th year of the reign of Emperor Wen of Song (451 CE).
    • 己丑岁 - The year of the Earth Ox.
    • 释昙典 - A person's name.
    • 元嘉十四年 - The 14th year of the reign of Emperor Wen of Song (437 CE).

    During the reign of the Song Dynasty, the Yingya Shenglan records a man named Bian Yuezhi from Jiyin. He held a court title and lived in Chao Gou. At the age of fifty, he had no children, so his wife arranged for him to take a concubine. After many years, the concubine remained barren. Intending to pray for descendants, he recited the Guanyin Sutra a thousand times. The Yingya Shenglan records that he made a vow to recite the Guanyin Sutra a thousand times, nearly completing the recitation. Soon after, the concubine became pregnant and gave birth to a son. At the time, the Yingya Shenglan records that it was the year of the Earth Ox in the 28th year of the reign of Emperor Wen of Song (451 CE).

    During the reign of the Song Dynasty, a Buddhist monk named Shi Tandian, who was a layperson at the age of thirty, suddenly died of a severe illness. After seven days, he came back to life. He recounted that at the moment of death, he saw two people leading him away, sent to carry rice. There were about several thousand people carrying rice, day and night without rest. He saw two monks who said, "We are your original teachers of the Five Precepts," and came to console him. The monks led him to the official, saying that he was their disciple, and that he had no great sin, and the calculation of fate had not run out. He was immediately released and sent away. The two monks escorted Tandian back home, stayed on the roof of the house, and explained everything to him. They advised him to become a monk and diligently practice the Dharma. After finishing speaking, the monks descended from the roof, pushed Tandian onto the body's armpit, and thus he revived. He later became a monk, practiced for twenty years, and died in the 14th year of the reign of Emperor Wen of Song (437 CE). [132]

    宋王淮之字元曾，琅琊人也，世以儒专，不信佛法。常谓：身神俱灭，宁有三世？元嘉中，为丹阳令，十年，得病气绝，少时还复暂苏。时建康令贺道力省疾，下床会，淮之语力曰：“始知释教不虚，人死神存，信有征矣。”道力曰：“明府生平置论不尔，今何见而乃异之耶？”上二字及乃字并依广记引补淮之敛?答云：“神实不尽，佛教不得不信。”语卒而终。珠林七十九广记九十九
    　　宋沙门慧和者，京师众造寺僧也。宋义嘉难，和犹为白衣，隶刘胡部下。胡尝遣将士数十人值谍东下，和亦预行。行至雀渚而值台军西上，谍众离散，各逃草泽，和得窜下至新林外会。见野老衣服缕弊，和乃以完整裤褶易其衣，提篮负担，若类田人。时诸游军捕此散谍，视和形色，疑而问之；和答对谬略，因被笞掠，登将见斩。和自散走，但恒诵念观世音经，至将斩时，祈恳弥至。既而军人挥刃屡跌，三举三折，并惊而释之。和于是出家，遂成精业。珠林二十七 [133]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [133]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 身神俱灭 - Philosophical expression meaning 'the body and spirit both perish,' reflecting a materialist view that consciousness ceases with death.
    • 三世 - Refers to the three temporal realms of past, present, and future, or the three realms of existence in Buddhist cosmology.
    • 得病气绝，少时还复暂苏 - Describes a medical condition where a person appears to die but briefly revives, indicating a near-death experience.
    • 始知释教不虚 - Means 'I now know that Buddhism is not empty or false,' marking a shift from disbelief to acceptance.
    • 人死神存 - A philosophical or religious statement that the spirit continues to exist after death, contrary to the materialist view.
    • 明府生平置论不尔 - Means 'Your Excellency, you have always held different views in the past,' addressing the official respectfully.
    • 神实不尽 - Means 'the spirit is indeed not exhausted,' affirming the continuity of consciousness.
    • 佛教不得不信 - Means 'one cannot help but believe in Buddhism,' expressing a newfound conviction.
    • 语卒而终 - Means 'he spoke these words and then passed away,' marking the end of the narrative.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋王淮之 - A historical figure, likely a high-ranking official during the Southern Song dynasty.
    • 琅琊 - A historical region in present-day Shandong province, known for its cultural and political significance.
    • 元嘉中 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Wen of Song (424–453 CE), a period of cultural and literary flourishing.
    • 丹阳令 - The title of a local administrative official in charge of the Dan Yang prefecture.
    • 建康令贺道力 - Another historical figure, likely a local official or scholar who visited Wēi Huái zhī during his illness.
    • 刘胡 - A historical figure, possibly a military leader or regional power during the Southern Song period.
    • 谍东下 - Refers to a military operation or reconnaissance mission conducted by Dōu Hú’s forces.
    • 雀渚 - A specific location, likely a strategic point on the Yangtze River.
    • 台军西上 - Refers to the central government’s military forces moving westward.
    • 散谍 - Refers to soldiers or spies who have been separated from their main force.
    • 游军 - Refers to mobile or patrol forces tasked with capturing fugitives.
    • 沙门慧和 - A historical figure, likely a Buddhist monk who converted after a miraculous event.
    • 京师众造寺僧 - Refers to a monk associated with a temple in the capital city.
    • 义嘉难 - A historical event, possibly a period of political or military turmoil.
    • 白衣 - A term for a layperson, as opposed to a monk or official.
    • 观世音经 - Refers to the *Guanyin Sutra*, a Buddhist scripture dedicated to the Bodhisattva Guanyin.
    • 精业 - Means 'deeply devoted' or 'proficient,' indicating a high level of spiritual achievement.

    Proper Nouns

    • 宋王淮之 - A historical figure, likely a high-ranking official during the Southern Song dynasty.
    • 琅琊 - A historical region in present-day Shandong province.
    • 元嘉 - The reign title of Emperor Wen of Song (424–453 CE).
    • 丹阳令 - The title of a local administrative official.
    • 建康令贺道力 - Another historical figure, likely a local official or scholar.
    • 刘胡 - A historical figure, possibly a military leader or regional power.
    • 沙门慧和 - A historical figure, likely a Buddhist monk.
    • 京师 - Refers to the capital city, likely Jiankang (modern-day Nanjing).
    • 义嘉难 - A historical event, possibly a period of political or military turmoil.

    Wēi Huái zhī, whose courtesy name was Yuán Zēng, was from Lángyá. His family had long been devoted to Confucianism and did not believe in Buddhism. He often said, "The body and spirit both perish; how can there be three temporal realms?" During the reign of Emperor Wen of Song (Yùnjiā era), he served as the magistrate of Dānyáng. After ten years, he fell ill and appeared to die, but after a short while, he briefly revived. At that time, the magistrate of Jiàn Kāng, Hè Dàolì, came to visit him as he lay on his deathbed. Wēi Huái zhī spoke to Hè Dàolì, saying, "I now know that Buddhism is not empty or false; the spirit continues to exist after death, and there is indeed evidence of this." Hè Dàolì replied, "Your Excellency, you have always held different views in the past. What have you seen now that makes you change your mind?" Wēi Huái zhī, gathering his strength, answered, "The spirit is indeed not exhausted; one cannot help but believe in Buddhism." With these words, he passed away.

    The Zūlín (Volume 79), Guǎngjì (Volume 99).

    During the reign of Emperor Wen of Song, there was a monk named Huì Hé, a resident of the temple in the capital city. During the turmoil of the Yìjiā era, Huì Hé was still a layperson and served under the command of Lǐu Hú. Lǐu Hú once sent dozens of soldiers on a reconnaissance mission to the east, and Huì Hé was among them. They reached Qièzhǔ, but encountered the central government’s troops marching westward. The reconnaissance force was scattered, and everyone fled into the wilderness. Huì Hé managed to escape and reached the outskirts of Xīnlín. There, he saw an old farmer whose clothes were tattered. Huì Hé exchanged his complete trousers and jacket for the old man’s clothes, then carried a basket and a burden, appearing like a farmer. At that time, the patrol forces were searching for the scattered reconnaissance soldiers. Seeing Huì Hé’s appearance, they suspected him and questioned him. Huì Hé gave vague and inconsistent answers, and as a result, he was beaten and was about to be executed. As Huì Hé fled, he constantly recited the Guānshìyīn Sūtra. When the executioner was about to strike, Huì Hé’s prayers grew more fervent. The executioner’s sword slipped three times, and each time it fell short. Startled, the executioner released him. Huì Hé then left the military and entered monastic life, eventually achieving great spiritual attainments.

    The Zūlín (Volume 27). [133]

    宋慧远沙门者，江陵长沙寺僧也，师慧印，善禅法，号曰禅师。远本印苍头，名黄迁，年二十时，印每入定，辄见迁先世乃是其师，故遂度为弟子。常寄江陵市西杨道产家，行般舟勤苦岁余，因尔遂颇有感变；或一日之中，赴十余处斋，虽复终日竟夜行道转经，而家家悉见黄迁在焉。众稍敬异之，以为得道。孝建二年一日，自言死期，谓道产曰：“明夕吾当于君家过世。”至日，道产设八关，然灯通夕。 [134]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [134]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 沙门 - Buddhist term for monk.
    • 禅师 - Zen master.
    • 般舟 - Specific Buddhist practice of continuous walking meditation.
    • 得道 - Formal term for enlightened.
    • 过世 - Formal term for pass away.
    • 八关 - Eight Precepts, a set of Buddhist precepts for laypeople.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋慧远 - Historical figure in Chinese Buddhism.
    • 江陵 - Important city in ancient China.
    • 长沙寺 - Significant Buddhist temple in Jiangling.
    • 慧印 - Zen master and Cui Huayan's teacher.
    • 孝建 - Period during the Liu Song dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 宋慧远 - Cui Huayan, a historical figure in Chinese Buddhism.
    • 江陵 - Jiangling, an important city in ancient China.
    • 长沙寺 - Changsha Temple, a significant Buddhist temple in Jiangling.
    • 慧印 - Huayan, a Zen master and Cui Huayan's teacher.
    • 般舟 - Banzhou method, a specific form of continuous walking meditation.
    • 孝建 - Xiaojian era, a period during the Liu Song dynasty.
    • 八关 - Eight Precepts, a set of Buddhist precepts for laypeople.

    Cui Huayan, a monk at the Changsha Temple in Jiangling, was a disciple of Huayan, who was skilled in Zen meditation and was known as a Zen master. Originally, Cui Huayan was Huayan's servant, named Huang Qian. When he was twenty years old, Huayan would often enter meditation and see in a vision that Huang Qian's previous life was his own teacher. Therefore, Huayan decided to ordain Huang Qian as a disciple. Cui Huayan often stayed at the home of Yang Daochan in the western part of Jiangling. He practiced the Banzhou method, involving continuous walking meditation, for over a year, and as a result, he experienced significant spiritual transformations. Sometimes, in a single day, he would attend more than ten religious ceremonies, continuing his spiritual practices throughout the day and night, and everyone who saw him would see Huang Qian. People began to respect and consider him enlightened. In the second year of the Xiaojian era, Cui Huayan announced his impending death, telling Yang Daochan, "Tomorrow night, I will pass away at your place." On that day, Yang Daochan prepared by setting up the Eight Precepts and lighting lamps all night. [134]

    初夜中夜，迁犹豫众行道，休然不异；四更之后，乃称疲而卧，颜色稍变，有顷而尽。○境为设三七斋，起塔，塔今犹存。死后久之，现形多宝寺，谓昙珣道人云：“明年二月二十三日，当与诸天共相迎也。”言已而去。昙珣即于长沙禅房设斋九十日，舍身布施至其日，苦乏气，自知必终，大延道俗，盛设法会。三更中，呼问众僧：“有闻见不？”众自不觉异也，珣曰：“空中有奏乐声，馨烟甚异，黄迁之契，今其至矣。”众僧始还堂就席，而珣已尽。珠林九十七又十九
    　　宋路昭太后大明四年，造普贤菩萨乘宝轝白象，安于中兴禅房，因设讲于寺。其年十月八日，斋毕解座，会僧二百人。于时寺宇始构，帝甚留心，辇跸临幸，旬必数四，僧徒勤整，禁卫严肃。尔日僧名有定，就席久之，忽有一僧，预于座次，风貌秀举，阖堂惊瞩，斋主与语，往还百余言，忽不复见。列筵同睹识其神人矣。珠林十七 [135]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [135]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 初夜中夜 - Refers to the early and middle watches of the night, indicating a specific time frame.
    • 休然不异 - Means 'resting peacefully without any difference,' suggesting a state of calm and normalcy.
    • 四更之后 - Refers to the fourth watch of the night, typically around 1 AM to 3 AM.
    • 乃称疲而卧 - Means 'then claimed to be tired and went to sleep,' indicating a sudden change in behavior.
    • 颜色稍变 - Means 'the appearance gradually changed,' suggesting a physical transformation.
    • 有顷而尽 - Means 'in a short while, it was over,' indicating the end of a process.
    • 当与诸天共相迎也 - Means 'will be welcomed by the heavenly beings together,' indicating a divine reception.
    • 言已而去 - Means 'after speaking, he left,' indicating the conclusion of the interaction.
    • 舍身布施 - Means 'offered his body as a sacrifice,' indicating a selfless act.
    • 苦乏气，自知必终 - Means 'suffering from lack of energy, he knew he would die soon,' indicating a terminal condition.
    • 大延道俗，盛设法会 - Means 'greatly invited both monks and laypeople, and held a grand Dharma assembly,' indicating a significant religious event.
    • 三更中，呼问众僧 - Means 'in the third watch of the night, he called out to the monks,' indicating a nighttime interaction.
    • 有闻见不 - Means 'did you hear or see anything?' indicating a question about perception.
    • 空中有奏乐声，馨烟甚异 - Means 'there was music playing in the air, and the incense smoke was very unusual,' indicating a supernatural experience.
    • 黄迁之契，今其至矣 - Means 'the covenant of Huang Qian has arrived,' indicating a fulfillment of a promise or agreement.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 黄迁 - Refers to Huang Qian, a historical figure or deity mentioned in the text.
    • 多宝寺 - Refers to the Many Treasures Temple, a significant religious site.
    • 昙珣道人 - Refers to the monk Tanxun, a key figure in the narrative.
    • 长沙禅房 - Refers to the Chan room in Changsha, a place of religious practice.
    • 三七斋 - Refers to a 21-day斋 (ritual or ceremony), a common practice in Chinese Buddhism.
    • 起塔 - Refers to the construction of a stupa, a Buddhist monument.
    • 舍身布施 - Refers to the act of offering one's body as a sacrifice, a significant Buddhist practice.
    • 大延道俗，盛设法会 - Refers to the invitation of both monks and laypeople to a grand Dharma assembly, a significant religious event.

    Proper Nouns

    • 黄迁 - A historical figure or deity.
    • 多宝寺 - The Many Treasures Temple.
    • 昙珣道人 - The monk Tanxun.
    • 长沙禅房 - The Chan room in Changsha.
    • 三七斋 - A 21-day斋 (ritual or ceremony).
    • 起塔 - The construction of a stupa.
    • 舍身布施 - The act of offering one's body as a sacrifice.
    • 大延道俗，盛设法会 - The invitation of both monks and laypeople to a grand Dharma assembly.
    • 珠林九十七又十九 - The source of the text, 'Zhu Lin' (珠林), volume 97, part 19.

    At the early and middle watches of the night, Huang Qian hesitated among the crowd on the road, resting peacefully without any difference; after the fourth watch, he claimed to be tired and went to sleep, his appearance gradually changing, and in a short while, it was over. The environment was set up for a three-seven斋 (a type of ritual or ceremony), a stupa was built, and it still exists today. After his death, for a long time, he appeared in the Many Treasures Temple, telling the monk Tanxun, "Next year on February 23rd, I will be welcomed by the heavenly beings together." After speaking, he left. Tanxun immediately set up a ninety-day斋 (ritual) in the Chan room of Changsha, offering his body as a sacrifice until that day, suffering from lack of energy, he knew he would die soon, greatly inviting both monks and laypeople, and held a grand Dharma assembly. In the third watch of the night, he called out to the monks, "Did you hear or see anything?" The monks did not feel any difference, but Tanxun said, "There was music playing in the air, and the incense smoke was very unusual, the covenant of Huang Qian has arrived." The monks just returned to the hall and took their seats, but Tanxun had already passed away. Zhu Lin 97, 19

    In the fourth year of Daming during the reign of Empress Dowager Lu of the Song Dynasty, a statue of the Bodhisattva Samantabhadra riding a white elephant in a precious carriage was placed in the Zongxing Chan room, and a lecture was held in the temple. On the eighth day of the tenth month of that year, after the斋 (ritual) was completed and the assembly was disbanded, there were two hundred monks present. At that time, the temple was just being constructed, the emperor paid great attention to it, visiting frequently, often several times a week, the monks were diligent and orderly, and the guards were strict. On that day, the names of the monks were determined, and they had been seated for a long time when suddenly one monk appeared among them, with a graceful and dignified appearance, causing the entire hall to be startled. The斋 (ritual) host spoke with him, exchanging over a hundred words, and then he suddenly disappeared. The guests at the banquet all recognized him as a divine being. Zhu Lin 17 [135]

    宋大明年中，有寺统法师名道温，居在棱陵县。既见皇太后睿鉴冲明。圣符幽洽，涤思净场研襟至境；固以声藻震中，事灵梵表，乃创思镕斲，抽写神华模造普贤彩仪盛像，宝倾宙珍，妙尽天饰。所设讲斋，迄今月八日，嚫会有限，名簿索定，引次就席，数无盈减。转经将半，景及昆吾，忽睹异僧，预于座内，容止端严，气貌秀发，举僧瞩目，莫有识者。斋主问曰：“上人何；名？”答曰：“名慧明。”问住何寺？答云：“来自天安。”言对之间，倏然不见。○堂惊魂，遍筵肃虑，以为明祥所贲，幽应攸阐，紫山可睹，华台不远。盖闻至诚所感还景移纬，澄心所殉，发石开泉；况帝德涵运，皇功懋洽，仁洞干遐，理畅冥外，故上王盛士，克表大明之朝，劝发妙身，躬见龙飞之室。意若曰：陛下慧烛海县，明华日月。故以慧明为人名，继天兴祚式垂无疆，故以天安为寺称。神基弥远，道政方凝，九服识泰，万寓齐悦。谨列言属县以诠天休。珠林十七 [136]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [136]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 宋大明年中 - Refers to the reign period of Emperor Tai Ming of the Song Dynasty, a specific historical context.
    • 寺统法师 - A title for a high-ranking Buddhist monk, often translated as 'Elder Monk' or 'Abbot.'
    • 睿鉴冲明 - A compound phrase meaning 'wise insight and profound clarity,' often used to describe a ruler's wisdom.
    • 圣符幽洽 - Means 'divine signs and harmonious mysteries,' suggesting a connection between the emperor's virtues and supernatural approval.
    • 涤思净场研襟至境 - A poetic phrase meaning 'purifying thoughts in a clean realm and delving into the highest realms of the heart,' referring to spiritual cultivation.
    • 声藻震中 - Means 'reverberating eloquence in the center,' suggesting the impact of the monk's teachings.
    • 事灵梵表 - Refers to 'affairs of the divine and the sacred,' emphasizing the spiritual nature of the activities.
    • 创思镕斲 - Means 'innovative thinking and meticulous carving,' describing the creation of the statue.
    • 抽写神华 - Means 'extracting divine beauty,' referring to the artistic rendering of the statue.
    • 普贤彩仪盛像 - Refers to the 'statue of Samantabhadra in splendid form,' a significant Buddhist figure.
    • 宝倾宙珍 - Means 'treasures from the universe,' indicating the value and rarity of the materials used.
    • 妙尽天饰 - Means 'exquisite and fully adorned,' emphasizing the beauty of the statue.
    • 所设讲斋 - Refers to 'the lecture and fasting sessions,' a common practice in Buddhist monasteries.
    • 迄今月八日 - Means 'until the eighth day of the month,' indicating a recurring event.
    • 嚫会有限 - Means 'donations are limited,' suggesting a structured and controlled environment.
    • 名簿索定 - Means 'names are registered and determined,' indicating an organized process.
    • 引次就席 - Means 'leading in order to the seats,' describing the orderly arrangement of participants.
    • 数无盈减 - Means 'the number remains constant,' indicating a consistent attendance.
    • 转经将半 - Means 'halfway through the sutra recitation,' indicating the progress of a religious ceremony.
    • 景及昆吾 - Refers to 'the sun reaching the Kunwu Mountains,' a specific time of day.
    • 忽睹异僧 - Means 'suddenly seeing a strange monk,' indicating an unexpected appearance.
    • 容止端严 - Means 'dignified and composed demeanor,' describing the monk's appearance.
    • 气貌秀发 - Means 'radiant and noble appearance,' further describing the monk's presence.
    • 举僧瞩目 - Means 'all monks looking at him,' indicating the attention he received.
    • 斋主问曰 - Means 'the host asked,' indicating a formal interaction.
    • 上人何名 - Means 'what is your name, revered one?' a polite way of asking for someone's name.
    • 名慧明 - The monk's name, 'Hui Ming.'
    • 来自天安 - Means 'coming from Tian'an Temple,' indicating his origin.
    • 天安 - The name of the temple, 'Tian'an Temple.'
    • 倏然不见 - Means 'suddenly disappeared,' indicating the monk's mysterious departure.
    • 堂惊魂 - Means 'the hall was filled with shock,' describing the reaction of the attendees.
    • 遍筵肃虑 - Means 'all the guests were solemn and thoughtful,' indicating the atmosphere.
    • 以为明祥所贲 - Means 'thinking it was a sign of good fortune,' suggesting a positive interpretation.
    • 幽应攸阐 - Means 'mysterious responses and explanations,' indicating the supernatural nature of the event.
    • 紫山可睹 - Means 'the purple mountain can be seen,' a metaphor for a spiritual vision.
    • 华台不远 - Means 'the splendid platform is not far,' another metaphor for a spiritual destination.
    • 盖闻至诚所感还景移纬 - Means 'it is said that sincere devotion can move the stars and change the constellations,' emphasizing the power of faith.
    • 澄心所殉，发石开泉 - Means 'a clear heart can move mountains and open springs,' further emphasizing the power of faith.
    • 帝德涵运 - Means 'the imperial virtue encompasses the universe,' praising the emperor's benevolence.
    • 皇功懋洽 - Means 'the imperial achievements are widespread and harmonious,' praising the emperor's accomplishments.
    • 仁洞干遐 - Means 'benevolence reaches far,' indicating the emperor's influence.
    • 理畅冥外 - Means 'principles extend beyond the visible world,' suggesting the emperor's wisdom extends to the spiritual realm.
    • 故上王盛士 - Means 'thus, the great scholars of the court,' referring to the learned individuals.
    • 克表大明之朝 - Means 'can represent the era of Great Ming,' indicating the era's significance.
    • 劝发妙身 - Means 'encouraging the manifestation of the wonderful body,' referring to the statue.
    • 躬见龙飞之室 - Means 'seeing the dragon's flight room personally,' indicating the emperor's involvement.
    • 意若曰 - Means 'as if saying,' introducing a hypothetical statement.
    • 陛下慧烛海县 - Means 'Your Majesty's wisdom illuminates the sea and counties,' praising the emperor's wisdom.
    • 明华日月 - Means 'bright as the sun and moon,' emphasizing the emperor's brilliance.
    • 故以慧明为人名 - Means 'therefore, the name Hui Ming is given to a person,' explaining the origin of the monk's name.
    • 继天兴祚式垂无疆 - Means 'continuing the heavenly mandate and enduring forever,' indicating the continuity of the dynasty.
    • 故以天安为寺称 - Means 'therefore, the temple is named Tian'an,' explaining the origin of the temple's name.
    • 神基弥远 - Means 'divine foundation extends far,' indicating the temple's spiritual significance.
    • 道政方凝 - Means 'the moral governance is solidifying,' suggesting the stability of the regime.
    • 九服识泰 - Means 'all nine regions recognize peace,' indicating widespread peace.
    • 万寓齐悦 - Means 'all beings are joyful,' indicating universal happiness.
    • 谨列言属县以诠天休 - Means 'carefully listing words for the county to explain the heavenly blessings,' indicating the purpose of the record.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋大明 - Refers to the reign period of Emperor Tai Ming of the Song Dynasty, a specific historical context.
    • 寺统法师 - A title for a high-ranking Buddhist monk, often translated as 'Elder Monk' or 'Abbot.'
    • 睿鉴冲明 - A compound phrase meaning 'wise insight and profound clarity,' often used to describe a ruler's wisdom.
    • 圣符幽洽 - Means 'divine signs and harmonious mysteries,' suggesting a connection between the emperor's virtues and supernatural approval.
    • 普贤彩仪盛像 - Refers to the 'statue of Samantabhadra in splendid form,' a significant Buddhist figure.
    • 天安 - The name of the temple, 'Tian'an Temple.'
    • 珠林十七 - Refers to the 'Seventeenth Chapter of the Pearl Forest,' a collection of Buddhist stories and teachings.

    During the reign of Emperor Tai Ming of the Song Dynasty, there was a high-ranking Buddhist monk named Dao Wen, who resided in Lengling County. Having witnessed the Empress Dowager's wise insight and profound clarity, her divine signs and harmonious mysteries, and the purification of thoughts in a clean realm and delving into the highest realms of the heart, he naturally achieved reverberating eloquence in the center and engaged in affairs of the divine and the sacred. Thus, he innovatively thought and meticulously carved, extracting divine beauty to create the splendid statue of Samantabhadra, using treasures from the universe and exquisite adornments. The lecture and fasting sessions he organized continued until the eighth day of the month, with donations limited and names registered and determined, leading participants in order to the seats, with the number remaining constant. Halfway through the sutra recitation, when the sun reached the Kunwu Mountains, a strange monk suddenly appeared among the attendees, with a dignified and composed demeanor and a radiant and noble appearance, attracting the attention of all the monks. The host asked, "What is your name, revered one?" The monk replied, "My name is Hui Ming." The host then asked where he lived, and the monk answered, "I come from Tian'an Temple." As they spoke, the monk suddenly disappeared. The hall was filled with shock, and all the guests were solemn and thoughtful, thinking it was a sign of good fortune and mysterious responses and explanations. They imagined the purple mountain could be seen and the splendid platform was not far. It is said that sincere devotion can move the stars and change the constellations, and a clear heart can move mountains and open springs. Given the imperial virtue that encompasses the universe and the widespread and harmonious imperial achievements, benevolence reaches far, and principles extend beyond the visible world, thus, the great scholars of the court can represent the era of Great Ming, encouraging the manifestation of the wonderful body and seeing the dragon's flight room personally. As if saying, "Your Majesty's wisdom illuminates the sea and counties, bright as the sun and moon. Therefore, the name Hui Ming is given to a person, continuing the heavenly mandate and enduring forever. Therefore, the temple is named Tian'an." The divine foundation extends far, and the moral governance is solidifying, with all nine regions recognizing peace and all beings being joyful. Carefully listing words for the county to explain the heavenly blessings. Seventeenth Chapter of the Pearl Forest. [136]

    宋蒋小德，江陵人也，为兵州刺史朱循时为听事监师，少而信向，勤谨过人，循大喜之，每有法事，辄令典知其务。大明末年，得病而死，夜三更，将殓便稣活。言：有使者，称王命召之，小德随去。既至，王曰：“君精勤小心，虔奉大法，帝敕精旨，以君专至，宜速生善地；而君算犹长，故令吾特相召也。君今日将受天中快乐欣然。”小德嘉诺。王曰：“君可且还家，所欲属寄及作功德，可速之，七日复来也。”小德受言而归。 [137]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [137]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 精勤小心 - Means 'diligent and careful.'
    • 虔奉大法 - Means 'faithfully adhere to the great law.'
    • 嘉诺 - Means 'expressed gratitude and assent.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋 - Refers to the Song Dynasty.
    • 蒋小德 - A specific individual, Jiang Xiaode.
    • 江陵 - Refers to Jiangling, a city in Hubei Province.
    • 兵州刺史 - Refers to a military governor.
    • 朱循时 - A specific individual, Zhu Xunshi.
    • 大明 - Refers to the Ming Dynasty.
    • 使者 - Refers to a messenger or envoy.
    • 王命 - Refers to the command of the king.
    • 帝敕 - Refers to the emperor's decree.
    • 天中快乐 - Refers to heavenly joy and happiness.

    Proper Nouns

    • 宋 - The Song Dynasty.
    • 蒋小德 - Jiang Xiaode.
    • 江陵 - Jiangling.
    • 兵州刺史 - Military governor.
    • 朱循时 - Zhu Xunshi.
    • 大明 - The Ming Dynasty.
    • 使者 - Messenger.
    • 王命 - Command of the king.
    • 帝敕 - Emperor's decree.
    • 天中快乐 - Heavenly joy and happiness.

    Jiang Xiaode, a man from Jiangling, served as a supervisor under the military governor Zhu Xunshi during the Song Dynasty. Young and trustworthy, he was diligent and meticulous beyond others, which greatly pleased Zhu Xunshi. Every time there was a legal matter, Zhu Xunshi would always instruct him to handle the affairs.

    In the late Ming Dynasty, Jiang Xiaode died of illness. In the third watch of the night, he was about to be buried when he suddenly revived. He said: "There was a messenger who claimed that he was summoned by the king." Jiang Xiaode followed him. After arriving, the king said, "You are diligent and careful, and you faithfully adhere to the great law. The emperor's decree and intent have specially appointed you, so you should quickly be reborn in a good place. But your life span is still long, so I have specially summoned you. You will now receive heavenly joy and happiness." Jiang Xiaode expressed gratitude and assent. The king said, "You may return home for now. Anything you wish to entrust or perform meritorious deeds, you should do it quickly, and return in seven days." Jiang Xiaode followed the instructions and returned home. [137]

    路由一处，有小屋殊陋弊，逢新寺难公于此屋前。既素识，具相问讯，难云：“贫道自出家来，未尝饮酒，且就兰公，兰公苦见劝逼，饮一升许，被王召，用此故也。贫道若不坐此，当得生天，今乃居此弊宇，三年之后，方得上耳。”小德至家，欲验其言，即夕遽遣人参讯难公，果以此日于兰公处睡卧，至夕而亡。小德既愈，七日内大设福供，至期奄然而卒。朱循即免家兵户。兰难二僧并居新寺，难道行大精，不同余僧。珠林九十四 [138]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [138]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 殊陋弊 - Describes a small house as extremely shabby and dilapidated.
    • 被王召，用此故也 - Means 'I was summoned by the king, because of this reason.'
    • 至期奄然而卒 - Means 'died suddenly by the time.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 新寺 - Refers to a new temple, which could be a significant religious or cultural site.
    • 难公 - A proper name, likely a monk or a religious figure.
    • 兰公 - Another proper name, likely a monk or a religious figure.
    • 小德 - A proper name, likely a person who is the subject of the story.
    • 朱循 - A proper name, likely a person who is mentioned in the context of being exempted from military service.
    • 珠林九十四 - Refers to a specific collection or record, likely a historical or literary source.

    Proper Nouns

    • 路由一处 - A specific location or route.
    • 难公 - A proper name, likely a monk or a religious figure.
    • 兰公 - A proper name, likely a monk or a religious figure.
    • 小德 - A proper name, likely a person who is the subject of the story.
    • 朱循 - A proper name, likely a person who is mentioned in the context of being exempted from military service.
    • 珠林九十四 - A specific collection or record, likely a historical or literary source.

    There is a place called Route One, where there is a small house that is extremely shabby and dilapidated. The monk "Nan Gong" encountered the new temple in front of this house. Since we are already acquainted, we exchanged greetings. The monk said, "Since I became a monk, I have never drunk alcohol, and I went to see Lan Gong, who strongly urged and pressured me to drink about one sheng. Because of this, I was summoned by the king. If I had not stayed here, I would have attained heaven, but now I am staying in this dilapidated house, and I will finally ascend three years later." Little De returned home to verify his words and immediately that evening, he hurriedly sent someone to inquire about Monk Nan. Indeed on that day, Monk Nan lay down to sleep at Lan Gong's place and died that evening. Little De was cured, and within seven days, a grand offering of merit was set up, but by the time, he died suddenly. Zhuxun was immediately exempted from military service. The two monks, Lan and Nan, both reside in the new temple, and Monk Nan practices with great diligence, unlike the other monks. This is recorded in "Zhu Lin" Volume 94. [138]

    宋吴兴沈僧覆，大明末，本土饥荒，逐食至山阳；昼入屯野乞食，夜还寄寓寺舍左右。时山阳诸寺小形铜像甚众，僧覆与其乡里数人，积渐窃取，遂囊箧数四悉满焉。因将还家，共铸为钱。事既发觉，执送出都，入船便云：见人以火烧之。昼夜叫呼，自称楚毒不可堪忍，未及刑坐而死；举体皆炘裂，状如火烧。吴郡朱亨亲识僧覆，具见其事。珠林七十九广记一百十六
    　　宋尼释慧玉，长安人也，行业勤修，经戒通备。尝于长安薛尚书寺见红白光十余日中，至四月八日，六重寺沙门来游此寺，于光处得弥勒金像，高一尺余。慧玉后南渡樊郢，住江陵灵收寺。元嘉十四年十月夜，见寺东树有紫光烂起，晖映一林，以告同学妙光等，而悉弗之见也。二十余日，玉常见焉。后寺主释法弘将于树下营筑禅基，仰首条闲得金坐像，亦高尺许也。珠林十六 [139]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [139]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 逐食 - Literally 'to seek food,' but in context, means 'to go out in search of food,' often implying a desperate situation.
    • 積漸 - Means 'gradually' or 'over time,' indicating a process of incremental accumulation.
    • 遂囊箧數四悉滿焉 - Describes the gradual filling of multiple bags or chests, emphasizing the quantity and completeness of the theft.
    • 因將 - Means 'therefore' or 'thus,' indicating a consequence.
    • 共铸為錢 - Means 'to melt down and cast into coins,' suggesting a collective effort.
    • 見人以火烧之 - '見人' can be interpreted as 'to see someone' or 'to be seen by someone.' '以火烧之' means 'to burn with fire,' which is likely a hallucination or a form of torture.
    • 楚毒不可堪忍 - '楚毒' refers to severe pain or torture, and '不可堪忍' means 'cannot be endured,' emphasizing the intensity of the suffering.
    • 未及刑坐而死 - '未及' means 'not yet' or 'before,' and '刑坐' refers to the act of being subjected to punishment. This phrase indicates that the person died before the official punishment was carried out.
    • 舉體皆炘裂 - '舉體' means 'the entire body,' and '炘裂' means 'to burst or split open,' suggesting a violent and painful death.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋吳興沈僧覆 - Refers to Shen Sengfu, a monk from Wu Prefecture during the Southern Song Dynasty.
    • 大明末 - Refers to the late period of the Southern Song Dynasty, specifically the reign of Emperor Gong (r. 453–464).
    • 山陽 - Refers to Shan Yang, a place in modern-day Shandong Province.
    • 寺舍 - Refers to the area around the temple, indicating where the thief stayed.
    • 弥勒金像 - Refers to a golden statue of Maitreya, the future Buddha, which is a significant religious artifact.
    • 元嘉十四年 - Refers to the 14th year of the Yuangang era (437 AD) of the Liu Song Dynasty.
    • 珠林十六 - Refers to the 16th chapter of the *Zhu Lin* collection, which is a compilation of Buddhist stories and miracles.
    • 宋尼释慧玉 - Monk Hui Yu, a nun from Chang'an.
    • 长安 - Chang'an, the capital of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 薛尚书寺 - Xue Shangshu Temple, a temple in Chang'an.
    • 六重寺 - Liu Chong Temple, a temple in Chang'an.
    • 江陵灵收寺 - Ling Shou Temple in Jiangling, Hubei Province.
    • 释法弘 - Monk Fa Hong, the head of the temple.
    • 妙光 - Venerable Miao Guang, a fellow monk.

    Proper Nouns

    • 宋吳興沈僧覆 - Shen Sengfu, a monk from Wu Prefecture.
    • 山陽 - Shan Yang, a place in modern-day Shandong Province.
    • 寺舍 - Temple, referring to the place where the thief stayed.
    • 弥勒金像 - Golden statue of Maitreya, the future Buddha.
    • 元嘉十四年 - The 14th year of the Yuangang era (437 AD).
    • 珠林十六 - The 16th chapter of the *Zhu Lin* collection.
    • 宋尼释慧玉 - Monk Hui Yu, a nun from Chang'an.
    • 长安 - Chang'an, the capital of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 薛尚书寺 - Xue Shangshu Temple, a temple in Chang'an.
    • 六重寺 - Liu Chong Temple, a temple in Chang'an.
    • 江陵灵收寺 - Ling Shou Temple in Jiangling, Hubei Province.
    • 释法弘 - Monk Fa Hong, the head of the temple.
    • 妙光 - Venerable Miao Guang, a fellow monk.

    During the reign of Emperor Gong, Shen Sengfu from Wu Prefecture, faced with famine in his hometown, went to Shan Yang in search of food. During the day, he would enter the fields to beg for food, and at night, he would stay near the temple. At that time, there were many small copper statues in the temples of Shan Yang. Shen Sengfu and several people from his hometown gradually stole these statues, eventually filling several bags and chests. They then took them back home and melted them down to make coins. When the theft was discovered, they were arrested and sent to the capital. On the boat, Shen Sengfu claimed to see someone burning him with fire. He screamed day and night, claiming that the pain was unbearable, and died before the official punishment could be carried out. His entire body burst open, as if burned. Zhu Hen from Wu Prefecture, who knew Shen Sengfu personally, witnessed the entire event. (From Zhu Lin 79 and Guang Ji 116)

    Nun Hui Yu, from Chang'an, was a diligent practitioner of the Dharma, well-versed in scriptures and precepts. One day, while at Xue Shangshu Temple in Chang'an, she saw red and white lights for over ten days. On the eighth day of the fourth month, a monk from Liu Chong Temple came to visit and found a golden statue of Maitreya, about a foot tall, where the light was. Hui Yu later moved south to Jiangling and stayed at Ling Shou Temple. In the fourteenth year of the Yuangang era, on a night in October, she saw a brilliant purple light rise from a tree in the temple grounds, illuminating the entire grove. She told her fellow monk Miao Guang about it, but he could not see it. Over the next twenty days, Hui Yu continued to see the light. Later, the temple master Fa Hong was building a meditation base under the tree when he found a golden seated statue, also about a foot tall. (From Zhu Lin 16) [139]

    宋费崇先者，吴兴人也，少颇信法，至三十际，精勤弥至。泰始三年，受菩萨戒，寄斋于谢惠远家，二十四日，昼夜不懈。每听经，常以鹊尾香炉置膝前。初学记二十五引云费崇先少信佛常以鹊尾香炉置膝前初斋三夕，见一人容服不凡，迳来举炉将去；崇先视膝前炉犹在其处，更详视此人，见提去甚分明，崇先方悟是神异。自惟衣裳新濯，了无不净，唯坐侧有唾壶；既使去壶，即复见此人还炉坐前，未至席顷犹见两炉，既即合为一，然则此神人所提者，盖炉影乎。崇先又当闻人说：福远寺有僧钦尼精勤得道，欣然愿见，未及得往，属意甚至。尝斋于他家，夜三更中，忽见一尼，容仪端严着赭布袈娑，正立斋席之前，食顷而灭。及崇先后觐此尼，色貌被服，即窗前所睹者也。珠林二十四 [140]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [140]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 宋費崇先者，吳興人也 - A formal introduction meaning 'Mr. Fei Chongxian of Song, from Wu County.'
    • 少颇信法 - Means 'in his youth, he had some faith in the Dharma (Buddhist teachings).'
    • 至三十际，精勤弥至 - Means 'by the age of thirty, his diligence and devotion became even more intense.'
    • 泰始三年，受菩萨戒 - Means 'in the third year of Tai Shi, he received the Bodhisattva precepts.'
    • 寄斋于谢惠远家 - Means 'he stayed for meditation at the home of Xie Huaiyuan.'
    • 二十四日，昼夜不懈 - Means 'for twenty-four days, without rest day or night.'
    • 每听经，常以鹊尾香炉置膝前 - Means 'every time he listened to the scriptures, he would place a magpie-tail incense burner on his knees.'
    • 初学记二十五引云 - Means 'according to the *Initial Learning Record* (卷二十五).'
    • 初斋三夕，见一人容服不凡 - Means 'on the third night of his initial meditation retreat, he saw a person of extraordinary appearance and attire.'
    • 迳来举炉将去 - Means 'who came straight to take the incense burner away.'
    • 崇先视膝前炉犹在其处 - Means 'Chongxian looked at the incense burner on his knees and found it still there.'
    • 更详视此人，见提去甚分明 - Means 'he looked more closely at the person and saw them take the incense burner away clearly.'
    • 崇先方悟是神异 - Means 'Chongxian then realized it was a supernatural phenomenon.'
    • 自惟衣裳新濯，了无不净 - Means 'he thought to himself that his clothes had just been washed and were completely clean.'
    • 唯坐侧有唾壶 - Means 'the only thing near him was a spittoon.'
    • 既使去壶，即复见此人还炉坐前 - Means 'after he removed the spittoon, he saw the person return with the incense burner to sit in front of him.'
    • 未至席顷犹见两炉，既即合为一 - Means 'for a short while he still saw two incense burners, but then they merged into one.'
    • 然则此神人所提者，盖炉影乎 - Means 'thus, what the divine person took away was probably just a shadow of the incense burner.'
    • 崇先又当闻人说 - Means 'Chongxian also heard from others that...'
    • 福远寺有僧钦尼精勤得道 - Means 'there was a nun named Qinni at Fuyuan Temple who was diligent and had attained enlightenment.'
    • 欣然愿见，未及得往，属意甚至 - Means 'he was delighted to see her, but before he could go, he had already developed a strong attachment.'
    • 尝斋于他家，夜三更中 - Means 'once, while staying for meditation at another's home, in the middle of the third watch of the night...'
    • 忽见一尼，容仪端严着赭布袈娑 - Means 'he suddenly saw a nun with dignified appearance, wearing a dark red robe.'
    • 正立斋席之前，食顷而灭 - Means 'standing upright in front of the meditation hall, she vanished after a meal's worth of time.'
    • 及崇先后觐此尼，色貌被服，即窗前所睹者也 - Means 'when Chongxian later met this nun, her appearance and attire were exactly as he had seen from the window.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋 - Refers to the Song Dynasty, a period in Chinese history.
    • 吳興 - Refers to Wuxing, an ancient county in Zhejiang Province.
    • 泰始三年 - Refers to the third year of the Tai Shi era during the Song Dynasty.
    • 菩萨戒 - Refers to the Bodhisattva precepts, a set of vows taken by those who aspire to become bodhisattvas.
    • 谢惠远 - Refers to Xie Huaiyuan, a historical figure.
    • 鹊尾香炉 - A type of incense burner with a magpie-tail design.
    • 初学记 - *Initial Learning Record*, a historical text.
    • 福远寺 - Refers to Fuyuan Temple, a Buddhist temple.
    • 僧钦尼 - Refers to Qinni, a nun.
    • 精勤得道 - Means 'diligent and attained enlightenment.'
    • 三更 - Refers to the middle watch of the night, typically around midnight to 2 AM.
    • 赭布袈娑 - Refers to a dark red robe worn by Buddhist nuns.
    • 珠林 - *Jewel Forest*, a historical text.

    Proper Nouns

    • 宋 - The Song Dynasty.
    • 吳興 - Wuxing, an ancient county in Zhejiang Province.
    • 泰始三年 - The third year of the Tai Shi era during the Song Dynasty.
    • 菩萨戒 - The Bodhisattva precepts.
    • 谢惠远 - Xie Huaiyuan, a historical figure.
    • 鹊尾香炉 - A type of incense burner with a magpie-tail design.
    • 初学记 - *Initial Learning Record*, a historical text.
    • 福远寺 - Fuyuan Temple, a Buddhist temple.
    • 僧钦尼 - Qinni, a nun.
    • 精勤得道 - Diligent and attained enlightenment.
    • 三更 - The middle watch of the night.
    • 赭布袈娑 - A dark red robe worn by Buddhist nuns.
    • 珠林 - *Jewel Forest*, a historical text.

    A Man Named Fei Chongxian of Song

    Mr. Fei Chongxian of Song was from Wu County. In his youth, he had some faith in the Dharma, and by the age of thirty, his diligence and devotion became even more intense. In the third year of Tai Shi, he received the Bodhisattva precepts and stayed for meditation at the home of Xie Huaiyuan for twenty-four days, without rest day or night. Every time he listened to the scriptures, he would place a magpie-tail incense burner on his knees. According to the Initial Learning Record (卷二十五), it is recorded that Fei Chongxian, in his youth, believed in the Buddha and often placed a magpie-tail incense burner on his knees during his initial meditation retreat. On the third night of his retreat, he saw a person of extraordinary appearance and attire who came straight to take the incense burner away. Chongxian looked at the incense burner on his knees and found it still there. He looked more closely at the person and saw them take the incense burner away clearly. Chongxian then realized it was a supernatural phenomenon. He thought to himself that his clothes had just been washed and were completely clean, except for the spittoon near him. After he removed the spittoon, he saw the person return with the incense burner to sit in front of him. For a short while he still saw two incense burners, but then they merged into one. Thus, what the divine person took away was probably just a shadow of the incense burner.

    Chongxian also heard from others that there was a nun named Qinni at Fuyuan Temple who was diligent and had attained enlightenment. He was delighted to see her, but before he could go, he had already developed a strong attachment. Once, while staying for meditation at another's home, in the middle of the third watch of the night, he suddenly saw a nun with dignified appearance, wearing a dark red robe, standing upright in front of the meditation hall. She vanished after a meal's worth of time. When Chongxian later met this nun, her appearance and attire were exactly as he had seen from the window. This account is found in the Jewel Forest (卷二十四). [140]

    东海何敬叔，少而奉佛，至泰始中，随湘州刺史刘韬珠林十四作刘韫监营浦县。敬叔时遇有旃檀，制以为像，像将就而未有光材；敬叔意愿甚勤，而营索无处，凭几微睡，见一沙门纳衣杖锡来，上五字依广记引补语敬叔云：“县后何家有一桐盾，甚堪像光，其人极惜之，苦求可得也。”敬叔寤，问县后，果有何家。因求买盾，何氏云：“实有此盾，甚爱惜之，明府何以得知？”敬叔具说所梦，何氏惊嘉，奉以制光。御览三百五十七广记二百七十六珠林十四云后为相府直省中夜梦像云鼠啮吾足清旦疾归视像果然矣 [141]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [141]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 少而奉佛 - Means 'young and devout in Buddhism,' indicating an early commitment to Buddhist practice.
    • 泰始中 - Refers to the period during the reign of Emperor Wen of Western Jin (266-290 CE).
    • 随湘州刺史刘韬 - Means 'followed as the governor of Xiang Prefecture,' where '刘韬' is likely a misspelling of '刘韫' (Liu Yun), a historical figure.
    • 监营浦县 - Means 'supervised the county of Yingpu.'
    • 旃檀 - Refers to sandalwood, a precious wood often used in Buddhist statues.
    • 制以为像 - Means 'carved into an image' or 'made into a statue.'
    • 未有光材 - Means 'lacking the material for the halo.'
    • 意愿甚勤 - Means 'with great eagerness' or 'with great effort.'
    • 营索无处 - Means 'searched everywhere in vain.'
    • 凭几微睡 - Means 'leaning against a table and dozing off.'
    • 见一沙门纳衣杖锡来 - Means 'saw a monk in a simple robe and holding a staff and alms bowl come.'
    • 上五字依广记引补语 - This phrase suggests that five characters are added based on a reference in the *Guangji* (Widely Recorded), a historical record.
    • 苦求可得也 - Means 'can be obtained with great effort.'
    • 寤 - Means 'awoke.'
    • 果有何家 - Means 'indeed there was a family named He.'
    • 奉以制光 - Means 'offered it to make the halo.'
    • 相府直省 - Refers to the office of the prime minister.
    • 中夜梦像云 - Means 'in the middle of the night, the statue said.'
    • 鼠啮吾足 - Means 'a mouse is biting my foot.'
    • 清旦疾归视像果然矣 - Means 'in the morning, hurriedly returned to see the statue, and indeed it was true.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 东海何敬叔 - Refers to a person named He Jingshu from the eastern sea region.
    • 泰始中 - The period of Emperor Wen of Western Jin (266-290 CE).
    • 湘州刺史刘韫 - Refers to Liu Yun, the governor of Xiang Prefecture.
    • 营浦县 - A county in Xiang Prefecture.
    • 旃檀 - Sandalwood, a precious wood used in Buddhist statues.
    • 像 - Refers to a Buddhist statue.
    • 光材 - The material for the halo of the statue.
    • 沙门 - A Buddhist monk.
    • 纳衣杖锡 - A monk's simple robe, staff, and alms bowl.
    • 广记 - *Guangji* (Widely Recorded), a historical record.
    • 相府直省 - The office of the prime minister.
    • 鼠啮吾足 - A common folk belief that mice can indicate misfortune or illness.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • 少而奉佛，至泰始中，随湘州刺史刘韬监营浦县 - This sentence introduces He Jingshu and his role, but the connection between his devotion to Buddhism and his official position is not immediately clear.
    • 敬叔时遇有旃檀，制以为像，像将就而未有光材 - This sequence describes the process of making a statue, but the transition between having the sandalwood and the need for the halo material is somewhat abrupt.
    • 敬叔意愿甚勤，而营索无处，凭几微睡，见一沙门纳衣杖锡来 - This part is a bit elliptical, but it describes He Jingshu's diligent search and his subsequent dream.
    • 上五字依广记引补语 - This phrase indicates that additional information is provided based on a historical record, which adds complexity to the narrative.
    • 鼠啮吾足，清旦疾归视像果然矣 - This final part is a bit disjointed, but it describes the statue's prediction and the subsequent verification.

    Proper Nouns

    • 东海何敬叔 - He Jingshu, a person from the eastern sea region.
    • 泰始 - The reign title of Emperor Wen of Western Jin (266-290 CE).
    • 湘州 - Xiang Prefecture, a region in southern China.
    • 刘韫 - Liu Yun, the governor of Xiang Prefecture.
    • 营浦县 - Yingpu County, a county in Xiang Prefecture.
    • 旃檀 - Sandalwood, a precious wood.
    • 像 - A Buddhist statue.
    • 光材 - The material for the halo of the statue.
    • 沙门 - A Buddhist monk.
    • 纳衣杖锡 - A monk's simple robe, staff, and alms bowl.
    • 广记 - *Guangji* (Widely Recorded), a historical record.
    • 相府直省 - The office of the prime minister.
    • 鼠啮吾足 - A common folk belief.

    He Jingshu from the eastern sea region was devoted to Buddhism from a young age. During the reign of Emperor Wen (泰始 period), he followed the governor of Xiang Prefecture, Liu Yun (刘韫), in supervising Yingpu County. At the time, He Jingshu obtained some sandalwood and carved it into a statue. The statue was nearly complete, but it lacked the material for the halo. He Jingshu was very eager to complete the statue, but despite his efforts, he could not find the necessary material. While leaning against a table and dozing off, he saw a monk in a simple robe and holding a staff and alms bowl come to him. The monk said, "There is a paulownia shield in the house of the He family after the county, which is very suitable for the halo of the statue. The owner values it greatly, but it can be obtained with great effort." He Jingshu awoke and asked about the He family after the county, and indeed, there was such a family. He then requested to buy the shield. The He family said, "We do have this shield, and we value it greatly. How did you know about it, Your Excellency?" He Jingshu explained his dream, and the He family was amazed and impressed. They offered the shield to help complete the halo of the statue. Later, when He Jingshu served in the office of the prime minister, he had a dream in the middle of the night where the statue said, "A mouse is biting my foot." In the morning, he hurriedly returned to check on the statue, and indeed, a mouse was biting its foot. [141]

    宋袁炳，字叔焕，陈郡人也。泰始末为临湘令。亡后积年，友人司马逊于将晓闲如梦见炳来，陈叙阔别，讯问安否，既而谓逊曰：“吾等平生立意置论，常言生为驰役，死为休息，今日始知，广记引有知字定不然矣。恒患在世有人，务驰求金币，共相赠遗，幽途此事，亦复如之。”逊问：罪福应报，定实如何？炳曰：“如我旧见，与经教所说，不尽符同，将是圣人抑引之谈耳！如今所见，善恶大科，略不异也。然杀生故最为重，禁慎不可犯也。 [142]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [142]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 亡后积年 - Refers to the passage of many years after someone's death.
    • 将晓闲如梦见 - A vivid expression meaning 'in a dream-like state just before dawn.'
    • 陈叙阔别 - Means 'to recount long separation.'
    • 讯问安否 - Means 'to inquire about one's well-being.'
    • 今日始知 - Means 'now I know for the first time.'
    • 广记引有知字定不然矣 - A complex phrase that can be interpreted as 'the extensive records and quotations containing the word 'know' are certainly not correct.'
    • 恒患在世有人，务驰求金币，共相赠遗 - Means 'always troubled by people in the world who are busy chasing after money and gold, exchanging gifts with each other.'
    • 幽途此事，亦复如之 - Means 'in the afterlife, this situation is also the same.'
    • 罪福应报，定实如何 - Means 'are the rewards and punishments for sins and virtues truly determined?'
    • 如我旧见，与经教所说，不尽符同 - Means 'as I saw it before, it does not fully agree with what is said in the scriptures.'
    • 将是圣人抑引之谈耳 - Means 'this is probably the sage's way of guiding and restraining.'
    • 善恶大科，略不异也 - Means 'the general categories of good and evil are not very different.'
    • 然杀生故最为重，禁慎不可犯也 - Means 'however, killing living beings is the most serious, and it must be strictly forbidden and not violated.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 宋袁炳 - Refers to Yuan Bing, a person from the Song Dynasty.
    • 字叔焕 - Yuan Bing's courtesy name.
    • 陈郡人也 - Indicates Yuan Bing's place of origin, Chen County.
    • 泰始末 - Refers to the end of the Tai Shi era, a period in the Song Dynasty.
    • 临湘令 - Refers to the position of magistrate in Linxiang, a county in Hunan Province.
    • 友人司马逊 - Refers to a friend named Sima Xun.
    • 经教 - Refers to religious or philosophical teachings, often associated with Buddhism or Confucianism.
    • 圣人 - Refers to a sage or wise person, often used to denote a highly respected moral authority.
    • 幽途 - Refers to the afterlife or the underworld.

    Proper Nouns

    • 宋袁炳 - Yuan Bing, a historical figure.
    • 字叔焕 - His courtesy name.
    • 陈郡人也 - Chen County, his place of origin.
    • 泰始末 - The end of the Tai Shi era.
