When Jian faced his end, he saw seven places of rebirth and death, which is why he cried so bitterly, as he was saying goodbye to seven different families." [159]

    ”问云：“汝昔生谁家？”建曰：“生江吏部，羊广州，张吴兴，王车骑，萧吴兴，梁给事，董越骑等家。唯此间生十七年，余处止五三年耳。目今以后，毒疠岁多，宜勤修功德。建见世人死，多堕三涂，生天者少；勤精进，可得免度，发愿生天，亦得相见，行脱差异，无相值期。”又问云：“汝母忧忆汝，垂死，可令见汝不？”建曰：不须相见，益怀煎苦耳！耶但依向言说之。诸天已去，不容久住。”惨有悲色，忽然不见。去后竹林左右，犹有香气，家人亦并闻余香焉。建云所生七家；江湛羊希张永王玄谟萧惠明梁季父也。贤明遂以出家，名法藏也。珠林五十二 [160]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [160]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 汝昔生誰家 - A question asking 'Who was your family in the past?' The phrase 昔 indicates the past, and 生 here refers to being born or living in a particular household.
    • 毒疠岁多 - A phrase indicating that diseases or epidemics are prevalent in a particular year.
    • 勤修功德 - A phrase meaning 'diligently cultivate virtue and merit,' a common Buddhist or Confucian ideal.
    • 三涂 - Refers to the three evil realms in Buddhist cosmology: hell, hungry ghosts, and animals.
    • 勤精进，可得免度，发愿生天，亦得相见，行脱差异，无相值期 - A series of statements about spiritual practice, rebirth, and the separation of souls in different realms.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 江吏部，羊广州，张吴兴，王车骑，萧吴兴，梁给事，董越骑 - These are likely historical figures or family names, each associated with a specific title or location. For example: Jiang (family name) with the title of Ministry of Personnel; Yang (family name) with the location of Guangzhou; Zhang (family name) with the location of Wu Xing; Wang (family name) with the title of Chariot Commander; Xiao (family name) with the location of Wu Xing; Liang (family name) with the title of Counselor to the Emperor; Dong (family name) with the title of Elite Cavalry Commander.
    • 珠林五十二 - Likely refers to a specific text or chapter in a collection of Buddhist or Confucian writings.

    Proper Nouns

    • 江吏部 - Jiang (family name) with the title of Ministry of Personnel.
    • 羊广州 - Yang (family name) with the location of Guangzhou.
    • 张吴兴 - Zhang (family name) with the location of Wu Xing.
    • 王车骑 - Wang (family name) with the title of Chariot Commander.
    • 萧吴兴 - Xiao (family name) with the location of Wu Xing.
    • 梁给事 - Liang (family name) with the title of Counselor to the Emperor.
    • 董越骑 - Dong (family name) with the title of Elite Cavalry Commander.
    • 珠林五十二 - Likely refers to a specific text or chapter in a collection of Buddhist or Confucian writings.

    The question was asked: "Who were your families in the past?" Jian replied: "I was born into the families of Jiang (the Ministry of Personnel), Yang (of Guangzhou), Zhang (of Wu Xing), Wang (the Chariot Commander), Xiao (of Wu Xing), Liang (the Counselor to the Emperor), and Dong (the Elite Cavalry Commander). I lived in this place for seventeen years, and in the others for only five or three years each. From now on, there will be many diseases and epidemics, so it is advisable to diligently cultivate virtue and merit. Jian has seen that when people die, most fall into the three evil realms, and few are reborn in heaven; by diligently practicing and making vows to be reborn in heaven, one can also meet again, but due to the differences in our paths, we cannot meet at the same time." The question was then asked: "Your mother is dying of grief and longing for you. Can she see you?" Jian said: "It is not necessary to see her, as it would only cause her more distress! She should follow my previous advice. The gods have already departed, and I cannot stay long." With a sad expression, Jian suddenly disappeared. After he left, the bamboo grove still had a lingering fragrance, and the family members also smelled the remaining scent. Jian mentioned seven families: Jiang Zhan, Yang Xi, Zhang Yong, Wang Xuanmo, Xiao Huiming, Liang Jifu, and Dong Yueqi. Xianming eventually became a monk and was named Faxiang. This is recorded in the Zhu Lin (Jewel Forest), Volume Fifty-Two. [160]

    齐王氏，名四娘，永明三年病死，下尸在地，为庄饰者，觉其心暖，故未殡殓。经二宿，肌体稍温。气息渐还，俄而能言。自说：有二人录其将去，至一大门有一沙门，踞胡床坐。见之甚惊，问：何故来？乃骂此二人云：“汝误录人来，各鞭四十余，此四娘女郎可去。答曰：“向来恍恍，不知道路，请人示津。”沙门即命一人力送之行，少地，见其先死奴子倚高楼上，惊问：“四娘那忽至此？欲见新妇不？”答：“不知处。 [161]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [161]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 覺其心暖 - Describes the body being found warm, suggesting that she was not yet fully dead.
    • 肌體稍溫 - Indicates that the body began to warm up.
    • 氣息漸還 - Describes the gradual return of breath or life.
    • 俄而能言 - Indicates that she soon regained the ability to speak.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 永明三年 - Refers to the third year of the Yongming era (485 AD) during the Southern Qi dynasty.
    • 沙門 - Refers to a Buddhist monk.
    • 胡床 - A type of folding chair or trapeze-like seat, commonly used in ancient China.
    • 先死奴子 - Refers to a servant who had died before her, indicating a connection between the living and the dead.

    Proper Nouns

    • 齊王氏 - A noble family name.
    • 四娘 - The given name of the woman.
    • 永明三年 - The third year of the Yongming era (485 AD).

    The Lady of the Qi family, named Si Niang, died in the third year of the Yongming era. Her body was found lying on the ground, and the person preparing it for burial noticed that her heart was warm, so she had not yet been buried. After two nights, her body began to warm up, and her breath gradually returned. Soon, she was able to speak. She began to speak for herself, saying that two people had taken her away. They arrived at a large gate where a monk was sitting on a trapeze-like seat. The monk was very surprised to see her and asked why she was there. The monk then scolded the two people, saying, "You have made a mistake by taking her. Each of you shall be whipped more than forty times. This girl can go." The two people responded that they were confused and did not know the way, asking for guidance. The monk then ordered one person to escort her. After a short distance, she saw a servant who had died before her, leaning against a high building. She was surprised and asked why she was there, and whether she wanted to see her new wife. [161]

    ”唤奴自送，奴云：“不得奉送，四娘但去，前路应相值也。”投一马鞭与之，曰：“谨执此鞭，自知行路。”可行数里，便见新妇，即四娘之?也，正被苦?，四体碜缚，如○鹅鸭法，县于路侧，相见悲号。新妇自说：生时作罪，今贻此楚毒，欲屈手搏颊，求乞哀助，而手被挛格，不得至颊。又闻左右受苦之声，而不睹形。四娘问：“此为何声？”答曰：“此是无行众僧，破斋犯戒，获此苦报，呼叫声也。”于是?路而归，须臾至家。见其尸骸，意甚憎恶，不复愿还；不觉有人排其踣着，乃得就身而稍稣活。其人今休然尚存。珠林九十一 [162]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [162]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 唤奴自送 - Literally 'call the servant to accompany,' but implies a request or command.
    • 不得奉送 - Means 'cannot accompany' or 'cannot escort,' indicating a refusal or inability.
    • 四娘但去 - Means 'Four Lady, just go,' a polite or dismissive instruction.
    • 前路应相值也 - Means 'you will meet on the way ahead,' a cryptic statement suggesting a future encounter.
    • 谨执此鞭 - Means 'hold this whip carefully,' implying a symbolic or practical gesture.
    • 自知行路 - Means 'you will know the way,' suggesting guidance or destiny.
    • 正被苦 - Means 'is suffering greatly,' indicating severe distress.
    • 四体碜缚 - Means 'four limbs bound tightly,' describing physical restraint.
    • 如○鹅鸭法 - Refers to a method of binding similar to how geese or ducks are tied, indicating a specific technique.
    • 县于路侧 - Means 'hanged by the roadside,' describing the manner of the person's suffering.
    • 相见悲号 - Means 'meet with sorrowful cries,' indicating emotional distress.
    • 生时作罪 - Means 'committed sins in life,' suggesting past actions leading to current suffering.
    • 欲屈手搏颊 - Means 'wants to hit the cheek with the hand,' indicating a desire to punish oneself.
    • 求乞哀助 - Means 'beg for mercy and help,' indicating a plea for assistance.
    • 手被挛格 - Means 'hands are restrained,' indicating physical inability.
    • 不睹形 - Means 'cannot see the form,' suggesting invisibility or intangibility.
    • 四娘问 - Means 'Four Lady asks,' indicating a question from Four Lady.
    • 此为何声 - Means 'what is this sound,' indicating curiosity about the source of a noise.
    • 此是无行众僧 - Means 'these are the unrighteous monks,' indicating criticism of monks.
    • 破斋犯戒 - Means 'break the fast and violate the precepts,' indicating religious transgressions.
    • 获此苦报 - Means 'receive this suffering as retribution,' indicating karmic consequences.
    • 呼叫声也 - Means 'the sound of their cries,' indicating the nature of the noise.
    • 于是路而归 - Means 'thus return on the way,' indicating a journey back.
    • 须臾至家 - Means 'soon arrived home,' indicating a quick return.
    • 见其尸骸 - Means 'see the remains,' indicating a corpse.
    • 意甚憎恶 - Means 'feel very disgusted,' indicating strong aversion.
    • 不复愿还 - Means 'no longer wish to return,' indicating a change of heart.
    • 不觉有人排其踣着 - Means 'unconsciously someone pushes him/her down,' indicating a sudden action.
    • 乃得就身而稍苏活 - Means 'then gradually regain consciousness,' indicating recovery.
    • 其人今休然尚存 - Means 'the person now still exists,' indicating survival.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 唤奴自送 - A common practice in ancient Chinese households where servants were often called to accompany or assist.
    • 四娘 - Likely a name or title, possibly a noblewoman or a character in a story.
    • 被苦 - Suffering is a common theme in Buddhist and Taoist literature, often linked to karma and moral actions.
    • 四体碜缚 - Binding methods were used in various contexts, including punishment and religious rituals.
    • 如○鹅鸭法 - Specific binding techniques might have been used in certain regions or periods.
    • 县于路侧 - Public displays of suffering or punishment were sometimes used as a form of deterrence.
    • 生时作罪 - The concept of karma and the consequences of one's actions is central in Chinese philosophy and religion.
    • 欲屈手搏颊 - Self-punishment is a practice found in various cultures, often associated with guilt or penance.
    • 求乞哀助 - Begging for help is a common theme in literature, reflecting human vulnerability and the need for compassion.
    • 手被挛格 - Physical restraints were used in various contexts, including punishment and religious practices.
    • 不睹形 - Invisibility or intangibility can be symbolic in literature, representing spiritual or metaphysical concepts.
    • 四娘问 - Questions from characters often drive the narrative forward and reveal deeper meanings.
    • 此为何声 - Curiosity about the unknown is a common theme in storytelling.
    • 此是无行众僧 - Criticism of religious figures is a recurring theme in literature, often highlighting moral failings.
    • 破斋犯戒 - Religious transgressions are a significant theme in Buddhist and Taoist literature.
    • 获此苦报 - Karmic consequences are a central concept in Chinese philosophy and religion.
    • 呼叫声也 - Sounds of suffering are often used to evoke empathy and emotional response.
    • 于是路而归 - Journeys and returns are common motifs in literature, symbolizing personal growth or change.
    • 须臾至家 - Quick returns can indicate urgency or the importance of home.
    • 见其尸骸 - Corpses and death are common themes in literature, often symbolizing loss and the impermanence of life.
    • 意甚憎恶 - Strong emotions, especially disgust, can drive the narrative and reveal character traits.
    • 不复愿还 - Changes of heart are a common theme in literature, reflecting personal growth or transformation.
    • 不觉有人排其踣着 - Sudden actions can create dramatic tension and reveal unexpected elements.
    • 乃得就身而稍苏活 - Recovery from a state of unconsciousness or near-death is a common theme in literature, symbolizing resilience.
    • 其人今休然尚存 - Survival and existence are recurring themes, often highlighting the enduring nature of life.

    Proper Nouns

    • 四娘 - A name or title, possibly a noblewoman or a character in a story.
    • 珠林九十一 - A specific section or chapter in a text, possibly a collection of stories or records.

    A servant was called to accompany, but the servant said, "I cannot accompany you, Lady Four, just go, you will meet on the way ahead." A horsewhip was given to her, saying, "Hold this whip carefully, you will know the way." After walking several miles, she saw a young wife, who was Four Lady's daughter-in-law, suffering greatly, her four limbs tightly bound, hung by the roadside, and they met with sorrowful cries. The young wife said, "In life, I committed sins, now I am receiving this suffering, I want to hit my cheek with my hand, begging for mercy and help, but my hands are restrained, I cannot reach my cheek." She also heard sounds of suffering around her, but could not see the forms. Four Lady asked, "What is this sound?" The answer was, "These are the unrighteous monks, who have broken their fasts and violated their precepts, receiving this suffering as retribution, the sound of their cries." Thus, she returned on the way, and soon arrived home. Seeing the remains, she felt very disgusted, no longer wished to return; unconsciously, someone pushed her down, and she gradually regained consciousness. The person now still exists. From the Jade Forest, Volume Ninety-One. [162]

    前齐永明中，扬都高座寺释慧进者，少雄勇游侠，年四十，忽悟非常，因出家。蔬食布衣，誓诵法华，用心劳苦，执卷便病。乃发愿造百部，以悔先障。始聚得一千六百文，贼来索物，进示经钱，贼惭而退。尔后遂成百部，故病亦愈。诵经既度，情愿又满，回此诵业，愿生安养。闻空中告曰：“汝愿已足，必得往生。”无病而卒，八十余矣。珠林九十五 [163]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [163]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 揚都 - Refers to Yangzhou, an important city in southern China during the Southern Dynasties.
    • 高座寺 - The name of a Buddhist temple in Yangzhou.
    • 釋慧進 - The name of a Buddhist monk.
    • 少雄勇游侠 - Describes a young man who was brave and adventurous.
    • 年四十，忽悟非常 - Indicates a sudden realization or enlightenment at the age of forty.
    • 因出家 - Refers to the act of becoming a monk.
    • 蔬食布衣 - Describes a simple and ascetic lifestyle, eating vegetables and wearing coarse clothing.
    • 誓诵法华 - A vow to recite the Lotus Sutra.
    • 用心劳苦 - Indicates hard work and effort.
    • 执卷便病 - Suggests that the monk became ill while reading or reciting scriptures.
    • 发愿造百部 - A vow to create or support the creation of a hundred copies of a text.
    • 以悔先障 - To atone for past obstacles or sins.
    • 始聚得一千六百文 - Initially collected one thousand six hundred coins.
    • 贼来索物 - Bandits came to demand things.
    • 进示经钱 - The monk showed the bandits the money intended for scriptures.
    • 贼惭而退 - The bandits were ashamed and left.
    • 尔后遂成百部 - After this, he completed the hundred copies.
    • 诵经既度 - Reciting the scriptures was completed.
    • 情愿又满 - His wishes were fulfilled.
    • 回此诵业，愿生安养 - He redirected his recitation efforts to wish for rebirth in the Pure Land.
    • 闻空中告曰 - Heard a voice from the air saying.
    • 汝愿已足，必得往生 - "Your wishes are fulfilled, and you will definitely be reborn."
    • 无病而卒，八十余矣 - Died without illness, over eighty years old.
    • 珠林九十五 - The source of the text, likely a collection or anthology.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 前齐永明中 - Refers to the Yongming period of the Southern Qi Dynasty.
    • 法华 - The Lotus Sutra, a major Mahayana Buddhist text.
    • 百部 - A hundred copies of a text, often referring to the Lotus Sutra.
    • 安养 - The Pure Land, a realm of bliss in Buddhist cosmology.
    • 珠林九十五 - Likely a reference to a collection or anthology, possibly '珠林' (Jade Forest) or a similar work.

    Proper Nouns

    • 前齐永明中 - The Yongming period of the Southern Qi Dynasty.
    • 扬都 - Yangzhou, a city in southern China.
    • 高座寺 - A Buddhist temple in Yangzhou.
    • 释慧进 - The name of the monk.
    • 法华 - The Lotus Sutra.
    • 安养 - The Pure Land.
    • 珠林九十五 - The source of the text, likely a collection or anthology.

    During the Yongming period of the Southern Qi Dynasty, in Yangzhou, at the Gaozuo Temple, there was a monk named Shihuijin. When he was young, he was brave and adventurous. At the age of forty, he suddenly had a profound realization and decided to become a monk. He lived a simple and ascetic life, eating vegetables and wearing coarse clothing, and vowed to recite the Lotus Sutra. His efforts were arduous, and he often fell ill while reading or reciting the scriptures. He then made a vow to create a hundred copies of the text to atone for past obstacles. Initially, he collected one thousand six hundred coins. Bandits came to demand things, and the monk showed them the money he had collected for the scriptures. The bandits were ashamed and left. After this, he completed the hundred copies, and his illness was cured. After finishing the recitation of the scriptures, his wishes were fulfilled. He redirected his recitation efforts to wish for rebirth in the Pure Land. He heard a voice from the air saying, "Your wishes are fulfilled, and you will definitely be reborn." He died without illness, over eighty years old. This story is recorded in the "Jade Forest" (珠林) volume 95. [163]

    沙门安法开者，北人也，尝见吴公，长三尺，自屋堕地，旋回而去。御览九百四十六
    　　元嘉八年蒲阪城中大灾火，里中小屋虽焚，而于煨烬下得金经，纸素如故。苏易简文房四谱四
    　　晋世有竺长舒者，本天竺人，专心诵观世音经为业，后居吴中。于时邑内遭火，屋宇连栋，甍檐相接，火至皆焚，无能为救。长舒家正在下风，分意烧毁，一心唤观世音。欲至舒家，风回火灭，竟家获免。合县惊异，叹其有神。时有凶恶少年，怪其老胡，有何灵应，火烧不然？到后夜风急，少年以火投屋，四投皆灭，年少嗟感，至明，乃叩头首过。舒云：“我无神力，常以观世音为业，每有事恒得免脱也。”辩正论八注 [164]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [164]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 沙门安法开者，北人也 - 沙门 refers to a Buddhist monk, 安法开 is the monk's name, and 北人也 indicates that he is from the north. This phrase introduces the subject of the passage.
    • 尝见吴公，长三尺，自屋堕地，旋回而去 - 尝见 means 'once saw,' 吴公 is a mysterious figure, 长三尺 describes his height, 自屋堕地 means 'fell from the house to the ground,' and 旋回而去 means 'turned around and left.' This describes an unusual encounter.
    • 御览九百四十六 - 御览 is a reference to the *Taiping Guangji* (太平广记), a vast collection of stories and anecdotes, and 九百四十六 is the specific volume number.
    • 元嘉八年蒲阪城中大灾火 - 元嘉八年 is the year 431 AD during the Liu Song dynasty, 蒲阪 is a place name, and 大灾火 means 'a great fire disaster.' This sets the historical and geographical context.
    • 里中小屋虽焚，而于煨烬下得金经，纸素如故 - 里中 means 'in the village,' 小屋 means 'small houses,' 虽焚 means 'though burned,' 煨烬 means 'ashes,' 金经 means 'golden sutra,' and 纸素如故 means 'the paper remained as before.' This describes an unusual event during the fire.
    • 苏易简文房四谱四 - 苏易简 is the name of the author, 文房四谱 is the title of his work, and 四 refers to the fourth chapter or section.
    • 晋世有竺长舒者，本天竺人 - 晋世 refers to the Jin dynasty, 竺长舒 is the name of the person, and 本天竺人 means 'originally from India.' This introduces another subject.
    • 专心诵观世音经为业 - 专心 means 'diligently,' 诵 means 'recite,' 观世音经 is the *Guanyin Sutra*, and 为业 means 'as his profession.' This describes his practice.
    • 后居吴中 - 后 means 'later,' 居 means 'lived,' and 吴中 refers to the area around Suzhou. This indicates his relocation.
    • 邑内遭火，屋宇连栋，甍檐相接，火至皆焚，无能为救 - 邑内 means 'in the town,' 遭火 means 'suffered a fire,' 屋宇连栋 means 'houses connected in rows,' 甍檐相接 means 'roofs touching each other,' 火至皆焚 means 'the fire burned everything,' and 无能为救 means 'no one could stop it.' This describes the extent of the fire.
    • 长舒家正在下风，分意烧毁，一心唤观世音 - 下风 means 'downwind,' 分意烧毁 means 'thought it would be burned,' and 一心唤观世音 means 'called out to Guanyin with all his heart.' This describes his reaction.
    • 欲至舒家，风回火灭，竟家获免 - 欲至 means 'about to reach,' 风回 means 'the wind changed,' 火灭 means 'the fire was extinguished,' and 竟家获免 means 'his whole family was saved.' This describes the miraculous outcome.
    • 合县惊异，叹其有神 - 合县 means 'the whole county,' 惊异 means 'were amazed,' and 叹其有神 means 'admired his divine power.' This describes the reaction of the townspeople.
    • 时有凶恶少年，怪其老胡，有何灵应，火烧不然 - 时有 means 'there was,' 凶恶少年 means 'a wicked young man,' 怪其老胡 means 'wondered about the old foreigner,' 有何灵应 means 'what miraculous response,' and 火烧不然 means 'the fire didn't burn him.' This describes the skepticism of a young man.
    • 到后夜风急，少年以火投屋，四投皆灭，年少嗟感，至明，乃叩头首过 - 到后夜 means 'on the following night,' 风急 means 'the wind was strong,' 以火投屋 means 'threw fire at the house,' 四投皆灭 means 'all four attempts failed,' 年少嗟感 means 'the young man was moved,' 至明 means 'until dawn,' and 乃叩头首过 means 'then knelt and apologized.' This describes the young man's change of heart.
    • 舒云：‘我无神力，常以观世音为业，每有事恒得免脱也。’ - 舒云 means 'Zhu Changshu said,' 我无神力 means 'I have no divine power,' 常以观世音为业 means 'I always recite the *Guanyin Sutra* as my profession,' and 每有事恒得免脱也 means 'whenever there is trouble, I always escape.' This is his explanation.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 沙门 - Refers to Buddhist monks, indicating the religious context of the story.
    • 吴公 - A mysterious figure, possibly a deity or spirit, associated with supernatural events.
    • 元嘉八年 - The year 431 AD during the Liu Song dynasty, providing a historical context.
    • 蒲阪 - A place name, likely in Shanxi province, indicating the geographical setting.
    • 观世音经 - The *Guanyin Sutra*, a Buddhist text dedicated to the Bodhisattva Guanyin, emphasizing the spiritual practice.
    • 晋世 - The Jin dynasty (266–420 AD), providing a historical context.
    • 竺长舒 - A person of Indian origin living in China, reflecting the cultural exchange during the period.
    • 吴中 - The area around Suzhou, indicating a specific location.
    • 辩正论八注 - A commentary on the *Bianzheng Lun* (辨正论), a Buddhist text, indicating the scholarly context.

    Proper Nouns

    • 安法开 - The name of the first monk.
    • 吴公 - A mysterious figure.
    • 御览九百四十六 - A reference to the *Taiping Guangji* (太平广记), volume 946.
    • 元嘉八年 - The year 431 AD during the Liu Song dynasty.
    • 蒲阪 - A place name.
    • 金经 - The golden sutra.
    • 苏易简 - The name of the author of *Wenfang Sishu* (文房四谱).
    • 竺长舒 - The name of the second monk.
    • 观世音经 - The *Guanyin Sutra*.
    • 辩正论八注 - A commentary on the *Bianzheng Lun* (辨正论).

    A Buddhist monk named An Fakai, from the north, once saw a figure known as Wu Gong, who was three feet tall. The figure fell from a house to the ground and then turned around and left. This account is found in Taiping Guangji, volume 946.

    In the eighth year of the Yuanjia era (431 AD), there was a great fire in Puzhou city. Although the small houses in the village were burned, a golden sutra was found beneath the ashes, and the paper remained as before. This is recorded in the fourth chapter of Wenfang Sishu by Su Yijian.

    During the Jin dynasty, there was a man named Zhu Changshu, originally from India, who devoted himself to reciting the Guanyin Sutra as his profession. He later settled in Wu. At that time, the town suffered a fire, and the houses, connected in rows with their roofs touching, were all burned, with no one able to stop it. Zhu Changshu's family was located downwind, and he thought they would be burned. He called out to Guanyin with all his heart. Just as the fire was about to reach his house, the wind changed, the fire was extinguished, and his entire family was saved. The whole county was amazed and admired his divine power. At that time, there was a wicked young man who wondered about the old foreigner, thinking he had some miraculous response, as the fire did not burn him. On the following night, when the wind was strong, the young man threw fire at Zhu Changshu's house, but all four attempts failed. The young man was moved and, until dawn, knelt and apologized. Zhu Changshu said, "I have no divine power. I always recite the Guanyin Sutra as my profession, and whenever there is trouble, I always escape." This is recorded in the eighth chapter of Bianzheng Lun (辨正论). [164]

    晋世沙门僧洪住京师瓦官寺，当义熙十二年时，官禁镕铸，洪既发心铸丈六金像：“像若圆满，我死无恨。”便即偷铸，铸竟，像犹有模，所司收洪，禁在相府，锁械甚严。心念观世音，日诵百遍，便梦所铸金像往狱，手摩头曰：无虑。”其像胸前一尺许铜色燋沸。当洪禁日，感得国家牛马，不肯人栏，时以为怪。旬日敕至彭城洪因放免像即破模自现。同上
    　　史俊有学识，奉道而慢佛，常语人云：“佛是小神，不足事耳。”每见尊像，恒轻诮之，后因病脚挛，种种祈福，都无效验，其友人赵文谓曰：“经道福中，佛福第一，可试造观音像。”俊以病急，如言灌像，像成梦观音，遂差。辩正论八注云出宣验冥祥等记
    　　陈玄范妻张氏，精心奉佛，恒愿自作一金像，终身供养，有愿皆从。专心日久，忽有观音金像，连光五尺，见高座上。辩正论八注云出宣验冥祥等记 [165]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [165]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 像若圆满，我死无恨 - A vow or resolution, meaning 'If the image is completed, I will have no regrets even if I die.'
    • 心念观世音，日诵百遍 - Meditated on Guanyin and recited his name a hundred times daily.
    • 感得国家牛马，不肯入栏 - Brought about a phenomenon where national cattle and horses refused to enter their pens.
    • 可试造观音像 - You might try making an image of Guanyin.
    • 像即破模自现 - The image broke free from the mold and appeared on its own.
    • 像成梦观音，遂差 - The image was completed, and he dreamed of Guanyin, after which his condition improved.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋世 - Refers to the Jin Dynasty (266–420 CE).
    • 沙门 - Refers to Buddhist monks.
    • 观世音 - The bodhisattva Guanyin, a central figure in East Asian Buddhism.
    • 相府 - Refers to the Chancellor's office, a high administrative position in the imperial government.
    • 彭城 - Refers to Pengcheng, an ancient city in present-day Xuzhou, Jiangsu Province.
    • 史俊 - A historical figure who respected Daoism but was indifferent to Buddhism.
    • 陈玄范 - A historical figure, likely a high-ranking official or scholar.
    • 张氏 - Refers to Chen Xuanfan's wife, a devout Buddhist.
    • 辩正论八注 - The *Dian Zheng Lun* (Eight Annotations), a Buddhist commentary.
    • 宣验冥祥等记 - *Xuan Yan Ming Xiang* (Records of Manifestations and Auspicious Signs), a collection of Buddhist stories.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋世 - The Jin Dynasty.
    • 沙门 - Buddhist monks.
    • 观世音 - The bodhisattva Guanyin.
    • 相府 - The Chancellor's office.
    • 彭城 - The city of Pengcheng.
    • 史俊 - A historical figure.
    • 陈玄范 - A historical figure.
    • 张氏 - Chen Xuanfan's wife.
    • 辩正论八注 - The *Dian Zheng Lun* (Eight Annotations), a Buddhist commentary.
    • 宣验冥祥等记 - *Xuan Yan Ming Xiang* (Records of Manifestations and Auspicious Signs), a collection of Buddhist stories.

    During the Jin Dynasty, the Buddhist monk Hong resided at the Wuguan Temple in the capital. In the twelfth year of the Yixi era, the government had banned metal casting. Hong, having resolved to cast a golden image of the Buddha measuring sixteen chi, vowed, "If the image is completed, I will have no regrets even if I die." He secretly began the casting, but when it was finished, the image still had a mold. The authorities apprehended him, and he was imprisoned in the Chancellor's office with strict shackles. In his heart, he meditated on Guanyin and recited his name a hundred times daily. One night, he dreamed that the golden image he had cast came to him in prison, placing his hand on his head and saying, "Do not worry." The image's chest bore a patch of copper that appeared to be boiling. During Hong's imprisonment, it was said that national cattle and horses refused to enter their pens, which was considered a strange omen. After ten days, an imperial decree arrived in Pengcheng, leading to Hong's release. At that moment, the image broke free from its mold and appeared on its own.

    Shi Jun, a man of learning who respected Daoism but was indifferent to Buddhism, often told others, "Buddha is a minor deity, not worth serving." Whenever he saw revered Buddha images, he would mock them. Later, due to a leg ailment, he prayed for blessings in various ways, but none worked. His friend Zhao Wen advised him, "Among all forms of blessings, the blessings of Buddhism are the greatest. You might try making an image of Guanyin." Urged by his illness, Shi Jun cast the image as advised. When the image was completed, he dreamed of Guanyin, and his condition improved. This account is cited in the Dian Zheng Lun (Eight Annotations) from Xuan Yan Ming Xiang (Records of Manifestations and Auspicious Signs).

    Chen Xuanfan's wife, Zhang, devotedly served Buddhism. She always wished to make a golden image herself, to be worshipped for life. All her wishes were fulfilled. After long and dedicated practice, she suddenly saw a golden image of Guanyin, five feet tall with radiating light, appearing on a high seat. This account is also cited in the Dian Zheng Lun (Eight Annotations) from Xuan Yan Ming Xiang (Records of Manifestations and Auspicious Signs). [165]

    晋世沙门僧洪住京师瓦官寺，当义熙十二年时，官禁镕铸，洪既发心铸丈六金像：“像若圆满，我死无恨。”便即偷铸，铸竟，像犹有模，所司收洪，禁在相府，锁械甚严。心念观世音，日诵百遍，便梦所铸金像往狱，手摩头曰：无虑。”其像胸前一尺许铜色燋沸。当洪禁日，感得国家牛马，不肯人栏，时以为怪。旬日敕至彭城洪因放免像即破模自现。同上
    　　史俊有学识，奉道而慢佛，常语人云：“佛是小神，不足事耳。”每见尊像，恒轻诮之，后因病脚挛，种种祈福，都无效验，其友人赵文谓曰：“经道福中，佛福第一，可试造观音像。”俊以病急，如言灌像，像成梦观音，遂差。辩正论八注云出宣验冥祥等记
    　　陈玄范妻张氏，精心奉佛，恒愿自作一金像，终身供养，有愿皆从。专心日久，忽有观音金像，连光五尺，见高座上。辩正论八注云出宣验冥祥等记

    During the Jin Dynasty, the Buddhist monk Hong resided at the Wuguan Temple in the capital. In the twelfth year of the Yixi era, the government had banned metal casting. Hong, having resolved to cast a golden image of the Buddha measuring sixteen chi, vowed, "If the image is completed, I will have no regrets even if I die." He secretly began the casting, but when it was finished, the image still had a mold. The authorities apprehended him, and he was imprisoned in the Chancellor's office with strict shackles. In his heart, he meditated on Guanyin and recited his name a hundred times daily. One night, he dreamed that the golden image he had cast came to him in prison, placing his hand on his head and saying, "Do not worry." The image's chest bore a patch of copper that appeared to be boiling. During Hong's imprisonment, it was said that national cattle and horses refused to enter their pens, which was considered a strange omen. After ten days, an imperial decree arrived in Pengcheng, leading to Hong's release. At that moment, the image broke free from its mold and appeared on its own.

    Shi Jun, a man of learning who respected Daoism but was indifferent to Buddhism, often told others, "Buddha is a minor deity, not worth serving." Whenever he saw revered Buddha images, he would mock them. Later, due to a leg ailment, he prayed for blessings in various ways, but none worked. His friend Zhao Wen advised him, "Among all forms of blessings, the blessings of Buddhism are the greatest. You might try making an image of Guanyin." Urged by his illness, Shi Jun cast the image as advised. When the image was completed, he dreamed of Guanyin, and his condition improved. This account is cited in the Dian Zheng Lun (Eight Annotations) from Xuan Yan Ming Xiang (Records of Manifestations and Auspicious Signs).

    Chen Xuanfan's wife, Zhang, devotedly served Buddhism. She always wished to make a golden image herself, to be worshipped for life. All her wishes were fulfilled. After long and dedicated practice, she suddenly saw a golden image of Guanyin, five feet tall with radiating light, appearing on a high seat. This account is also cited in the Dian Zheng Lun (Eight Annotations) from Xuan Yan Ming Xiang (Records of Manifestations and Auspicious Signs).
